## Thread: COBOL v Other Languages — 40 msgs

### Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com>`

```
Arelem wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 30 Aug 1999 17:56:51 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> can see the code we used at:

Many thanks. That's what I was after - specific examples. You are right,
why re-invent the wheel if the algorithm is already written and proven
in another language. Basically Volke Bandeke in his reply made the same

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D003D3.8C0FE9F8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37cd5b2d.23091245@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com>`

```
Re : COBOL v Other Languages. Thanks to those who responded - and I
agree there are instances where it makes sense to use other languages,
particularly when linking to specific 'canned' routines, packages  etc.

Programming on a PC I haven't as yet found the urgency to jump into
other languages, and given OO COBOL I think I can overcome the points
that Volke Bandeke made about using link-lists and growing/shrinking
tables by using OO collections/dictionaries, (invoke myCollection
"growto" to 100 or invoke myCollection "growto(decrease)" to 2). I
should add, that as yet I haven't got into the Internet stuff -
familiarity with Java comes later. Agreed, you can only program with the
tools your specific COBOL compiler provides.

As an observation - under a separate thread "Move right justify", Alan
Russell said that's why he wrote an Assembler routine to 
left/centre/right-justify - and yet Judson McClendon shows, with an
example, that the problem can be solved using COBOL.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37cd5b2d.23091245@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com> <37D003D3.8C0FE9F8@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message +ADw-37D003D3.8C0FE9F8+AEA-home.com+AD4-...
+AD4-Re : COBOL v Other Languages. Thanks to those who responded - and I
+AD4-agree there are instances where it makes sense to use other languages,
+AD4-particularly when linking to specific 'canned' routines, packages  etc.
+AD4-

+AD4-that Volke Bandeke made about using link-lists and growing/shrinking
+AD4-tables by using OO collections/dictionaries, (invoke myCollection
+AD4AIg-growto+ACI- to 100 or invoke myCollection +ACI-growto(decrease)+ACI- to 2). I
+AD4-should add, that as yet I haven't got into the Internet stuff -
+AD4-familiarity with Java comes later. Agreed, you can only program with the
+AD4-tools your specific COBOL compiler provides.
+AD4-
+AD4-As an observation - under a separate thread +ACI-Move right justify+ACI-, Alan
+AD4-Russell said that's why he wrote an Assembler routine to
+AD4-left/centre/right-justify - and yet Judson McClendon shows, with an
+AD4-example, that the problem can be solved using COBOL.
+AD4-
+AD4-Jimmy, Calgary AB

Quite true Jimmy.  In fact, I wrote my original left/center/right routine in
COBOL in 1978 and wrote it to be callable from any of our mainframe
languages (COBOL, FORTRAN, PL/1, Assembler, at the time).  However, in order
to put this routine into our system subroutine library, the +ACo-powers that be+ACo-
in the tech support group would not allow use of such +ACo-inefficient+ACo-
languages as COBOL so I was forced to rewrite the COBOL into assembler.  It
may be more efficient in CPU usage, but I would venture that the
milliseconds of CPU time saved in the last 20 years has still not made up
for the loss in readability (I write very readable assembler just like I
write very readable COBOL, but many people can't read assembler not matter
how readable) and the extra time I needed to get it rewritten.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah+AEA-apci.com
Home - AHRussell+AEA-computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r4vlp$3t9$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37cd5b2d.23091245@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com> <37D003D3.8C0FE9F8@home.com> <7qp1mf$b8b@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
Hi yall

Not trying to make anyone mad, I guess my token is related to the need to
continuing to use COBOL at all. IMHO, monolithic processes and procedural
languages would eventually fade away to distributed objects and event-driven
languages and OSes (or implementations of such languages in the context of
OO under OSes.) So although I agree that the level of dependency to legacy
software is real, I advocate all new engagements to be gear towards n-tier
apps with standardized language pools, readily accessible at all levels
(trainee to Senior). COBOL is not an option for future, though ...

Just my 0.02 ...

Andre Rosenthal - moderator for comp.os.tpf
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D65F92.7BC3E1E1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37cd5b2d.23091245@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com> <37D003D3.8C0FE9F8@home.com> <7qp1mf$b8b@netnews1.apci.com> <7r4vlp$3t9$1@linuxfr.org>`

```
TPF Moderator wrote:
> 
> Hi yall
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just my 0.02 ...

The word monolithic implies large, as well as single purpose.  But it
has been my experience that OO's strength is in very large, very complex
systems.  What if your function is straight forward?  Why pay the cost
of the extra complexity of OO in that case?  Or what if the only event
you care about is CREATE THIS REPORT.  Event driven code is another
complexity.

Don't get me wrong, we there are many times when we need the complexity
of OO, of event-driven, and of distributed processing.  But their very
advantages, such as hidden and inaccessible code are also disadvantages
(where in that hidden, inaccessible code is that bug).

Sometimes simpler is better.  Sometimes straightforward is better.

Sometimes it is better to have several tools, designed to solve
different needs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D699B8.8A0DD688@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37cd5b2d.23091245@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com> <37D003D3.8C0FE9F8@home.com> <7qp1mf$b8b@netnews1.apci.com> <7r4vlp$3t9$1@linuxfr.org> <37D65F92.7BC3E1E1@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> TPF Moderator wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Howard,

Agreed - keep it simple.

I agree with you about "hidden" code - I know that is one of the mantras
put out about OO - you can use a method without knowing its inner
workings - but it's not for real. As you know I'm using NetExpress and I
can animate the source of all classes, (mine and Micro Focus), until the
point that I or MicroFocus make API calls.

If you are going to use a pre-canned method, then you have to know, as a
minimum, the parameters for the sending/receiving fields and whether
they are pic ??? or object references. For the most part, I don't need
to animate M/F classes, but it's there if I need it and they also
provide the source for all their classes.

I don't know the approach of the other OO languages, but for COBOL - I
think it is an absolute MUST that you have the source code of all
invoked classes. (Anybody trying to market COBOL pre-compiled classes
without source is on a hiding to nothing).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
TPF Moderator <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> wrote in message

> Hi yall
>
> Not trying to make anyone mad, I guess my token is related to the
need to
> continuing to use COBOL at all. IMHO, monolithic processes and
procedural
> languages would eventually fade away to distributed objects and
event-driven
> languages and OSes (or implementations of such languages in the
context of
> OO under OSes.) So although I agree that the level of dependency to
legacy
> software is real, I advocate all new engagements to be gear towards
n-tier
> apps with standardized language pools, readily accessible at all
levels
> (trainee to Senior). COBOL is not an option for future, though ...

Written by an academic with typical ivory tower thought processes
(four
sentences, one with 37 words - not even COBOL is that verbose).
Suppose
you are a real-world boss of a large (say insurance) company. You have
over 3,000 COBOL programs (comprising 15,000,000 lines of code) in
production. These programs run daily. You have 100 COBOL programmers
and 250,000 reels of magnetic tape containing historical data.

Your company decides to offer a new product (say, Y2K insurance). Are
you
gonna gear up for the language de jour (say JAVA++) on the faddiest OS
(say
Linux-Plus)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37cd5b2d.23091245@netnews.worldnet.att.net> <37CD9057.AF19BABB@home.com> <37D003D3.8C0FE9F8@home.com> <7qp1mf$b8b@netnews1.apci.com> <7r4vlp$3t9$1@linuxfr.org> <%SFB3.705$q%5.35934@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Hello again

In response to the question posted:

If I have to move away from the problem space below:

You have over 3,000 COBOL programs (comprising 15,000,000 lines of code) in
production. These programs run daily. You have 100 COBOL programmers and
250,000 reels of magnetic tape containing historical data.

I will spend some time with the architecture and analysis team to collect
the business logic for the problem above. Out of the 15M lines, about 30-45%
is UI related, either via collecting a value or performing syntax/semantic
analysis.

Then parallel to the actual system said company have, install an array of
Dec Alpha boxes running Windows NT 4 SP5 or even W2K beta, said machines are
clustered and front-ended by load-balancing servers also running in the OS
above, install MTS, SQL and MSMQ, then convert the spagetti code above to
VB. Front-ends are on a web browser using whatever script language you
desire for it is virutally the same. Convert the data base into SQL 7 as the
last step and have the system running in parallel for about 6 months.

I would also note that I wont need the reel anymore. SCSI tapes can do just
a good job for a fraction of the cost.

After my new system is running near beta completion, cut-over 2 geogrical
regions and check the results. Then fase-off completely your MVS and party
hard.

SOunds simple .. it is, and works .... I would even considering maintaining
the DB2 side of the equation at MVS for a while accessing data via COM-TI
but that is another topic altogether ....

Best regards,
Andre Rosenthal - moderator for comp.os.tpf ... which is a pure Assembler OS
!
```

```
Right. 

I'm no COBOL lover, but the equivalent of 15,000,000 lines of COBOL in VB and
JAVA gives me the willies.

PC gurus at work seem to have problems deploying phone books :-).

BTW, it's good to see you're ready to go with the Microsoft-Alpha combo, but

work (http://www.microsoft.com/NTServer/nts/news/msnw/compaq.asp)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com>`

```
Howdy

Indeed MS discontinued the support for AXR processors but as of today's
market, one can deploy them without much fuss. WHen IA64 becomes de-facto
the 64 bit processor of choice, we can port all WIn32 into Win64 ....

As for the 15M lines of Cobol -> VB, there is a misundestanding about the
business logic of these lines in COBOL. Needless to say that well over 1/3
is UI related and about 1/3 syntax analysis, leaving a miniscule 1/3, or
500K lines of actual business logic, completely obsolete and patched over
the years. The end result is a compressed < 200K lines of actual portable
information, easily packageable onto MTS components in either VB or VC.
Let's face it, COBOL was a good language, such as APL once was, but the
future is dimm. Having a /390 housing DB2 makes sense. Having MVS run COBOL
code to generate reports makes no sense. Neither having COBOL programs
running obsolete business logic.

As an example, I opened an account at Wells Fargo yesterday. The UI was dry,
meaningless, cryptic, and slow as molasses. Much more can be achieve by
having ASP pages running (or any equivalent server side CGI-based code) on a
web server, DHTML or XML pages, and app servers controlling the transaction.
I had the oportunity to port a whole bank from MVS with CICS into Windows
and SQL servers, slashing millions of said COBOL lines, and ending up with a
system that scales correctly and performed 7.2 times faster than the MVS
equivalent. That is not to say that MVS is a bad OS, on the contrary, I
still consider it the nicest OS ever written. And I also once taught COBOL
and Advanced COBOL, but that was in the mid 80's.

No serious CIO/CTO would invest new software money into a COBOL solution.
And the ones that do it have a very bad understanding of modern software
engineering and the level of sofistication quadra boxes have, being them
Intel or Alpha. The technology of clustering quadra processors or
8-processor boxes is real and works like a charm. Nowadays, we can do
whatever was done by MVS on Windows.

To conclude, last year I served as an advisor for a University asking if
COBOL should be eliminated from the curriculum. I voted yes, some others
don't. At the end, it was removed from the core, and placed as an elective.
Last semester NO Students enrolled in the elective ...

Best regards,
Andre Rosenthal - moderator for comp.os.tpf

news:19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com...

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >You have over 3,000 COBOL programs (comprising 15,000,000 lines of code)
in

> >250,000 reels of magnetic tape containing historical data.
> >

> >the business logic for the problem above. Out of the 15M lines, about
30-45%

syntax/semantic
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

- **Message-ID:** `<37d9bf08.85017229@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Fri, 10 Sep 1999 20:14:04 -0500, "TPF Moderator"
<tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> wrote:

>No serious CIO/CTO would invest new software money into a COBOL solution.
>And the ones that do it have a very bad understanding of modern software
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I work at one of the largest, if not the largest, processors of the
type of data we process (sorry to be cryptic, but I must).  We run a
bevy of OSs and machines.  Our MAIN processes run on the DEC OpenVMS
Cluster. We have an OS/390 machine that works as a transaction servoer
for some processes that communicate with IP sockets, we do client
server all day long.   We know clustering and its power well.  Guess
what all the code is written in?  COBOL!  

*IF* the solution is Windows Clustering instead of mainframes or Dec
servers,in the future, there's no reason the applications can' t be
most easily converted, most CHEAPLY maintained, most accurately
updated, and most QUICKLY developed  - in COBOL.  Windows does not
mean VB and C++.  

COBOL means business.
```

```
The number of tn3278 sessions were near 3000. The scalabilty factor was to
go to 6000 and then to 9000. Without entering in more details, the
architecture scaled w/o loss of measured response time from 3000 to 12000
hitting the servers and the response time for all bank xsactions were
epsilon+/-7 seconds. I cannot disclose the actual topology or architecture
but trivial readings on Windows DNA documents would give you the correct
idea. All machines were Intel and the OS was NT 4.0 SP3.

Best regards,
Andre
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net>`

```
I understand the frustration of some people in not comprehending the move
towards a leaner process. My goal here is not to steer people's emotions
towards a defendent vs. plaintiff situation. Rather, since I myself came
from the big iron era, I was voicing my experiences.

Now once more let's examine the details

1) 20 years of patching software (80-99) gives lots of reduntant business
logic that is not only obsolete but has been patched with IF/THEN blocks or
sometimes commented out.
2) 200 thousand lines of Business logic does not qualify for small potatoes.
An average distributed system under Windows would not pass that value when
comparing apples to apples. That is, in the counter above, one should not
take into consideration ID, ENVIRONMENT, nor data division as these parts do
not influence the business logic, only setting the context.
3) By actual portable info one consideres legacy code that can be converted
to modern technology without loss of generality. Please consider attending a
class on Software reduction techniques (normally a level 7000 on the PhD
program.)
4) Eliminate ALL report related programs. Cristal does a wonderfull job in
doing the ad-hoc SQL based reports or pre-programmed ones directly on the
LAN without any problems. Remember that on a distributed network, the LAN
has authority under its constraints thus taking over WAN whenever necessary.
5) As for being a troll, there is no need to go that route. I am simply an
architect that knows how to replace legacy monolithic apps (or semi TP ones
running under CICS) to distributed systems. Nothing special about it, just
another skill one acquires if the desire is there.
6) Again, when you eliminate syntax and semantic analysis from your code,
then remove ALL UI stuff from it, you will be amazed by the 2/3 reduction in
size. That is not to say that the FP values would not go up/down when you
start reverse engineering the code onto a design for n-tier platforms. Now
from the 1/3 left, there are several error-handling situations such as calls
from CICS that should be rolled back and thus that code itself can go away.
If you still want to use DB2 at the POS level, you can get the results from
your ODBC or OLEBD drivers and call MTS to roll-back then pass a web page as
result. After that, connect to your mail server and email the admin alerting
the fact or write the event log, or both.
7) You conclude that a Business logic is obsolete by interviewing the key
analists from your organization and asking if the BS in there should remain
or it is old. For instance, there is no need to maintain code dealing with
the exchange rate of Australes when the Peso has replaced it, but some Latin
American banks still have tons of code dealing with both currencies because
the country was dual currency for some time. The same happened in Brazil and
Mexico. As it turned out, reports were written in both currencies,
transactions as well, transations from them to Dollar and Brt.Pound, etc.
This is a simple clue that this BS should not be ported.
Another is when the moneraty laws changes, creating or eliminating a method
of CDs that were deemed legal but became ilegal, such as overnight
applications to foreign banks, and how to roll the profits ...
8) Again about the <200K portable code, it is not to say that the system
ended up having <200K because in WIndows we do not use KLOC as a metric.
Rather, serious Software engineers use FPs and other techiniques to measure
the code complexity. Another good class (also 7000 level) is SOftware
Complexity Metrics at your nearest PhD-offering University. The first cut
was to roll the BS from these 200K of lines onto VB components running on
MTS, sill accessing the DB2 back-end. More components were written, some in
VB some in VC, to accomplish different task. The whole project took 5 months
AFTER IDENTIFYING THE CORRECT 200K OF LINES OF COURSE, which itself took 6
months!

To end this, I believe COBOL was the main language for  business for the
longest time, and will continue to exists as far as maintenance is
concerned. But when facing the power of desktops running quadra processors
at 64bit OSs having App Servers deployed easily via WAN+LAN topologies, and
coding techiniques in VB and VC widely accessible and documented as KBs,
there is little incentive to spend $$$ in a COBOL solution. It may be true
that one or two CIOs still believe this is the way to go, but remember that
IBM did think OS2 was the way to go as well. Everything has it time, one day
VB/VC will be replaced by other technologies and Windows as an OS may not
even be what we know of it today, and such is life. You will be surprised by
the amount of Fortune 1000 corporations leaving the COBOL arena into
EJB/CORBA on Unix or WinDNA/COM on Windows ...

Best regards,
Ande Rosenthal, moderator of comp.os.tpf

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rtl0503k1iv42@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org>`

```
A massive re-engineering exercise runs the serious risk of losing the
detailed
business rules.   One can ask the "analysts", but it is probably much safer
to preserve the logic from the COBOL programs and delete it on a case by
case basis rather than rebuilding all that code from scratch.

Regarding the "Phd Level 7000 Software Reduction class..."
Here's the PhD in a nutshell:  Parse that legacy code.  All 15 million
lines.
State which parts are useless ("dump the UI") in some formal notation
processable by a tool.    Apply the tool to remove the UI.   Define formal
transforms to map your source language(s! It won't all be just COBOL)
to you desired target (this part is actually hard), and run it over the
source code.
The result: code in a new language with no loss of business rules.

A tool that can do this can be seen at
http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMSToolkit.htm.
Seriously.

>6) Again, when you eliminate syntax and semantic analysis from your code,
```

```
I agree that there exists automation engines that would parse the BL from a
code in a given language and spit out the new code on another. Since you
seemed to have done a PhD as well, you know the power of Co-Co and its
derivatives!

What we did was similar, removing UI based and syntax/semantic analysis out
via cross-decompilation, but having rules spit out instead of a language.
That way we could sense what language was appropriate for a given task,
being them VB or VC++; cross-boundaries were tied using DCOM couplers and
all worked well.

I will look into the URL sent ... thanks for the kind reply :)

Andre
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DA8613.1AB6E5F2@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org>`

```
As someone who is still programming in COBOL and on mainframes I may be
biased.  I disagree with the idea that everything can be ported to
Visual Basic and C.  My skepticism about Visual Basic comes from things
I have seen from other posters.  My basic skepticism about C comes from
the fact that it doesn't have a fixed decimal data type.  Writing a
class in C++ to handle this is an extremely inefficient way of
overcoming a basic shortcoming in the business and administrative
computing environment.

I agree with the poster that much of the "business" logic in our
application inventories is obsolete and even potentially dangerous.  Of
course, if our organization is using packages, its applications will
have a large percentage of code that is not used by the organization. 
Examples would be the latest office suite or ERP package from vendor of
choice or affliction.
Further, while COBOL seems better than C, FORTRAN and maybe PL1 for
reports, that is legitimately damning it with faint praise.  The report
writer is so feeble that most IBM customers never noticed that it went
away when they converted to the version of IBM COBOL where it went away
(either COBOL 74 or 85) because IBM dropped support of that optional
component. Replacing COBOL code with report writers like Crystal (which
I don't know anything about),  DYL280, Natural or similar tool can bring
about some people productivity improvements.  If you are using
databases, some of the validation code and cross checking can and should
be done by the database environment and the rules kept and maintained in
it.  Further much of our organizational processing should be driven by
tables (income tax calculations and handling the sales plan of the
minute come to mind as prime examples).  Using web interfaces also seems
to make sense.  Thus I agree that various methods and tools provide far
better ways of handling certain portions of our "business" processing
than will any procedural language. However, this still leaves a large
amount of code to be written with some procedural tool or procedural
portion of a tool.  I have used both DYL280 (now Vision) from Sterling
Software and Natural from Software AG.  Their procedural constructs look
a lot like COBOL.  Further for some things COBOL beats them hands down. 
Use of COBOL 89 can cut the amount of coding required to do many
functions such as the handling of the tables mentioned earlier.  COBOL
will generate at least as efficient as that generated by C for most of
these functions.  Since Visual Basic is an interpretive language, I
doubt that it will be in the ball park from an execution time
perspective.

In so far as UNIX and Windows NT servers being able to replace
mainframes, both environments seem to be even more mediocre than the
mainframe for maintaining a reliable service that performs in accordance
with management expectations. As a former MVS systems programmer, I know
that I am more confused by both Windows 9x and OS2 Warp than I was by
MVS.        

In short, COBOL is a lot more powerful for handling today's business
needs than its detractors will admit.  It would be even more powerful if
the standards organizations would reorganize so that features and
functions are sent through the process separately and simple changes
don't get held up by complex ones.  There should be a new standard every
year.  The first attempt at this type of processing failed so instead of
fixing the organizational problems, they gave up and went back to
monolithic releases that take forever and a century to get done.

Similarly, the mainframe can do a lot more than many of its detractors
believe it can.  Many of the tools developed for the other environments
have been ported to it and combined with tools that are not easily
available in the other areas to make the mainframe the most powerful
server environment for many purposes.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, cmorris@fox.nstn.ca
  Posting while in the province of New Brunswick Canada from
morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca     
TPF Moderator wrote:
> 
> I understand the frustration of some people in not comprehending the move
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7re5h0$qkt$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA8613.1AB6E5F2@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Hi Clark

VB is not interpreted. You can compile the code using VB6 and the result is
independent of the interpreter. Also, these are DLLs and not exes, running
surrogated onto MTS. Now as far as having a decimal representation, that on
itself is not needed because the BL is independent of the internal format
the data is stored. For instance, is of no used debating if a counter should
be COMP-2 or COMP-4 because it is internal to the DLL calculating it, and it
is returned to the caller in a variant type. You can dynamically alter it
w/o loss of generality. That on itself is again useless for FORTRAN apps,
since the precision is tightly couple with the UI representation, and said
apps are monolythic by nature. In fact, there are several libraries for C++
that contain pre-defined classes for high-precision math, but MFC is not one
of them. As far as string manipulation is concerned, C is lowsy, but C++ is
awesome. Using MFC for instance, you can get CString values handled in
various ways, reverse syntax and the likes. The vast majority of business
apps handle simple math types, and setting them as currency and binary
suffice for most cases. To this day I have not seen a hard conversion from
any COBOL type into a simpler atomic value of OLECURRENCY or long/double.

Tables are things of the past. They are all maskarated into recordsets
handled directly by the DBMS or faked under ADO classes. Like I said, one
cannot convert an app in COBOL using its technology, and thus the concept of
tables are non existent. Whenever said concept is needed, a temp recordset
or SQL-table is created and handled by the object model w/o loss of
generality.

I agree with the confusion insofar as Win9x and OS/2 is concerned. No one in
the full possession of their intellect would deploy a system on these
platforms. Windows NT is a superb app server when used in conjunction with
MTS, MSMQ a SQL servers, either native of clustered. As in MVS, the app
server architect is not in the full domain of all NT technology and thus an
integrator person should be part of the architecture team to handle the NT
environment itself. Both architects would then define the topology and
security models, deployment models, WAN/LAN schemas, etc. A DBA for SQL is
also needed. It is not a trivial job to engineer a legacy system onto NT and
that is why we charge 250 - 350 per hour to do such things!

All that I am saying is that under that topology, using COBOL is non-sense.
Mainframes still are the best POS one could find, far superior than any
Oracle and SQL servers all combined. But using it to house BL is passe big
time. I agree in keeping DB2, IMS, Oracle for MVS, etc, under a /390 but to
deploy a new system using the mainframe is way too unflexible for today's
needs. Consider the remote utilization, done via private lines costing a
fortune. All clients running a tn3278 emulator accessing the crude UI of
80's. Syntax analysis done at the app level, error handling as well ... it
costs too much, guys ....

Andre

Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote in message
news:37DA8613.1AB6E5F2@nbnet.nb.ca...
> As someone who is still programming in COBOL and on mainframes I may be
> biased.  I disagree with the idea that everything can be ported to
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I understand the frustration of some people in not comprehending the
move
> > towards a leaner process. My goal here is not to steer people's emotions
> > towards a defendent vs. plaintiff situation. Rather, since I myself came
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 1) 20 years of patching software (80-99) gives lots of reduntant
business
> > logic that is not only obsolete but has been patched with IF/THEN blocks
or
> > sometimes commented out.
> > 2) 200 thousand lines of Business logic does not qualify for small
potatoes.
> > An average distributed system under Windows would not pass that value
when
> > comparing apples to apples. That is, in the counter above, one should
not
> > take into consideration ID, ENVIRONMENT, nor data division as these
parts do
> > not influence the business logic, only setting the context.
> > 3) By actual portable info one consideres legacy code that can be
converted
> > to modern technology without loss of generality. Please consider
attending a
> > class on Software reduction techniques (normally a level 7000 on the PhD
> > program.)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > concerned. But when facing the power of desktops running quadra
processors
> > at 64bit OSs having App Servers deployed easily via WAN+LAN topologies,
and
> > coding techiniques in VB and VC widely accessible and documented as KBs,
> > there is little incentive to spend $$$ in a COBOL solution. It may be
true
> > that one or two CIOs still believe this is the way to go, but remember
that
> > IBM did think OS2 was the way to go as well. Everything has it time, one
day
> > VB/VC will be replaced by other technologies and Windows as an OS may
not
> > even be what we know of it today, and such is life. You will be
surprised by
> > the amount of Fortune 1000 corporations leaving the COBOL arena into
> > EJB/CORBA on Unix or WinDNA/COM on Windows ...
> >
```

```
<tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> wrote:


>6) Again, when you eliminate syntax and semantic analysis from your code,
>then remove ALL UI stuff from it, you will be amazed by the 2/3 reduction in
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Where I work, we use Oracle, and not DB2, but you are missing my point
from before.  We use COBOL and do all these things you mention above -
including the e-mail/pager system monitoring!  You don't have to give
up COBOL if you decide to go with the architecure you describe.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7re62c$qst$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org> <37da8b86.137375324@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Indeed you dont have to give up COBOL, mostly because under the big iron it
wont run. All that I am saying is, provided you want to move away from
having BL housed at /390's, using COBOL is meaningless. WHy in heavens one
will use MF COBOL is beyond my mind. SInce you moved away from mainframes,
it is just logical to leave its tools where they belong.

Also, since more and more companies are indeed leaving the Mainframe arena
to a more flexible and cheaper data processing platform, COBOL will remain
on the maintenance levels of said enterprises and eventually become a thing
of the past.

Consider the events that lead to the demise of Assembler as a professional
tool for programming /370+. With the exception of TPF and ALCS, no one uses
BAL anymore. Even the most complex things can be ported to PL/1 or FORTRAN
or even COBOL w/o loss of generality. BAL is used solely when the OS is so
thightly coupled with the app server that you can't break clean. And even
then, C/370 is already moving on and c++/370 is a reality. In no time TPF
will be completely ported to C or C++ leaving BAL as the last resource for
maintenance of legacy apps too convoluted to be touched.  It is very hard to
find a job these days as a PL/1 or BAL programmer away from the airlines and
banking industries, and even those will eventually move onto a slimmer
solution.

This is not a bad thing for COBOL. It is simply sign of the times ...

Regards ...
Andre



Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37da8b86.137375324@news1.ibm.net...
> On Sat, 11 Sep 1999 10:03:26 -0500, "TPF Moderator"
> <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

in
> >size. That is not to say that the FP values would not go up/down when you
> >start reverse engineering the code onto a design for n-tier platforms.
Now
> >from the 1/3 left, there are several error-handling situations such as
calls
> >from CICS that should be rolled back and thus that code itself can go
away.
> >If you still want to use DB2 at the POS level, you can get the results
from
> >your ODBC or OLEBD drivers and call MTS to roll-back then pass a web page
as
> >result. After that, connect to your mail server and email the admin
alerting
> >the fact or write the event log, or both.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7reath$rg6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org> <37da8b86.137375324@news1.ibm.net> <7re62c$qst$1@linuxfr.org>`

```
TPF Moderator <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:7re62c$qst$1@linuxfr.org...
> Indeed you dont have to give up COBOL, mostly because under the big iron
it
> wont run. All that I am saying is, provided you want to move away from
> having BL housed at /390's, using COBOL is meaningless. WHy in heavens one
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> on the maintenance levels of said enterprises and eventually become a
thing
> of the past.

I really wonder how many of the facilities of Micro Focus (or Fujitsu or
even IBM VisualAge) COBOL "TPF Moderator" is aware of.  I am not saying that
I agree with his "paradigm shift" view of rewriting/redoing existing
applications (much less "mandating" what language should be used to do new
development) BUT it seems like all the "wonderful" new things that he/she
thinks are in NON-COBOL programming languages (and tools) are already
available in many, MANY of the workstation COBOL tools/products.

Certainly, OO, "GUI UI builders," encapsulation, etc are all available.
Also the fact that COBOL often (certainly not always) "beats" the
performance of VB and C++ in some independently run tests gives one "pause"
to wonder what the actual advantage is supposed to be!

There is (see other recent and past threads) LOTS of workstation and
client/server based COBOL production code (as well as existing mainframe
applications).  Some use "character based" interfaces BECAUSE that is what
the end users wanted - but the assumption that "modern" COBOL doesn't have
the facilities to do what modern application development requries is usually
made by people who haven't looked at a COBOL compiler since 1985
enhancements - much less more recent advances.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

- **Message-ID:** `<7revol$ftl@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Now don't be dissing Visual Age COBOL!! :) :) 
But the answer has to be 'not many.' Probably just
not experienced with enterprise sized problems
yet - most of which are ideally suited for mainframes.

-Paul
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> I wish we could loose some of the generality.  I will not be around to see
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Good on you, Warren and Bill Klein.

I was getting a little bemused by the TPF Moderator's 'tehcnicaleese' -
I was beginning to wonder if I had really been speaking English all
these years ?

It would be pointless to suggest that some other language wont supersede
COBOL at some future date - but certainly not in the foreseeable future.
When these 'champions' talk about the verbosity of COBOL they always
forget to mention all the comment statements included in those neat C,
C++, Pascal et al languages - telling you what the hell the code is
doing - a rarity in a well-written COBOL program.

And if it ever happens, COBOL2000+ with functions, OO and all the other
'goodies' is a direct challenge to the 'crypto' languages.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "The Programmer" <someone@somewhere.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hWGC3.7871$ES2.72075@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org> <37da8b86.137375324@news1.ibm.net> <7re62c$qst$1@linuxfr.org> <7rej2l$3sl$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37DB31D3.81815192@home.com>`

```
Hoo boy... here we go again... ::sigh::

I remember back in 1982, when the spanking new VAX was rolled in through the
front door, I was summarily informed by my new MIS Director that COBOL is a
dead language. The wondrous new 4GLs were taking over, so I'd better learn
them. Six months later the company purchased VAX/COBOL, and within another
six months I was busy busting my hump converting all my DTR code to COBOL.
The MIS Director was fired for incompetence. YeahRightSure... a dead
language.

Then, in 1988, at my next job, I was urged to learn C by one of the company
vice-presidents, because COBOL is just going nowhere. No one would be
developing any new applications using an obsolete language. Everyone knew
that. I think it was about three months after that, I ended up ripping my
hair out trying to learn RM/COBOL, purchased to develop a new application
that would run on the IBM PC/XT. That vice-president was among those who
were fired for incompetence. YeahRightSure... an obsolete language.

In 1992, I think it was, I was told that my COBOL coding skills had become
useless. Since Microsoft, and Bill Gates, was on the brink of taking over
IBM, the only application development language worth considering was
obviously C++. In fact, this academic from FAU couldn't understand why I
just smiled at him. It was about a week or so later that I was introduced to
COBOL/400. I picked it up real fast, and haven't bothered looking back ever
since. The academic is still teaching at FAU: Computer Science, I believe.
YeahRightSure... a useless language.

Then, in 1994, 1995, 1996, 1997, 1998 I kept being told that as soon as the
Y2K crisis has been solved by the new and improved version of Visual BASIC
(or Visual C, or Visual SmallTalk, or Visual DuckSpeak, or whatever...), old
COBOL dinosaurs like me will be buried and forgotten and everyone on the
planet will go forth and celebrate their freedom by buying a new PC running
Windows v234.56 or MacOS or OS/2 or something.

YeahRightSure... whatever... just let's hope the S/390 printing everyone's
paycheck doesn't decide to hiccup, or guess who's gonna catch hell.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rjp65$8sa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org> <37da8b86.137375324@news1.ibm.net> <7re62c$qst$1@linuxfr.org> <7rej2l$3sl$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37DB31D3.81815192@home.com> <hWGC3.7871$ES2.72075@news3.mia>`

```
ROTFL  ...  I know exactly what you mean (and maybe even the same VPs!).
You forgot to mention, however, all of the end-users who (after said VP
implemented all that wonderful new technology) were making comments
like: this used to run much faster.  What do you mean I can't do that
anymore.  If I reboot aren't I going to lose all this work?

I have to wonder whether, or not, those who project the demise of COBOL
have any concept of how computer hardware actually works.  IMHO it
seems that, if they did, they would choose the programming language
best suited for the task at hand (COBOL or otherwise) based upon it's
ability to perform that task in the most efficient and cost-effective
manner possible given A) the real costs of development and B) the real
costs of continued usage of the end-product; rather than waste all of
this time touting unsupportable and meaningless "statistics" concerning
such things as lines of source code (as if that determines
efficiency!).  It's okay with me though.  Think of all the work it is
going to generate after the "real" Y2K bomb (all of the unreliable,
crash-prone, inefficient, programs and systems that were sold to the
unwary and uninformed) hits!  Granted; MS and others will continue to
spend millions in an effort to convince end-users that it is "normal"
for there systems to be crashing on a regular basis but, I suspect that
once the "fear factor" is no longer a factor there will be many who
will want systems with a MTBF greater than 24hrs and consistently
reliable information. :-)

  "The Programmer" <someone@somewhere.com> wrote:
> Hoo boy... here we go again... ::sigh::
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DD7089.CE847186@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DA07D6.C710B0A7@att.net> <7rdqof$lqc$1@linuxfr.org> <37da8b86.137375324@news1.ibm.net> <7re62c$qst$1@linuxfr.org> <7rej2l$3sl$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37DB31D3.81815192@home.com> <hWGC3.7871$ES2.72075@news3.mia> <7rjp65$8sa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
trblshtr wrote:

> I have to wonder whether, or not, those who project the demise of COBOL
> have any concept of how computer hardware actually works.  

I am more concerned in them having a concept on how the businesses which
are being supported work.

> IMHO it
> seems that, if they did, they would choose the programming language
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> efficiency!).

The #1 criterion of efficiency is "does it do what the users want"?
After that, criteria aren't as solidly ordered, but arguably, 
#2 = Can it be efficiently changed to meet new business requirements?
#3 = Can it be efficiently debugged?
#4 = Is its life cycle signifcantly longer than its cost payback period?
#5 = Can it be efficiently migrated to a new platform as hardware costs
and needs change?

Also, Is it reliable?  Are its cycles tested in the real world over
time?  Is it secure? Can users control what they care about (here Cobol
falls short)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<36276D9548FD10C1.8187837D3F675086.D06BDE2CAE9AEA5D@lp.airnews.net>`

```
I really didn't want to get into a "language war" thing here with this
thread, but TPF has made so many assumptions, I feel I must make some
reply.  See comments below.




On Sat, 11 Sep 1999 13:16:28 -0500, "TPF Moderator"
<tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> enlightened us:

>Indeed you dont have to give up COBOL, mostly because under the big iron it
>wont run. All that I am saying is, provided you want to move away from
>having BL housed at /390's, using COBOL is meaningless. WHy in heavens one
>will use MF COBOL is beyond my mind. SInce you moved away from mainframes,
>it is just logical to leave its tools where they belong.

Whoever said Cobol was a tool only for mainframes?  If that were true
then what you are implying is that Fujitsu, Merant and others are just
plain stupid for producing a Cobol compiler(s) for the PC.  I'd think
the CEO's of those companies might have something to say about that.
And how does your position fit with minis?  Cobol can be run and is
used extensively on AS400 and other similar machines.


>Also, since more and more companies are indeed leaving the Mainframe arena
>to a more flexible and cheaper data processing platform, COBOL will remain
>on the maintenance levels of said enterprises and eventually become a thing
>of the past.
>

I'd like to see some proof that more and more companies are leaving
the mainframe arena.  It just isn't true.  If anything, companies are
adding servers to compliment their mainframes.  Remember, some years
back when distributed processing was hot, people were predicting the
demise of the mainframe and the mid-range, mini would take over.
Didn't happen.  Distributed processing died.  It's now back in a
slightly different form, but it certainly won't totally replace
mainframes.

>Consider the events that lead to the demise of Assembler as a professional
>tool for programming /370+. With the exception of TPF and ALCS, no one uses
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Demise of Assembler!!!  What planet do you live on anyway?  Certainly
there aren't many large applications written in Assembler these days,
but there are plenty of support applications and programs written in
Assembler and it remains a viable language in the IBM mainframe world
today.  The company that I work for writes and markets software for
the telecom industry and the banking industry.  Approximately 75% of
the code is Cobol.  Another 20% is Assembler and the rest is C.  I
personally spend just as much time writing Cobol as I do Assembler.
Your assertions are just not based in fact.  


>This is not a bad thing for COBOL. It is simply sign of the times ...
>

You should retake that Reading Signs 101 class.  

>Regards ...
>Andre
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I started out with nothing, I still have most of it.



 Steve
```

```
<grin> 
Well, we certainly disagree. I have a proprietary system up on the 
Web right now, and even when it uses http, it is faster by
an order of magnitude than ASP. 

And that's before the system gets loaded and starts rocking... 

Mainframes are going to continue to have a long and productive
life. Thinking you can move a mainframe application that size
to a client/server environment *easily* is like saying we can 
replae the air traffic control system in the next year or so...
-Paul


TPF Moderator (tpf_moderator@hotmail.com) wrote:
: Howdy

: Indeed MS discontinued the support for AXR processors but as of today's
: market, one can deploy them without much fuss. WHen IA64 becomes de-facto
: the 64 bit processor of choice, we can port all WIn32 into Win64 ....

: As for the 15M lines of Cobol -> VB, there is a misundestanding about the
: business logic of these lines in COBOL. Needless to say that well over 1/3
: is UI related and about 1/3 syntax analysis, leaving a miniscule 1/3, or
: 500K lines of actual business logic, completely obsolete and patched over
: the years. The end result is a compressed < 200K lines of actual portable
: information, easily packageable onto MTS components in either VB or VC.
: Let's face it, COBOL was a good language, such as APL once was, but the
: future is dimm. Having a /390 housing DB2 makes sense. Having MVS run COBOL
: code to generate reports makes no sense. Neither having COBOL programs
: running obsolete business logic.

: As an example, I opened an account at Wells Fargo yesterday. The UI was dry,
: meaningless, cryptic, and slow as molasses. Much more can be achieve by
: having ASP pages running (or any equivalent server side CGI-based code) on a
: web server, DHTML or XML pages, and app servers controlling the transaction.
: I had the oportunity to port a whole bank from MVS with CICS into Windows
: and SQL servers, slashing millions of said COBOL lines, and ending up with a
: system that scales correctly and performed 7.2 times faster than the MVS
: equivalent. That is not to say that MVS is a bad OS, on the contrary, I
: still consider it the nicest OS ever written. And I also once taught COBOL
: and Advanced COBOL, but that was in the mid 80's.

: No serious CIO/CTO would invest new software money into a COBOL solution.
: And the ones that do it have a very bad understanding of modern software
: engineering and the level of sofistication quadra boxes have, being them
: Intel or Alpha. The technology of clustering quadra processors or
: 8-processor boxes is real and works like a charm. Nowadays, we can do
: whatever was done by MVS on Windows.

: To conclude, last year I served as an advisor for a University asking if
: COBOL should be eliminated from the curriculum. I voted yes, some others
: don't. At the end, it was removed from the core, and placed as an elective.
: Last semester NO Students enrolled in the elective ...

: Best regards,
: Andre Rosenthal - moderator for comp.os.tpf
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DC66F5.22B0C5EB@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org>`

```


TPF Moderator wrote:
...
> No serious CIO/CTO would invest new software money into a COBOL solution.

Only a CIO aware of current price/performance trends in MVS mainframes;
only a CIO who has seen the C/S hype of  five years ago be updated by a
sober realization of costs of usage, management, and maintenance of C/S 
and distributed systems; only a CIO who has the maturity to have seen
dozens of "COBOL killer" platforms and languages go into obscurity while
COBOL still survives...

> And the ones that do it have a very bad understanding of modern software
> engineering and the level of sofistication quadra boxes have, being them
> Intel or Alpha. The technology of clustering quadra processors or
> 8-processor boxes is real and works like a charm. Nowadays, we can do
> whatever was done by MVS on Windows.
...

Right.  I've got a field for you to plow.  You can either use this
old-fashioned single-image "ox" and mature "automated operator" to pull
the plow or these 5,000 chickens.  There are still a few "minor" bugs in
our new C[hicken] S[---] Operating System, but we expect to have a new
release out soon (about by the time you get the 5000 chickens
harnessed--of course you will have to unharness them all to install the
new CSOS on each and pay an upgrade fee on each!).

So you're advocating that a functional application be re-engineered and
moved from a secure, stable, proven platform capable of easily
sustaining application size growth of better than 1:10000 (with no
application changes) and instead run this mission-critical application
on an marginally-stable operating system and on platforms where the
"rules" for cooperative processing and I/O interfaces seem to change
with regularity every 6 months; and to bet your corporate survival and
ability to grow on the ability of MS to redesign their Operating Systems
(which are currently at the level of reliability and sophistication of
mainframe Operating Systems of 20 years ago) to handle the issues of
multiprocessing, multitasking, and large centralized databases reliably
in time for your requirements? Gosh, MS had barely heard of the concept
of multitasking, multi-user environments only 6 years ago, and from
their products (which I do use) it's clear they are still in the process
of understanding what the "problems" are, much less understanding the
solutions to these problems, that have already been developed in the
mainframe world over the last 30 years.  You only have to look at the
security holes engineered into Windows and Office which require an
anti-virus package to maintain 40K+ virus signatures (as of last count)
with weekly updates to get a small appreciation of how far these
platforms have yet to go to be secure and stable (which go
hand-in-hand).

If the goal is to improve the user interface, there are better ways to
do that than by eliminating the most reliable and now easiest-to-resize
platform in house -- let the nice graphic user interfaces be done by
workstation platforms, which are ideally suited for that purpose; let
centralized multiuser databases (including access, manipulation,
security, integrity, control, backup, & recovery), application logic,
multi-threaded serving, and automated periodic repetitive tasks (also
known as "batch jobs") be done by MVS, which is ideally suited for that
purpose.

If you have a business that is in the process of down-sizing itself out
of existence so that everything that used to require a "real" operating
system can now be done comfortably on a few Windows platforms, then by
all means the management that has brought you to that state should
expend more money to continue the process to its logical conclusion.

If instead, you are a company that needs to be positioned to handle
rapid or unpredictable growth, to move critical applications off an MVS
platform that is the best positioned to adjust to that growth should be
considered an act of gross incompetence.  The current trend appears to
be movement to the mainframe, not from.

Whether the mainframe language of choice should be COBOL or something
else is really a separate issue, but a company that expects to be around
longer than 10 years wants to use a language that will continue to have
excellent support after 10 years.  A proven track record (including
upward compatability of source AND object code, plus ease of
maintenance) is much more signficant here than whether ComSci
Departments find the language "convenient" to teach at the moment, or
whether students ignorant of history find the language appealing.  

I personally find REXX (compiled and interpretive) and Macro Assembler
much more appealing and functional than COBOL for much of the support
functions that I have to deal with on a daily basis, but the fact that
more than 90% of our installation application code is COBOL has
contributed greatly to our ability to adapt to changing business
requirements over the last 20 years. During that period, those
requirements have driven mainframe system growth by a factor of at least
500. During that same 20-year period, the number of persons engaged
full-time in supporting the mainframe platform itself has only grown by
only about a factor of 3 (and most of that was because of the
introduction of CICS, MVS, and DB2).  By contrast, we have found that
growth in the number of workstation platforms and server platforms has
an almost linear relationship to the number of support personnel
required.
  
Considering the steady historical trend for personnel cost consuming
more and more of the DP budget, it shouldn't take a rocket scientist to
decide which will be cheaper ten years from now, a large centralized
server (i.e., mainframe) or multiple smaller servers.  Unless your
business is small and will stay small, Windows and Intel PC platforms
(ignoring reliability and security issues) are simply not capable of
playing that way today .  MVS and S/390-capable platforms are.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DC89CD.12F5CDA8@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <37DC66F5.22B0C5EB@acm.org>`

```
Simply put, it cannot be said better than that. Only ones completely unaware
of the level of sophistication and power provided by contemporary mainframe
super machines (by the way, not even called 'mainframes' any more) can state
nonsense like '...we can do whatever was done by MVS on Windows'. Not only
Windoze, may I add, which have a long (and uncertain) way to become a true
operating system good for serious computing, but even awesome UNIX machines
will _NEVER_ stack up to the power of G5/G6 Enterprise Servers, let alone
ones combined into sysplex complexes. Those amazed by 'quadra processors'
and 'parallel computing' available on Intel platforms can point their
browsers at www.ibm.com and do a find on 'G6' to become a bit more sober.
Even those jumping out of their socks in 1993, at the peak of 'mainframe is
dead hysteria' have now shut up as the business world has moved towards time
series decision support based on historical data, whose sheer volume only
mainframes are able to swallow and process without choking, no matter what
the front end - and that is what VB and VC are realistically confined to -
might be. 

As to CIOs/CTOs brainwashed by the preachers of C/S panacea, they may sober
up by looking at the statistics related moving MF applications to C/S: 80
percent of them have failed miserably, cost the companies fortunes, and
brought many of them to the brink of bankruptcy. Those MF-to-C/S projects
that have not failed completely, are not much better, either, for in the
place of several COBOL (of PL/I, or SAS, or...) analysts who used to support
the entire smoothly running production MF process, there is now and army of
business analysts, C/C++, VB, Oracle and who knows what else programmers
barely keeping the C/S monster from drowning. In one of the most egregious
instances of this madness (I'm intimately familiar with), one COBOL and one
SAS consultant (admittedly, both extremely competent) maintained the entire
acquisition process, after it had been properly designed and established,
during several years for a credit card company with 40-million portfolio,
without a single major problem. Then a decision was made by CIO to move
acquisitions to a C/S application combining VB, VC, Powerbuilder, Oracle on
NT, and Oracle on UNIX. The two consultants (with the combined compensation
of $100/hr) were let go. Instead, over 30(!) people ($70/hr on the average)
are supporting the C/S wonder, permanently in the state of emergency.

It is amazing, though, how rapidly lessons like this are forgotten! Those
very CIOs/CTOs, if they are so busy implementing trendy C/S moves that
researching the issue seriously cannot fit in their schedules, may want to
take a look at the article "MAINFRAME" in the latest Alan Freedman's
Computer Desktop Encyclopedia. If they are still busy to even open it up,
here is the part pertaining to the issue at hand:

"   There Is a Difference!
One might wonder why mainframes cost up to several millions of dollars when
their raw megahertz (MHz) or MIPS rates are no higher than a PC costing
1,000 times less.  There are reasons.  Firstly, in a small computer such as
a PC, the CPU does almost all the processing.  Unless direct memory transfer
(DMA) is used, the CPU is also involved with getting data to and from the
peripherals, the most time-consuming part of the whole operation.
  A mainframe provides enormous amounts of throughput by offloading its
input/output processing to a peripheral channel, which is a computer in
itself.  Mainframes can support hundreds of channels, up to 512 in some
models. Mainframes also have multiple ports into memory and especially into
high-speed caches, which can be 10 times faster than main memory. 
Additional computers may act as I/O traffic cops between the CPU and the
channels and handle the processing of exceptions (what happens if the
channel is busy, if it fails, etc.).  All these subsystems handle the
transaction overhead, freeing the CPU to do real "data processing" such as
computing balances in customer records and subtracting amounts from
inventories, the purpose of the computer in the first place.
  Secondly, the internal bus transfer rates of mainframes are also higher
than small computers.  A 200MHz Pentium has a data bus that runs at 66MHz,
but a 200MHz mainframe may have a data bus that also runs at 200MHz, three
times as fast.  The multipliers add up. Three times the bus speed, 10 times
the cache speed, perhaps 32 or 64 overlapped data transfers.  Multiply one
times the other, and the combination of fast buses, fast caches, multiple
memory ports and independent channels and subsystems makes a processing
machine unlike anything else.
  Thirdly, much of the hardware circuitry in a mainframe is designed to
detect and correct errors. Every subsystem is continuously monitored for
potential failure, in some cases even triggering a list of parts to be
replaced at the next scheduled downtime.  As a result, mainframes are
incredibly reliable.  The mean time between failure (MTBF) is generally 20
years!
  In addition, mainframes are highly scalable. Based on symmetric
multiprocessing (SMP), mainframes can be expanded by adding CPUs to a system
or by adding systems in clusters.

   They're Here to Stay

Once upon a time, mainframes meant "complicated" and required the most
programming and operations expertise.  That is no longer.  Networks of
client/server-based PCs make mainframes look easy. Nothing is more
complicated than the DOS/Windows environment.  Add NetWare, throw in a
little UNIX for good measure, and you have enterprise computing at its most
complex ever.  With more than two trillion dollars worth of mainframe
applications in place, mainframes are here to stay, and their centralized
architecture, which is easiest to manage, may just be the wave of the
future!"

To put things in perspective, currently, each of the 32 S/370 G6's _main_
processors operates not at 200, but at about 700 MHz, and nowadays the Big
Blue is about of the size of a bookcase. So, size-wise, the roles between MF
and CS have reversed: To build a Intel-based or UNIX server equal to G6 in
sheer computing power (of course, it will never do the job quite the same
way), it will take many times the footprint.

As far as the issue at hand is concerned, and Joel is absolutely right about
it, the choice of the language is a different issue. I am primarily a SAS
programmer, and SAS, as many know, runs under virtually any OS. I have had
an opportunity to compare the way it operates on a number of them, and given
a choice, will take the big iron any time.

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================     

"Joel C. Ewing" wrote:
>

…[105 more quoted lines elided]…
> Data-Tronics Corp.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <ZZDL3@tdbank.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZeaG3.1$3B3.32@198.235.216.4>`

```
I may be just a dumb COBOL dinosaur, but 1/3 of 15M lines of code is 5M,
(5000K not 500K!) lines of code.  Converting 5 million lines of *anything*
to *anything-else* is a major proposition.

TPF Moderator <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org...
> Howdy
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZsbG3.11$TA.748@wdc-read-01.qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<7rb47a$2in$1@linuxfr.org> <19990910195445.29668.00007113@ng-fk1.aol.com> <7rca5l$qr8$1@linuxfr.org> <ZeaG3.1$3B3.32@198.235.216.4> <7sbbol$b87$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Why convert?  PERCobol (www.synkronix.com) lets you keep your business logic
in Cobol where it belongs, but runs it on the Java Virtual Machine.  It lets
you access the latest Java technologies from Cobol, and has many powerful
features to enable you to modernize legacy code.  Cobol programs compiled
with PERCobol are output as JavaBeans which can be connected to other
JavaBeans, even to other PERCobol programs.

David


Ken Mullins wrote in message <7sbbol$b87$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
>>>>As for the 15M lines of Cobol -> VB>>>
>
>I have customers dumping VB applications for native API windows apps
written
>in cobol...VB is too slow (they say)...
>
>
>
```

```
Despite what my instructors told me about ease of maintenance in OO,
MAINTAINING the equivalent of 15,000,000 lines of VB and JAVA are what I
can't fathom.
```

- **From:** skcunningham@my-deja.com

- **Message-ID:** `<7qjdkk$kd8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
FOR THOSE WORKING IN THE US ONLY.

I would appreciate it if you could help me with some research regarding
IT Training.  Please answer the following questions and select �reply
to author� to send it to me directly.

Thank you,

Scott Cunningham


1) Have you had any formal training in the following areas ?
a) Analysis
b) Design
c) Construction
d) Testing
e) Estimating
f) Project Management

2) How many years of IT experience do you have ?

3) Does your company have a documented process for developing software ?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
OpenVMS???
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol language translator

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AAdz3.3$jl.229@wdc-read-01.qwest.net>`

```
    I'm not sure about Cobol to C/C++, but if platform independence is what
you need, PERCobol (www.synkronix.com) allows you to keep your business
logic in COBOL.  It's based on Java technology, so your Cobol can be
deployed on virtually any platform, even in a Web browser.  Best of all,
Cobol can be made to integrate easily with the latest Java Beans and the
like.

David Sanders
Synkronix, Inc.
```

```
customized to a the specific client.  For the specific client certain of
their modules could not be covered in the IEF generated code, that's where
we aksed to develop the convertor.

Graeme Conn
Abraxas Technologies
```

<a id="t-1e850a547b"></a>
## Thread: Some Introductory Cobol Info... — 11 this month / 12 total

### Some Introductory Cobol Info...

- **From:** Nicola Shields <njs@holyrood.ed.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.GSO.3.95.990901012326.25486A-100000@holyrood.ed.ac.uk>`

```

As a student just about to complete a one year conversion course in IT,
I've been offered a job which will initially involve mostly Cobol
programming.

I guess I'm really looking for some general info about Cobol.  
My current understanding (I'm sure I'll be corrected) is that it is
largely used within the financial sector, that a lot of old programs,
originally written in Cobol, are still in use.  I get the impression that
much of the work involving Cobol is related to maintenance work but that
'new systems' are now being written in other langs.  Fair comments or am I
completely wrong?

As certain langs may be better for certain types of tasks (eg Java useful
for web-based tasks), is Cobol better for certain types of tasks than
others?


Any recommendations for useful books and/or informative web sites would be
much appreciated.

Hope my requests aren't too far removed from the normal newsgroup
discussions.

Cheers

Nicky Shields
```

#### ↳ Re: Some Introductory Cobol Info...

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cd1c2c.172132026@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.3.95.990901012326.25486A-100000@holyrood.ed.ac.uk>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 1999 01:35:30 +0100, Nicola Shields
<njs@holyrood.ed.ac.uk> wrote:

>As certain langs may be better for certain types of tasks (eg Java useful
>for web-based tasks), is Cobol better for certain types of tasks than
>others?
>
>

There is a general misconception about COBOL and "the Web".  COBOL can
easily sit behind a browser interface, and can BETTER be used in
creating a thin client - a client much BETTER than a browser. 


>Any recommendations for useful books and/or informative web sites would be
>much appreciated.
>

Try my book - Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.

You can get to it easily from my web site:
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006
```

#### ↳ Re: Some Introductory Cobol Info...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CD2FC3.1CEDB890@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.3.95.990901012326.25486A-100000@holyrood.ed.ac.uk>`

```
Nicola Shields wrote:
> 
> As a student just about to complete a one year conversion course in IT,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> completely wrong?

I suppose it depends on how you define "systems".  There is a lot of new
COBOL being written in COBOL shops, but I suspect that the number of
COBOL shops is not increasing, as new companies often go other routes.

I have never worked within the financial sector, and I have worked with
COBOL my whole career.  That is, unless you call business data
processing "financial sector", because it deals with money.
 
> As certain langs may be better for certain types of tasks (eg Java useful
> for web-based tasks), is Cobol better for certain types of tasks than
> others?

It is an excellent tool for business data processing.  
 
> Any recommendations for useful books and/or informative web sites would be
> much appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Some Introductory Cobol Info...

- **From:** paul richelieu & michelle cox <paulaukai@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D1DCA3.978B9EA3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.GSO.3.95.990901012326.25486A-100000@holyrood.ed.ac.uk>`

```
I have worked with cobol for 12 years now...  It is a very outdated and
not really even supported much anymore.  But most large shops cannot
afford to convert there programs.  Cobol is a very easy language to
learn.  But its pretty lengthy and outdated compared to others.  Most
jobs places that have cobol are like DMV, DHR (human resources like
welfare), large trucking companies, and large insurance companies. 
Great thing about cobol though is most places arnt teaching it anymore,
the so called dead language.  So contracting pays quite well.  There is
allot of Y2K work right now with cobol and next year around march there
should be alot of jobs opening up for new projects.

Nicola Shields wrote:
> 
> As a student just about to complete a one year conversion course in IT,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nicky Shields
```
