## Thread: Getting rid of trailing spaces — 9 this month / 12 total

_Thread started [1999-08](1999-08.md#t-3172d30bdd)._

#### ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** "icare" <icare@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qjr7c$2mt5$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
If you need to load this file into oracle you can use the RTRIN in the
loader control statements.
If you do not want to use this than you need to write a COBOL subroutine
that will trim the field and add it to the output stream which is going to
have a record description with the occurs depending on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d110ac@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Some implementations of COBOL allow you to specify removal of trailing
blanks from LINE SEQUENTIAL files by means of an external (run-time)
parameter, or a compiler directive.

This will NOT remove blanks from embedded fields. Neither is it
platform independent. COBOL allows us to write reasonably tight code that
will achieve both.
(The following is ALMOST platform independent; you would need to change the
file definition from LINE SEQUENTIAL to SEQUENTIAL (and specify variable
format  through JCL) if you were to run it on a mainframe...Across the PC
spectrum I believe it will run on any COBOL supported platform.)

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. 'STRTEST'.
      *AUTHOR. Peter E. C. Dashwood.
      * This program demonstrates the use of COBOL to remove trailing
      * blanks.
      *DATE-WRITTEN. September,1999.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       configuration section.
       source-computer. IBM-PC.
       object-computer. IBM-PC.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select INFILE assign "input.csv"
                       organization is line sequential
                       access mode is sequential.
           select OUTFILE assign "output.csv"
                       organization is line sequential
                       access mode is sequential.

      *------------------------  DATA   DIVISION  ---------------------
       DATA DIVISION.
       file section.
       FD   INFILE
                        LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
                        DATA RECORD is INPUT-RECORD.
       01   INPUT-RECORD               pic x(255).



       FD   OUTFILE
                        LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
                        DATA RECORD is OUTPUT-RECORD.
       01   OUTPUT-RECORD              pic x(255).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *   The 3 work fields are all max size + 1 to enable a space
      *    delimiter for unstringing.
       01  field-1 pic x(7).
       01  field-2 pic x(53).
       01  field-3 pic x(8).

       01  record-counts.
           12 in-count                 pic s9(9) comp-5.
           12 out-count               pic s9(9) comp-5.
           12 display-count        pic 9(9).

       01  end-flag                    pic x.
           88 not-finished             value zero.
           88 finished                 value '1'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
       a000.
           perform startup-housekeeping
           perform main-logic until finished
           perform close-down
           .
       a999.
           stop run.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------
       STARTUP-HOUSEKEEPING            section.
       sh000.
           open input INFILE
                output OUTFILE
           initialize record-counts
           set not-finished to TRUE
           .
       sh999.
           exit.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------
       MAIN-LOGIC                      section.
       ml000.
           read INFILE
              at end
                 set finished to TRUE
                 go to ml999
           end-read
           add 1 to in-count
      *
      *   Remove the trailing blanks with COBOL string functions...
      *
           move spaces to field-1
                          field-2
                          field-3
           unstring input-record
              delimited by ','
                into field-1
                     field-2
                     field-3
           end-unstring
      *
      *   Unfortunately, there is a special case where field
      *   2 may terminate with a single space. This will NOT
      *   be removed by delimiting with a double space (which is
      *   the easy option). If it is absolutely necessary to remove
      *   ALL trailing spaces (even when there is only one), then
      *   we can handle it with the following inspect..
      *
           inspect field-2
              replacing all ' "' by '  '

      *
      *   ...this will convert the final space/quote to double space
      *   which will then be handled by the standard stringing.
      *
           move spaces to output-record
           string
             field-1
                 delimited by space
             ','
                 delimited by size
             field-2
                 delimited by '  '
             '",'
                 delimited by size
             field-3
                 delimited by space
                      into output-record
           end-string
           write output-record
           add 1 to out-count
           .
       ml999.
           exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------
       CLOSE-DOWN                      section.
       cd000.
           close INFILE
                 OUTFILE
           move in-count to display-count
           display "Records read    =" display-count
           move out-count to display-count
           display "Records written =" display-count
           display space
           display "END OF RUN"
           .
       cd999.
           exit.
      *----------------    END OF PROGRAM 'STRTEST' ----------------

raider4967@my-deja.com wrote in message <7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Can someone tell me how to get rid of trailing
>spaces in cobol.  I can't seem to find any answers
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qr8jv$8ac$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There are a number of possibilities.  One rather simple and fairly
portable solution is to combine a perform varying with a reference
modified move.

In article <7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  raider4967@my-deja.com wrote:
> Can someone tell me how to get rid of trailing
> spaces in cobol.  I can't seem to find any answers
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d1f05f@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7qr8jv$8ac$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Sorry, I have to take issue with "rather simple"..(I concur on the other
points about many possibilities and portabilities...:-) )

Doing it the way you suggest is a bit more complex than you may believe. In
fact, it is likely to be more than one perform varying, with variable
reference modification, especially if you are to handle the special case
noted in my code sample. Furthermore, all this really does is make visible
the code being generated from the COBOL string functions (the object code
would be almost identical (hopefully :-) ) but the compiler has the
advantage of using its own workfields and avoiding arithmetic conversions
which it will certainly have to do with the method you are suggesting.)

Don't get me wrong; I love nested performs (and varying) and I consider
reference modification to be an extremely powerful and useful function
(although I try and limit it to fixed modifiers, so it can be resolved at
compile time rather than at run time).

My real objection is that in this case, this is NOT simple, especially for
someone learning COBOL. Reference modification with variable modifiers has
lead many maintenance programmers in the middle of the night to call down
curses on the author...we should hesitate to recommend it as desirable
coding practice...especially for people just starting.

Of course, all of the above is a matter of opinion and yours is every bit as
valuable (or otherwise) as mine is. If you really think it is so easy, why
not knock up a code sample of a working program to do it and post it
here...? The one I posted took 20 minutes (I modified an existing skeleton)
to write, and another 15 to compile, create test data, find the problem with
the single trailing space, and get a final successful test. (Fortunately, I
had a very rare slow day, and became intrigued by what appeared to be a
positively trivial problem...it ain't.) It was fun and made some light
relief from "real" problems...

Regards,

Pete.





trblshtr wrote in message <7qr8jv$8ac$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>There are a number of possibilities.  One rather simple and fairly
>portable solution is to combine a perform varying with a reference
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qttu8$v5d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7qr8jv$8ac$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d1f05f@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
sorry, haven't much time or an available skeleton on this system.

here is a q&d example. i'm not sure why you think it is not simple.

77 SUB1 PIC 99 VALUE ZERO.

01 PARSER.
   03 P-WHOL  PIC X(80).
   03 P-EACH REDEFINES P-WHOL OCCURS 80 PIC X.
01 SOME-RIGHT PIC X(??) JUST.
01 SOME-LEFT  PIC X(??).
.
.
MOVE SOME-LEFT TO P-WHOL.
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 80 BY -1
        UNTIL P-EACH(SUB1) <> SPACE OR
              SUB1 = ZERO
    CONTINUE
END-PERFORM.
COMPUTE SUB1 = SUB1 + 1.
MOVE P-WHOL(1:SUB1) TO SOME-RIGHT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i89B3.16491$KM3.136913@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7qhc00$5k6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7qr8jv$8ac$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d1f05f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7qttu8$v5d$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7r0gap$lo8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
trblshtr wrote:
>trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com> wrote:
>> sorry, haven't much time or an available skeleton on this system.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>> END-PERFORM.

>> IF SUB1 > ZERO
>>     MOVE P-WHOL(1:SUB1) TO SOME-RIGHT.

It is more complicated than you think. ;-)  The above perform will
inevitably bomb if SOME-LEFT is all spaces: when SUB1 becomes zero,
the "P-EACH(SUB1) <> SPACE" condition will be evaluated and get an
invalid subscript error.  Beyond that, when SOME-LEFT is all spaces,
SOME-RIGHT is not changed, leaving in whatever garbage was already
in SOME-RIGHT.

>Shouldn't have posted the first 1 - was distracted by news of an
>unexpected death in the family. presume you can do the housekeeping.
>added the 'just' for similar thread. will be gone for a bit.


We had a long thread on this issue just a few months ago.  Turns out,
at least on the compilers we tested it on, loops with reference
modification were *significantly* faster than STRING/UNSTRING and
FUNCTION REVERSE, and even faster than subscripts or indexes.  The
speed difference was more than 100:1 in some cases!
```

```
They  have code freely available for many common cases (date
manipulations, conversions, etc...) and one for string manipulations
called strnpk.
It might help.

Alain Reymond
```

<a id="t-b902fefbea"></a>
## Thread: COBOL v Other Languages — 12 this month / 15 total

_Thread started [1999-08](1999-08.md#t-b902fefbea)._

#### ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qjei6$l0t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>`

```
In article <37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
> Through various threads, contributors have said that where necessary
> they will use other languages (C, C++, Java etc.) to do things more
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> examples of where you do this in preference to using COBOL,
(presuambly
> caused by deficiencies in the latter ?)

I can not imagine writing device drivers in COBOL.

Also for the most part I feel like doing intensly (e.g. numerically
integrating and such and you don't have to get that complex to feel this
way) mathematical work with COBOL is like a dog walking on its hind
legs, possible but clumsy.

I have a feeling a lot of communications requirements quickly put COBOL
to its limits, maybe not so much on the application side but certainly
on the system side of it all.

While I have done online programming with COBOL (CICS) again I feel that
there are better ways to do online programming.

You need to look at what COBOL means:  COmon Business Oriented Language
and its origin mainframe environments.

COBOL is great for generating reports and for basic business processing
requirements but get out of that environment and you need another tool.

The main problem I have with COBOL is that every variable is global in
scope and that you can not make variables constant (I'm talking about
COBOL II on OS/390 systems) and that you can't break up your source code
into smaller modules.

My favorite feature of COBOL is easily its record oriented I/O approach
which of course comes from the fact that COBOL grew up in a record I/O
hardware environment.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cd5577.38994931@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <7qjei6$l0t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Wed, 01 Sep 1999 14:51:55 GMT, Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote:

>The main problem I have with COBOL is that every variable is global in
>scope and that you can not make variables constant (I'm talking about
>COBOL II on OS/390 systems) and that you can't break up your source code
>into smaller modules.


Sure you can - Just use nested programs, then you can local and global/shared variables.

Also, you can combine small modules to a large main using the linkage editor.  What is the
problem?


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7qm07p$gp5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <37cd5577.38994931@news2.ibm.net>,
  vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com wrote:
> On Wed, 01 Sep 1999 14:51:55 GMT, Mark Framness
<framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote:
>
> >The main problem I have with COBOL is that every variable is global
in
> >scope and that you can not make variables constant (I'm talking about
> >COBOL II on OS/390 systems) and that you can't break up your source
code
> >into smaller modules.
>
> Sure you can - Just use nested programs, then you can local and
global/shared variables.
>
> Also, you can combine small modules to a large main using the linkage
editor.  What is the
> problem?

Well I guess I know not of the intricacies of the COBOL II linker.  We
had "canned" jcl that called a proc that did the linking.  We just
substituted in the name of the object module into a symbolic parm and
then we ran the jcl.  That proc was written by our instructors and I
never looked too deeply into it.  So I guess that is why I thought that
multiple source module cobol programs were not doable.

thanks
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CE8E18.BD05958D@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <7qjei6$l0t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37cd5577.38994931@news2.ibm.net> <7qm07p$gp5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Mark Framness wrote:

> Well I guess I know not of the intricacies of the COBOL II linker.  We
> had "canned" jcl that called a proc that did the linking.  We just
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> multiple source module cobol programs were not doable.

I just created a multiple source module COBOL program in my work place. 
Using our canned procedures, I compiled it, and both programs were
clean.  But when I ran it, the first program could not find the second
program to link to it.  Separating them out solved that problem.

It's usually not a good idea to use compiler options (other than those
used for temporary debugging and for displaying cross-references), which
are different from the production standards.  If they make your program
behave differently, your test is insufficient.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cfa5d5.6330693@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <7qjei6$l0t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37cd5577.38994931@news2.ibm.net> <7qm07p$gp5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 02 Sep 1999 14:05:54 GMT, Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote:

>
>Well I guess I know not of the intricacies of the COBOL II linker.
Perhaps, because there is no such thing like a COBOL linker?  It' the DFP Binder, formerly
known as MVS Linkage Editor <s>


>>..  We
>had "canned" jcl that called a proc that did the linking.  We just
>substituted in the name of the object module into a symbolic parm and
>then we ran the jcl.  That proc was written by our instructors and I
>never looked too deeply into it.

I am a COBOL instructor, and do exactly this.  Much too little time to teach JCL,
Programming, and COBOL , Binder, et al. in one week, thus pre-canned JCL it is  

>So I guess that is why I thought that
>multiple source module cobol programs were not doable.

Okay, here are two approaches to the problem.  I use TWO modules as an example, but of
course, using induction, you can then use any number of programs

A)  Separately compile the Programs PGMA and PGMB to full grown Program Objects (formerly
known as Load Modules)

In PGMA you code

	MOVE 'PGMB' TO pgmname
	CALL pgmname USING parm1, parm2 parm3

This constitutes a "dynamic call".  In the JCL for the execution of PGMA you code

//PGMA  EXEC PGM=PGMA
//STEPLIB DD DISP=SHR,DSN=lib.where.PGMA.is.a.member
//         DD DISP=SHR,DSN=lib.where.PGMB.is.a.member

The PGMA will find PGMB and invoke it


B)  Separately compile the programs PGMA and PGMB into Object Modules.  Use JCL similar to
this:
 //COMPILE  PROC MEMBER=MISSING
 //COBOL   EXEC PGM=IGYCRCTL,REGION=2048K
 //SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*
 //SYSLIN   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,DISP=(MOD,PASS),SPACE=(TRK,(3,3,10)),
 //             DCB=(BLKSIZE=3200),DSN=&&TEMPPDS(&MEMBER)
 //SYSUT1   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSUT2   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSUT3   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSUT4   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSUT5   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSUT6   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSUT7   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
 //SYSIN    DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=YOUR.SOURCE.COB(&MEMBER)
 //SYSLIB   DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=YOUR.SOURCE.COPYBOOK
//               PEND
//PGMA   EXEC  COMPILE,MEMBER=PGMA
//PGMB   EXEC  COMPILE,MEMBER=PGMB
//LKED    EXEC PGM=HEWL,COND=(4,LT),REGION=4096K
//SYSLIB   DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=CEE.SCEELKED   or whatever your LE370 libs are called
//OBJLIB   DD  DISP=(OLD,DELETE,DELETE),DSN=&&TEMPPDS
//ANOTHER DD DISP=SHR,DSN=SOME.OTHER.OBJ.OR.LOADLIB
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSLIN   DD  DDNAME=SYSIN
//SYSLMOD  DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=YOUR.PGM.LOADLIB
//SYSUT1   DD  UNIT=SYSDA,SPACE=(TRK,(10,10))
//SYSIN DD *
 INCLUDE OBJLIB(PGMA)
 INCLUDE OBJLIB(PGMB)
 INCLUDE ANOTHER(PGMC)
 INCLUDE SYSLMOD(BIGMODUL)
 NAME BIGMODUL(R)

Please keep in mind that I am writing this off the top of my head, without being near a
mainframe.  But this should give you the general idea



Perhaps this sample will help you to create multi-source COBOL modules

//COBCOMP PROC MEMBER=MISSING
//COB      
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D080BA.8816178C@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <7qjei6$l0t$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37cd5577.38994931@news2.ibm.net> <7qm07p$gp5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Mark Framness wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >
> > Also, you can combine small modules to a large main using the linkage
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well I guess I know not of the intricacies of the COBOL II linker.

(IBM mainframe disclaimer) For openers, there's no COBOL II linker. MVS
has had a linkage editor since its birth, called the Binder in its
current implementation. It's used to create load modules from object
code compiled by anything.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CDDC0F.53B4@coredcs.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>`

```
** James J. Gavan wrote:
** ... they will use other languages (C, C++, Java etc.)
** to do things more effectively than in COBOL.
** ... examples of where you do this in preference to using COBOL, 

Admitting that I don't know much about either C/C++ or Java, let me pass
on a couple of comments I overheard at last week's SHARE conference
anyway --

"C/C++ is not a very good candidate for business applications because it
does not support fixed decimal arithmetic (e.g. dollars and cents) which
is what most business applications require."

"Java, being an interpretive language, is much too slow for most
mainframe (i.e. os/390) applications."

"There is no Inter-Language Communication facility to connect Java to
COBOL applications." (COBOL calling Java or vice versa)

On the other hand: "1/3 of the os/390 system components require the LE
runtime libraries, primarily because they are written in C."

So I guess what it boils down to is choose the language that fits the
application.  Don't try to fit all applications to a single language.

================================
Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
================================
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qkt0c$q47$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37CDDC0F.53B4@coredcs.com>`

```
A number of people in this NG are or have expressed a sensible opinion about
choosing a programming language.

I think that's good thinking.

Then there is the sub-thread that dwells on the range of a language is good
for as as a means for choosing the programming language.

I feel some people are missing a very important point.

Documentation.

How long does it take to learn to read and write the language?

Will the language make a clear statement of the problem being solved?

Do you need to be a rocket scientist to understand the problem solution?

Will the changes in technology be reflected in the language?

In my opinion, in 1960 it was important to restrict a common language to
business applications because selling a replacement for Fortran, Assembly,
Comtran, etc. was/are remains a difficult sell.  It is much easier to
demonstrate the programming language range of applications than to tout it
as a do all end all tool. An example might be the Communications Section and
it's poor reception yet TCP/IP is or can be done with todays Business or
otherwise application languages if it is desired.

If there is a problem with languages, it is that not enough work has gone
into generalizing the funtions required except in a GLOBAL way.  Global is
good in a way, or it is bad but how do you learn this, and what are the plus
and minus
results of going one way or another.  We are talking about intra-program,
inter-application, and intra-company interfaces.  Computer operating system
development is not usually approached on a network basis. Much is also being
done inter-and-intra-industry.

In my view, there is only the need for one basic set of key words with
defined attributes and results.  We are not yet willing to look at it that
way so we have several sets of words (loosely speaking) many of which
intersect partially or completely.  Who would want to try to merge them and
clarify them into a single set?  Webster or others have done it.  Yet for
the subset we need, it's still an infant.

Warren Simmons
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f9oz3.1137$C74.8959@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <37CDDC0F.53B4@coredcs.com>`

```
i know they are just hearsay, but here's some input anyway!

Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com> wrote:
> "Java, being an interpretive language, is much too slow for most
> mainframe (i.e. os/390) applications."

for most platforms, Just In Time (JIT) Java compilers do exist (including
the 390). these do offer performance improvements over purely interpreted
code (but i don't know if it's enough). don't know what the story is wrt
(static) native Java compilers (a la C/C++/COBOL). anyone?

> "There is no Inter-Language Communication facility to connect Java to
> COBOL applications." (COBOL calling Java or vice versa)

you can use the Java Native Interface (JNI) to mix/connect COBOL & Java in
the same application. this is certainly the case with MERANT Micro Focus
compilers (Net Express for NT and Server Express for UNIX).

> So I guess what it boils down to is choose the language that fits the
> application.  Don't try to fit all applications to a single language.

extremely sound advice.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** dnalor@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qj1g4$bbh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>`

```
Hi Jimmy,
your question is extremly important, more exactly: it eventully starts
religion wars.
The point of EVERY(!) Programming language is, that it was designed for
a very specific group of applications. That means it is the wrong way
to take a language, no matter which, and say, okay, lets write a
program that solves problem X for us. U must go the opposite way.
Consider ur problem, specify it, and THEN decide which language.
This is the reason why there are lots of languages, you know.
If u have to travel through trees (least-cost-way-seeking problem)
COBOL is definitley the most inperformant approach, because it does not
support recursion (MF-Cobol does, but not really useful).
I think u got the clue. Be careful to consider opinions from 1-language-
freaks!!!

In article <37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
> Through various threads, contributors have said that where necessary
> they will use other languages (C, C++, Java etc.) to do things more
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> examples of where you do this in preference to using COBOL,
(presuambly
> caused by deficiencies in the latter ?)
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CD28FC.D3577E24@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37CAC7AC.EC687EAB@home.com> <7qj1g4$bbh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
dnalor@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi Jimmy,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Consider ur problem, specify it, and THEN decide which language.

There are several criteria to consider in any problem though.  One need
may push to one tool, another to a different tool.  Some criteria
include:
1.  Does that tool solve the problem most accurately.
2.  Can you find people who are skilled in using that tool.
3.  Will you need to have that tool around a long time in the future?
3a. Will you find people/support for that tool in the future?
4.  How much does that tool cost?  
5.  Is maintenance easy or hard for that tool?
6.  How stable is that tool's environment?

> This is the reason why there are lots of languages, you know.

While there are thousands of languages, only a few are cost effective
for a typical environment though.  A typical workplace would be silly to
evaluate each programming need and purchase the language which is best
for that individual program.  And even if the workplace decides that the
best all around language is PL/1, it might find that the local labor
market dictates COBOL is better.  Or maybe it needs LISP - does the cost
of training & keeping LISP programmers still make it the best?  Or if it
likes ADA - does it figure ADA tools will be available in 5 years to
match its changing environment?
```

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00

```
Thanks for being polite and honest. It's sure a welcome change when a
recruiter actually ASKS where to post instead of spamming. Unfortunately I
can't advise you much on perm. since we do mostly contract, I guess
headhunter.net is good for both, but for contracts:

If you're the actual client or end user looking for consultants, you can
post to news://alt.computer.consultants.ads.norecruiters and
http://www.realcontracts.html and I wish I could remember more. These 2,
especially the newsgroup, are for actual clients only, not recruiters or
brokers or consulting firms in the usual sense. They allow small consulting
firms like ourselves that are more like co-ops or groups of independent
consultants practicing together and especially doing in-house projects, but
the usual broker/recruiter type postings will produce only hostility and
will alienate your best candidate prospects.

On the other extreme are DICE etc. which are either for brokers only or are
of little use to end clients because they're so flooded by brokers that the
real clients are hard to find. This is why many of us never even look at
news://alt.computer.consultants.ads and headhunter.net and computerjobs
etc. any more, and especially DICE which is notorious for brokers lying by
setting the "Independent Contractors OK" flag to make it through our
filters when they really want to raid us for their own payroll.

In between are Reqs@rmpcp.com and http://www.scguild.com and I forget what
else, actual clients are preferred but brokers post there too. Also
http://www.icca.org for searching only, no posting capability yet.

Be sure never to post ads to news:alt.computer.consultants - it's only for
business discussions, not free technical support or for recruiting or other
ads.

If you need any contractors or consultants anywhere in the USA, please
check http://rmpcp.com/members.html for a partial list of our people,
including 2 available mainframe Cobol programmers right here in Dallas.
Thanks.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
```

```
learn COBOL as a toy, it is designed for business, and business has many
requirements which go beyond coding.  We need to work with bosses,

product which is needed, accepted, used, and maintained.

take part in psychology experiments.  I would like to see senior IS
students doing some role playing for a required class for younger
students - in requirements gathering!

The actual person-to-person skills involved in requirements gathering
needs to be part of an IS education.
```

```
Pat
```

#### ↳ Re: passing an array of strings from a C++ DLL to Cobol

- **From:** scott@aravis.softbase.com (Scott McMahan)
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vldz3.2048$Dj2.139280@monger.newsread.com>`
- **References:** `<7qj52a$3ge$1@autumn.news.rcn.net>`

```
webmaster (webmaster@merallis.com) wrote:
> Hi,
>   I'm a C++ programmer so I don't really know much of Cobol.  However, we
> have a need to pass an array of strings from a C++ DLL to a Cobol Program.
> Has anyone done this before?  If so, how have you done it.   Any help would
> be greatly appreciated.

This will be tough. COBOL uses fixed-length strings, and C uses
null-terminated ones. I may be one of the few people who actually knows
how to do this :)

Normally, you will have a CALL USING for your DLL in the COBOL code.
Something like:

CALL STRINGTHING USING STRINGS

Where STRINGS is an 01 level of some sort. You can either
do it as a bunch of strings:

	01 STRINGS
		05 STRING1 PIC X(...)
		05 STRING2 PIC X(...)
		05 STRING3 PIC X(...)
		05 STRING4 PIC X(...)

Or as an OCCURS clause.

	01 STRINGS PIC X (...) OCCURS ... TIMES

Your choice. If you do an occurs, you will have to pass the number of
times on to the DLL so the C code will know how many positions the table
has! Remember COBOL has fixed length strings and fixed size tables,
there is no dynamic allocation at all: either your C code knows in
advance what to expect, or you pass it in as a parm to the call.

Anyway, your C program has an interface like:

	stringthing(char *strings) {
		...
	}

The point is, to the C program, "strings" is one HUGE chunk of memory
the size of either all the PIC X clauses added up, or the size of the
PIC X times the TIMES in the OCCURS clause. It's a pointer (all COBOL
variables are passed by pointer unless you say otherwise, and you usually
never want BY VALUE when you're dealing with C!) to a block of memory
the right size for all the strings.

You will have to copy one string at a time into the HUGE chunk of memory,
and padd it out with spaces. Be SURE to remove the null terminator,
too. You can use strcpy if you want to, but be sure to keep track of
your positions accurately!

You can also define a struct the same size as the COBOL 01 or table, and
put your strings in there (careful, get rid of that null terminator!) and
write a routine to do the space padding, then memcpy that into your
pointer's HUGE chunk of memory.

Have fun,

Scott
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: passing an array of strings from a C++ DLL to Cobol

- **From:** "Carsten A. Arnholm" <arnholm@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3yfz3.671$nW5.5806@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<7qj52a$3ge$1@autumn.news.rcn.net> <Vldz3.2048$Dj2.139280@monger.newsread.com>`

```
Scott McMahan wrote in message ...
>webmaster (webmaster@merallis.com) wrote:
>> Hi,
>>   I'm a C++ programmer so I don't really know much of Cobol.  However, we
>> have a need to pass an array of strings from a C++ DLL to a Cobol
Program.
>> Has anyone done this before?  If so, how have you done it.   Any help
would
>> be greatly appreciated.
>
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>Scott


See my CHARACTER class in http://home.sol.no/~arnholm/cppf77.htm which I use
to pass arrays of CHARACTER strings to Fortran 77. Maybe you can use it as
an inspiration. Similar to what is described for COBOL above, Fortran77
strings are all fixed size in a contiguous block of memory.

Have more fun,

Carsten A. Arnholm
arnholm@online.no
http://home.sol.no/~arnholm/
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <37CD31B3.8342014D@NOSPAMhome.com>...

>Then vote against someone.  Or better yet, join the party you don't care
>for and vote against someone you fear in the primary.  Your vote will
>count more there.


>But anyway, certainly there are candidates you
>DON'T want to get elected.  If so, get out there and vote against that
>candidate.  And certainly there are issues you care about which are
>being voted on.  Do your homework and make an informed vote.

Unfortunately, the ballot only asks for my first choice and not a list of
ordered preferences.  Without a system of runoffs with the top 2 vote
getters (or top 3 or top 4, whatever is necessary to represent 50% + 1) and
a plurality rules, a voter doesn't have the luxury of voting for whom he
wants.  He can only vote for whom he thinks has the best chance of beating
the No-Way-Jose candidate.

BTW, is there a discussion group where political discussions are routine?
This is off topic big time; my apologies.
```

- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qlp4e$g5j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<37CBDCCE.C773215E@gfc-net.de> <7qgk6p$80f$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <37CBEC2A.399434C7@gfc-net.de> <7qj8nh$2vl1@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
    It sounds like there is something in your function that
is slower on OS/2, rather than call overhead.  Is the source code for this
function large?  What does it do that involves the operating system?

    At this time I can think of nothing better than eyeballing the source
code
to locate something that uses an operating system call.  It could be any
number of things:  keyboard, screen, com, print, disk, memory allocation.

    It would probably not be a compute or something "local".


> >
> >many thanks for your hint, but, I'm sorry to say, the response time is
exactly
> >the same (differs about 20 milliseconds for a whole transaction with
about 100
> >calls).
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >>I ran into a serious runtime problem under OS2. I have Microfocus
Cobol
> >> 4.0.36
> >> for Win32 and for OS2. I have one application that makes extensive use
(120
> >> calls per transaction) of calling a function from a DLL (created with
> >> cbllink
> >> -d).
> >>
> >> Try making the function(s) in the dll a subroutine(s) in the main
program
> >> and eliminate the dll...Compile the module that is currently a dll as
an
> >> .obj and include the .obj in the link for the main program...Should be
very
> >> little source changes required...
> >>
> >> kenmullins
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Problem fixed: Cobol being very slow under OS/2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CF7CD6.7B8C8ECF@gfc-net.de>`
- **References:** `<37CBDCCE.C773215E@gfc-net.de> <7qgk6p$80f$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <37CBEC2A.399434C7@gfc-net.de> <7qj8nh$2vl1@enews3.newsguy.com> <7qlp4e$g5j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```


Russell Styles schrieb:
> 
>     It sounds like there is something in your function that
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Russell,

perfectly right. In the meantime I found the reason in OS2's dossleep function.
We had to issue a sleep-time of one millisecond for some reasons. OS2 always
seems to round it up to the next 30 milliseconds, while NT rounds it up to about
10 milliseconds. We found a solution that does not need the sleep function. Now
everything works as fast as expected.

Thanks to all contributors in this branch

Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

## Thread: Micro Focus User-Written File Handler Problems — 1 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-08](1999-08.md#t-3dbc85fc16)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus User-Written File Handler Problems

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cd24f5@news1.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37cae3a8@news1.us.ibm.net> <7qeqi1$cnf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken,

Thanks for the response - I figured it out.  Just for your knowledge, when
using a calling-convention of 74 (win32api) you must use an "exit program
returning" statement instead of a "goback" in any subroutines that are
called.  This is the only thing that I changed in my program, and it fixed
the problem.  Weird, huh?

Thanks again for taking the time.

TL

>MF has a problem with being able to handle duplicate entry points within a
>module and a dll being called by that module...Is the normal MF file
handler
>loaded also?
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-f2bfb2e53c"></a>
## Thread: Java for the COBOL Programmer — 1 msgs

### Java for the COBOL Programmer

- **From:** art03@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qjbmf$iu2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Java for the COBOL Programmer
E. Reed Doke
Southwest Missouri University
Bill C. Hardgrave
University of Arkansas, Fayetteville
 When the Y2K crisis winds down , Cobol
programmers will look for new ways to apply their
skills.  Drawing upon the numerous similarities
between COBOL and JAVA, Java for the COBOL
Programmer teaches Java to professional COBOL
programmers.  To quicken and simplify the
transition from one language to the other, the
authors describe the features and syntax of Java
while contrasting them with their parallels in
COBOL.  The book is completely modular, allowing
a reader to skip back and forth from one chapter
to another.  With each example, the authors
present a COBOL program that accomplishes the
same task.  Sample programs are available from a
website specified within the book, and an
accompanying CD-ROM contains all of the Java
programs developed in the book.  It also contains
a TrialWare copy of Symantec�s Visual Caf�
Database Edition 3.0.  A running case study
evolves with increased functionality as new
material is presented in subsequent chapters.  No
other book uses this approach to teach Java to
COBOL programmers.
Contents: 1. Why you should learn Java; 2. An
introduction to object-oriented programming; 3.
Program format and structure; 4. Defining data;
5. Arithmetic operation; 6. Decision making; 7.
Looping; 8. Tables and arrays; 9. Input and
output; 10. Additional Java topics.
1999    253 X 156 mm     284pp

0 521 65892 6   Paperback

For further details see
http://www.cup.cam.ac.uk/Scripts/webbook.asp?
isbn=0521658926
The book is available for sale at the above
address or through your favorite bookstore.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-b5f5df665d"></a>
## Thread: Upgraded free software — 1 msgs

### Upgraded free software

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Esgz3.800$Am.19733@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
September 1, 1999

The following upgraded free file is available for download at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html :

COBOL FD Analyzer
COBOL FD Analyzer This is Tal Systems' COBFD COBOL FD Analyzer v 1.50 (Aug
'99). This freeware tool runs in PC/MS-DOS and parses COBOL source or
copylibs. It reports the size and offset within each '01' level for all
datanames (group and elementary). COBFD supports any level OCCURS/REDEFINES
and most specialty compiler directives. Included are the executable,
documentation and a demo file. Interactive and command-line modes allow
operation with prompts or in MS-DOS batch files. Version 1.50 adds runtime
choice of word or byte storage for COMP (binary) items.
COBFD.ZIP (111,957; August 8, 1999)

   * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
The addition of the WORD storage option for binary datatypes means that
COBFD will now report the correct offsets and record sizes for FDs with
COMP/BINARY data used on IBM mainframes. The previous release of COBFD used
BYTE storage with no option.

The only other change in the software is that it now supports the literal
"PACKED-DECIMAL" as a usage, treating that as COMPUTATIONAL-3 (BCD).

Three releases ago I said "this" was the *LAST* MS-DOS release of COBFD.
Well, let's make it four: this is the last MS-DOS release.

I have already started migrating this to Windows. When available, the new
software will feature the source code in one window with the report in
another window.  You will be able to select your COBOL source/copylib using
a "regular" Windows-style "Open File" dialog OR (shamelessly copying the
Progeni "sizer" utility)  you will be able to pop data into the source
window using the clipboard. The report window will allow you to "save as..."
so you may then print the file from Notepad or Word or Joe's Word Processor
or whatever you use.  I'm still undecided if I will let you edit the source
code in the source code window, and will almost certainly not include
printing functions in the first release.

The Windows design is not locked in stone yet, so if you want to send
suggestions, be my guest. I already have some other features I am planning
on adding, but I'm not telling you about those right now. We'll see if they
are still on The Wish List.

Thanks again to Bob Wolfe and The Flexus Corp for the web space and other
assistance.
-
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

```
Hi Juergen!
Thx4ur reply!
> Make a .DLL file rather than a .INT file. That should work.
Nope. I made it this way:
I made a dll that is loaded from the cobol with
CALL "MYDLL".
Now this dll makes the calls to the master. It is the only way. Merant-
Hotline agreed this, to be the only possible solution.
Bye!
Roland.
```

<a id="t-b030e8920b"></a>
## Thread: RM Cobol empy's datafile — 1 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-08](1999-08.md#t-b030e8920b)._

#### ↳ Re: RM Cobol empy's datafile

- **From:** Juan Maria <juanmaria@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qiu5i$96i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7pe2ho$lcl$1@news.worldonline.nl>`

```
In article <7pe2ho$lcl$1@news.worldonline.nl>,
  "Eugene Broeren" <csnl@worldonline.nl> wrote:
> A file that was  was 207.020.220 kb was reduces to 9600 kb. All data
was
> lost.

Hi Eugene.

Of course if some program made an OPEN OUTPUT to this file that's the
natural outcome.
But after a lot of years using RMCobol I've suffered, very scarcely,
this same problem without any OPEN OUTPUT to put the blame on. After a
lot of testing I found that the problem might come from the 'SELECT
OPTIONAL' clause that creates a file if it doesn't exist. Under some
circumstances -data corruption on the file header- RunCobol thinks that
the file does not exist and opens it in output mode thus recreating the
file and index structure.

Hope it helps you.
Regards. Juan Maria.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
What do you mean by "long lines"?  The mainframe compiler ONLY accepts 80
column lines (of which only the first 72 columns "count" for the compiler).
```

```
one byte at a time. In a loop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qok4e$dlg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id> <E3Hz3.429$vT4.23231@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37cf3f38.9933363@news.idola.net.id>`

```
Actually you *can* define an alphanumeric field as right justified and
move will take care of it (at least on the COBOLS i've worked with).
This will, however, include any trailing spaces contained in the field
being moved from.  Various schemes available to take care of that
depending upon what you are dealing with and the desired results. For
example, an inline perform from tail of string to determine size and a
reference modified move will handle just about anything needed in this
regard. Or move to left justified field to truncate and move result to
right justified. Or....


In article <37cf3f38.9933363@news.idola.net.id>,
  yatno@muslimemail.com (Yatno) wrote:
> it is pity  :(
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d11daf@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id> <E3Hz3.429$vT4.23231@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```

Jerry Peacock wrote in message ...
>
>Yatno <yatno@muslimemail.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry Jerry, I have to disagree. It CAN be done.

Standard COBOL supports the JUSTIFIED clause, as follows:

01   some-data.
        12 text-field    pic x(10) JUSTIFIED RIGHT.
         [JUSTIFIED can only be specified on an elementary alpha or
alphanumeric item]

Then...

move 'elevenbytes' to text-field

results in...'levenbytes'

move 'twobytes' to text-field

results in 'bbtwobytes'   [where bb are 2 padding spaces inserted by the
COBOL system]

Contrast this with what would happen had JUSTIFIED not been specified...

move 'twobytes' to text-field

results in ...'twobytesbb'  [where bb are 2 padding spaces inserted by the
COBOL system]

Hope this makes it clear,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d11fbd@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id> <E3Hz3.429$vT4.23231@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37d11daf@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
No, Peter,
the justified clause does not do the job.
We had a big discussion with a LOT of code
examples on this subject a while back.
Consider:
01 Fields.
    02 field-a       pic x(10).
    02 field-b       pic x(20) justified.

move "ABC" to field-a
move field-a to field-b

results in bbbbbbbbbbABCbbbbbbb
where the b's are blanks.


peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote in message
news:37d11daf@eeyore.callnetuk.com...
>
> Jerry Peacock wrote in message ...
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xx9A3.887$YH4.54219@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id> <E3Hz3.429$vT4.23231@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37d11daf@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```

peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote in message
news:37d11daf@eeyore.callnetuk.com...

> Sorry Jerry, I have to disagree. It CAN be done.

Well, yes, but:

JUST RIGHT moves the entire sending field from right to left
and, if the receiving field is longer than the sending field, pads
the receiving field with blanks to the left. Unfortunately, in the
in the history of COBOL, no one has found a use for this feature.

I took the poster's question to be the (much) more common
lament over the effort to right-justify a number, i.e.,

'123     '    ==> '     123'   (or even '000123')

As have the other posters to this question.
```

#### ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IiQz3.2958$KM3.29161@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id>`

```
Yatno wrote
>
>Is it a simple way moving one field to another (filling field),
>but received right justify?

The program below demonstrates a right justify routine that can
handle input and output fields of any size.  It is also very
efficient; 100,000 out-of-line performed loops on 30 byte fields,
compiled with MF COBOL 3.2 take about 0.11 seconds on my PII450.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.    RJUST.
      *******************************
      *    Judson D. McClendon      *
      *    Sun Valley Systems       *
      *    4522 Shadow Ridge Pkwy   *
      *    Pinson, AL 35126-2192    *
      *       205-680-0460          *
      *******************************
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       77  INPUT-FIELD                 PIC  X(30).
       77  INPUT-PTR                   PIC S9(07)  COMP.
       77  OUTPUT-FIELD                PIC  X(20).
       77  OUTPUT-PTR                  PIC S9(07)  COMP.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       TESTER.
           MOVE "ABC" TO INPUT-FIELD.
           DISPLAY ":" INPUT-FIELD ":".
           PERFORM RJUST.
           DISPLAY ":" OUTPUT-FIELD ":".
           DISPLAY " ".
           MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLM" TO INPUT-FIELD.
           DISPLAY ":" INPUT-FIELD ":".
           PERFORM RJUST.
           DISPLAY ":" OUTPUT-FIELD ":".
           DISPLAY " ".
           MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRST" TO INPUT-FIELD.
           DISPLAY ":" INPUT-FIELD ":".
           PERFORM RJUST.
           DISPLAY ":" OUTPUT-FIELD ":".
           DISPLAY " ".
           MOVE "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ" TO INPUT-FIELD.
           DISPLAY ":" INPUT-FIELD ":".
           PERFORM RJUST.
           DISPLAY ":" OUTPUT-FIELD ":".
           STOP RUN.

       RJUST.
           MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD.
           IF (INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES)
               MOVE FUNCTION LENGTH(INPUT-FIELD) TO INPUT-PTR
               PERFORM UNTIL INPUT-FIELD(INPUT-PTR:1) NOT = SPACE
                   SUBTRACT 1 FROM INPUT-PTR
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE OUTPUT-PTR = FUNCTION LENGTH(OUTPUT-FIELD)
                                    - INPUT-PTR + 1
               MOVE 1 TO INPUT-PTR
               PERFORM UNTIL OUTPUT-PTR > 0
                   ADD 1 TO INPUT-PTR OUTPUT-PTR
               END-PERFORM
               MOVE INPUT-FIELD(INPUT-PTR:) TO OUTPUT-FIELD(OUTPUT-PTR:)
           END-IF.

C:\COBOL\>RJUST
:ABC                           :
:                 ABC:

:ABCDEFGHIJKLM                 :
:       ABCDEFGHIJKLM:

:ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRST          :
:ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRST:

:ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ    :
:GHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ:
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** Clifton Ivy <clif@purdue.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D57904.2EE7613D@purdue.edu>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id> <IiQz3.2958$KM3.29161@news1.mia>`

```
I've modified Judson's program to fit our somewhat old-fashioned
mainframe, just-getting-to-OS/390, using-COBOL-II world. It is much like
what Judson did, just using an "occurs depending on" to create a field
that contains only the data (without trailing spaces).

see my notes:

Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Yatno wrote
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiled with MF COBOL 3.2 take about 0.11 seconds on my PII450.

I didn't compile and run mine, but I "know" :-) it will work, and the
run time should be good even though not comparable to Judson's, since we
would be comparing PC run times to mainframe run times.

> 
>        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>        77  INPUT-PTR                   PIC S9(07)  COMP.
I added "justified":
         77  OUTPUT-FIELD                PIC  X(20) JUSTIFIED. 
>        77  OUTPUT-PTR                  PIC S9(07)  COMP.
> 
I added:
         01  INPUT-PROCESSOR.
             05 INPUT-WO-SPACES.
               10 FILLER                 PIC X
                  OCCURS 0 TO 29 DEPENDING ON INPUT-PTR.
               10 IWS-SPACE-CHECKER      PIX X.
With our IBM compiler, the group items INPUT-WO-SPACES and
INPUT-PROCESSOR vary in size depending on the value of INPUT-PTR, which
controls how many FILLER byte occurrences there are.
>        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>        TESTER.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>        RJUST.
I changed RJUST to become: 

             MOVE 29 TO INPUT-PTR.
             MOVE INPUT-FIELD TO INPUT-PROCESSOR.
 (Yes, you could move the data directly to the processor area...)
             PERFORM RJUST2
                 VARYING INPUT-PTR BY -1
                 UNTIL INPUT-PTR = 0 
                     OR IWS-SPACE-CHECKER NOT = SPACE.
             MOVE INPUT-WO-SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD.

>            MOVE SPACES TO OUTPUT-FIELD.
>            IF (INPUT-FIELD NOT = SPACES)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
They're just my opinions...
Clifton Ivy, Mgmt Info, Purdue University
clif@purdue.edu
```

#### ↳ Re: Move right justify

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01befb07$120b0ed0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id>`

```
Yatno <yatno@muslimemail.com> wrote in article
<37cf2253.2535500@news.idola.net.id>...
> 
> Is it a simple way moving one field to another (filling field), but
> received right justify  ?
> is it defined on working storage  or on move statement ?

If the content of Field-x is left-justified with no embedded blanks, you
can right-justify the content by

UNSTRING Field-x DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO Field-x.
 
Gunnar.
```

<a id="t-553917d0d0"></a>
## Thread: Forcing a volser change on a tape — 3 msgs

### Forcing a volser change on a tape

- **From:** Monstercycle <scotty76@webtv.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qm4i5$kdh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hey all, does anyone have any ideas how I can force a tape change
(volser change) in a cobol program?

Thanks in advance,
```

#### ↳ Re: Forcing a volser change on a tape

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cea564.6217730@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7qm4i5$kdh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 02 Sep 1999 15:19:49 GMT, Monstercycle <scotty76@webtv.net> wrote:

>Hey all, does anyone have any ideas how I can force a tape change
>(volser change) in a cobol program?
>
>Thanks in advance,

Have someone write a small assembler routine that you can call, this Routine would use
SVC 55 (X'37') to FEOV (Force End Of Volume)

 

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Forcing a volser change on a tape

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CEB257.4F25@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<7qm4i5$kdh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Monstercycle wrote:
> 
> Hey all, does anyone have any ideas how I can force a tape change
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.


There is a "REEL" option on the COBOL CLOSE statement.
Here is a snip out of our Production COBOL source:

      ****     CLOSE DOCUMENT-OUTPUT-FILE-1, REEL POSITIONING          
      ****     THE "REEL" OPTION CAUSES IT TO NOT CLOSE THE FILE, BUT  
      ****     SKIP TO A NEW REEL WITH A CLEAN END-OF-REEL BREAK.      
               CLOSE DOCUMENT-OUTPUT-FILE-1 REEL                       

Hope this is what you need.
Pete
```

---

<a id="t-b84941c46a"></a>
## Thread: CREATE TABLE PROBLEM -Reply — 1 msgs

### CREATE TABLE PROBLEM -Reply

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7ce7321.059@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
This appears to be a DB2 problem rather than a COBOL problem.  

If you're looking for a subset of another table look in your DB2 book under CURSORS.  If not try looking under temporary tables.

The keyword CREATE is DB2 not COBOL and obviously it isn't available within a program.

Eileen


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF NetExpress Question

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990902172919.17669.00002873@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Hi Lee,

I have not used the datepicker, but I have used the microsoft control
MSACAL70.OCX that allows you to bring up a calender.  If you can not get the
datepicker to work you might want to consider that OCX control.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

<a id="t-f5e547dbfc"></a>
## Thread: Latest draft of the next COBOL Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Latest draft of the next COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qn3a8$95t@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Sent to the comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol newsgroups (with miscellaneous
CC's) )

    ***

(I think I have updated the FAQ for this - but I am not certain that the
web-page FAQ is getting refreshed correctly.)

The most recent draft of the next COBOL Standard is available (in PDF
format) at:
      http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4htm/cd16all.pdf

A couple of (conflicting in my opinion) bits of information on this.

The title page of this document includes the following explicit statement,

"Permission is granted to members of ISO, IEC, and JTC1 and to their member
bodies, subcommittees, and working groups to reproduce this document for the
purposes of standardization activities without further permission, provided
this notice is included. Permission is granted to members of NCITS, its
technical committees, and their associated task groups to reproduce this
document for the purposes of NCITS standardization activities without
further permission, provided this notice is included. All other rights are
reserved. Any commercial or for-profit duplication is strictly prohibited."

However, on page xviii of the same document it states just as "explicitly",

"Any organization interested in reproducing the COBOL standard and
specifications in whole or in part, using ideas from this document as the
basis for an instruction manual or for any other purpose, is free to do so."

  ***

I sent a letter to the NCITS secretariat a while ago asking about this
apparent conflict (and requesting an acknowledgment of receipt of my
letter).  So far I have heard "zilch".  THEREFORE,  I suggest that anyone
who "accesses" this file/document consider these two statements and process
them according to whatever legal, ethical, or other considerations they
think best.

NOTE WELL:
  In general, if you are interested in the "latest" status updates from J4,
check out their "external" web page at:

 http://merant.microfocus.com/Standards/
```

## Thread: printing to lpt2 and lpt3 — 2 msgs

### printing to lpt2 and lpt3

- **From:** MARK Lavine <gcsii@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37CDF805.71F8@erols.com>`

```
is it possible to print to lpt1, and lpt2(or lpt3) etc. from one work
station.


Mark lavine
```

#### ↳ Re: printing to lpt2 and lpt3

- **From:** "Jason Bailey" <jayandtonya@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JJuz3.899$Sb1.96754@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<37CDF805.71F8@erols.com>`

```

> is it possible to print to lpt1, and lpt2(or lpt3) etc. from one work
> station.

Absolutely!  Anything is possible...it's a matter of just knowing how to do
it.  Which compiler and platform are you running on?  You might want to map
a printer object to the LPT3 or any other LPT port and call the Windows
Print API which will vary depending on your compiler and platform.  Just one
possible solution that has served me well.

Regards,

Jason
```

<a id="t-c49fb175cf"></a>
## Thread: REDEFINES STATEMENT (LONGISH) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### REDEFINES STATEMENT (LONGISH)

- **From:** "Teresa A. Robinson" <trobinson@wworld.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d094da@news.wworld.com>`

```
If you are using the following input layout:
Department    xxx
Course            9999
Section            999
with alphanumeric, and numeric as shown, can you do this and will it work?
08   t-course               pic x(10).
                       08   us-string redefines t-course.
                               10  us-dept           pic xxx.
                                10  us-course       pic 9999.
                                 10  us-section      pic 999.
The 3 elements come in separately, must be strung together, loaded into a
variable length table and sorted.  Then they must be compared to another
input file to compare the department numbers for a match.  If they match a
counter must be incremented for 5 class levels (2 dimensional), if not an
error line is to be written with the bad info.  After the file is read and
the table complete, and unstringing the elements again, the table is finally
printed out with control breaks on the department.  I can't get it to work.
I think it is hanging up on the search all module during the process and
never gets to printing the table.  It is printing the data twice in
succession and all the data is coming out as errors.  That is probably why
the table doesn't print, it thinks it is done.  It has no flagged
statements, but is still wrong.  I had the above data as all alphanumeric
but wondered if the above will work.  I don't know how to read the
diagnostics to help locate where it bombed.  If anyone can help I would
certainly appreciate it!  Thanks in advance!
```

#### ↳ Re: REDEFINES STATEMENT (LONGISH)

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D1093F.BAEF12E@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37d094da@news.wworld.com>`

```
Without source code to see, diagnostics is at best difficult. Have you verified
that your table is sorted as specified by the 'ASCENDING'  key clause. This
clause only tells the compiler you expect your data to be in the correct order.
Another possibility is that you have not set  a value for the DEPENDING object.
There are a couple of ways to handle this:

One is to  set the DEPENDING object to the maximum value in the beginning of the
program (or using a value clause in working storage). Then, after the table has
been loaded, move the number of occurrences to the DEPENDING object.

A second method is to initialize  the DEPENDING object to zero  in the beginning
of the program and then increment the DEPENDING object before adding an element
to the table.

A third, (my preference) is to avoid the use of variable tables altogether in
working storage. It only adds to program complexity, execution overhead, and
saves no storage, since the compiler must allocate sufficient storage for the
maximum possible size anyway.

Your code snippet is correct, but here is an alternative which uses a group and
no redefines.  Both are (almost) equivalent (group level vs. alphanumeric).
08   t-course.
      09   us-string.         *> not really necessary, but included to use all
the names.
          10  us-dept           pic xxx.
           10  us-course       pic 9999.
           10  us-section      pic 999.

If you need additional help, pls send/post your source code.

Regards,
John


"Teresa A. Robinson" wrote:

> If you are using the following input layout:
> Department    xxx
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> certainly appreciate it!  Thanks in advance!
```

#### ↳ Re: REDEFINES STATEMENT (LONGISH)

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qslrc$2qg_002@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<37d094da@news.wworld.com>`

```
In article <37d094da@news.wworld.com>,
   "Teresa A. Robinson" <trobinson@wworld.com> wrote:
+If you are using the following input layout:
+Department    xxx
+Course            9999
+Section            999
+with alphanumeric, and numeric as shown, can you do this and will it work?
+08   t-course               pic x(10).
+                       08   us-string redefines t-course.
+                               10  us-dept           pic xxx.
+                                10  us-course       pic 9999.
+                                 10  us-section      pic 999.

The REDEFINES is legal, but completely unnecessary.
08 t-course.
   10 us-dept          pic xxx.
   10 us-course        pic 9999.
   10 us-section       pic 999.
will produce the desired result. By definition, *all* group items (such as
t-course) are treated as alphanumeric; thus, t-course is by default pic x(10).
```

#### ↳ Re: REDEFINES STATEMENT (LONGISH)

- **From:** bpkprsnl@erols.dot.com (Boris)
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d6ce4a.2161905@news.erols.com>`
- **References:** `<37d094da@news.wworld.com>`

```

Folks here are more than willing to help, but for homework problems most of us
would appreciate it if you identified that its homework in the subject and show
what you have figured out so far on your own.

Good luck.

PS:  All COBOL questions should reference the platform and dialect of COBOL in
use.
```

<a id="t-27ade904c8"></a>
## Thread: Why are things the way they are? — 8 msgs

### Why are things the way they are?

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qq7hn$2aa$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
We often seek answers to why things are the way they are.  Col. 8, and 72
come to mind. So, while not a COBOL subject, the following "history" of
railroad gauge may help the newcomer or perhaps even a
Senior, or even a manager understand this "thing" better.
In that spirit, the following came to me from an inertia guidance engineer.

Warren Simmons
-----------------------------

The U.S. Standard Railroad Gauge (distance between the rails) is 4 feet,
8-1/2 inches. That's a exceedingly odd number. Why was that size used?
Because that's the way they built them in England, and the U.S. railroads
were designed & built during the Industrial Revolution by English expatriate
engineers to accommodate English-built locomotives.

Why did the English people build them like that? Because the first rail
lines were built by the same people who built the pre-railroad tramways, and
that's the gauge they used. Why did they use that gauge then?
Because the people who built the tramways used the same jigs and tooling
that they used for building wagons, which used that wheel spacing.

Okay, why did the wagons use that odd wheel spacing? Well, if they tried to
use any other spacing the wagons would break on some of the old long
distance roads in Europe & England. This was due to the old wheel ruts worn
into the roads. So who built these old rutted roads? The first long distance
roads in Europe were built by Imperial Rome for the benefit of their
legions. The thoroughfares have been used ever since.

And the ruts? The initial ruts, which everyone else had to match for fear of
destroying their wagons, were first made by Roman war chariots. These
chariots were designed to be pulled by two horses hitched side-by-side. In
order to roll smoothly, the chariot wheels had to be spaced far enough apart
to avoid the hoofmarks left by the horses, yet not protrude past the flanks
of the horses to prevent entanglement with opposing traffic or roadside
vegetation. Since the chariots were made for or by Imperial Rome, they were
all alike in the matter of wheel spacing.

Thus, we have the answer to the original questions. The United States
Standard Railroad Gauge of 4 feet, 8-1/2 inches derives from the original
specification for an Imperial Roman army war chariot. Two thousand years
later and a continent away, the track layout of the entire U.S. railway
network is based upon the fact that Imperial Roman chariots were made to be
just wide enough to accommodate the rear-ends of two war horses. Specs and
bureaucracies live forever.
```

```
Hmm, interesting enough that in Europe, there are five different
railroad track systems:

Normal (1 435 mm)

Small tracks:
Meter (1 000 mm)
Kap (1 067 mm)

Wide tracks:
Russian (1 524 mm)
Spain (1 668 mm)

And only Russia was never captured by the Romans!
```

```
Not only that...  Denmark and Germany use the same track size.  But when Denmark
introduced there new ICE express train some years back, they made the coaches a little
wider than "normal", and suddenly they could use the tracks, but couldn't pass through the
station in Germany, as the platforms got a little bit too close to the coaches - some
heavy refurbishing on the the German station was the consequence....
```

<a id="t-1674cc8c3c"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu support and sales - experiences — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu support and sales - experiences

- **From:** sta.REMOVETHIS@a-info.dk (Steffen Arnecke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d00ce3.743957@news.uni2.dk>`

```
Over the times, in this NG there has been some "Fujitsu-bashing",
partly about late deliveries of software, partly about their support.

I have now used the 4.X Cobol compiler for aprox. one year. I only use
the GUI-builder Power-Cobol, and uses MF for all other works.

During that period, the need for support has come up several times.
The first time we didn't even have a support agreement, since I was
just testing the product. But they did a great job in helping me, and
they even put me in direct contact with the guy who wrote the "faulty"
part of their product, so we could solve the problem as fast as
possible.

At all times, the support has been very professional, and _very_
prompt and fast. It is my clear impression, that they are doing a
great job, once you need them. Our main problem is the time-zones;
when we go home, they come in.

I should however note, that every support-call I've made, was because
of a bug i their RunTime-System. So on the other hand, if they didn't
help me, hell would break loose.

As for their sales : After testing 4.0, we wanted to by an upgrade to
4.2 and maintenance. After sending several e-mails to their sales
department and not becoming an answer, I had to send an e-mail to one
of their support-people, I order to get her to get their sales-people
to answer me!

Just my two cents; when we have somthing to complain about, we always
make sure to make a post - so when we are satisfied costumers, we
should post as well.

Best regards

Steffen Arnecke
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu support and sales - experiences

```

> 
> Over the times, in this NG there has been some "Fujitsu-bashing",
> partly about late deliveries of software, partly about their support.
> 
You are not the first to say that Fujitsu have been supportive - so I'm
not sure where the "bashing" comes in.

Peter Dashwood was one of those who enthused about Fujitsu at $499 US,
and all that he was able to achieve - so can I ask - exactly what
whistles and bells do you get from Fujitsu for paying the additional
$3,000 (?) for the full-blown version ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu support and sales - experiences

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990903180028.29235.00000479@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37D028CA.5C1B1750@home.com>`

```
In article <37D028CA.5C1B1750@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
writes:

>
>Peter Dashwood was one of those who enthused about Fujitsu at $499 US,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't spend a lot of time looking at their web-site checking prices, but
they were offering their products for support fees only @$500 for the 
Enterprise package (full blown $3000 purchase price).   My company 
bought in when the support price was only $250, and we have been happy
with the product ever since.   There are many features that come with the
Enterprise package that we haven't even begun to explore.
You get the COBOL compiler/language reference/usage guide
                  PowerCOBOL GUI development environment/ and related docs
                  PowerBsort enhanced sort utility
                  PowerForm forms designer
                  PowerGEM editor/source archival system
                  Application packager (creates windows installation wizard
wrapper)
                  Data Editor/convertor  (slick utility to intelligently
convert EBCDIC 
                          files to ASCII for testing on the PC)
The IDE / Debugger allows you to step thru code and view / modify data as it
passes thru your program.

As stated by others, support is usually very good about acknowledgement of
e-mailed questions.   I have had the misfortune to enquire on a few topics that
they do not have a great deal of support for or have not had their products
used in the way that I was attempting to complete a task.  Even so, they have
done a great job of digging for answers and offering solutions.
```

```
>with the product ever since.   There are many features that come with the
>Enterprise package that we haven't even begun to explore.

After all the work I did on IP sockets, I found that the Enterprise
editiion has a communiucations interface server, that lets you use the
communicates inteferface of COBOL to TCP/IP socket processes.
```
