##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu support and sales - experiences

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d11889@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37d00ce3.743957@news.uni2.dk> <1PWz3.210$YH4.12906@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
I haven't seen any "Fujitsu bashing" and would be very interested to see
what was said.

I have been using Fujitsu since V3.00, over the last year, converted
successfully all our existing MicroFocus COBOL/GUI DIALOG applications, have
now standardised my company on Fujitsu COBOL, and have persuaded a major
corporate client to run a pilot project using Fujitsu PowerCOBOL to leverage
programmers' legacy skills. (I certainly wouldn't recommend them to do this
if I had any doubts about the Fujitsu Product...and I am hoping to persuade
a couple more companies to go the same route.)

I have found version 4.2 of PowerCOBOL to be stable and useful. Support from
Fujitsu (on the very rare occasions it has been needed) has been exemplary.
I have even had a personal phone call from one of their consultants in
Colorado to help me solve a particular problem which resulted in a new
addition to their product (the point being, that they do take on board user
feedback...). By e-mail I have never had to wait more than 24 hours for an
effective response (bear in mind that I am being supported from the USA
while I am situated in England).

No, I am not on commission, neither am I accepting any favours or
backhanders from Fujitsu (they haven't offered...he said ,ruefully :-)). I
just think it is an EXCELLENT COBOL implementation.

Half a dozen reasons why I like it:

1. Total support for COM and ActiveX with a development environment which
easily allows custom controls from different languages.

2. Extremely easy integration with C++ and VB. (you can step through COBOL
and VB source code in the same debugging session...)

3. Event processing written in COBOL (no need for separate DIALOG language)

4. External sharing of variables allows tight modular on-line code.

5. Simple switching between .dll and .exe structures without requirement to
know complex compiler/linker switches and commands. (Select whether you want
DEBUG or RELEASE and .EXE or .DLL, it is very simple, and you can build
either or both and switch between them.)

6. Comprehensive on-line documentation on CD-ROM, with the software.

If other people have had a different experience it's a pity.

Pete.

Jerry Peacock wrote in message <1PWz3.210$YH4.12906@typhoon01.swbell.net>...
>
>Steffen Arnecke <sta.REMOVETHIS@a-info.dk> wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
editor wrote in message ...
>1.  Free Utility Download: SIZER (No More Counting Lines of COBOL Code)
>www.aboutlegacycoding.com
>

2. Another Free Utility Download: COBFD.ZIP at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html

Actually, the SIZER utility is pretty neat, if limited. COBFD gives you the
sizes and offsets of ALL the elementary and group items in the input file,
not just a single value for the size of the largest '01' record in the
input. SIZER can take in input from the Windows(r) clipboard which makes it
more convenient if that's all you need.


MCM
(author of #2)
(biased!)
```

<a id="t-6f5fc2b11b"></a>
## Thread: Getting rid of trailing spaces — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [1999-08](1999-08.md#t-6f5fc2b11b)._

#### ↳ Re: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37cf3155@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7qhcc2$60a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Some implementations of COBOL allow you to specify removal of trailing
blanks from LINE SEQUENTIAL files by means of an external (run-time)
parameter, or a compiler directive.

This will NOT remove blanks from embedded fields. Neither is it
platform independent. COBOL allows us to write reasonably tight code that
will achieve both.
(The following is ALMOST platform independent; you would need to change the
file definition from LINE SEQUENTIAL to SEQUENTIAL (and specify variable
format  through JCL) if you were to run it on a mainframe...Across the PC
spectrum I believe it will run on any COBOL supported platform.)

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. 'STRTEST'.
      *AUTHOR. Peter E. C. Dashwood.
      * This program demonstrates the use of COBOL to remove trailing
      * blanks.
      *DATE_WRITTEN. September,1999.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       configuration section.
       source-computer. IBM-PC.
       object-computer. IBM-PC.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select INFILE assign "input.csv"
                       organization is line sequential
                       access mode is sequential.
           select OUTFILE assign "output.csv"
                       organization is line sequential
                       access mode is sequential.

      *------------------------  DATA   DIVISION  ---------------------
       DATA DIVISION.
       file section.
       FD   INFILE
                        LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
                        DATA RECORD is INPUT-RECORD.
       01   INPUT-RECORD               pic x(255).



       FD   OUTFILE
                        LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
                        DATA RECORD is OUTPUT-RECORD.
       01   OUTPUT-RECORD              pic x(100).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  field-1 pic x(7).
       01  field-2 pic x(50).
       01  field-3 pic x(8).

       01  record-counts.
           12 in-count                 pic s9(9) comp-5.
           12 out-count                pic s9(9) comp-5.
           12 display-count            pic 9(9).

       01  end-flag                    pic x.
           88 not-finished             value zero.
           88 finished                 value '1'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
       a000.
           perform startup-housekeeping
           perform main-logic until finished
           perform close-down
           .
       a999.
           stop run.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------
       STARTUP-HOUSEKEEPING            section.
       sh000.
           open input INFILE
                output OUTFILE
           initialize record-counts
           set not-finished to TRUE
           .
       sh999.
           exit.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------
       MAIN-LOGIC                      section.
       ml000.
           read INFILE
              at end
                 set finished to TRUE
                 go to ml999
           end-read
           add 1 to in-count
      *
      *   Remove the trailing blanks with COBOL string functions...
      *
           move spaces to field-1
                          field-2
                          field-3
           unstring input-record
              delimited by ','
                into field-1
                     field-2
                     field-3
           end-unstring
      *
      *   Unfortunately, there is a special case where field
      *   2 may terminate with a single space. This will NOT
      *   be removed by delimiting with a double space (which is
      *   the easy option). If it is absolutely necessary to remove
      *   ALL trailing spaces (even when there is only one), then
      *   we can handle it with the following inspect..
      *
           inspect field-2
              replacing all ' "' by '  '

      *
      *   ...this will convert the final space/quote to double space
      *   which will then be handled by the standard stringing.
      *
           move spaces to output-record
           string
             field-1
                 delimited by space
             ','
                 delimited by size
             field-2
                 delimited by '  '
             '",'
                 delimited by size
             field-3
                 delimited by space
                      into output-record
           end-string
           write output-record
           add 1 to out-count
           .
       ml999.
           exit.
      *----------------------------------------------------------
       CLOSE-DOWN                      section.
       cd000.
           close INFILE
                 OUTFILE
           move in-count to display-count
           display "Records read    =" display-count
           move out-count to display-count
           display "Records written =" display-count
           display space
           display "END OF RUN"
           .
       cd999.
           exit.
      *----------------    END OF PROGRAM 'STRTEST' ----------------



raider4967@my-deja.com wrote in message <7qhcc2$60a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Can someone help me get rid of trailing spaces in COBOL.  I have a file
>that the fields are enclosed in quotes and is comma delimited.  And
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
<mazuryk@litech.net> writes:

programmers in colleges and universities all over the world. (Also translated
into 4 languages). It has an easy, hands on approach.  The third edition - soon

Compiler. If you need the MicroFocus COBOL compiler ( Windows or DOS) King
Computer Services has them. 818-951-5240
```

<a id="t-cbca3eb71a"></a>
## Thread: Getting rid of trailing spaces — 1 msgs

### RE: Getting rid of trailing spaces

- **From:** Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698498E1F@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
Pardon me for intruding, but this approach requires previous knowlegde of
both number of fields and maximum field size, and thereby making the program
single-purpose. Furthermore, the inspect and string/unstring statements are
overkill.

For the task at hand, I would take another approach.

read the input record back to front on a character by character basis using
reference modification, 
skipping the trailing spaces, and the spaces after (=before) ending quotes,
moving all other characters
to a ws-field. Then write the output record from the ws-field backward
again.

This would require only two perform varying... loops and require no
knowledge of the fields and their
contents. Only the maximum record size. And the program becomes
multi-purpose.

For a real nice job, I would make the output file a byte-stream to eliminate
trailing spaces at record level in the outfile.

Would you agree ?


> -----Original Message-----
> From:	peter dashwood [SMTP:dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk]
```

## Thread: MF-COBOL and DLL's: Call shows strange errors — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF-COBOL and DLL's: Call shows strange errors

- **From:** dnalor@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qoc0v$7ni$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi *.*!

I use NT4/SP3 and MF-Cobol 4.00.32
I have a DLL, and call it with:
CALL "L00C002".
When I use the DLL in a C program, it works pretty fine, but COBOL
raises a pretty strange Error-Message DIRECTLY after the CALL Statement:
It opens a MessageBox and says:

ERROR!
Call failed:(not readable characters)
Errorcode:2

I do not know where I can read something about this errorcode, nor do I
know why it is raised. I call another DLL from the COBOL-Program, it is
no problem. In fact this call is the second call, and it is located
DIRECTLY after the 1st:
This 2 statements are the very first statements in the entire program.

      *----------------------------------------------------------------

        S-INIT-GUI SECTION.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------

        INIT-GUI.

           CALL "cob2c".
           CALL "L00C002".

After the error-window has been clicked away, the program continues
pretty fine. All calls to functions in the DLL are working fine!
Except one thing: when the program stops, the DLL claims to be dropped
back to the system 2 times, instead of 1 time. Really strange!!
Does somebody have a hint?

Thanks a lot!

Bye!
Roland.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

## Thread: Recursion in Cobol — 32 msgs

### Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "Ir. HS Hartkamp" <hartkamp@casema.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net>`

```
I have worked as a Cobol and CSP programmer and with a few other languages
jost for fun. In Cobol, it is absolutely not possible to make a recursive
call (i.e. a program or routine that calls itself). It's not even possible
if you use multiple programs that -sneaky- call itself via a chain of
programs.

CSP is more or less a fourth genetation programming language that generates
Cobol code which compiles to a program. In CSP it   is   possible to use
recursive calls.

Can anyone explain how this is possible ?
Hans Hartkamp
```

#### ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** PAL3000 <pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com>

- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net>`

```
What kind of code does it create for recursion?  My guess is that the
run-time has something built in that will simulate recursion.  All it
would have to do is keep a stack of the variables for each call, and
unwind them on exits.

Paul
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D3AB86.91FE54F3@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net> <fcbRN2lLZr9HUNnJomedwp07=4vh@4ax.com>`

```
PAL3000 wrote:
> 
> What kind of code does it create for recursion?  My guess is that the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Paul

My experience with RM/COBOL (quite a while ago, don't know how newer
versions would work) is that PERFORMs don't nest either. You have a
routine that does something, does a PERFORM to itself, and then does
something else. The "something else" only gets called once, no matter
how many times you did the recursion. So you would somehow have to stack
the code too, not just the data. Actually, that's easy with a
conditional GO TO the beginning, at the end of the loop.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff

PD: I'm not an anti-goto fanatic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d25c49@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net> <7qt4sj$11u8$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <37d22f17$0$19815@reader2.casema.net>`

```

Ir. HS Hartkamp <hartkamp@casema.net> wrote in message
news:37d22f17$0$19815@reader2.casema.net...
> I think it's called   VS Cobol II   (IBM mainframe)
>
> icare heeft geschreven in bericht <7qt4sj$11u8$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>...
> >What kind of cobol due you use.
> >Recursion is posible with MicroFocus COBOL.

Even if the Cobol compiler you use does not support recursion, it is still
possible to do it.

Here is an example:


The Quicksort algorithm is a perfect example of an algorithm which can best
be expressed in a recursive manner. This means that when one procedure calls
another (or itself) two things happen:
instruction pointers are stacked so that at the end of the called procedure,
execution can continue in the calling procedure.
local data is also stacked, so that when the calling procedure resumes, its
local data is reinstated.
COBOL saves only one instruction pointer per paragraph, and has no concept
of local data. This is, of course, not true of the latest Cobol standard,
but often we have to labor in a legacy-setting without the benefit of the
latest technology.
We adapt to the limitations of COBOL by using a stack to store `requests'
and a loop which takes the next request and processes it. A `recursive' call
is in the form of new requests, which are stacked. When the stack is empty,
all recursive calls have been processed. Any local data is also stacked.

Take an (unrealistic) Pascal procedure which does a recursive call:

             procedure doit (number: integer);
             begin
                 if number > 1 then doit (number - 1)
             end;

In COBOL, we would implement this as follows:

              EXAMPLE.
                 MOVE   1    TO STACK-PTR
                 MOVE NUMBER TO STACKED-NUMBER (STACK-PTR)
                 PERFORM DOIT
                   UNTIL STACK-PTR = ZERO
                 .

             DOIT.
                 MOVE STACKED-NUMBER (STACK-PTR) TO NUMBER
                 SUBTRACT 1 FROM STACK-PTR
                 IF NUMBER > 1
                     SUBTRACT 1 FROM NUMBER
                     ADD      1 TO   STACK-PTR
                     MOVE NUMBER TO STACKED-NUMBER (STACK-PTR)
                 .

A good way to understand this technique is to take a recursive procedure
such as a tree-printing algorithm, and to implement it in COBOL. We show
below the famous `Towers of Hanoi' problem

1. Towers of Hanoi
In a monastery in Hanoi there are three diamond rods. God placed 64 gold
disks of different sizes - each with a central hole- on rod number 1 such
that the largest disk is at the bottom of the stack and the smallest disk is
on top. The monks' mission is to move all the disks onto rod number 2. Only
one disk may be moved at a time and never may a larger disk be placed on a
smaller one. If needed, disks may temporarily be placed on the third rod,
again in such a manner that no larger disk rests on a smaller one.
This classical problem can be solved by a recursive procedure that first
moves all disks except the last one from the source rod to the spare rod,
then moves the last - and largest - disk from the source rod to its final
position on the destination rod, and finally moves all disks from the spare
rod to the destination rod. Moving N-1 disks is then performed in the same
manner recursively until all disks have been moved:

               BEGIN INTEGER M;

                  PROCEDURE MOVE (N, SOURCE, DEST, SPARE);
                  INTEGER N; STRING SOURCE, DEST, SPARE;
                  BEGIN
                     IF N > 1 THEN MOVE (N-1, SOURCE, SPARE, DEST);
                     WRITE (OUT, "MOVE DISK ", N,
                                   " FROM ", SOURCE,
                                    " TO " ,  DEST);
                     IF N > 1 THEN MOVE (N-1, SPARE, DEST, SOURCE)
                  END;

                  WRITE (OUT, "NBR OF DISKS = "); READ (IN, M);
                  IF M > 0 THEN MOVE (M, "SOURCE", "DEST", "SPARE")
               END

The COBOL version of the program follows below. As we wish to illustrate
pseudo-recursion rather than input/output, we exceptionally tolerate a
non-portable program that uses ACCEPT and DISPLAY. The program counter is
explicitly managed with the variable WHAT-TO-DO and parameters (which are in
a sense local to each invocation of the procedure) are stacked along with
the program counter. The call of the procedure in the main program is
simulated by stacking the first set of parameters.
             000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
             000200 PROGRAM-ID.    HANOI.
             000300
             000400 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
             000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  91/04/22
             000600     -REVISED:  91/05/01.
             000700
             000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
             000900
             001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
             001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. ALMOST-PORTABLE.
             001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. ALMOST-PORTABLE.
             001300
             001400 DATA DIVISION.
             001500
             001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
             001700
             002000 01  STACK-SPACE.
             002100     02  STACK-PTR               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
             002200     02  STACK-ITEM                         OCCURS 64.
             002300         03  DISK-NBR            PIC 9(2).
             002400         03  SOURCE-ROD          PIC X(6).
             002500         03  DEST-ROD            PIC X(6).
             002600         03  SPARE-ROD           PIC X(6).
             002700         03  WHAT                PIC 9(1).
             002800
             002900 01  LOCAL-VARIABLES.
             003000     02  THE-DISK-NBR            PIC 9(2).
             003100     02  THE-SOURCE-ROD          PIC X(6)   VALUE
"SOURCE".
             003200     02  THE-DEST-ROD            PIC X(6)   VALUE "DEST".
             003300     02  THE-SPARE-ROD           PIC X(6)   VALUE
"SPARE".
             003400     02  THE-WHAT                PIC 9(1).
             003500
             003600 01  GLOBAL-VARIABLES.
             003700     02  SWAP-ROD                PIC X(6).
             003800     02  WHAT-TO-DO              PIC 9(1).
             003900
             004000 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
             004100 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
             004200     DISPLAY "NBR OF DISKS = " NO ADVANCING
             004300     ACCEPT THE-DISK-NBR
             004400
             004500     IF THE-DISK-NBR > ZERO
             004600         MOVE 1 TO STACK-PTR, WHAT-TO-DO
             004700         MOVE LOCAL-VARIABLES TO STACK-ITEM (1)
             004800         PERFORM MOVE-DISK
             004900           UNTIL STACK-PTR = ZERO
             005000     .
             005100     STOP RUN
             005200     .
             005300
             005400 MOVE-DISK.
             005500     MOVE STACK-ITEM (STACK-PTR) TO LOCAL-VARIABLES
             005600     IF WHAT-TO-DO = 1
             005700         PERFORM MOVE-DISKS-AWAY
             005800     ELSE
             005900     IF WHAT-TO-DO = 2
             006000         PERFORM SHOW-DISK-MOVED
             006100     ELSE
             006200     IF WHAT-TO-DO = 3
             006300         PERFORM MOVE-DISKS-BACK
             006400     ELSE
             006500         MOVE WHAT (STACK-PTR) TO WHAT-TO-DO
             006600         SUBTRACT 1 FROM STACK-PTR
             006700     .
             006800
             006900 MOVE-DISKS-AWAY.
             007000     MOVE THE-SPARE-ROD TO SWAP-ROD
             007100     MOVE THE-DEST-ROD  TO THE-SPARE-ROD
             007200     MOVE SWAP-ROD      TO THE-DEST-ROD
             007300     PERFORM MOVE-THE-DISKS
             007400     .
             007500
             007600 MOVE-THE-DISKS.
             007700     ADD 1 TO WHAT-TO-DO
             007800     IF THE-DISK-NBR > 1
             007900         SUBTRACT 1 FROM THE-DISK-NBR
             008000         MOVE WHAT-TO-DO TO THE-WHAT
             008100         ADD 1 TO STACK-PTR
             008200         MOVE LOCAL-VARIABLES TO STACK-ITEM (STACK-PTR)
             008400     .
             008500
             008600 SHOW-DISK-MOVED.
             008700     ADD 1 TO WHAT-TO-DO
             008800     DISPLAY "MOVE DISK " THE-DISK-NBR
             008900               " FROM "   THE-SOURCE-ROD
             009000                " TO "    THE-DEST-ROD
             009100     .
             009200
             009300 MOVE-DISKS-BACK.
             009400     MOVE THE-SPARE-ROD  TO SWAP-ROD
             009500     MOVE THE-SOURCE-ROD TO THE-SPARE-ROD
             009600     MOVE SWAP-ROD       TO THE-SOURCE-ROD
             009700     PERFORM MOVE-THE-DISKS
             009800     .

In a small program like this, the extra machinery needed to handle the
recursion looms large. In realistic programs  the extra code to implement
recursion is negligible. If a problem is inherently recursive - and many
are - it pays to think recursively and use the pseudo-recursive techniques
shown here. It is no excuse to say: COBOL does not support recursion
directly, therefore I cannot solve my problem. The programming language used
is very rarely the true blocking factor.
It is often said that recursion is too expensive in terms of overhead. This
is only true for trivial, small programs that use recursion where simple
iteration should have been used. In realistic programs, recursion is not in
the inner loop anyway and thus carries little or no run-time penalty. It is
also not true that recursion is difficult or esoteric to use.
```

```
I don't know what you mean by "COBOL/390" - because IBM doesn't have any
such product.  HOWEVER, "IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM" which is the current
product DOES excplicitly support recursive CALLs - and "local storage" or
local variables.
```

#### ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
"Ir. HS Hartkamp" wrote:
> 
> I have worked as a Cobol and CSP programmer and with a few other
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hans Hartkamp

I assume MVS because you mention CSP.

Use the latest release of cobol, alter the progr id section to:

program id.  prog1  with recursion.

RTM to check the actual syntax, worked for me perfectly.

If you perform a routine three times it looses itself.  You can
perform twice and get away with it though.  The third time it just
goes into an infinite loop.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qtta7$37o@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net>`

```
"CSP" is the name of an "OLD" IBM mainframe product (Now VisualGen - as I
recall).  If you are using the CURRENT IBM mainframe COBOL compiler it
*does* support recursion.   I do not remember what the older CSP product
did, but the latest version certainly COULD use COBOL's own recursion.
```

#### ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D33449.434F61D5@home.com>`

```
Ir. HS Hartkamp wrote:
> 
>  In Cobol, it is absolutely not possible to make a recursive
> call (i.e. a program or routine that calls itself).


       *>------------ recursion1.cbl ---------------------

       Program-id. recursion1.

       Working-storage section.
       01 answer                        pic x.
       01 DataTable.
          05 pic x(16) value "Adam      ,02,03".        *>  1
          05 pic x(16) value "Barbara   ,04,05".        *>  2
          05 pic x(16) value "Charles   ,99,99".        *>  3
          05 pic x(16) value "David     ,06,07".        *>  4
          05 pic x(16) value "Eileen    ,08,99".        *>  5
          05 pic x(16) value "Frederick ,09,10".        *>  6
          05 pic x(16) value "George    ,99,99".        *>  7
          05 pic x(16) value "Harry     ,99,99".        *>  8
          05 pic x(16) value "Helen     ,99,99".        *>  9
          05 pic x(16) value "Ivor      ,99,99".        *> 10
       01 DataTableB redefines DataTable.
          05 Person occurs 10.
             10 PersonName            pic x(10).
             10                       pic x.
             10 EldestPointer         pic 9(02). *> = 99 if none
             10                       pic x.
             10 SiblingPointer        pic 9(02). *> = 99 if none

       Local-storage section.
       01  TablePointer               pic 9(02).

       Linkage section.
       01  ParentPointer              pic 9(02).

       Procedure Division.
           move 1 to TablePointer
           call "Descendants" using TablePointer
           accept answer
           STOP RUN.

       entry "Descendants" using ParentPointer

       *> If no eldest child, merely displays name

           move EldestPointer(ParentPointer) to TablePointer

           if     TablePointer = 99
                  display PersonName(ParentPointer)

           else   perform until TablePointer = 99
                     call "Descendants" using TablePointer
                     move SiblingPointer(TablePointer)to TablePointer
                  end-perform
           End-if.

       *>-------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r16ug$7bc@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net> <37D33449.434F61D5@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:37D33449.434F61D5@home.com...
> Ir. HS Hartkamp wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
   <much snippage>
>
>        Local-storage section.
>        01  TablePointer               pic 9(02).
>
   <more snippage>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Any compiler that has the EXTENSION "LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION" almost certainly
is documented as supporting an EXTENSION of recursion.  The "whole point" of
Local-Storage is to distinguish between "working-storage" which is
"persistent" from "local-storage" which exists for each individual
recursion.
```

```
>I have worked as a Cobol and CSP programmer and with a few other languages
>jost for fun. In Cobol, it is absolutely not possible to make a recursive

You may do recursion with Acucobol, both to call programs and nested
performs. The latter however, is not something I would recommend as Cobol in
general does not have local variables.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990907232355.17169.00005845@ng-fe1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7r3vav$pq2$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote...

>You may do recursion with Acucobol, both to call programs and nested
>performs. The latter however, is not something I would recommend as Cobol in
>general does not have local variables.

COBOL for MVS & VM has local variables (using the local-storage section) and
supports recursive calls.

Do not confuse recursive calls with recursive performs (which I believe one
poster did). Recursive calls are supported on current IBM COBOL compilers, but
recursive performs are not.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
Howdy

True recursive calls cannot touch persistent variables. Thus insofar as
trying to call a PERFORM recursively it will most likely drive you nuts.
Since any half baked algorithm implementing recursion mandates a stack
engine for the entry points, I see no way to accomplish what you want to
do...

regards,
andre
```

```
You can recurse infinitely on any multi-threaded OS, in any language, even
mixing languages.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DC77DA.58E52BD1@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7rgcl1$du1$4@news.igs.net> <19990912210133.20349.00004522@ng-fc1.aol.com> <37dc677b.259238160@news1.ibm.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On 13 Sep 1999 01:01:33 GMT, frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY) wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> recursively.

And this could of course be implemented without using recursion if one
had the time and endurance to do so.  It's not that people are
"enthused" about recursion, or that the same effect could not be
achieved by other means.  It's just that there are some algorithms that
can be described with "less effort", "more naturally", or "more
elegantly" by using recursion (subjective measures to be sure, but "less
effort" should at least mean  "fewer statements").  One might even argue
that a very smart optimizing compiler ought to be able to transform the
generated code for a recursively used procedure into code that would be
at least as efficient as that from a nonrecursive source code
implementation that used stacks or lists to emulate the "recursive"
aspects of the algorithm.

Recursion is never "necessary"--after all, there is no real recursion at
the hardware level (you don't create a new physical instance of the
hardware running a copy of the same program)--it's all handled by saving
variable storage and other state information in an array, stack, or
entry of a list of finite size.  So it's not a matter of whether you can
do without the recursion, only whether using it in some way enhances the
comprehension of the algorithm by your intended audience.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** farmer@NOSPAMnetnet.net (Mark Framness)
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8Sd1uPikEGru-pn2-igfiT1tJxbnC@www.framnett.net>`
- **References:** `<7rgcl1$du1$4@news.igs.net> <19990912210133.20349.00004522@ng-fc1.aol.com> <37dc677b.259238160@news1.ibm.net> <37DC77DA.58E52BD1@acm.org>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 1999 04:04:42, "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org> wrote:

> 
> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> comprehension of the algorithm by your intended audience.

Correct! 

Recursion is never necessary (as is drinking beer is never necessary or 
driving cars is never necessary etc.).  I don't know how such things work 
in a mainframe environment but I do know that in the C/C++ world of PCs the
variables and such involved in a recursive call are placed on a stack.  
Therefore one can simulate stack behavior via recursion.  Of course one 
could write all their own code (or use a library) for stack functionality 
but then go through the hassle when you can force the system to simulate a 
stack for you!

As you say recursion is not for any benefit related to any system 
performance consideration but rather programmer performance consideration.



Mark & Lorie
farmer@netnet.net
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53cD3.933$Bw1.7994@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7rgcl1$du1$4@news.igs.net> <19990912210133.20349.00004522@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
FRLSFLY wrote:
>
>I'm still not sure why people are so enthused about recursion; I have
>yet to see a real-world problem where iteration structures would not
>only do the job; but would do it faster, cleaner and with fewer
>resource constraints.

I don't want to offend, but your comment speaks more to the limitations
of your personal experience than lack of need for recursive logic.  ;-)
I would agree that there is no recursive code that cannot be written
using iteration instead.  But there are many algorithms that are far
easier to write and much clearer and easier to understand when written
recursively.  Quicksort is just one very simple example.  A much more
complex one is doing component parts explosions and implosions in
manufacturing applications.  Yes, you *can* do it using iteration, but
you don't want to. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00

```

FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990912210133.20349.00004522@ng-fc1.aol.com...
> Well, not infinitely, eventually you'll blow your OS file size limits or
system
> stowage..
>
> I'm still not sure why people are so enthused about recursion; I have yet
to
> see a real-world problem where iteration structures would not only do the
job;
> but would do it faster, cleaner and with fewer resource constraints.

At a former shop where working the "Jumble" puzzle in the paper was the big
thing, I wrote a COBOL program to display all 720 permutations of a six
character string using several arrays and without recursion.  Of course
there was a limit to the time I was willing to put into that program but I
saw no simple way to eliminate repeated characters or change the length of
the character string.  Being able to use recursion in Pascal and C made
these features easy to implement.

Like all tools, recursion has its time and place.  If you would like to
transcribe my code into COBOL w/o recursion, be my guest.

http://personal.lig.bellsouth.net/~wbport/programmer/jumble.c
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rjvqj$2pi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7rgcl1$du1$4@news.igs.net> <19990912210133.20349.00004522@ng-fc1.aol.com> <2edD3.8655$N77.663731@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
Warren Porter <warrenp123@netdoor123.com> wrote:

: At a former shop where working the "Jumble" puzzle in the paper was the big
: thing, I wrote a COBOL program to display all 720 permutations of a six
: character string using several arrays and without recursion.  Of course
: there was a limit to the time I was willing to put into that program but I
: saw no simple way to eliminate repeated characters or change the length of
: the character string.  Being able to use recursion in Pascal and C made
: these features easy to implement.

Usually the only advantage of recursion is that it looks after
the stack for you, and recursion can be done from within the
routine itself.

In converting from a recursion to an iteritive equivalent it
is necessary to add a stack for the intermediate results and
code to step up and down this and to ensure that the actual
repitition is done externally.

That last point can be exemplified as:  convert

             PERFORM Factorial

          Factorial.

              ....
                   PERFORM Factorial
              .

(this is invalid and cannot work) into

              PERFORM Factorial
                   UNTIL Factorial-Complete

           Factorial.
                ....
                    SET Factorial-Complete TO TRUE
                .

While managing one's own stack does impose an actual limit, this
does not provide a real constraint in practical cases.  In fact
there is always a limit to recursion even if it is the system stack
size.

Somewhere I have a QuickSort that I had converted from recursive
to iteritive when I did a Cobol version.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** farmer@NOSPAMnetnet.net (Mark Framness)
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00

- **References:** `<7rgcl1$du1$4@news.igs.net> <19990912210133.20349.00004522@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 1999 01:01:33, frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY) wrote:

> Well, not infinitely, eventually you'll blow your OS file size limits or system
> stowage..
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> but would do it faster, cleaner and with fewer resource constraints.

Most stuff I've seen working with dynamic data structures use recursion.


> 
> Implementing recursion is like learning to fart 'The Warsaw Concerto": it is a
> talent of interest only to a limited audience, and has little practical
> application outside of that small circle of flatulent enthusiasts.

Bull stuff!

I at one time thought recursion was used for efficiency purposes.  I did 
some experimenting (with OS/2, Visual Age C++ and its various tools) and 
found out I was right.  Coding efficiency not run time or resource 
efficiency.  Recursion quickly lags behind iteration as the number of 
calls/iterations gets larger.  But there are coding applications that are 
most easily coded with recursive techniques.

I can't remember the exact problem we were trying to solve but a buddy & 
myself were trying to do some recursion in COBOL II.  It didn't work but I 
wish it did!


Mark & Lorie Framness
farmer@netnet.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_O9B3.16565$KM3.137919@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net>`

```
Ir. HS Hartkamp wrote:
>I have worked as a Cobol and CSP programmer and with a few other languages
>jost for fun. In Cobol, it is absolutely not possible to make a recursive
>call (i.e. a program or routine that calls itself). It's not even possible
>if you use multiple programs that -sneaky- call itself via a chain of
>programs.

This is simply not true.  It is entirely dependent on the compiler,
not the language.  Every COBOL compiler I have used professionally
does support recursive PERFORMS, and I have written recursive COBOL
code on occasion, when it was particularly useful.  In general, a
stack oriented system architecture will provide a naturally recursive
COBOL compiler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D5954E.48918B69@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net> <_O9B3.16565$KM3.137919@news1.mia>`

```
Recursive performs are dangerous and limited on the MVS COBOL's (and
cause less efficient code to be generated) and would not work on the
long ago obsoleted RCA301 I progammed for between 1961 and 1966.  The
semi-recursive perform generation on MVS COBOLs is 
PERFORM 2000-PARA.
	MVC  PERFORM-EXIT-HOLD,2000-PARA-EXIT-RETURN-ADDRESS save the current
exit return 								address
	MVC  2000-PARA-EXIT-RETURN-ADDRESS,=A(*+14) address following the
branch to 2000-PARA
	L    R15,=A(2000-PARA) load register 15 with the address of 2000-PARA
	BR   R15     branch to 2000-PARA
	MVC  2000-PARA-EXIT=RETURN-ADDRESS,PERFORM-EXIT-HOLD restore the saved
exit return 				address    

The exit is
        L    R15,2000-PARA-EXIT-RETURN-ADDRESS load the address of the
return, initial the 				address of the next paragraph
	BR   R15

        If the preceding PERFORM is executed twice without the first
PERFORM completing due to recursion, things will get interesting.

The RCA code for the above would have been
PERFORM 2000-PARA.
        V1   5429,5300          Save the address of the next instruction
in the exit instruction 				for 2000-PARA and branch to 2000-PARA

The exit for this example was at location 5420 and was
	V1   5429,5430          Branch to next instruction or return point and
reset instruction 				to branch to next instruction.

Clark F. Morris, JR. CFM Technical Programming Services,
morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
                    
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
>>  snipped..
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Judson McClendon      judmc123@bellsouth.net  (remove numbers)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D667C5.7A957812@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net> <_O9B3.16565$KM3.137919@news1.mia> <37D5954E.48918B69@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> Recursive performs are dangerous and limited on the MVS COBOL's (and
> cause less efficient code to be generated) and would not work on the
> long ago obsoleted RCA301 I progammed for between 1961 and 1966.  The
> semi-recursive perform generation on MVS COBOLs is

...snipped the formal proof of a limited compiler...

I raised this with IBM and they responded with:

It is a documentation problem and this will be fixed in the next
release.

i.e.: You cannot recursively perform.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Thanks: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** "Ir. HS Hartkamp" <hartkamp@casema.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d6e9ca$0$19254@reader1.casema.net>`
- **References:** `<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net>`

```

Ir. HS Hartkamp heeft geschreven in bericht
<37d1b57d$0$18591@reader2.casema.net>...
>   <original question>

Thanks all,

I have downloaded a cobol compiler for my pc, and written a recursive
program in Cobol74. It worked.

As a programmer, I often tell my colleagues to keep in mind that "computers
should do what *we* want them to do" and not "we should want what computers
tell us is possible"

Helped by the answers on my original posting I feel connfident that this
statement is still true.

Hans Hartkamp
```

## Thread: Help needed COBOL 6.50 Compiler — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [1999-08](1999-08.md#t-110196d3f5)._

#### ↳ Re: Help needed COBOL 6.50 Compiler

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bef67a$1d999500$0100007f@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<eQ63B4k8#GA.145@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
Go to the MSLFILES area of Microsoft's site and download the
LNK563.EXE
file, which is a self-extracting archive containing MS's last 16 bit
linker,
version 5.63 (surprise, surprise!) and see if that works better.  V3.05
is a really OLD version of LINK.

Alternatively, download Fujitsu's free win16 targeting Cobol, which is
a stable and mature product.  COBOL 6.50 was never finished as
the developer died.
```

## Thread: HELP IN DEBUGGING PROGRAM-LONG — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP IN DEBUGGING PROGRAM-LONG

- **From:** "Teresa A. Robinson" <trobinson@wworld.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d1901b@news.wworld.com>`

```
It never gets to the print table section.  I would appreciate any help in
where my problem is.  The sort is a bubble sort taken from a handout so I
don't know if it works.  Input file is in previous post also.  Course
layout:  Department  A/N    3
              Course         N     4
                Section       N     3
                Filler             A/N  70

Student file layout is
SSN          A/N 9
               CLASS     N  9
               DEPT        A/N  3
                 COURSE   N  4
                 SECTION    N   3

I will list code as is currently, I was going to try the change I listed and
change the input data to exactly as shown in the layouts.  I am bringing the
data in as alphanumeric in this listing.

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  PGM1A.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
        SELECT STUDENT-FILE   ASSIGN TO DA-S-STUDENT.
        SELECT COURSE-FILE    ASSIGN TO DA-S-COURSE.
        SELECT PRINT-FILE          ASSIGN TO UR-S-PRINT.
        SELECT ERROR-FILE        ASSIGN TO UR-S-ERROR.
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
FD  COURSE-FILE
    RECORDING MODE IS F
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  COURSE-REC                PIC X(80).
FD  STUDENT-FILE
    RECORDING MODE IS F
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  STUDENT-REC            PIC X(80).
FD  PRINT-FILE
    RECORDING MODE IS F
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  PRINT-REC                    PIC X(132).
FD  ERROR-FILE
    RECORDING MODE IS F
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  ERROR-REC                PIC X(132).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
*VARIABLE LIST
*EOF-1 = SWITCH FOR COURSE-FILE
*EOF-2 = SWITCH FOR STUDENT-FILE
*PAGE-CT = PAGE ACCUMULATOR
*SAVE-DEPT = COMPARISON AREA FOR CONTROL BREAK
*HOLD-COURSE = USED FOR BUBBLE-SORT HOLD AREA
*SUB AND SUB2 ARE SUBSCRIPTS FOR TABLE USE
01  MISC.
        03    EOF-1                    PIC X  VALUE 'N'.
        03    EOF-2                    PIC X VALUE 'N'.
        03    PAGE-CT                PIC 99 VALUE 0.
        03    SAVE-DEPT        PIC XXX VALUE '???'.
        03    ARRAY-SIZE        PIC 999 VALUE 0.
        03    SUB                        PIC S999 COMP VALUE +0.
        03    SUB2                    PIC S999 COMP VALUE +0.
*VARIABLES FOR BUBBLE SORT
        03    HOLD-COURSE        PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
        03    BUBBLE-SORTED        PIC X VALUE 'N'.
        03    SLIST-SIZE                    PIC 99 VALUE 0.
        03     LOOP-COUNT            PIC 99 VALUE 1.
        03      ARRAY-FULL            PIC X VALUE 'N'.
        03     NUM-SWITCHES        PIC 99 VALUE 0.
*COURSE RECORD LAYOUT
01    COURSE-DATA.
        03    CO-DEPT                PIC XXX.
        03    CO-COURSE          PIC XXXX.
        03    CO-SECTION          PIC XXX.
        03    FILLER                     PIC X(70).
*STUDENT RECORD LAYOUT
01    STUDENT-DATA.
        03    ST-SSN                PIC X(9).
        03     ST-CLASS           PIC 9.
        03      ST-DEPT-COURSE-SECTION.
                05    ST-DEPT        PIC XXX.
                05    ST-COURSE    PIC XXXX.
                05    ST-SECTION    PIC XXX.
        03    FILLER                PIC X(60).
*SYSTEMS DATE AREA
01    TODAYS-DATE-AREA.
        03    TODAYS-YEAR        PIC  XX.
        03    TODAYS-MONTH    PIC XX.
        03    TODAYS-DAY        PIC XX.
*PAGE HEADER 1 LAYOUT
01    PAGE-HEADER1.
        03    FILLER            PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
        03    FILLER            PIC X(4) VALUE 'PGM1'.
        03    FILLER        PIC X(46) VALUE SPACES.
        03    FILLER        PIC X(8) VALUE 'COM3290'.
        03    FILLER        PIC X(51) VALUE SPACES.
        03    FILLER        PIC X(5) VALUE 'PAGE'.
        03     H1-PAGE-NUM        PIC ZZ9.
*PAGE HEADER 2 LAYOUT
01  PAGE-HEADER2.
        03    FILLER            PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
        03    H2-MONTH        PIC XX.
        03    FILLER            PIC X VALUE '/'.
        03    H2-DAY        PIC XX.
        03    FILLER        PIC X VALUE '/'.
        03    H2-YEAR        PIC XX.
        03    FILLER            PIC X(38) VALUE SPACES.
        03    FILLER            PIC X(18) VALUE 'NUMBER OF STUDENTS'.
*PAGE HEADER 3 LAYOUT.
01  PAGE-HEADER3.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(58) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(14) VALUE 'BY CLASS LEVEL'.
*PAGE HEADER 4 LAYOUT.
01  PAGE-HEADER4.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(61) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(9) VALUE 'BY COURSE'.
*GROUP HEADER LAYOUT
01    GROUP-HEADER.
    03  FILLER                PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(4) VALUE 'DEPT'
    03    FILLER                PIC X(9) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(6) VALUE 'COURSE'.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(9) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(7) VALUE 'SECTION'.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC XX    VALUE 'FR'.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC XX  VALUE  'SO'.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC XX  VALUE 'JR'.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC XX  VALUE 'SR'.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                PIC XX  VALUE 'GR'.
*ERROR LINE LAYOUT
01    ERROR-DATA.
    03    FILLER                PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
    03     FILLER                PIC X(15) VALUE 'ERROR --- SSN = '.
    03    ER-SSN                PIC X(9).
    03    FILLER                    PIC XXX VALUE SPACES.
    03    FILLER                    PIC X(8) VALUE  'CLASS = '.
    03    ER-CLASS                PIC 9.
    03    FILLER                    PIC X(12) VALUE '    COURSE  =  '.
    03    ER-DEPT-COURSE-SECTION     PIC X(10).
    03    FILLER                PIC X(20) VALUE '0500-ERROR - NO MATCH'.
*PRINT LINE LAYOUT
01    PRINT-DATA.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
    03    PR-DEPT        PIC XXX.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(12) VALUE SPACES.
    03    PR-COURSE    PIC XXXX.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(12) VALUE SPACES.
    03    PR-SECTION    PIC XXX.
    03    FILLER            PIC X(12) VALUE SPACES.
    03    PR-CTR-1 OCCURS 5 TIMES.
            05    PR-CTR            PIC ZZ9.
            05    FILLER            PIC X(12) VALUE SPACES.
*LAYOUT FOR ARRAY-STORAGE TABLE
01    COURSE-TABLE.
        03    COURSE-ARRAY.
                05    TABLE-ITEM OCCURS 1 TO 25 TIMES
                                DEPENDING ON ARRAY-SIZE
                                ASCENDING KEY IS T-COURSE
                                        INDEXED BY C1.
                                        08    T-COURSE                PIC
X(10).
                                        08    US-COURE-STRING REDEFINES
T-COURSE.
                                                10    US-DEPT            PIC
XXX.
                                                10    US-COURSE        PIC
XXXX.
                                                10    US-SECTION        PIC
XXX.
                                        08    T-CTR OCCURS 5 TIMES     PIC
999.
*
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000-MAINLINE.
    OPEN INPUT    STUDENT-FILE, COURSE-FILE
            OUTPUT        PRINT-FILE, ERROR-FILE.
    PERFORM 9998-READ-COURSE-FILE.
    PERFORM 0010-LOAD-TABLE
            VARYING C1 FROM 1 BY 1
                    UNTIL C1 > 25 OR EOF-1 = 'Y'.
    CLOSE COURSE-FILE.
    PERFORM 0030-SORT-TABLE
            UNTIL BUBBLE-SORTED = 'Y' OR LOOP-COUNT = ARRAY-SIZE.
    PERFORM 9999-READ-STUDENT-FILE.
    PERFORM 0100-PROCESS-DATA
            UNTIL EOF-2 = 'Y'.
    PERFORM 1000-PRINT-TABLE.
    CLOSE PRINT-FILE
                    STUDENT-FILE
                    ERROR-FILE.
    STOP RUN.
*LOAD TABLE
0010-LOAD-TABLE.
        SET ARRAY-SIZE TO C1.
         STRING CO-DEPT DELIMITED BY SIZE
                        CO-COURSE DELIMITED BY SIZE
                        CO-SECTION DELIMITED BY SIZE
                                INTO T-COURSE (C1).
                                PERFORM  0015-INITIALIZE-TABLE VARYING SUB2
                                        FROM 1 BY 1
                                                UNTIL SUB2 > 5.
PERFORM 9998-READ-COURSE-FILE.
*INITIALIZES TABLE CTRS
0015-INITIALIZE-TABLE.
        SET SUB TO C1.
        MOVE 0 TO T-CTR (SUB, SUB2).
*SORT THE ARRAY
0030-SORT-TABLE.
        MOVE 0 TO NUM-SWITCHES.
         MOVE ARRAY-SIZE TO SUB.
          PERFORM 0035-MOVE-IT
                    UNTIL (SUB = SLIST-SIZE + 1).
            IF NUM-SWITCHES = 0 THEN
                    MOVE 'Y' TO BUBBLE-SORTED.
            ADD 1 TO SLIST-SIZE.
            ADD 1 TO LOOP-COUNT.
*MOVE IT FOR SORT
0035-MOVE-IT.
        IF T-COURSE (SUB) < T-COURSE (SUB - 1) THEN
                MOVE T-COURSE (SUB - 1) TO HOLD-COURSE
                MOVE T-COURSE (SUB) TO T-COURSE (SUB - 1)
                MOVE HOLD-COURSE TO T-COURSE (SUB)
        END-IF.
        SUBTRACT 1 FROM SUB.
*PROCESS THE TABLE COUNTERS AND PRODUCE AN ERROR REPORT
0100-PROCESS-DATA.
        SEARCH ALL TABLE-ITEM
                    AT END PERFORM 0500-ERROR-REPORT
                    WHEN T-COURSE (C1) = ST-DEPT-COURSE-SECTION
                    PERFORM 0200-FIND-MATCH.
                    PERFORM 9999-READ-STUDENT-FILE.
*FIND MATCHING RECORD  TO INCREMENT COUNTERS
0200-FIND-MATCH.
        IF ST-CLASS IS NUMERIC
                IF ST-CLASS < 6 AND ST-CLASS > 0 THEN
                        SET SUB TO C1
        ADD 1 TO T-CTR (SUB, ST-CLASS).
*PROCESS ERROR REPORT
0500-ERROR-REPORT.
        MOVE ST-SSN TO ER-SSN.
        MOVE ST-CLASS TO ER-CLASS.
        MOVE T-COURSE (C1) TO ER-DEPT-COURSE-SECTION.
        WRITE ERROR-REC FROM ERROR-DATA
                AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
*PRINT THE TABLE
1000-PRINT-TABLE.
        PERFORM 1001-GET-TODAYS-DATE.
        PERFORM 1050-PROCESS-ONE-DEPT
                UNTIL ST-DEPT NOT = SAVE-DEPT OR EOF-2 = 'Y'.
*ACCEPT TODAYS SYSTEM DATE
1001-GET-TODAYS-DATE.
         ACCEPT TODAYS-DATE-AREA FROM DATE.
        MOVE TODAYS-MONTH TO H2-MONTH.
        MOVE TODAYS-DAY TO H2-DAY.
        MOVE TODAYS-YEAR TO H2-YEAR.
*PROCESS ONE DEPT THEN CONTROL BREAK
1050-PROCESS-ONE-DEPT.
        IF SAVE-DEPT NOT = US-DEPT (C1) THEN
                PERFORM 2000-PAGE-HEADER1
                PERFORM 3000-PAGE-HEADER2
                PERFORM 4000-PAGE-HEADER3
                PERFORM 5000-PAGE-HEADER4
                PERFORM 5500-GROUP-HEADER
                    MOVE US-DEPT (C1) TO SAVE-DEPT.
                PERFORM 6000-PRINT-IT
                        VARYING C1 FROM 1 BY 1
                                UNTIL C1 > ARRAY-SIZE.
*PAGE HEADERS
2000-PAGE-HEADER1.
            MOVE PAGE-CT TO H1-PAGE-NUM.
            WRITE PRINT-REC FROM PAGE-HEADER1
                    AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.
3000-PAGE-HEADER2.
            WRITE PRINT-REC FROM PAGE-HEADER2
                    AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
4000-PAGE-HEADER3.
            WRITE PRINT-REC FROM PAGE-HEADER3
                    AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
5000-PAGE-HEADER4.
            WRITE PRINT-REC FROM PAGE-HEADER4
                    AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
5500-GROUP-HEADER.
            WRITE PRINT-REC FROM GROUP-HEADER
                    AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
            MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-REC.
            WRITE PRINT-REC
                AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
*PRINT THE TABLE (FINALLY)
6000-PRINT-IT.
            MOVE US-DEPT (C1) TO PR-DEPT.
            MOVE US-COURSE (C1) TO PR-COURSE.
            MOVE US-SECTION (C1) TO PR-SECTION.
            PERFORM 6001-MOVE-COLUMNS
                    VARYING SUB2 FROM 1 BY 1
                            UNTIL SUB2 > 5.
             WRITE PRINT-REC FROM PRINT-DATA
                        AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
*MOVE THE COLUMNS
6001-MOVE-COLUMNS.
        SET SUB TO C1.
        MOVE T-CTR (SUB, SUB2) TO PR-CTR (SUB2).
*READ COURSE-FILE
9998-READ-COURSE-FILE.
        READ COURSE-FILE INTO COURSE-DATA
                AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-1.
*READ STUDENT-FILE
9999-READ-STUDENT-FILE.
        READ STUDENT-FILE INTO STUDENT-DATA
                AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-2.

SAMPLE DATA FOR  COURSE IS:
COM3330001
BUS3500004
COM3290001
COM3290002
ACC2101001
ENG1001002
CHM4610001
ART2000001
COM3330002
BUS3600002
ENG2100001
ENG2100002
ENG2400009
ACC3101001 AND SO ON
SAMPLE STUDENT DATA
0001002221COM001
0001002221COM002
0001112223ACC001
0001112223COM001
0001112223ZOO001
0001113322COM001
0002112225COM001
0088775564COM001
0112345674MAR001
1101112224COM001
1234537891BUS004
1234537891COM002 AND SO ON

If you can help me locate the problems I would be most appreciative.  Thanks
in advance for your assistance.
```

## Thread: Call c++ from Cobol — 3 msgs

### Call c++ from Cobol

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-09-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7OF7KAglOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

I want to get an environment variable.
It seems to me that i have to use c++ instead of cobol.

I want to get the values of the environment variable
COMPUTERNAME

In c++ there is a getenv and putenv.

I have to use a pointer of type char.
At the other site i only want to use 8characters.

So here my questions:
=====================
Must i translate the charset between netexpress 3.0 and c++ 5.0?
(have to do this with cobol workbench 4.0.26)

How can i convert a null terminated cstring to a parameter in
cobol like that:
----------------
01 PARAMETER.
   05  DATA PIC X(008).
                   ^
C++:               !
----               !
char *envvalue ----+


Or is there any function in netexpress out to do this job?
```

<a id="t-d14c322953"></a>
## Thread: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs — 8 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** Barry <bgumleyNOlfSPAM@tibinc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com>`

```
I am working at a shop that has some pretty ugly code.  
I've always been accustomed to Call statements being 
immediately followed by Cancel statements.  We are running 
Microfocus Cobol on a HP-UX box and are receiving "out of 
memory errors from the OS.  I suggested that they may be 
happening because none of the programs have Cancel verbs 
used and that MF may not be releasing them from memory.  If 
cancels are not used, then when does Cobol free them? When 
the calling program executes an "exit program"?  Or does it 
not happen until a stop run is encountered??

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com>`

```
Interestingly enough - there is nothing in the COBOL Standard that REQUIRES
that a COBOL run-time "release storage" when a CANCEL is done.  The only
thing that CANCEL is "guaranteed to do" is to place the sub-routine in
"initial state" when it is NEXT called.

It is true that many (not all) implementations DO "free storage" - but with
Micro Focus (for example) this is actually subject to a run-time switch.
With IBM mainframe COBOL, it is dependent upon the DYNAM compiler option.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** Barry <bgumleyNOfeSPAM@tibinc.com>

```
William,  Thanks for responding to my post. So if I have 
the MF "free memory" runtime switch set, when will the 
memory be freed? When the called program exits? Do we still 
need to put Cancels in?  Since the called programs do not 
have the Cancels now, inserting them and having them set to 
their Initial state, may end up causing some undesired 
effects as many of these programs are called more then once 
by the calling programs during a single execution.  Many, 
but not all of the programs use a common (copybook) cleanup 
routine so inserting Cancels would not be as difficult as 
it may sound.  One more thing, I believe AcuCobol had a 
"All" or "All Programs" option for the Cancel verb.  Does 
MF have something similar?  Obviously I have no manuals 
otherwise I wouldn't be asking these questions, so please 
bear with me.

The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r51t0$mea@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <13c3b2b7.2d2a1050@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com>`

```
I waited a day or two hoping that some CURRENT MFer would respond to this -
but hearing none, let me give you my (possibly out of date) information:

1) Storage will still NOT be freed unless you do an explicit CANCEL

2) No, Micro Focus does NOT have a "CANCEL ALL" option (that I am aware of)

Again, this may be out of date, but I believe that it is PROBABLY still
true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D6323F.885CEEAA@MERANT.com>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <13c3b2b7.2d2a1050@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r51t0$mea@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> 1) Storage will still NOT be freed unless you do an explicit CANCEL

Correct, and sometimes not even then (the Runtime System has a cache).
As someone else mentioned, there is a configuration option (runtime
switch) to disable the cache.

> 2) No, Micro Focus does NOT have a "CANCEL ALL" option (that I am aware of)

The CHAIN (or CALL PROGRAM) verb will effectively do this.

Cheers, Kev.
```

#### ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "Ian Marshall" <Ian.Marshall@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r631h$1kt$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com>`

```
As already mentioned by several other people, if you don't CANCEL, the
memory will not be free'ed. However, you may be able to get better memory
usage by changing how the application is built.

If you compile to int or gnt code, the program is loaded by the COBOL
runtime into data space - each user on the system will get their own unique
copy of the program.  This can rapidly drain system memory as you add users.

If you compile the modules to shared object (shared library on HP) or shared
gnt (.snt - an HP specific format, really just a shared library), then there
will be one copy of the program in memory for all users. This doesn't affect
memory usage for one user but is much more efficient system wide for
multiple users.

If you're currently using int or gnt format, I'd recomment giving shared
gnts or libraries a try.

NB: Later releases of the HP-UX product replaced gnt's with snt's (but
called them gnt's) - check your specific product docs to see what you have.

Final question - you mention that you get "out of memory" errors from the
OS.  Is this the COBOL runtime reporting the error or is it the OS reporting
it on the system console?
```

<a id="t-5e684d9b97"></a>
## Thread: A User-Defined Function in COBOL — 8 msgs

### A User-Defined Function in COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Laments for the lack of user-defined functions in COBOL are a regular
feature of this newsgroup.  However, I will claim that the code below
is a COBOL user-defined function.

This code does a right justified move of the data from one field to
another.  (Something else that is often missed in this newsgroup.)

You execute it with the statement

CALL 'ritejust' USING BY CONTENT   LENGTH OF source-field
                                             source-field
                                   LENGTH OF destin-field
                      BY REFERENCE           destin-field

Notes.

1. The format for the LENGTH OF value varies by compiler.  The format
given here (COMP-5 PIC S9(9)) is for Fujitsu's Power COBOL.  IBM's
COBOL II uses COMP PIC 9(9).  You may have to change the format to
agree with your compiler.

2. The format for link-fields - X(10000) - would appear to violate
memory protection.  However, there is only an error if you try to
access protected memory.  Just defining a field that would violate
protected memory is not an error.  As it is, this format only defines
the maximum field size.


 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. ritejust.

* This program right justifies data.

* This program accepts four parameters
* - the length of the source field as produced by the
*   LENGTH OF function
* - the source field (up to 1000 characters)
* - the length of the destination field as produced by the
*   LENGTH OF function
* - the destination field (up to 1000 characters)

 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  sub1                                     COMP   PIC S9(9).

 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01  link-source-length                       COMP-5 PIC S9(9).
 01  link-source-field                               PIC X(1000).
 01  link-destin-length                       COMP-5 PIC S9(9).
 01  link-destin-field         JUST RIGHT            PIC X(1000).

 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING link-source-length
                          link-source-field
                          link-destin-length
                          link-destin-field.
 0000-main.
* Find the last character used in the source field
     PERFORM
       VARYING sub1 FROM link-source-length BY -1
       UNTIL sub1 <= 1 OR
             link-source-field(sub1:1) NOT = SPACE
       CONTINUE
     END-PERFORM
* right justify the data into the destination field
     MOVE link-source-field(1:sub1)
       TO link-destin-field(1:link-destin-length)

     GOBACK
     .

 END PROGRAM ritejust.
```

#### ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I5uA3.9571$KM3.79161@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne wrote:
>Laments for the lack of user-defined functions in COBOL are a regular
>feature of this newsgroup.  However, I will claim that the code below
>is a COBOL user-defined function.

The code you posted is a 'procedure', not a 'function'.  The difference
is that a function returns a value but a procedure does not, meaning
that a function can be included in an expression.  For example, the
COBOL intrinsic function 'COS' can be used like this:

  COMPUTE C = (A * A + B * B - 2 * A * B * FUNCTION COS(ANGLE)) ** .5

Such is not the case with a procedure, because the procedure can only
return values indirectly through a data field.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7quhh3$c88$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <I5uA3.9571$KM3.79161@news1.mia>`

```
In article <I5uA3.9571$KM3.79161@news1.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> Ed Milne wrote:
> >Laments for the lack of user-defined functions in COBOL are a regular
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The code you posted is a 'procedure', not a 'function'.  The
difference
> is that a function returns a value but a procedure does not, meaning
> that a function can be included in an expression.  For example, the
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not necessarily.  From the Wedopedia

"(1) In programming, a named section of a program that performs a
specific task. In this sense, a function is a type of procedure or
routine. Some programming languages make a distinction between a
function, which returns a value, and a procedure, which performs some
operation but does not return a value.
Most programming languages come with a prewritten set of functions that
are kept in a library. You can also write your own functions to perform
specialized tasks. "

The documentation that I look at tend to use the wider interpretation
of a function.

In any case, the important point is that you can write COBOL code to
perform the same processing on any field.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r1a9q$qfd$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
    I am afraid that I must join the Chorus, and disagree.  The purpose of a
label
is to identify.  Calling a procedure a function, even if you find an
authority that agrees with you serves no usefull purpose.

    Only when the secondary definition serves a purpose, such as what is a
"K", 1024 or 1000, do we have reason for confusion.  Note: 1000 vs 1024 is a
"religious" topic - no comments on that subject are necessary.





Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:7quhh3$c88$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TlPA3.303$oo5.2224@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <I5uA3.9571$KM3.79161@news1.mia> <7quhh3$c88$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>> Ed Milne wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>operation but does not return a value.

It is not a question of how any individual language is implemented,
but a question of technical terminology that is quite non-trivial.

>Most programming languages come with a prewritten set of functions that
>are kept in a library. You can also write your own functions to perform
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>of a function.

Unfortunately, the people who write books often don't understand all
the ramifications of what they are writing.  The distinction between
the words 'procedure' and 'function' is very important.  To try and
make them synonyms is a big mistake; else how would we distinguish
between the two concepts without a lot of verbage?

>In any case, the important point is that you can write COBOL code to
>perform the same processing on any field.

Yes, I agree. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r16d5$742@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <I5uA3.9571$KM3.79161@news1.mia> <7quhh3$c88$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TlPA3.303$oo5.2224@news3.mia>`

```
FYI -

A) Check your current compiler's documentation. Many (but not all) current
COBOL products provide a "RETURNING" or "GIVING" option which allows a
"sub-routine" to return a value to its "invoking" code.

B) In the draft of the next Standard, there are two "enhancements' related
to the specific addition of user-defined functions:
 - Function-ID paragraph (and function proto-types)
 - RETURNING on the PROCEDURE DIVISION header (now called a "marker")

NOTE WELL:
   There is an "incredibly odd" situation in the draft of the next Standard.
The RETURNING phrase can be specified on the EXIT PROGRAM statement (or the
new GoBack statement) which allows a "traditional" COBOL called sub-routine
(aka procedure) to be used as a FUNCTION in *any* application written in any
programming language OTHER than COBOL - which ONLY allows a "function" (with
a function-id paragraph) to be used as an "inline" reference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

- **Message-ID:** `<37d48571.36894266@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <I5uA3.9571$KM3.79161@news1.mia> <7quhh3$c88$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <TlPA3.303$oo5.2224@news3.mia> <7r16d5$742@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>FYI -
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

FYI - I was suprised NOT to find this feature in 4.0 of Fujitsu COBOL
- I have not checked 4.2.  In 4.0 the "return" is stored in a reserved
word called "PROGRAM-STATUS", and returning is not accepted in a CALL
statement.
```

#### ↳ Re: A User-Defined Function in COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qugbm$2le$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7qtgdn$n17$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote:
: Laments for the lack of user-defined functions in COBOL are a regular
: feature of this newsgroup.  However, I will claim that the code below
: is a COBOL user-defined function.

: CALL 'ritejust' USING BY CONTENT   LENGTH OF source-field
:                                              source-field
:                                    LENGTH OF destin-field
:                       BY REFERENCE           destin-field


It is a sub-routine but is not a 'function', it is user-defined
but it is not a function.

The characteristics of a function include: returning a value
and being able to be used in an expression where the returned
value is substuted for the function call.

For example:

              COMPUTE the-result = 1 + ( 2 * myfunction(mydata) )

If it cannot be used that way it is not a function.
```

## Thread: Creating Sample Data for COBOL Program Using Personal Microfocus — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Creating Sample Data for COBOL Program Using Personal Microfocus

- **From:** robert2504@aol.com (Robert2504)
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990905052225.07582.00001528@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Hi,
 I'm having problems creating sample data for my COBOL programs.
The text editors that I use keep adding strange characters to my data.
I have several editors including QEdit, Edit and some others. Maybe I just
don't know how to use them. How do I tell it that that is the end of the first
record or the end of line. The program reads the first record fine but seems to
add a space and then the end of line character to the first field of the next
record. Is there an editor that is easier. I keep getting invalid character in
numeric field. Any help is appreciated.

Robert Hernandez
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Sample Data for COBOL Program Using Personal Microfocus

- **From:** "RedRiver" <instruct@zaz.com.br>
- **Date:** 1999-09-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qtrn2$sui$1@srv4-poa.nutecnet.com.br>`
- **References:** `<19990905052225.07582.00001528@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```

Robert2504 <robert2504@aol.com> escreveu nas not�cias de
mensagem:19990905052225.07582.00001528@ng-fq1.aol.com...
> Hi,
>  I'm having problems creating sample data for my COBOL programs.
> The text editors that I use keep adding strange characters to my data.
> I have several editors including QEdit, Edit and some others. Maybe I just
> don't know how to use them. How do I tell it that that is the end of the
first
> record or the end of line. The program reads the first record fine but
seems to
> add a space and then the end of line character to the first field of the
next
> record. Is there an editor that is easier. I keep getting invalid
character in
> numeric field. Any help is appreciated.
>
> Robert Hernandez

Mr. Robert,

If you are using Line Sequential files created by editors, try to append

FILLER PIC x(001)

at the end of your record definition in the FD. The record separator (LF
and/or CR) will be ignored and the next READs will proceed OK.

Hope this can help you and let me know.

Nilo mailto:npaim@zaz.com.br
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Creating Sample Data for COBOL Program Using Personal Microfocus

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r1a9r$qfd$2@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<19990905052225.07582.00001528@ng-fq1.aol.com> <7qtrn2$sui$1@srv4-poa.nutecnet.com.br>`

```
RedRiver <instruct@zaz.com.br> wrote in message
news:7qtrn2$sui$1@srv4-poa.nutecnet.com.br...
>
> Robert2504 <robert2504@aol.com> escreveu nas not�cias de
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I have several editors including QEdit, Edit and some others. Maybe I
just
> > don't know how to use them. How do I tell it that that is the end of the
> first
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    When reading user created line sequential files, it is always wise to
add several extra fillers
to the end of the record definition.  Most cobols will split a long input
record into 2 or more
records if the program defined record is too short.

    Also, the record separator varys from unix to "dos" (dos/win95/98/nt,
etc).  If you create
the data file in one world, and use it in another, you will have problems.
There
are many easy ways to fix this problem.  sometimes the runtime can be caused
to accept
files with the "wrong" format with a runtime switch.
```
