<a id="t-0c0fcaea10"></a>
## Thread: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0 — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D3E77B.A739FE0E@worf.netins.net>`

```
Can anybody verify that parameter passing is only by
reference when invoking a method in Fujitsu's brand of OO
COBOL.  The general information I have (and the new
standard, if I am reading it correctly) specify that
parameters can be passed BY CONTENT or BY VALUE.  It appears
that Fujitsu has not implemented these options.

The following statement (forgive the application - it is a
demonstration for a programming language class that required
a common problem for a number of languages)

     invoke self "calculate-gcd" using by value u v 
                                          returning result

returns an error indicating that the "BY VALUE" is not
valid.  The help files indicate that the BY VALUE class is
not available when invoking a method.

Am I missing something?  Thanks for any help.
```

#### ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<37d4861f.37068334@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37D3E77B.A739FE0E@worf.netins.net>`

```

<floydj@worf.netins.net> wrote:

>Can anybody verify that parameter passing is only by
>reference when invoking a method in Fujitsu's brand of OO
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't speak to "INVOKE" but I do know that regular "CALL" statements
allow by value, and by reference.
```

```
> reference when invoking a method in Fujitsu's brand of OO
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

Floyd,

This just sucks man, not even being able to execute that statement.
Sorry, I dont have a solution for you but I will tell you to use Micro
Focus
OO Cobol, in my experience, Micro Focus handles it best, and it has'nt
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

- **From:** ENOJON <enojon@delphi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E693DC.5F86C26A@delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<37D3E77B.A739FE0E@worf.netins.net> <01bef8d7$0dbb7a40$1cf0abc3@default>`

```


Simon R Hart wrote:

> Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net> wrote in article
> <37D3E77B.A739FE0E@worf.netins.net>...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Am I missing something?  Thanks for any help.

INVOKE <object-ptr>"calculate-gcd" USING u v
                                              BY VALUE
                                              RETURNING result

always works for me in Fj OOCobol.

It's basic A-B-C's of invoking a CALL statement where
the qualifying BY REF, BY VAL, or BY CONT *follows*
the variables passed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00

```
<37E693DC.5F86C26A@delphi.com>...

> 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> the variables passed.
 
Why?, this is a strange method of programming using OO constructs.
This is unnessary using MF, "BY VALUE" is optional, but this
is placed *before* the varibles as it should.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E6F0F2.666B4387@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37D3E77B.A739FE0E@worf.netins.net> <01bef8d7$0dbb7a40$1cf0abc3@default> <37E693DC.5F86C26A@delphi.com>`

```
ENOJON wrote:
> 
> Simon R Hart wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >

I don't know how Simon does his, but programming purely in OO using
NetExpress I use only the following syntax :-

 01 textstring			pic x(30).
 01 numvalue			pic x(4) comp-5  (could be comp-3 etc..)
 01 anObject		        object reference.

 01 ls-values.
    05 newtext			pic x(12).
    05 newnumber		pic x(4) comp-5 (could be comp-3 etc..)
    05 newObject		object reference.

 invoke MyClass "mymethod" using textstring
		                 numvalue
			         anObject
			   returning ls-values.

I haven't as yet found any need to use 'by reference', 'by value' etc...
Probably same applies with Fujitsu - DON'T make sending and receiving
fields the same, ie 'using x returning x' gives you problems in
NetExpress.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s72dc$akl@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37D3E77B.A739FE0E@worf.netins.net> <01bef8d7$0dbb7a40$1cf0abc3@default> <37E693DC.5F86C26A@delphi.com> <37E6F0F2.666B4387@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
  <snip>
>
> I don't know how Simon does his, but programming purely in OO using
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

OK - it's "philosophy" time.  I think that there is definitely a reason to
use BY CONTENT (in both INVOKE and CALL statements).  If (and this is an
"if") you believe in "encapsulation," using BY CONTENT guarantees that your
invoked/called method/program canNOT "play" with your program's data.  I
think that the only time data should be passed to a subprogram or method "by
reference" (which is the default) is IF you want to have that program modify
your invoking/calling program's data.  With the CALL statement, I think that
the "mindset" of COBOL programmers is that the subprogram can (and often
will) change anything it can get its hands (i.e pointers) on.  ON THE OTHER
HAND, when it comes to NEW methods, I really do think that they should be
"designed" NOT to play with the invoking program's data - but to "return"
data for use in the outside program.

As for BY VALUE - there are two issues here.  First, there MAY (depending
upon implementation) be performance advantages for doing this.  Second (and
I think more prevalent), the method (or even called subprogram) may be a
NON-COBOL program that is expecting data "on the stack" as BY VALUE does it.
For that matter, if you are writing your own method and expecting it to be
invoked by non-COBOL programs, you may well want to program for BY VALUE
arguments coming in.

All of this does boil down to the fact that doing everything BY REFERENCE
(explicit or implicit) probably will work - but may not be the best design
decision (IMHO).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990921133154.06411.00000275@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7s72dc$akl@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <7s72dc$akl@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:

>
>All of this does boil down to the fact that doing everything BY REFERENCE
>(explicit or implicit) probably will work - but may not be the best design
>decision (IMHO).
>

I would think that using BY REFERENCE also limits the amount of physical 
data that has to be passed, making runtime more efficient.
In my particular case, I have to be able to get access to a record area that
could vary from 450 to 8000 bytes.  My sub-program is using BY REFERENCE
on the record area and BY CONTENT on a RecordSize parameter to prevent
data overrun situations in the sub-program.   Currently the plan is for this
action
to operate on 1% of a program's data.   Ultimately, I hope to have procedures 
in place for the sub-program to 'see' 100% of the records passing thru the 
Main program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL using Fujitsu 4.0

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E7E465.A0ECBA0@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7s72dc$akl@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <19990921133154.06411.00000275@ngol07.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <7s72dc$akl@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Main program.

I think I'm correct in saying the same problem doesn't prevail with
NetExpress - your table handling problem can be covered by creating
either a variable length collection or dictionary, (its size is
determined by ' invoke MyCollection "size" returning ws-size'), and it
is merely the object reference handle which is passed. If you're getting
really interested in OO - take a look at Adele Goldberg's Smalltalk-80
which neatly describes the structure of collections/dictionaries, and
how you can apply them. 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-81d5954e55"></a>
## Thread: MF Callable Rebuild Problem — 5 msgs

### MF Callable Rebuild Problem

- **From:** dlmax@netcom.com (David L Maxwell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qv10k$jhq@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Hi all,

I want to create a program with MF NetExpress V 1.0 using the
"Callable Rebuild" facility (CALLRB) to test the status of
Indexed files. If it detects a corrupt file it should then
perform a rebuild of the file(s). So far my code is just to
check for file status.  The rebuild portion will be coded once
I overcome the current problem.

My problem is that the call to "CALLRB" can only be executed
once in a run unit. The second call results in the following
runtime message:

        object Code error : file 'idxcheck'
        error code: 153, pc=A99, call=1 seg=0


The definition of error code 153 is:

        153     Subscript out of range  (Fatal)

        A subscript which you have used in your program is out
        of the defined range, that is, it is either less than
        one or it is greater than the number of occurrences of
        the item.

        Resolution:
        You should recode your program.

The Object code 'idxcheck' is linked into the program by the
following:
        cbllink -t dbcheck.cbl callrb rbldmain rbldsub idxcheck
                   cdfh fhredir

Program code follows:

      -----------------------------------------------------------------
      $SET COMP NOALTER NOQUAL ANS85 SCHEDULER TARGET"486"
       identification division.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------
      *  Program must be Compiled and linked as follows:
      *    cbllink -t dbcheck.cbl callrb rbldmain rbldsub idxcheck cdfh
      *               fhredir
      *----------------------------------------------------------------
       program-id. dbcheck.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 Parameters        pic x(600).
       01 Call-Status.
          03 CS-Stat        pic x.
          03 CS-Code        pic x comp-x.
       01 Delay             pic x.

       linkage section.
       procedure division.
      *
      * Set Input & Output Filenames with options to Rebuild
      *
           initialize Call-Status parameters
           cancel "callrb"
           move "idrsmas /f" to Parameters
           call "callrb" using
                Parameters
                Call-Status
           end-call

           if return-code = 0
              display ">>> IDRSMAS  Database is OK ... "
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           else
              display ">>> IDRSMAS  is Bad!! ..."
              display "             Return-Code .. " return-code
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           end-if

           display ">>> Hit Enter Key"
           accept delay

           initialize Call-Status parameters
           cancel "callrb"
           move  "logmas /f" to Parameters
           call "callrb" using
                Parameters
                Call-Status
           end-call

           if return-code = 0
              display ">>> LOGMAS   Database is OK ... "
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           else
              display ">>> LOGMAS   is Bad!! ..."
              display "             Return-Code .. " return-code
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           end-if

           display ">>> Hit Enter Key"
           accept delay

           initialize Call-Status parameters
           cancel "callrb"
           move "idrsparm /f" to Parameters
           call "callrb" using
                Parameters
                Call-Status
           end-call

           if return-code = 0
              display ">>> IDRSPARM Database is OK ... "
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           else
              display ">>> IDRSPARM is Bad!! ..."
              display "             Return-Code .. " return-code
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           end-if

           display ">>> Hit Enter Key"
           accept delay

           initialize Call-Status parameters
           cancel "callrb"
           move "iddrno /f" to Parameters
           call "callrb" using
                Parameters
                Call-Status
           end-call

           if return-code = 0
              display ">>> IDDRNO   Database is OK ... "
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           else
              display ">>> IDDRNO   is Bad!! ..."
              display "             Return-Code .. " return-code
              display "             Call-Status .. " CS-Stat
              display "             Call-Code   .. " CS-Code
           end-if
           stop run
           .

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
The First call works normally but the next call causes the program
to terminate with the RTS message about 'idxcheck'.

Anybody run across this situation?  And if so how did you solve
it. Any suggestions or ideas would be greatly appreciated.

Dave
```

## Thread: Help Accessing Btrieve files in MicroFocus COBOL — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help Accessing Btrieve files in MicroFocus COBOL

- **From:** kapoor <in_rkapoor@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r0u7e$vjl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Can anybody guide me on how to access btrieve data files (under Novell
Netware environment) using MicroFocus COBOL programs. I use MFCOBOL
version 3.2 under DOS
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Accessing Btrieve files in MicroFocus COBOL

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d486ca.37239399@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7r0u7e$vjl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Mon, 06 Sep 1999 17:38:56 GMT, kapoor <in_rkapoor@my-deja.com>
wrote:

>Can anybody guide me on how to access btrieve data files (under Novell
>Netware environment) using MicroFocus COBOL programs. I use MFCOBOL
>version 3.2 under DOS

Give the index structure of the BTRIEVE files, and the BTRIEVE
runtime, you can do it.  There is a chapter in the TOOLS (I think)
manual about using the btrieve file handler instead of the External
file handler.  Years ago I wrote a system to import Great Plains
accounting data into a Purchase order system for payment balancing and
I used this feature.   I wrote a simple program to read the btrieve
file and write my own indexed file.  I used the btrieve runtime as
loaded by the Great Plains software.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Accessing Btrieve files in MicroFocus COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i9B3.16507$KM3.137105@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7r0u7e$vjl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
kapoor wrote:
>Can anybody guide me on how to access btrieve data files (under Novell
>Netware environment) using MicroFocus COBOL programs. I use MFCOBOL
>version 3.2 under DOS

I have used Btrieve with MF COBOL for years in exactly that environment.
There are two methods to do what you want.  One is to define the files
as Indexed files and specify to the compiler to use Btrieve as the Indexed
file handler, which MF COBOL 3.2 can do.  The other, more complex but more
flexible, method is to define the necessary file structures in Working
Storage and call Btrieve directly.

The Indexed method is much quicker and easier, but the direct method lets
you use all the Btrieve capabilities, including those not supported by
the COBOL Indexed file model.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help Accessing Btrieve files in MicroFocus COBOL

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7r0u7e$vjl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8i9B3.16507$KM3.137105@news1.mia>`

```

Indexed

more

lets

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PygB3.3827$Fp.61972@news2.mia>`

```
kapoor wrote:
>thanks a lot for your response to my call for help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Parsing COBOL

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TB8B3.107$I3.6172@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<dM%A3.7701$r5.537773@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
What are you looking to parse "for?" A verb, procedure  or dataname cross
reference, a "find" for likely "date" fields, record layouts for FDs or
working storage?

Much of the above are already available at little or no cost.

There are probably some generic "text parsers" around, too; no reason you
could not use one of those if you want to do something a little different.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on Visual Object Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d48676.37155354@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37D3FB9F.3D18BA6@mail.teleweb.pt>`

```
On Mon, 06 Sep 1999 18:36:32 +0100, JoseCapucho
<jcapucho@mail.teleweb.pt> wrote:

>Hello,
>I have a program in DOS enviroment compiled with Visual Object Cobol
>V1.0 on WIN98. However I can't make reports with my printer HP720.
>Someone, can help me ?
>

The 7XX series of HP printers are virtually All "Win Printers"
although some models come with driver software to allow printing from
DOS.  Look for a disk with the printer that says "DOS DRIVERS" or "DOS
PRINTING SERVICES".  You may be out of luck.
```

<a id="t-c6ebcebaf4"></a>
## Thread: Indexed file searching — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Indexed file searching

- **From:** Cameron McAllister <s369539@student.uq.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.4.10.9909061211340.21170-100000@student.uq.edu.au>`

```
Does anyone know of a good example for searching for an entry in an
indexed file when you only have part of the key?

key is a string, but a partial string should match, but I cannot get it to
this.

ta

cameron mcallister
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed file searching

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D339EF.A79715F3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.4.10.9909061211340.21170-100000@student.uq.edu.au>`

```
Cameron McAllister wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know of a good example for searching for an entry in an
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> cameron mcallister

Assuming :  01 Myrecord.
	       05 MyPrimeKey	pic x(06).
	       05 .......

and you only know the PrimeKey starts "JAC   "

	set FileNotFinished to true
	move "JAC" to MyPrimeKey
	Start Myfile key is > MyPrimeKey
 	    invalid key set FileFinished to true
	End-start

	if  FileNotFinished
	    read MyFile next record
		at end set FileFinished to true
	    end-read
	End-if

	if  FileNotFinished and
	    MyPrimeKey(1:3) = "JAC"
            display "Strewth Mate - I've found it !"
	End-if

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed file searching

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d3bb23.42615957@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.4.10.9909061211340.21170-100000@student.uq.edu.au>`

```
On Mon, 6 Sep 1999 12:12:56 +1000, Cameron McAllister
<s369539@student.uq.edu.au> wrote:

>Does anyone know of a good example for searching for an entry in an
>indexed file when you only have part of the key?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well that all depends.   Is it the first part of the key field you
want to match or any old part?  If under CICS there is a feature
called "Generic Keys" that you can use for this that I discussed in my
portion of the book COBOL Unleashed.  What is your platform and what
portion of the key are you trying to match?
```

## Thread: MF-Cobol throws strange error — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF-Cobol throws strange error

- **From:** dnalor@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7qvmd6$444$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi *.*!
I ve got a pretty nice COBOL-Problem. (NT4/SP3 + MF-Cob. 4.0.32)
I call a DLL from MF-COBOL and exactly AFTER the statement
CALL "DLL".
it produces a messagebox that says:
---------------
Titlebar: Error!
Message: Call-Error Errorcode:2
---------------
And after clicking it away, it continues.
It seems to be no problem at all to call any function from that DLL.
So whats going on there?
Ah! It IS REALLY COBOL that throws the message, because if I use the DLL
in a C prog, everything works fine.
If u have any ideas, that would be great!

I dont want to tell a user:
"Hey user, u get an error, dont worry, thats normal! Just accept it and
forget it, u use windows so u are used to it anyway!" :-))

Have a nice day!
Roland.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-53e48ae895"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal — 35 msgs

### COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990908100344.17169.00005937@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
In yesterday's (7 September) Wall Street Journal, page B4, under a headline
"Y2K Tests, Upgrades Causee Their Own Chaos", Kevin Delaney writes:

"The fixes involve tinkering with millions of lines of software code, much of
it in programming languages such as Cobol that have been all but forgotten."

Gee, my memory must be better than I thought, 'cause I remember COBOL. Mr.
Delaney is probably too young to remember COBOL.

Not sure why I'm posting this, but I found the article somewhat disquieting.
Any reactions?

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

- **From:** "Dave Shufon" <dshufon1@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QUVE3.12083$r6.369959@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<19990908100344.17169.00005937@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
I can't tell you how many times I've heard or read about the death of COBOL.
Some people just can't seem to understand why after some new languages are
developed, we don't have everything converted over to them within a matter
of months.  They just don't appreciate the millions - if not billions - of
lines of code that are out there already (I'm starting to sound like Carl
Sagen).  Whole systems are coded in those older languages.  There's some
ignorance about what's involved here - especially among non-programmers.  I
mean most people like this reporter probably think converting a system from
COBOL to C++ is no different than running the Windows98 upgrade on a
Windows95 PC.

There are people out there who are trying to set people straight about this.
I've had some programming teachers who said that the death of COBOL is
highly exaggerated.  Though they had some of their own misconceptions about
other things that were dead, like Assembly language.  One teacher also
talked about key punch machines as dinosaurs that went out with the '70s.
This at a time when the office I worked in at a state agency had a keypunch
machine that was used everyday!  And this was only about 6 years ago (though
I think that keypunch machine has been retired since then).  When I got a
job as a programmer - at the same state agency - I found that most of the
existing code was still in BAL.  We're still converting over to COBOL!  Of
course we have to train most of our Assembler programmers in house, because
nobody teaches it anymore.

S Comstock <scomstock@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990908100344.17169.00005937@ng-fe1.aol.com...
> In yesterday's (7 September) Wall Street Journal, page B4, under a
headline
> "Y2K Tests, Upgrades Causee Their Own Chaos", Kevin Delaney writes:
>
> "The fixes involve tinkering with millions of lines of software code, much
of
> it in programming languages such as Cobol that have been all but
forgotten."
>
> Gee, my memory must be better than I thought, 'cause I remember COBOL. Mr.
> Delaney is probably too young to remember COBOL.
>
> Not sure why I'm posting this, but I found the article somewhat
disquieting.
> Any reactions?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

- **From:** "Jude Robert" <juderobert@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eD5F3.1440$Bw3.20365@news.rdc1.tx.home.com>`
- **References:** `<19990908100344.17169.00005937@ng-fe1.aol.com> <QUVE3.12083$r6.369959@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
It's more than just 'non-oriented business' programmers, it's marketing.
VB and C++ have been marketed as consumer products. You pick up any PC
magazine and
you will see advertisements for these products but not for the COBOL
products.

It's like I see lots of advertisements for pick up trucks and none for
freight trains. They can both used
for the same job, to transport things from place to place, but no one
believes that pick up trucks are
going to replace freight trains. It's just that freight trains are not
marketed as a consumer product.

Ok, so you what I'm getting at?


Dave Shufon <dshufon1@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cosmic Knight" <cosmicknight>>removethis<<@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dhCG3.7935$sK1.14903@news.rdc1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<19990908100344.17169.00005937@ng-fe1.aol.com> <QUVE3.12083$r6.369959@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <eD5F3.1440$Bw3.20365@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <37E6F9DF.27974F65@home.com>`

```
I've heard so much on this topic lately, this post really strikes a nerve.

I hate to rant, but the idea that COBOL is dead, and that COBOL jobs will
disappear soon is *totally ridiculous*.  I graduated school fairly recently,
and there are many people my age working in shops that use COBOL.  DePaul
University has a program (CCP) that allows people to transition from one
career to programming, and what do they teach? COBOL! On an MVS mainframe no
less!

These students are getting snapped up as fast as DePaul can crank them out.
Some would say it's because of Y2K, but that's just as absurd as the notion
that COBOL is dead.

The fact is, new COBOL applications are being written everywhere, and more
will be written.  Look at all of the mega-mergers happening in corporate
America lately.  Think the mainframe is dead?  Think again.  MVS sure is
optimized nicely for I-O, isn't it?

Another thing a lot of people just don't get:  Companies will almost *never*
get rid of stuff that *works*.  Think your boss is going to shell out the
bucks to replace all of those "old" COBOL applications that work great, just
so they can be migrated to a "newer" language like C++ or Java?

No chance.   Even if your boss would like to, someone up the ladder will
almost certainly shoot it down.  Need instantiates change, and if there is
no need, you don't go messing around with entire systems.

Fact is, COBOL, although cumbersome in some ways, is an ideal tool for a lot
of businesses.  This is an important point I think, languages are tools, a
means to accomplish a task.  Maybe COBOL isn't as "flexible" as C, (or so
the C purists would say) but it sure is handy for a lot of COmmon Business
Oriented stuff, isn't it?

I really wish these bozos would shut their pie holes forever.


John Wynton wrote in message <37E6F9DF.27974F65@home.com>...
>Perhaps the reporter should ask his own employer (Dow Jones) just how
>many lines of COBOL code run their own systems. They have two mainframe
>data centers the last time I heard.
>
>Cheers,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Evaluators sought for COBOL development tool

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** bsisa@us.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sgbue$ctt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<dhCG3.7935$sK1.14903@news.rdc1.il.home.com> <19990924002421.20399.00001226@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
In order to help us design user-friendly products with functions and
features customers want, designers and developers at IBM follow
User-Centered Design (UCD), an approach involving input from users.
Specifically, we are looking for COBOL developers who have experience
with MVS or S/390, and are familiar with Windows based Integrated
Development Environments (IDE�s), e.g., Micro Focus, ACUCOBOL, Visual
Basic, Visual C++, VA Java.  If interested in participating in a
usability test of a COBOL development tool, please send email to
STLUCD@US.IBM.COM.  Once your email is received, you will be sent a
questionnaire to see if you meet the criteria we are looking for in a
participant for this project.

The usability test will take place in October in San Jose, California.
For those who are selected, the honorarium will be $150 for three hours,
and we will work around your availability, including evenings.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XvKG3.4527$2Q5.37283@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990908100344.17169.00005937@ng-fe1.aol.com> <QUVE3.12083$r6.369959@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com> <eD5F3.1440$Bw3.20365@news.rdc1.tx.home.com> <37E6F9DF.27974F65@home.com> <dhCG3.7935$sK1.14903@news.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Cosmic Knight wrote
>
>Another thing a lot of people just don't get:  Companies will almost
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>like C++ or Java?

I certainly agree with almost all sentiments expressed in your post.
And up until recently, I would have agreed with the above paragraph,
too.  But I know of two mainframe shops who are doing almost exactly
what you describe.  In both cases, the decisions are being made by
upper management (not MIS), because of 'perceived benefits' having
nothing to do with the actual efficacy of COBOL.  Both decisions are
'political', and have more to do with personalities, so called
'conventional wisdom', and ignorance, than anything else.  In both
cases, many millions of dollars are being literally thrown in the
trash.  When the dust clears, I guarantee you that they will be much
worse off than before.  This is already becoming evident at one of
the shops, though the new software is not live yet.
```

```
That's probably because they read the WSJ article (or any of the many
similar articles financed directly/indirectly by companies providing or
supporting "new" technology) and, like the WSJ article writer, are
totally ignorant with regard to the technical merits, or lack thereof,
of either COBOL or the technology they switched to (costs a lot ergo it
must be good mindset).  I still say the real "Y2K problem" will result
from all of the new, untested, inefficient, slow, technology and
systems that will be crashing, providing incorrect data, and generally
making a mess of the economy in the next few years. <G>

BTW .. Anyone have the (last estimate) $85 billion to engage in the
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990925141245.28422.00002336@ng-xb1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<XvKG3.4527$2Q5.37283@news3.mia>`

```
>But I know of two mainframe shops who are doing almost exactly
>what you describe.  In both cases, the decisions are being made by
>upper management (not MIS), because of 'perceived benefits' having
>nothing to do with the actual efficacy of COBOL

I believe that story.  In my previous work, before studying programming, I was
a music copyist.  I prepared orchestral parts for Broadway shows in a studio
which does this by hand.  Even though the traditional technology is fully
adequate for the task, Broadway producers (as many lay people) have the
perception that computer technology would be cheaper and/or faster.  In this
case it isn't, as the Theater League in NY is finding out.

The bills are paid by people who don't, themselves, understand the product they
are buying.  Therefore, they go with their perception, reality-based and
accurate or not.  When reality hits 'em in the face, they keep their jobs by
"scape-goating".  I'm not at all surprised that a similar dynamic is also
evident in data processing.

RoadRaat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and The Wall Street Journal

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00

```
Dave Shufon writes ...

[snip]

>We're still converting over to COBOL!  Of

own instructors. Check out our website: http://www.trainersfriend.com
```

<a id="t-b668e8e1c1"></a>
## Thread: Uses of PC-COBOL — 13 msgs

### Uses of PC-COBOL

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7d5172c.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
After reading the many discussions here of how to do things in PC based COBOLs I figure this old mainframer should get herself a compiler and learn a different flavor.  

I mentioned my desire to my husband (also a mainframe COBOL programmer) and he looked at me like I had turned several shades of some putrid green.  He asked why in the world I would want to do so as he didn't thing there was really a use for it other than development to then go to the mainframe.

I really didn't have an answer for him as he hasn't been reading the group and hasn't seen the backgrounds etc so I'm putting out a request for uses of PC based COBOLs.  I'm not looking at a particular flavor - just the uses there of like accounting apps, or payroll apps etc.  If possible can you cite specific companies that use them (if not that's ok too - I'm not trolling for another job).

Thanks
Eileen


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Uses of PC-COBOL

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d56b6f.49745079@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<s7d5172c.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
On Tue, 07 Sep 1999 13:45:49 -0400, "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:

>After reading the many discussions here of how to do things in PC based COBOLs I figure this old mainframer should get herself a compiler and learn a different flavor.  
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Eileen
Eileen,

I have heavily invested in PC based COBOL/PLI/C/CICS/DB2 development, because I cannot
afford to carry a mainframe around on my travels.  I am an instructor fo rmainframe
topics, (mainly sytem programming stuff, but quite a lot of application programming as
well), and I prepare all the samples, exercises, demonstrations and such on my laptop.  So
far I still have to find a feature that is present on the mainframe, but not on the PC

(ok - There ain't no ADABAS, DATACOM, IDEAL, et. al. on the PC, but these are things I can
do without) 
As long as it stays in the applcation development part, the PC is great (but then, the
compilers I use are not exceptionally cheap:  Visual Age for COBOL enterprise edition,
Visual Age for PL/I enterprise edition, Visual Age for JAVA, enterprise edtion each cost
about $3,000 here, Visual Age C++ is somewhat cheaper.  I don't know what DB/2 for NT
costs, as I got it as part of the Solution Developer Program, but I would guess it isn't
cheap either.  CICS for NT (and OS/2) is included with the COBOL and the PLI compiler

When it comes to system programming stuff, I have to try to get access to an IBM mainframe
(I wouldn't be able to afford one of those....)
```

#### ↳ Re: Uses of PC-COBOL

- **From:** "George E. Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7B8BDB39D26D27CD.E1FEB5334565BD47.37A71097347CC495@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<s7d5172c.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
I've been programming in COBOL on the PC for about 15 years now.  I've
programmed for a variety of companies/clients.  For proprietary reasons I
won't match the projects with the companies but the companies have included:
        The US Government (USAF)
        JC Penney
        National Group Life
        CSC Logic
        Excel Telecommunications
        United Insurance Companies
        Blockbuster Video
        Chancellor Media
        DeVry Institute

The projects I have worked on are Agent Commission Programs, Group Health
Care programs, Front-end Policy submission programs, Payroll, Benefits,
Customer File Maintenance, National Flood Insurance Program, Real Property
programs, Work Information Programs, warehouse programs, Material
acquisition programs.

So as you can see, there are a wide variety of PC flavored programs out
there.  Don't look for much of a difference in the COBOL - COBOL is COBOL.
The major difference is in the environments.  And there are at least as many
in the PC arena as there are in the Mainframe world.

We've got DOS, Windows, Windows 95/98, Windows NT, Novell, UNIX, XENIX,
OS/2, etc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Uses of PC-COBOL

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r3k8g$qcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s7d5172c.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
I've used PC Cobol since about 1990...I develop specialized applications for
customers like CNN, Headline News and CNNSI...Election Coverage software,
Sportsticker software...Most of this software has to interface directly with
the special computers used to air television pictures and text (character
generator computers or graphics equipment) so it has to be relatively
fast...Seems to work pretty good...IO with the external devices is much
faster than could be achieved with a mainframe and a CICS application per
say...

I know there are some accounting packages out there that use cobol...Look at
all that mainframe code that can be converted to a PC
application...Client/server apps and GUI interfaces on the PC is the name of
the game today...
```

#### ↳ Re: Uses of PC-COBOL

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d5e1f3.126125654@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<s7d5172c.045@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
On Tue, 07 Sep 1999 13:45:49 -0400, "Eileen Preston"
<epreston@lear.com> wrote:

>After reading the many discussions here of how to do things in PC based COBOLs I figure this old mainframer should get herself a compiler and learn a different flavor.  
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I have a very successful system for tracking Archery shoots - all in
PC COBOL.

I have a pedigree system.

I have a bowling center management system.

The company I used to work for has an entire retail store unit control
system, from order entry to POS, including layaway and in house charge
accounts.  All in COBOL.

Any business activity can be serviced by PC based COBOL provided the
transaction volume is not so large as to require the services of a
mainframe.

On this group you will find MANY of a rare breed of COBOL programmer.
Those who know "both" worlds.  Daily I program on an OS/390 mainframe,
an OpenVMS system and on the PC.  All in COBOL.  There are not a lot
of us that have experience in all these platforms - I've met more here
in this group than anywhere else.  I think you will find some
interesting answers.

Oh, and I have a simple invoicing system that I wrote for my wife, and
her small business -- Written in COBOL to run under Windows.
```

## Thread: FORTRAN ==> COBOL Question — 6 msgs

### FORTRAN ==> COBOL Question

- **From:** aleven@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r3rni$2mr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Can anyone tell me what the COBOL equivalent is of the FORTRAN function DEXP?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN ==> COBOL Question

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d57ed1.54705782@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7r3rni$2mr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 07 Sep 1999 20:14:56 GMT, aleven@my-deja.com wrote:

>Can anyone tell me what the COBOL equivalent is of the FORTRAN function DEXP?
>
>
>Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't
How about

001400 Identification Division.
001500  Program-Id. DEXP.
001600 Data Division.
001700  Working-Storage Section.
001800   01 Result USAGE COMP-2.
001900   01 Exponent USAGE COMP-2.
002000 Procedure Division.
002100     PERFORM VARYING EXPONENT FROM 0 BY 0.01 UNTIL
002200           EXPONENT > 20
002300      COMPUTE RESULT = 2.7182818 ** Exponent
002400      DISPLAY 'DEXP(' exponent ') =' RESULT
002500     END-PERFORM
002600     .

You should add enough digits to the E constants as you require.  I do not remember more
than those written here


       |\      _,,,---,,_
 ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,    Some people know
      |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'  the price of everything,
     '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)       but the value of nothing

With kind Regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN ==> COBOL Question

- **From:** aleven@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r6lh3$56m$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7r3rni$2mr$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d57ed1.54705782@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Thanks. I didn't need the PERFORM. My results with the COBOL module
match the results with the old FORTRAN subroutine.  Thanks again!

Alan
```

##### ↳ ↳ e, This game show is hosted by Alex Trebek, Ursoid gestation

- **From:** Barticus@usa.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ea5999.7123483@netnews.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7r3rni$2mr$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d57ed1.54705782@news2.ibm.net>`

```
On Tue, 07 Sep 1999 21:11:31 GMT, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
wrote:

>002300      COMPUTE RESULT = 2.7182818 ** Exponent

>You should add enough digits to the E constants as you require.  I do not remember more
>than those written here

And I do not remember more than are written here:
2.7182818284590452353602874713526624977

Old Business:

A:  This game show is hosted by Alex Trebek
Q:  What is JEOPARDY!?

In the Alex Trebek era the name is all caps, though it was not so in
Art Fleming's time, and some modern abberations occur, but you will
never,  ever see Sony (or  Griffin before them) spell JEOPARDY!
without the exclamation point.  Reference
http://www.station.sony.com/jeopardy/ compare to 
http://www.station.sony.com/wheel/ .

Umm, except there's an error on that page where they left off the
exclamation mark.  

Ursoid Gestation

When I previously cited the remarkable short gestation period of
bears, I noted that the information was off the top of my head and
might be somewhat erroneous.  James then cited gestation periods for
several bear species which were substantially longer.  It tuns out
that the bear I was remembering was a bearoid:  The Giant Panda.

Panda gestation is about eight weeks.  That is if you measure
gestation from the time the embryo implants in the uterus until birth.
As it says at  http://www.nature-net.com/bears/panda.html 

>Through a remarkable process referred to as delayed implantation,
>the fertilized ovum divides a few times and then floats free within the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>deliver one or two cubs. 

Anyway,  my original point was that generally  larger mammals have
longer gestation periods, but there are exceptions such as the Giant
Panda.
RB |\  Randall Bart
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN ==> COBOL Question

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r3i7s$jqb$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7r3rni$2mr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
77 DEXP COMP-2.
77 E COMP-2.
77 A COMP-2.

COMPUTE DEXP = E ** A.

This is dependent upon COMP-2 being implemented as
equivalent to DOUBLE PRECISION. This is typical of
many, perhaps most, COBOL implementations.
------------------
Rick Smith

aleven@my-deja.com wrote in message <7r3rni$2mr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Can anyone tell me what the COBOL equivalent is of the FORTRAN function
DEXP?
>
>
>Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

## Thread: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand. — 27 msgs

### The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** Olivier Miakinen <Olivier.Miakinen@bull.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net>`

```
Hello,

I've heard many times that some programs could have a 9/9/99 problem
because of certain error-or-eof codes with value 9999.

I can't understand how this could arise. My opinion is that the 9/9/99
could be represented by 090999, maybe 90999, but not by 9999. Could someone
give me light on this subject ?

Olivier.

P.S.: My native language is french, so please excuse my bad english.
```

```
>give me light on this subject ?
>


Welcome to the wonderful world of "Y2K Remediation Services Marketing"

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** "Mark Undrill" <marku@affirm.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d68dcf.0@news2.cluster1.telinco.net>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net>`

```

news:37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net...
> Hello,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> could be represented by 090999, maybe 90999, but not by 9999. Could
someone
> give me light on this subject ?
>
> Olivier.
>
A Colleague tells me that in some systems the month is encoded as 1 to C for
January to December. In this case a date of 9/9/99 could be represented as
9999 and if that's used as an end-of-time marker...

Mark Undrill
```

#### ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** Olivier Miakinen <Olivier.Miakinen@bull.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D68B62.E730A06C@bull.net>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net>`

```
Thank you for all your answers. I understand now several possible
failures (all due to a very poor programmation) :
- Intentionally set 9/9/99 by a guy who don't bother that this date
  will happen.
- 9999 is the number of days since a reference date (but then the bug
  won't occur at 9/9/99, but at an obscure date).
- Month is encoded in hexa, which saves one digit (9/9/99 is 9999 and
  31/12/99 is 31C99)

Wonderful !
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <hillock@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<bQEB3.172$vh6.11797@cac1.rdr.news.psi.ca>`

```
Olivier Miakinen <Olivier.Miakinen@bull.net> wrote in message
news:37D68B62.E730A06C@bull.net...
> Thank you for all your answers. I understand now several possible
> failures (all due to a very poor programmation) :
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

De Rein

your english is better than my french.

Any how,  I remember back in the good olde days of college (70's) of using
999999 (please note the lack of zeroes) for one language to stop the run of
a program.  It was a training language and it didn't have much going for it
(less than Basic).   If there are some programs that are using a date of all
9's, they wouldn't have a problem, however, if they had/are using the
month/day of 0909 then I hate to be the programmer who used that type of
coding.

Using a field with a high value (if it's in the input) is bad coding if the
language has end of file handling.  I've used the high values (999999) in
programs only for control breaks, which were set AT an end of file
condition.  I was using 3 files as input, comparing on differences.  Each
file would contain a different number of transactions/records.  I think that
I'm getting off topic.

09/09/99, 99/09/09 problems are cousins to Y2K.  Gee, after 17 years hearing
about Y2K I'm starting to get tired.

Good morning, good afternoon, good evening and good night (where ever you
are).

Tim


>
> --
> Salut ! Je suis un virus de signature :) Mettez-moi dans la v�tre.
```

#### ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Wed, 08 Sep 1999 13:45:04 +0200, Olivier Miakinen
<Olivier.Miakinen@bull.net> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Salut ! Je suis un virus de signature :) Mettez-moi dans la v�tre.


It is a myth that has grown to urban legend proportion.  I have HEARD
if 09/09/99 being used to indicate a "date not available" value, but
never EOF.  EOF would be 999999.  The whole thing is utterly silly.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D72C89.4DA1461B@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37d6f002.195271234@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
(snip)
> It is a myth that has grown to urban legend proportion.  I have HEARD
> if 09/09/99 being used to indicate a "date not available" value, but
> never EOF.  EOF would be 999999.  The whole thing is utterly silly.

Early FORTRAN (pre version IV) did use all 9's in a record (a punched
card in everything I ever saw) to indicate. IIRC, version IV supported
the "END=" parameter on the READ command. NB, this is not necessarily in
a date field, plus the system had some pretty crummy date validation
routines if '999999' (or '99999999' to be Y2K compliant <g>) was a valid
date.

I've worked for one of the big banks since 1990 on a securities movement
& control system, and we used to use 990909 to indicate an open
settlement date. That has since been updated to use 770707 instead. Keep
in mind that our world of business dates is 5 years, current +/- 2
years.

I fully expect I'd have had a call or more by now if we were having
batch problems with 990909 - we'd be settling all those open trades and
everything would be out of whack.

Very interested in hearing of any other systems' problems/adventures
regarding 990909.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<avnXN0aDBTUThZ9fS7WH1dPD1HmJ@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37d6f002.195271234@news1.ibm.net> <37D72C89.4DA1461B@att.net>`

```
On Wed, 08 Sep 1999 23:42:01 -0400 William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>I've worked for one of the big banks since 1990 on a securities movement
:>& control system, and we used to use 990909 to indicate an open
:>settlement date. That has since been updated to use 770707 instead. Keep
:>in mind that our world of business dates is 5 years, current +/- 2
:>years.

I can accept that the original developers made a mistake by using 990909, a
valid date, as the indicator as they did not expect the same code to be used
in 20 years.

Why on earth was it changed to another valid (although quite future/past)
date? If an external flag could not be used and the indication has to be
carried in the date, why wasn't something obviously invalid used, such as
999999 or 000000?

   [ snipped ] 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D881CA.9C87B11E@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37d6f002.195271234@news1.ibm.net> <37D72C89.4DA1461B@att.net> <avnXN0aDBTUThZ9fS7WH1dPD1HmJ@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
(snippage)
> 
> I can accept that the original developers made a mistake by using 990909, a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 999999 or 000000?

We're not prepared to accommodate an invalid date in the settlement date
field (a required field). I would certainly not want to use zeros, which
suggests an initialized but unpopulated numeric field. This application
is based on a package which began life in the early 80's. 

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** corky <corky@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D833D1.6406BB38@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37d6f002.195271234@news1.ibm.net> <37D72C89.4DA1461B@att.net> <avnXN0aDBTUThZ9fS7WH1dPD1HmJ@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 08 Sep 1999 23:42:01 -0400 William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel

This is all some Y2K consultant's way of making a buck. If i remember
correctly
back in the IBM 1401 days autocoder (that's a language) could not detect
EOF so 
it was common practice to place a 9's record at the EOF. When the same
programers
converted these programs to cobol some of them would still move 9's to
the last record but
they used a "move all ". There might be a program or 2 out there where
some one used 090999
but I don't think there are many why use 090999? 123199 is the more
common problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D882A6.8C03DDB@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37d6f002.195271234@news1.ibm.net> <37D72C89.4DA1461B@att.net> <avnXN0aDBTUThZ9fS7WH1dPD1HmJ@4ax.com> <37D833D1.6406BB38@compuserve.com>`

```
corky wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> This is all some Y2K consultant's way of making a buck. If i remember
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> programers

As I said in an earlier post, I've seen all 9's used as EOF in FORTRAN
(pre version IV) programs, but never in Autocoder or COBOL. From '65 on,
Autocoder didn't have a problem recognizing EOF. I actually did a fair
amount of Autocoder in the '65 - '67 time frame, even installed a 12K
1440 in a hospital (yes, 12K, which was a rather large machine, too).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <hillock@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3MWB3.69$at6.934@cac1.rdr.news.psi.ca>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37d6f002.195271234@news1.ibm.net> <37D72C89.4DA1461B@att.net>`

```
A fellow worker came to a meeting today at 9:00 am.  He said that this is
his last Sprite that he will drink.  I asked him why and his reply was the
090999 end of the world problem.  I countered that if the world was to end
at 09/09/99-09:09:09, then about 2/3rd of the world had already
isappered( the parts of the world where this time had come and gone).  That
atlantic Canada is gone (funny didn't hear anything on the news).  After the
time had come and went, he sighed.  I then told him that there's still the
Pm time to come...........

The world didn't end in this part.  I don't know or heard anything about if
there were some problems at work.  By next week we should know if there were
any problems reported by the news.  A sort of minne Y2K.

   Now the Y2K problem is more troubling.  Where I live, by the time that
Midnight strikes Canada on Dec 31, we'll know what's been going on in the
rest of the World.  About 15 hours from the first time zone that reaches Jan
1, 2000.  The first major countries that will reach midnight will be New
Zealand, Austrailia, and Japan(developed).  These are the countries that
would be better prepared for problems.

Gee....I'm  blabbing away again with the Y2K bug.  Just can't seem to help
it since I first ran into it in 1982.

sorry.

Tim
```

#### ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D9tB3.10061$r5.732017@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net>`

```

Olivier Miakinen wrote in message <37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net>...

>I've heard many times that some programs could have a 9/9/99 problem
>because of certain error-or-eof codes with value 9999.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>give me light on this subject ?


In some (poorly written) systems, 9/9/99 is the "date that will never
happen."  For example, a salesman is taking an order but doesn't have all
the necessary information or has something he needs to verify first.  He
gives a shipping date of 9/9/99 with the idea of changing it to something
more reasonable when everything is ready.

Another example might be someone is supposed to enter a range of dates but
only wants everything after a certain date so 9/9/99 is entered (perhaps by
default in the program) as the upper limit for the date range.  Needless to
say, if anyone has something like that in their system now they will soon
have a problem if they don't have one now.
```

#### ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

```
Bon Jour,

In my (not so) humble opinion this is snake oil salesmen trying for a
beat up.  I had someone trying to tell me a PC would crash on the
9/9/99 The problem is that it is internally represented as an integer
value. All 9's would occur at some really obscure date.

Did anyone have the slightest problem with this date?  My PC
successfully clocked without complaint.

Ken
Those high school years of french taught me something :-}
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00

```
We had a problem with an invoicing system. The all 9's date was used by
an online system to represent the highest possible date and so the code
had to be amended (now looks like spaghetti) to cater for the problem.
This was all done as part of the y2k project but as someone got the code
for that wrong, we still had problems (despite best intents).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The 9/9/99 problem ? I can't understand.

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r8oci$204$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<37D64C40.F66BC02@bull.net> <37D66D8F.195999A5@zip.com.au> <0mvB3.19025$KM3.167447@news1.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:0mvB3.19025$KM3.167447@news1.mia...
> Ken Foskey wrote:
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

    I agree - I can't imagine a hardware problem.

    Most software that uses this sort of stop date would use 99/99/99,
and the software would check for this before the check for valid
date failed.  Since 99/99/99 is not a valid date, this would not cause
a real problem.

    Any fool could see the 9/9/99 was a real date, and would have avoided
using it formally.  The programmer can not be responsible for user
created "extensions" to the program, re salesman using 9/9/99 as date
that never happens.

    This reminds me of the clever fellow that requested the custom tag
"NO TAG".  After he got it he started getting tickets from all over the
state.  It seems that "NO TAG" was used by officers/clerks in the tag #
field  when entering a ticket into the system for someone with no tag
on a vehicle.  The state had to change procedure to stop this idiot from
getting hundreds of tickets every day.
```

<a id="t-a32076c114"></a>
## Thread: Numeric picture clause — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Numeric picture clause

- **From:** "Bart Lupker" <lupker@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>`

```
Hi,

Does anybody out there knows whether the First level in this example is :
a. Alphanumeric or
b. Numeric

Example:
01    First level.
   03  Second Level   PIC 9(08).
   03  Second Level   PIC 9(08).
   03  Second Level   PIC 9(08).

I thought it is numeric, because the other clauses are also numeric, but I
also heard that the First Level is always alphanumeric. Now, can anybody
help me out with this?

Let me know if you have an answer.

Thanks,

Bart.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<QiiB3.644$2H4.31010@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>`

```

news:7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl...
> Hi,
>
> Does anybody out there knows whether the First level in this example
is :
> a. Alphanumeric or
> b. Numeric
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Think about it. If it was numeric, how big would the number be?
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>`

```
In article <7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>,
  "Bart Lupker" <lupker@hotmail.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>
> Does anybody out there knows whether the First level in this example
is :
> a. Alphanumeric or
> b. Numeric
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought it is numeric, because the other clauses are also numeric,
but I
> also heard that the First Level is always alphanumeric. Now, can
anybody
> help me out with this?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A group level is always alphanumeric. I'd suggest that you do some
research on your own before asking homework questions. This COBOL rule
regarding group levels is one of the basics that you should know
without having to ask.

Most folks here on this group don't appreciate questions that obviously
have not been researched by the poster.

God helps those who help themselves (No religious overtones, just a
fact of life).

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

```
>research on your own before asking homework questions. This COBOL rule

This is nitpickng, but a group level item is not "Technically" an
alphanumeric data item, although is most cases it is treated as such.
It is a separate animal all to it's own.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r5uoi$jo7@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net> <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting wrote in message <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net>,
>  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There are a few differences.  For example - alphanumeric-edited

01  DATE-GROUP.
       05  DATE-ITEM      PIC X(6).
01  DATE-EDITED        PIC XX/XX/XX.

If DATE-ITEM contains 050199
    then MOVE DATE-ITEM TO DATE-EDITED results in 05/01/99
    but MOVE DATE-GROUP TO DATE-EDITED results in 050199bb  (b represents a
space).

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<24vB3.18993$KM3.167170@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net> <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting wrote:
>
>A group item is always treated as alphanumeric. Why is this
>not "Technically" correct ? AFAIK, it always has been defined and used
>this way. Please present an example.

           03  WS-ALPHA       PIC  X(06) VALUE "ABCDEF".
           03  WS-GROUP.
               05  FILLER     PIC  X(06) VALUE "ABCDEF".
           03  WS-EDIT        PIC  XX/XX/XX.

           MOVE WS-ALPHA TO WS-EDIT.
           DISPLAY WS-EDIT.

Displays: "AB/CD/EF"

           MOVE WS-GROUP TO WS-EDIT.
           DISPLAY WS-EDIT.

Displays: "ABCDEF  "
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r63kn$hla@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net> <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com> wrote in message >
> A group item is always treated as alphanumeric. Why is this
> not "Technically" correct ? AFAIK, it always has been defined and used
> this way. Please present an example.
>
>

The "strange" example that I first learned about in this newsgroup is the
following:

01 aGroup.
     05   Elem1  Pic X Value "X".
     05   Elem2  Pic X Value "Y".
01 NotaGroup2 redefines aGroup Pic XX.

01 Alpha-Ed     Pic   XBX.

  Compare the "results" of the following 3 MOVE statements:

 Move aGroup to Alpha-Ed
 Move NotaGroup to Alpha-Ed
 Move a Group (1:) to Alpha-Ed
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37dc7660@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net> <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7r63kn$hla@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7r63kn$hla@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>...
>
>The "strange" example that I first learned about in this newsgroup is the
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bill,

There is nothing "strange" about the above. A non-elementary move to a field
containing a B edit will always behave in the way you found. This simply
demonstrates that the group level behaves as an Alphanumeric GROUP, rather
than an Alphnumeric ELEMENT. The issue is whether it behaves as ALPHANUMERIC
or not. It does. End of story.

Pete.
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ri7dd$fil@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net> <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7r63kn$hla@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <37dc7660@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote in message
news:37dc7660@eeyore.callnetuk.com...
>
> William M. Klein wrote in message
<7r63kn$hla@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>...
> >
> >The "strange" example that I first learned about in this newsgroup is the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> There is nothing "strange" about the above. A non-elementary move to a
field
> containing a B edit will always behave in the way you found. This simply
> demonstrates that the group level behaves as an Alphanumeric GROUP, rather
> than an Alphnumeric ELEMENT. The issue is whether it behaves as
ALPHANUMERIC
> or not. It does. End of story.
>
> Pete.

I consider it "strange" because your concept of a "ALPHANUMERIC GROUP" just
doesn't exist in the current COBOL Standard.   Although you are correct that
it works this "way" there is no term for what it "is" and "alphanumeric"
(which can be either a class or a category) in the Standard really only
applies explicitly to the elementary items when it is a category - and the
MOVE rules seem to be talking about CATEGORIES and not CLASSes.

MOSTLY, this is just "semantics" - as you do acknowledge that GROUPS work
differently than ELEMENTARY items - when both are "alphanumeric".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d6eeed.194993615@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a05$keb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net> <7r5hnm$8pc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Wed, 08 Sep 1999 11:36:22 GMT, WOB Consulting
<wobconsult@sprynet.com> wrote:

>In article <37d5e106.125888464@news1.ibm.net>,
>  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Rick showed you one, here is another:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.  TESTCY.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  GROUP-ITEM.
     03  GROUP-DATA PIC X(10) VALUE "ABCDEFGHIJ".
 01  DATA-TARGET PIC XXX/XXX/XXX/X.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 START-OF-PROGRAM.
     MOVE GROUP-ITEM TO DATA-TARGET
     DISPLAY DATA-TARGET
     MOVE GROUP-DATA TO DATA-TARGET
     DISPLAY DATA-TARGET
     STOP RUN
     .

The move of the group level item to the edited data item does not
behave the same way as the move of the alphanumeric item.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r581c$2f52$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
>  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>this way. Please present an example.

INITIALIZE group-name REPLACING ALPHANUMERIC BY SPACE
treats the group as a separate animal.

MOVE SPACES TO group-name
treats the group as ALPHANUMERIC.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r3a7q$ki8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>`

```
In article <7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>,
  "Bart Lupker" <lupker@hotmail.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>
> Does anybody out there knows whether the First level in this example
is :
> a. Alphanumeric or
> b. Numeric
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought it is numeric, because the other clauses are also numeric,
but I
> also heard that the First Level is always alphanumeric. Now, can
anybody
> help me out with this?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is c. compiler error. You may not name a variable with a space in it.
And you may not use the same name in the same structure.
So correct both and then RTFM, chapter 'Group Items'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D7107D.24406848@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <7r3a7q$ki8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You're half right. You CAN use the same name in the same structure. Though
it would not be very usable, since you would not be able to reference the
fields.  Many programmers used this feature to save keystrokes instead of
using FILLER (when the compilers insisted that every field have a name)  .

01 SOME-GROUP.
     02 F                     PIC X.
     02  REAL-FIELD PIC X.
     02  F                    PIC X.
 etc.

Daniel Jacot wrote:

> In article <7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>,
>   "Bart Lupker" <lupker@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iD9B3.16547$KM3.137608@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>`

```
Bart Lupker wrote:
>
>Does anybody out there knows whether the First level in this example is :
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>help me out with this?

The answer has absolutely nothing to do with 'First Level' being an
01.  It has to do with 'First Level' being a group item in your example
above.  Group items are always Alphanumeric Display.  The class of
a group item also has nothing whatever to do with the items contained
within it.  So you must be very careful when moving group items.

01  Example-1    Pic 9(08).

The above item 'Example-1' is numeric.  Other than a few ways in which
01 level items are treated (e.g. no OCCURS), the level number has
absolutely nothing to do with what the class of an item is, which are
determined by it's Picture, Usage and Group/Elementary attributes.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

- **From:** ales.romaniuk@zag.si
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12406c39d4cfdf17989686@news.siol.net>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>`

```
In article <7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>, lupker@hotmail.com 
says...
> Hi,
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
It is be alphanumeric. Actually, 03 level is also alphanumeric.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric picture clause

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79790E8594A73B36.76AC87DC0BC077D2.2A52BD08A31883DE@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl> <MPG.12406c39d4cfdf17989686@news.siol.net>`

```
On Wed, 8 Sep 1999 13:51:43 +0200, ales.romaniuk@zag.si enlightened
us:

>In article <7r34ns$272$1@hdxl22.telecom.ptt.nl>, lupker@hotmail.com 
>says...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

Wrong.  The 03 level is numeric display, not alphanumeric.  

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I started out with nothing, I still have most of it.



 Steve
```

<a id="t-47d9024f61"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol object code addresses wrong? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol object code addresses wrong?

- **From:** Jürgen Ibelgaufts <ibelgaufts@gfc-net.de>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D4E827.DE1C488C@gfc-net.de>`

```
Hello all

I have a huge program (compiled with Microfocus 4.0.36 as .gnt) that comes with
a Error 153 (subscript out of range) at PC 13d6b which should be the object code
address where the error occurs.

I compiled the program with the /ref directive that prints out the source
listing with the corresponding code addresses, but I can't find the object code
address that is issued in the error message, and the code addresses near to it
don't use indexes.

Are the object code addresses issued in the MF error message and compiler
listings all lie or what's going on here?

Any suggestions highly welcome

Juergen Ibelgaufts
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol object code addresses wrong?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r7da9$1r0$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<37D4E827.DE1C488C@gfc-net.de>`

```
    The ref listing lists address in INT form.  If you can make it fail as
an unsegmented
INT, that would be by far the easiest.

    You might be able to get an address listing with ASMLIST.  But rerun to
get the new address after a compile with a different option.
```

## Thread: I've got one email dated 100 years. — 3 msgs

### Re: I've got one email dated 100 years.

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>

## Thread: Cobol Upgrade from Dos to Windows? — 6 msgs

### Cobol Upgrade from Dos to Windows?

- **From:** "Innerlohinger Erwin" <csab1101@uibk.ac.at>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d54a6a.0@sia.uibk.ac.at>`

```
Dear

Can anyone tell me an easy way to transform an old DOS Source code (built in 1984), 
with many integrated linked files (50 little programfiles) to a modern Windows source code?

Any cobol links or good programs also to purchase for could help.

Best regards.

Erwin Innerlohinger
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Upgrade from Dos to Windows?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r3nn5$mkg$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37d54a6a.0@sia.uibk.ac.at>`

```
  Innerlohinger Erwin wrote in message <37d54a6a.0@sia.uibk.ac.at>...
  Dear

  Can anyone tell me an easy way to transform an old DOS Source code (built in 1984), 
  with many integrated linked files (50 little programfiles) to a modern Windows source code?

  Any cobol links or good programs also to purchase for could help.

I would suggest you take a look at Acucobol (www.acucorp.com) When I did the move a number of years ago it was right out of the box. You do not get new source though, the Acucobol runtime allow you to run your applications as is and then you can migrate the user interface step by step as your old ui are allowed to coexist with your new one. You are even able to distribute one single code as the runtime will distinguish this where it is necessary.

Acucobol has in my opinion a very good implementation of graphical user interface. The syntax is very similar to what you are used to.

What cobol are you currently using?

Cheesle
```
