###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37dc68d7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37D7318B.2BE7C6E7@bigfoot.com> <c6SB3.442$IS4.12327@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D7FCFB.320F479A@NOSPAMhome.com> <37D870B1.B5B857C8@bigfoot.com> <Wk8C3.122$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D93C1E.3A6D47E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Thanks for that, Howard. I found the article most interesting.

Whether COBOL is a shark or a dinosaur will be irrelevant within the next 15
to 20 years.

ALL the sharks and dinosaurs will perish when the general public raise the
temperature of the ocean by demanding to swim in it, and requiring it to be
safe...

As I have stated elsewhere in this NG, it is extremely unlikely that the
world population, as they gain access to computers, will tolerate being at
the mercy of a technocracy. The man in the street is not about to become a
computer programmer. Instead he will demand systems that he can interact
with and achieve results from.

If we are smart, we will start looking at the feasibility of delivering such
systems. (my company is already moving in this direction using component
logic with added intelligence. )The tools of today may be primitive, but
they are all we have with which to build the tools of tomorrow.

COBOL, C, Fortran, Basic, ...all the procedural languages will be replaced
within the next 2 decades. Computer programming as we know it will be
overtaken by events and it deserves to be. We are STILL using the same
waterfall approaches and techniques for software development which we used
in the 60's (with the possible exception of some very limited,currently
academic, areas like heuristic programming and neural networks.)

The Japs threw billions at AI and achieved very little. Another approach is
required and it will come.
Watch for "conceptual computing" (I call it this because that is the closest
analogy to a human thought process which I can find) where machines can
"understand" recognisable "concepts" and discuss them with a human to
achieve a result by iteration.

History will show that "computer programming" was a phenomenon of the latter
half of the twentieth century. By the twenty first century, it was no longer
relevant.

As for the Indians and Pakistanis taking our jobs, some of the most
innovative and technically excellent code I have seen recently is coming out
of the Asian sub-continent. These people are tele-working and doing it with
cutting edge OO technology. They don't have to come to America (though I
daresay most of them enjoy the experience). The difference is that they see
technical excellence as a means to a better life, while we just see it as a
way to make a living. It's like the difference between the lean "hungry"
challenger and the old champ...if the champ doesn't get in shape he is about
to have his ass kicked....

I totally agree with Michael and others in this thread who are saying that
it's no good bleating about the issuing of visas, we have to get better and
compete in a world market. As a career contractor, that has always been the
case for me and I love it!

Personally, I believe that the imagination, flair, and know-how which made
America great in the first place, needs to be re-stimulated (and, no, I am
NOT American...). There is still the potential there to be world beaters but
it won't happen whining about the fact that there is competition (whether
it's fair or unfair). The only way to get competitive advantage is to work
and be, smarter and more imaginative than the competition....

Pete.



Howard Brazee wrote in message <37D93C1E.3A6D47E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Michael Mattias wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.zdnet.com/pcweek/archive/960513/pcwk0029.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** kittu234@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7risdb$i59$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37D7318B.2BE7C6E7@bigfoot.com> <c6SB3.442$IS4.12327@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D7FCFB.320F479A@NOSPAMhome.com> <37D870B1.B5B857C8@bigfoot.com> <Wk8C3.122$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D93C1E.3A6D47E@NOSPAMhome.com> <37dc68d7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
This has been the way of life always.
People from all over the world came to USA looking for better
oppurtunities. I agree to the fact that the amount of input from India
and other Asian countries has been quite high during the recent years
and I also agree to that some of them might not have the desired
credentials for the job that they represent to do.
But has this not always been the case with the world/, the effort to
find a better place to live?, make more money?? , get rich? get
famous?? make a name for themselves their parents?? , be at a place
where the cutting edge technologies are??, look for a domain where they
get real returns for their value?? , or places to better invest their
values??

At the same time I also believe these people on the average brought in
good knowledge with them.

They also help make  USA the the diverse nation that is always has ben
thanks

better and

been the
> case for me and I love it!
>
> Personally, I believe that the imagination, flair, and know-how which
made
> America great in the first place, needs to be re-stimulated (and, no,
I am
> NOT American...). There is still the potential there to be world
beaters but
> it won't happen whining about the fact that there is competition
(whether
> it's fair or unfair). The only way to get competitive advantage is to
work
> and be, smarter and more imaginative than the competition....

peter dashwood wrote:

> History will show that "computer programming" was a phenomenon of the latter
> half of the twentieth century. By the twenty first century, it was no longer
> relevant.

Very few skilled laborers of past generations would recognize the skills
needed to do the same job today.  We have to adjust to change.  I did
just get my masters in OO with this in mind - but I am old enough that I
will be surprised if there isn't a good demand for my COBOL skills until
after I retire.

There will be SOMETHING like "computer programming" in the next century.
(just as there are modified needs for most skills of the last century).
 

> Personally, I believe that the imagination, flair, and know-how which made
> America great in the first place, needs to be re-stimulated (and, no, I am
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

And again I am old enough that I value the future (my children and
grandchildren) more than the present (my ability to find a job doing
what I do now).  Protectionism can keep me fat a while, but it
definitely makes future generations vulnerable.  They need to be lean,
smart, and quick.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rj9qf$43k$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<37D7318B.2BE7C6E7@bigfoot.com> <c6SB3.442$IS4.12327@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D7FCFB.320F479A@NOSPAMhome.com> <37D870B1.B5B857C8@bigfoot.com> <Wk8C3.122$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D93C1E.3A6D47E@NOSPAMhome.com> <37dc68d7@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood wrote in message
>
>COBOL, C, Fortran, Basic, ...all the procedural languages will be replaced
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree with about 90% of what you said, but this I do not believe. While it
is true that pure procedural languages just cannot cut it for everything,
the fact remains that they can handle a very high proportion of tasks, and
are quite easy to set up.

What I see as the next paradigm shift is not a replacement of such tools,
but a higher level language that uses exe files as the base component.
"Batch" has long been the standard whereby complex procedures have been
simplified.  "Batch", even in the home computer, has been pretty well
eliminated.

An oops language that is specifically invented for OS tasks, perhaps with a
very simple starting point (like forth), is where I see the next major
advance being made.  We need a method of taking thousands of working modules
and tying them together in a neat package, not a way of scrapping everything
and starting over.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37dd80ad@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37D7318B.2BE7C6E7@bigfoot.com> <c6SB3.442$IS4.12327@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D7FCFB.320F479A@NOSPAMhome.com> <37D870B1.B5B857C8@bigfoot.com> <Wk8C3.122$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37D93C1E.3A6D47E@NOSPAMhome.com> <37dc68d7@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7rj9qf$43k$1@news.igs.net>`

```

donald tees wrote in message <7rj9qf$43k$1@news.igs.net>...
>peter dashwood wrote in message
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>I agree with about 90% of what you said, but this I do not believe. While
it
>is true that pure procedural languages just cannot cut it for everything,
>the fact remains that they can handle a very high proportion of tasks, and
>are quite easy to set up.
>
Yes, Donald, they are easy for us (as trained programmers) to set up. What
is happening is that you are seeing millions of people across the world who
want to be able to use their computers. They have neither the time nor
inclination to learn "computer programming". Nor will they accept having to
wait until a technical resource is available to do the job for them.

They will expect (and IMHO, quite rightly) the machines to be smart enough
to communicate with them in their own native (natural) language, or at least
in a language which is intuitive enough for them to recognise it without
training...(Why has GUI superceded CLI?). They will also expect machines to
"understand" what they are talking about. Although this may sound like
science fiction right now, it isn't. The cutting edge of OO technology makes
it possible to build self-modification into methods, there are vast
libraries of "components" (Active-X controls, OO objects, etc) and these are
growing every day. It is a logical step to combine these components with a
smarter OS (certainly smarter than the ones we have at present which are
really just resource managers and job schedulers...) into a user interface
which is interactive and responsive and can produce results for an untrained
user by iterating with him until it gets it right...



>What I see as the next paradigm shift is not a replacement of such tools,
>but a higher level language that uses exe files as the base component.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>advance being made.  We need a method of taking thousands of working
modules
>and tying them together in a neat package, not a way of scrapping
everything
>and starting over.
>
"the next major advance" in what? Computer programming? What for? Forget
it...it's days are numbered. What you have described is not a major paradigm
shift but it IS an essential step towards the "concept computer" I keep
talking about. (I never suggested that we scrap everythng and start over; on
the contrary, I said we will build tomorrow's tools using today's because
they are all we have..). The process you describe is exactly accurate and
some of us are already working towards it, but it is only the beginning...


Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People

- **From:** Monstercycle <scotty76@webtv.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rjki0$5b0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37D7318B.2BE7C6E7@bigfoot.com>`

```
Well, here are my thoughts on the subject:  I am a programmer/analyst
for a large company of around 8,800 people with around 5,000 being
programmers.  I am just a regular programmer that works with COBOL,
DB2, VSAM etc, no fancy stuff yet.  I have been a programmer for 2
years now. We have been trying to hire people in the area, but are
having a hard time finding people with the experience we need.  So, we
have resorted to contractors.  Every one of the contractors we have
gotten are from India.  They are definetly a nice bunch, but geez, how
in the heck do they consider them programmer/analyst when they don't
even know simple COBOL verbs!  This is rediculous, so here we are
hiring these guys and gals to help us get projects done, but we are
having to train them in COBOL and DB2.  My manager has admitted that it
is cheaper to go with the Indian contractors than American, but my
thoughts are you get what you pay for!  I have seen too many companies
cut costs here and there and their quality goes to sh*t!  I am sure
there are some great programmers from other countries out there and I
do not mind them coming over and getting hired as programmers here in
the U.S..  But when people come over without near the experience they
claim to have, that just causes more time, work and money spent on
these foreigners that shouldn't have been sent over!  With unemployment
down and technology growing at the rate it is, I do not worry about
losing my job as a programmer right now, but if companies keep hiring
people from other countries as cheap labor like they are at this rate,
I may start to worry. Hell, I wouldn't even be able to work at Wal-Mart
or a factory around here because they already figured out they can hire
people from Mexico for next to nothin.  And some of you people think we
should stay competitive and ahead of the game to keep our jobs from
being overtaken by a foreigner?  I am busy enough with my projects that
I don't have a whole lot of time to keep up with all the new
technology.  I spend time with my family on my time off and on
activities I enjoy.  I should not have to worry about losing my job to
a person who is willing to work 80 hours/week for half my salary, when
I am a very hard productive worker that IMO makes exactly what I
deserve.

Scott


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

---

<a id="t-dceb5bc6a0"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL: Opinions re file access techniques — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu PowerCOBOL: Opinions re file access techniques

- **From:** jgwolfe@netcom.ca (Gerry Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d706d5.4339669@nntp.netcom.ca>`

```
Greetings... I've been a lurker for a while, and now have a few
questions

My background is about 30 years usage of COBOL and ASM, and 13 years
teaching at a local polyech/university.  I've used Fujitsu (r4) COBOL
in batch COBOL (*it's great*) and am now building a club management
system in PowerCOBOL to learn the PC aspects...

A few questions / requests for advice...

Q1: Tutorials show INVOKEs using MS/Access and SQL under a server
environment.  They are both ODBC-accessed -- is it safe to assume that
I could use SQL statements against a MS/Access database?

Q2: None of the PowerCOBOL tutorials address file access with the
traditional SELECT / FD / READ etc COBOL verbs.  Again, I assume this
is available???

Q3: What about the relative merits of the INVOKE vs SQL vs traditional
COBOL READ/WRITE access of data under PowerCOBOL.  I'm wondering about
"robustness",  control, performance etc.

Q4: Any suggestions on how to build an environment where the
application can define data file paths?  For example, it would be
great to provide a Tools|Preferences|DataFiles|Path approach for users
to identify where they would like to keep the data files.

Q5: Any suggestions on how to build a file environment that would make
it difficult for a user/client to access passwords etc. outside the
application?

advTHANKSance to those who have more experience...

rgds, G.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL: Opinions re file access techniques

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lwFB3.675$q%5.33870@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<37d706d5.4339669@nntp.netcom.ca>`

```

Gerry Wolfe <jgwolfe@netcom.ca> wrote in message
news:37d706d5.4339669@nntp.netcom.ca...
>
> Q1: Tutorials show INVOKEs using MS/Access and SQL under a server
> environment.  They are both ODBC-accessed -- is it safe to assume
that
> I could use SQL statements against a MS/Access database?

Yes. If you have the MSA (or SQL or FoxPro or whatever) drivers.
>
> Q2: None of the PowerCOBOL tutorials address file access with the
> traditional SELECT / FD / READ etc COBOL verbs.  Again, I assume
this
> is available???

Sure. ISAM, Relative, Sequential, Line Sequential, etc.
One feature I like (coming from Realia COBOL) is file and record
locking for all file types.
>
> Q3: What about the relative merits of the INVOKE vs SQL vs
traditional
> COBOL READ/WRITE access of data under PowerCOBOL.  I'm wondering
about
> "robustness",  control, performance etc.

All the standard arguments apply: With SQL you can change
database orginazation or easily access the data with other
programs. Some programming techniques are easier with ODBC
techniques. With ODBC, there is a learning curve and, sometimes,
a performance penalty (though slight).
>
> Q4: Any suggestions on how to build an environment where the
> application can define data file paths?  For example, it would be
> great to provide a Tools|Preferences|DataFiles|Path approach for
users
> to identify where they would like to keep the data files.

The easiest way is during the installation of the resultant program
suite. Fujiusu provides a scaled-down version of InstallShield and
one of the parameters there asks the user for a destination. FJ
programs
and their associated run times are LARGE. One DLL in our suite is
approaching 2MB and so far we are up to about 30MB worth of
executable code for a POS/Inventory system. But that's normal for
a Windows application. Get a CD duplicator (I know where they
can be had for $2200 - the unattended kind. Does a stack of 50 at
up to 8X.).
>
> Q5: Any suggestions on how to build a file environment that would
make
> it difficult for a user/client to access passwords etc. outside the
> application?

Hash the password file.

Have fun.
```

```
Can any-one out there help me with microfocus personal cobol for win 95
I have a pentium 500 mhz system with windows 98, my problem is, I cannot get
it to run after installation. I have tried all the usual things like,

## Thread: FILE LOCKING — 4 msgs

### FILE LOCKING

- **From:** shanmugam@my-deja.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r66bb$p0n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Gurus,

I want deny access to a file which is being created by a COBOL process,
to the UNIX commands (eg. cp, ls, rm, mv, etc.).

Thank you


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: FILE LOCKING

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RfyB3.10442$r5.772092@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<7r66bb$p0n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
shanmugam@my-deja.com wrote in message <7r66bb$p0n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I want deny access to a file which is being created by a COBOL process,
>to the UNIX commands (eg. cp, ls, rm, mv, etc.).

I can only take a SWAG since I don't know what kind of compiler/runtime (if
any) you have, so you may need a different name on the call statement.

01  CHMOD-COMMAND  PIC X(??) VALUE "chmod 600 /bla/bla/bla/MyFile".

------------
    OPEN OUTPUT MY-FILE.
    CLOSE MY-FILE.
    CALL "SYSTEM" USING CHMOD-COMMAND.
    OPEN OUTPUT MY-FILE.

Then do a chmod 666 to your new file after you close the file (or 644 if
others can only read it).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FILE LOCKING

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>

To deny access to ls as well you would also need to do this on the

<a id="t-da0efcbb13"></a>
## Thread: Scrolling banners? — 3 msgs

### Scrolling banners?

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <REMOVE SPACES rob _ a @ unipharm . com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d69221$0$17811@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Greetings,

I am using AcuCOBOL 4.2 on a Windows NT client.
My data is on a UNIX file server and the client and filesever interact via
AcuServer.

I have an application that checks a file every 5 seconds for record content.
If there is a record it displays the record to all the users of the system.
The users are expected to 'take' the record from the file and process it.

I want to make some changes though, I'd like to use a scrolling banner
that scrolls the record found from the very right most column of the
window to the very left most column of the screen.
Take a stock ticker for example.

Currently the routine that checks for record content and displays it
is a threaded routine from the main application thread.
This thread runs and checks for a record whilst allowing users to interact
with the main thread.

I can get the scrolling to work using a PERFORM TEST and PERFORM VARYING
routine that decreases the column size by 1 until it equals 2, but the speed
at which
the label scrolls by is lightening fast.
A call to C$SLEEP seems like the only way to do this... and I not too crazy
about sleeps.
Does anyone have an algorithm or suggestions on how to to do this without
slowing
down the performance of my application or interfering with thread operation?

Regards,

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
----
Robert Annandale

Please remove spaces and 'NO SPAM' from address to reply.
All replies will be appreciated.  Replies to newsgroup will be appreciated
more.
```

#### ↳ Re: Scrolling banners?

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <REMOVE SPACES rob _ a @ unipharm . com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d6ae03$0$24629@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<37d69221$0$17811@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Okay I've found a way to do this, I can even allow the user to set different
scrolling speeds... I did the following.

This is still unpolished...

       77  Ws-Column              Pic 99v99.
       77  Ws-Speed               Pic Sv99 Value -.02.

           Perform Varying Ws-Column From 47.00 By Ws-Speed
                   Until Ws-Column = 2
                   Display Main-LA-80-Screen
           End-Perform.

If anyone has a different or better way, I'd love to hear from you.

Regards,

RBA.

> Greetings,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have an application that checks a file every 5 seconds for record
content.
> If there is a record it displays the record to all the users of the
system.
> The users are expected to 'take' the record from the file and process it.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> routine that decreases the column size by 1 until it equals 2, but the
speed
> at which
> the label scrolls by is lightening fast.
> A call to C$SLEEP seems like the only way to do this... and I not too
crazy
> about sleeps.
> Does anyone have an algorithm or suggestions on how to to do this without
> slowing
> down the performance of my application or interfering with thread
operation?

> Regards,

> --------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Scrolling banners?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r6h7g$ge4$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37d69221$0$17811@fountain.mindlink.net> <37d6ae03$0$24629@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Robert Annandale <REMOVE SPACES rob _ a @ unipharm . com> wrote:
:> A call to C$SLEEP seems like the only way to do this... and I not too
: crazy
:> about sleeps.

I bet the other users are not too crazy about you then.
The point about doing a 'sleep' is that it releases the
CPU resource so that other processes, or other users on
a multiuser system, can have their program run.

If you program in such a way that it uses all the CPU it can
get hold of to do trivial tasks then this will detract from
all other processes including servers and print spoolers or
even network responders.

While it can be determined an effective rate of scrolling on
one machine by experimentation this will vary for different
machines.  Using a 'sleep' function should give a stepping
rate that is effective for all machines.


A
that one machine 
```

re-developed and the results of this full re-write are now
ready for use. The  Programmer's is designed to run on the
Windows 95, Windows 98 or NT platforms.

So what is new:

## Thread: API calls from MF OO COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### API calls from MF OO COBOL

- **From:** "Guus" <guus.van.der.kleij@twinsoft.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r583a$sou$1@zonnetje.nl.uu.net>`

```
I've done some simple API calls from MF OO COBOL allready, but I now want to
try something much more complicated.

I would like to use the CreateProcess API to start an application, without
losing control of my current application.

When I use a normal call or chain command from COBOL, the controls are
always passed to the called program.
I want to create a kind of scheduler, that keeps on running and queries a
database. Depending on the database contents, the program hast to start one
or more programs.

The COBOL/API documentation from MF is rather poorly. Does anyone know of
more extensive documentation? The standard API-documents usually provide
only C-examples. But how do I declare pointers and booleans from COBOL?

Has anybody ever used the CreateProcess API and if so, can you provide me
with a sample source-file?

Awaiting your responses,

Marco Klomp/Guus v/d Kleij
```

#### ↳ Re: API calls from MF OO COBOL

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D6346B.F30C8874@MERANT.com>`
- **References:** `<7r583a$sou$1@zonnetje.nl.uu.net>`

```
Guus wrote:
> 
> I've done some simple API calls from MF OO COBOL allready, but I now want to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> losing control of my current application.

Hi.

You might want to consider just using the CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT call
instead. In NetExpress, this can be used to run system executables as
well as COBOL programs.

> The COBOL/API documentation from MF is rather poorly. Does anyone know of
> more extensive documentation? The standard API-documents usually provide
> only C-examples.

Remember that the APIs themselves don't 'belong' to MF/MERANT - it would
be unreasonable to expect the COBOL documentation to document all the
OS APIs. Unfortunately for the COBOL programmer, most OS API's are
documented in C or C++ terms only.

> But how do I declare pointers and booleans from COBOL?

01 native-pointer USAGE POINTER.
01 native-function-pointer USAGE PROCEDURE-POINTER.
01 c-boolean PIC X(4) COMP-5.

The last one is not neccessarily correct - it depends what 'boolean' is
defined as in the C code (it's a typedef, not part of the C language).
The above example assumes it's just another name for a 32-bit unsigned
integer, native byte ordering.

Cheers, Kev.
```

What certification?  There is no standard, so this sounds like a con job to
me.

I personally think that most of these private schools are a total rip-off.
You get better training, the diploma is worth more, and the cost is
generally far less when you train at state (provincial here in Canada)
colleges.  The biggest problem with the state schools is that they will not
promise to train you in twenty minutes.  They expect you to do the job
right.

rustydvs@my-deja.com wrote in message <7r8nkp$kg7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d6f8ac.9865228@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <37A2A27E.E8A0CDA3@att.net> <37A5957E.45873012@NOSPAMhome.com> <37A5EC12.8D84A7E5@home.com> <37A98A80.95CA29D@IMN.nl> <D612B2778BA4883E.1BDDCED0C4E7FF5C.3D3D28DC22FCFC01@lp.airnews.net> <37B1B403.89D22CAB@home.com> <37B1D9E3.4D183B4C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Wed, 11 Aug 1999 14:15:31 -0600, Howard Brazee wrote:

[snip]

>There are a couple of reasons for mainframes:

[...]
>2.  Power
[...]

>As PCs get more powerful, so will mainframes.  We will be able to do
>current tasks on smaller machines, but so far we have always found new
>tasks which want even more power.  When will we stop coming up with
>needs which require this kind of power?

Likely, we will not.

No matter how efficient and powerful small motors get, an economy car
will never be able to do the work of a large truck.

In the past few years, despite huge increases in the number of PCs and
the number of PC/network applications, and despite the fact that some
mainframe applications have become replaced by distributed systems, we
have still had to *increase* mainframe resources.  This increase has
been in nearly all areas (CPU speed and capability, disk space, higher
performance peripherals, I/O channels, etc.).
```

---

<a id="t-d50818e9d9"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL Questions re: File Access — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu PowerCOBOL Questions re: File Access

- **From:** jgwolfe@netcom.ca (Gerry Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d70223.3136953@nntp.netcom.ca>`

```
I've been a luurker for a donkey's age, and am now posing a question
for feedback about different file access techniques under PowerCOBOL
r4.

I've been a mainframe COBOL & ASM programmer for over 30 years, have
taught it at the local Polytechnic/University for 13, so have a pretty
good fix on using the batch version of Fujitsu -- I find it an
*excellent* alternative to MicroFocus etc.  However, visual OOD stuff
is new to me, and I'm building a club management system under Win98 as
a learning experience (perhaps as a revenue generating one as well).

Q1.  The PowerCOBOL tutorials use MS/Access ODBC examples for the
INVOKE examples.  They use server examples for the SQL examples.  Both
are run under ODBC.  Is it correct to assume that I could use SQL
under MS/Access?

Q2. Probably a dumb question, but does PowerCOBOL support normal FD
and SELECT and READ and WRITE etc.  Reference manuals suggest this,
but none of the user guides even mention this is possible.

Q3. What are the relative merits of the 3 methods of file access --
intrinsic INVOKEs vs: SQL statements vs: COBOL READ/WRITE etc.  I'm
primarily interested in versatility, robustness, performance etc.

advTHANKSance 
to all who have been "respondents" as opposed to "lurkers" like
myself, looking forward to some comments/opinions from those with more
experience than myself.

Rgds, Gerry
```

- **From:** "George E. Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<CFD2F31DF0071A65.2F877FA112ADD66E.D621AD03ACD86A24@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Forgive me, I am not a Visual Basic Programmer, if I phrase this wrong.  An
associate of mine is attempting to access a couple of my 32 bit utility
DLL's, (generated using Micro Focus [MERANT] COBOL v. 4.038).  He is using
VB5

He is not having much success at it.  Does anyone have any concrete examples
(preferably simple) that demonstrates a VB5 program calling a Micro Focus
DLL?  Is there anything unique that he (or I) need to do?  The VB program
seems to have difficulty finding the DLL even though the paths are set
correctly.  He has even tried it in the default directory -- No Luck.

He has had previous success using Fujitsu COBOL, but as I don't have it
available to me he wanted to try to use the going DLL

Appreciate any guidance or references you could provide.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D7EF30.B6E0264@rms.moore.com>`

```

and AIX.  Any vendors handle all three?   Any vendors for Mac? Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cross Platform Development

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7r8kmv$3ac$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37D7EF30.B6E0264@rms.moore.com>`

```

JonB wrote in message <37D7EF30.B6E0264@rms.moore.com>...
>I'm looking for a COBOL development environment for Apple Mac, IBM PC
>and AIX.  Any vendors handle all three?   Any vendors for Mac? Thanks.

If you leave out Mac, there are several choices, otherwise ... .

For development on Mac OS, no.

For deployment on Mac OS, see PERCobol < www.LegacyJ.com >.

For Mac A-UX, see AcuCobol < www.acucorp.com >.

Is A-UX still available or used?
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-5ef19660ba"></a>
## Thread: COBOL v Other Languages -Reply — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL v Other Languages -Reply

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7d7cb18.064@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Having worked for many years in a previous life for a large insurance company I can unfortunately say - yes they would - and not retrain the programmers they had but hire kidlings right out of school for � the money.

>>> Jerry Peacock 09/08 11:30 pm >>>
 
Written by an academic with typical ivory tower thought processes
(four
sentences, one with 37 words - not even COBOL is that verbose).
Suppose
you are a real-world boss of a large (say insurance) company. You have
over 3,000 COBOL programs (comprising 15,000,000 lines of code) in
production. These programs run daily. You have 100 COBOL programmers
and 250,000 reels of magnetic tape containing historical data.

Your company decides to offer a new product (say, Y2K insurance). Are
you
gonna gear up for the language de jour (say JAVA++) on the faddiest OS
(say
Linux-Plus)?

<a id="t-1ad840670a"></a>
## Thread: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Re: Rates and Salaries being Cut by H-1B Visa'd People

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7d7ded3.043@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
If this is true then those of us in the trenches here haven't seen anything. 

There are currently at least 5 contracts here that fit into this category.  I have worked directly with 3 of them and have found them to be very competent.  Unlike one US type person that came in that must have flunked COBOL 101 as in 2 weeks he couldn't write a simple 'read file write report' program that required nothing more than moving the fields to the report and a final total.  

If you can do the job you're in - if not you're out.  

>>> Cheesle 09/09 1:05 pm >>>
 

I read in an article in Computerworld, that one of the conditions for a
company to hire someone on a H-1B visa, is that the salary of that person is
to be made public at the work place, and that this persons salary should be
higher than the other staff, to avoid the issue of pressing down the salary.

Whether this is functional in real life, I have no idea.

Cheesle

```
If you are getting a linkage editor error message, be aware that
the 'CBL-xxxxxxxx' routines are contained in a separate library
which you must include during the link step. The library name is
F3BICBLR.LIB.

euro <euromercante@mail.telepac.pt> wrote in message
news:7rbd4t$rp1$1@duke.telepac.pt...

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

- **References:** `<7rbd4t$rp1$1@duke.telepac.pt> <skBC3.220$kL2.23207@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
In the project right-click on "Module".
There click on the tab "Link" and insert the lib-file "F3BICBLR.LIB"

Hope that helps
bye
```

## Thread: AcuCobol and SQL Server 7.0 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### AcuCobol and SQL Server 7.0

- **From:** "Jean Franck" <jean_franck@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rbnpk$dic$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```
Hello,

We're looking at AcuCobol to develop some client/server apps
with MS SQL Server 7 as database on our NT server.
Can anyone tell me which tool is "better" to connect to
the database : Acu4GL or AcuServer ?

Thanks for any kind of reaction !

Jean
Belgium
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol and SQL Server 7.0

- **From:** "Jason Bailey" <jayandtonya@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8seC3.191$9q1.11468@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7rbnpk$dic$1@news0.skynet.be>`

```
> We're looking at AcuCobol to develop some client/server apps
> with MS SQL Server 7 as database on our NT server.
> Can anyone tell me which tool is "better" to connect to
> the database : Acu4GL or AcuServer ?

Jean,
    First of all, AcuServer won't work with SQL.  You'll need to either use
Acu4GL or AcuODBC to manipulate the data.  The systems engineers at AcuCorp
told me that both are slow and that we would be better off using AcuServer
with Vision files.
    Not sure what sort of warehousing you intend to do, but we decided
against SQL with AcuCOBOL just for this reason and have stayed with our
indexed file scheme.
    There's a Nigel (don't recall the last name) who is a system engineer
for AcuCorp who lurks around this newsgroup.  Maybe he'll see your post and
give you more information that I can.

    Regards,

    Jason Bailey
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AcuCobol and SQL Server 7.0

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FsZQ4CAe1t23Iw+i@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7rbnpk$dic$1@news0.skynet.be> <8seC3.191$9q1.11468@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
A Nigel? A Nigel? A Nigel? THE Nigel, if you don't mind... :^)

I'm a little concerned by the systems engineer who told you that
Acu4GL would be slow. Slow'ER' than Vision I'll buy (for most
applications), but 'slow' is a relative term. Looks like it's time
break out the bright lights and sharp spikes again!  :^)

AcuODBC allows you to access Vision from ODBC compliant
apps, so doesn't really figure here. As Jason says AcuServer isn;t
appropriate here either (it's a Vision only product).

Your options for SQL Server are Acu4GL which will allow you to
use standard Cobol I/O verbs on your SQL Server database, or
AcuSQL which will let you embed SQL in your programs.

Which one? Depends....... For ease of use, speed of deployment
and simplicity Acu4GL is your answer. For ultimate performance
AcuSQL may be the way to go. Sometimes a combination of the
two technologies gets best results.

The simple answer is to try it and see what works. We (Acucorp)
will be very happy to work with you to figure out a workable,
affordable solution to your problem. Contact your local office, or
drop me an email and I'll set it up.

Best regards

Nigel

In article <8seC3.191$9q1.11468@typhoon01.swbell.net>, Jason
Bailey <jayandtonya@prodigy.net> writes

>> with MS SQL Server 7 as database on our NT server.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Really wierd problem!!! — 18 msgs

### Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** "Judy Shepherd" <sheps@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
As of 1.00pm today I coded my first (very rudimentary) COBOL program, in
fact, my first program of any type at all.  I haven't even been in DOS for
years, so I am a total novice, just doing my first programming subject at
UNI.

However in the space of 3 days after a lot of hand wringing I bit the
bullet, structured the program, designed data files and coded, compiled and
ran it.... and to my absolutely delight it worked.

The problem I have is this....I have to print a report with Headings,
sub-heading, detail etc...with control breaks and with each heading printing
on a new page after the control break. When I first coded it I just advanced
the heading after printing the total by 5 lines....and this printed fine.

But after getting the program to work I went in to tidy it up and changed
the heading to print after advancing page......( the heading has a constant
field and a variable field).

When I advanced 5 lines the two fields printed fine both on the disk file
and on the actual printout....but when I advanced a page....The initial
heading (COMPANY: ATR Insurance) prints perfectly after advancing a blank
page, but the heading after the control break, prints way out to the left of
the screen on the disk file and with half the information lopped off on the
printout..... so instead of printing COMPANY: Lotus Insurance it printed
just COMPANY: Lot....


I have no idea where to start looking, as by all accounts the coding is
fine....the problem starts when I advance a page rather than just 5 line.

This assignment is due by next Friday and I've exhausted all my thought
processes, I would appreciate any help that you can give.....as
unfortunately there is very little help coming from the staff at the Uni.

By the way I am running RM/Cobol ~ 85 (2nd Edn) in the Dosshell from Windows
98 (also 2nd Edition) and am printing to a Canon BJC 4200.

Cheers
Judy Shepherd.
```

#### ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d8f932.34363654@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
On Fri, 10 Sep 1999 21:43:27 +1000, "Judy Shepherd"
<sheps@bigpond.com> wrote:

>As of 1.00pm today I coded my first (very rudimentary) COBOL program, in
>fact, my first program of any type at all.  I haven't even been in DOS for
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>and on the actual printout....but when I advanced a page....The initial
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Any chance we could get you to post the code?  You did an excellent
job describing the problem.

I've seen things like this happen when you have data defined sort of
like this:

01  Print-Lines.
     03  Detail-Line Pic X(20).
     03  Heading-:Line Pic X(20).

Write Print-Rec from Detail-Line after 1.
Write Print-Rec from Print-Lines after Page. 

instead of 

Wrinte Print-Rec from Heading-Line after Page.

I think if you posted the program to the newsgroup someone could see
and help solve the problem.  If there is sample data for the program,
post that too.

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
You have to be aware of the way that the ASCII codes work.  Each line is
terminated by a <CR,LF> in your output.  The page mark (^L) that advances
the page is just a character that fits into this. It sounds as if you are
getting the page character in the wrong place. Looking at the output with a
decent editor that can show you the result in hex or octal should show you
exactly what is happening.

There are a couple of ways that you can fiddle around to fix it. One is to
print a blank line at the end of the page, so that you know you are
positioned at the left column.  You can also printing that last line BEFORE
advancing PAGE, rather than AFTER.  Similar stunts can be done with the
header.
Judy Shepherd wrote in message ...
>As of 1.00pm today I coded my first (very rudimentary) COBOL program, in
>fact, my first program of any type at all.  I haven't even been in DOS for
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>sub-heading, detail etc...with control breaks and with each heading
printing
>on a new page after the control break. When I first coded it I just
advanced
>the heading after printing the total by 5 lines....and this printed fine.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>page, but the heading after the control break, prints way out to the left
of
>the screen on the disk file and with half the information lopped off on the
>printout..... so instead of printing COMPANY: Lotus Insurance it printed
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>By the way I am running RM/Cobol ~ 85 (2nd Edn) in the Dosshell from
Windows
>98 (also 2nd Edition) and am printing to a Canon BJC 4200.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sv7C3.2366$ES2.27168@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
>
>There are a couple of ways that you can fiddle around to fix it.
> ...
>You can also printing that last line BEFORE
>advancing PAGE, rather than AFTER.

I was just about to suggest that myself.  The only reason that AFTER
ADVANCING logic ever got off the ground in the first place is because
the very early IBM 1403 printers actually functioned that way (one
command: advance, then print).  Since then IBM printers, even later
1403 printers used BEFORE ADVANCING logic (one command: print, then
advance).  I understand that now IBM system software does a conversion
of AFTER logic to BEFORE logic during printing.  But we are still
stuck with millions of programmers who are trained to use AFTER logic,
just because of a single dumb design decision by an IBM engineer that
was realized to be a blunder and corrected decades ago!
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d98f12.72737377@news1.ibm.net>`

<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>ADVANCING logic ever got off the ground in the first place is because
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are talking about be again.


I know when I am on the FIRST line of a page, but not necessarily the
LAST.  So I'm  an AFTER ADVANCING man myself.

Write Print-Line from Heading after Page.

Not before, I want to write it immediately after I skip to a new page!
<G>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AyuC3.6806$Yu3.54471@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net> <Sv7C3.2366$ES2.27168@news3.mia> <37d98f12.72737377@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>You are talking about be again.

Sorry. :-)

>I know when I am on the FIRST line of a page, but not necessarily the
>LAST.  So I'm  an AFTER ADVANCING man myself.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Not before, I want to write it immediately after I skip to a new page!

Is it not true that you were taught to use AFTER ADVANCING logic? :-)
I was not taught either way.  Around 1970 I spent two to three years
seriously researching report writing and printing issues.  During that
time I wrote several hundred report programs.  Among other issues, one
thing became clear: that AFTER logic was less efficient than BEFORE
logic for several reasons.  One is that virtually all line printers
support single commands to print, then advance, with the early 1403's
being a rare anomaly (that IBM corrected, please note :).  Second,
AFTER logic leaves the print position on top of the previously printed
line, making that line, in a sense, 'incomplete', which violates the
rule of least astonishment.  Third, there are far more situations where
you want to place extra space after the data you are printing than
otherwise.  For example, why test in many places in your program if
you just printed a header of some kind which needs extra space?  Much
better to print the extra space at the one place associated with the
extra space than the many other places not associated with the extra
space.  Like I said, this is not a conclusion I came to casually, but
one I reached after a great deal of research.  :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37da8db4.137933289@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net> <Sv7C3.2366$ES2.27168@news3.mia> <37d98f12.72737377@news1.ibm.net> <AyuC3.6806$Yu3.54471@news4.mia>`

```
On Sat, 11 Sep 1999 10:28:01 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

... Posted and mailed ...

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>one I reached after a great deal of research.  :-)

I think I agree with you.  The AFTER logic lets me reprint over a
line.  (but you can do that with before).  I handle the blank lines in
the header by using a "blank line" record.  Check out page 360 of my
book.  For header I don't want to advance on the first page, but I do
on the rest of the pages.

Using the BEFORE logic, can you post the alternative?  (Serious query,
I think it might be cleaner).  

What do you do for an end of page using before logiic?  How do you
handle that?  Counting lines?  It all seems simpler to be using the
After logic (which I was taught, using a 1403 printer no less).

One thing - I think people should use one or the other in a program
and not BOTH, it really makes it a mess.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DB373F.E6CE9CFD@zip.com.au>`

> and not BOTH, it really makes it a mess.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f_8D3.561$Bw1.4932@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net> <Sv7C3.2366$ES2.27168@news3.mia> <37d98f12.72737377@news1.ibm.net> <AyuC3.6806$Yu3.54471@news4.mia> <37da8db4.137933289@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>Using the BEFORE logic, can you post the alternative?  (Serious query,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>After logic (which I was taught, using a 1403 printer no less).


Here is an example, using a 4 line header as in your book.  As in your
example, the initial 99 in LINE-COUNT forces the first page heading,
eliminating a perform of page heading in initialization.  But it also
signals the page heading routine that the first page is being printed,
in order to avoid printing a blank page at the front of the report.  I
prefer to de-couple page number from this this, because you sometimes
need to reset the page number after a control break, and still skip to
a new page.  I also prefer to make item MAX-LINES a constant, when the
compiler supports that, which you probably do, too. :-)

77  PAGE-COUNT         PIC  9(04) VALUE 0.
77  LINE-COUNT         PIC  9(02) VALUE 99.
77  MAX-LINES          PIC  9(02) VALUE 60.

PRINT-DETAIL-LINE.
    IF (LINE-COUNT > MAX-LINES)
        PERFORM PRINT-PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.
    <move detail line to print record>
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD BEFORE 1.
    MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-RECORD.
    ADD 1 TO LINE-COUNT.

PRINT-PAGE-HEADING.
    IF (LINE-COUNT < 99)
        WRITE PRINT-RECORD BEFORE PAGE
    END-IF.
    ADD 1 TO PAGE-COUNT.
    MOVE PAGE-COUNT TO PH1-PAGE-COUNT.
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-1 BEFORE 1.
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-2 BEFORE 1.
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-3 BEFORE 2.
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-4 BEFORE 2.
    MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-RECORD.
    MOVE 3 TO LINE-COUNT.

I have used the control break method in your book, but prefer another
method that I feel is cleaner, and more flexible.  Perhaps we can
discuss that in another thread. :-)

>One thing - I think people should use one or the other in a program
>and not BOTH, it really makes it a mess.

Absolutely!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990911113152.20930.00002421@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7rci75$op$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <7rci75$op$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>, "Frank Swarbrick"
<infocat@sprynet.com> writes:

>
>So are you saying that the first line of a printed report should be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Whatever you do, don't mix the use of BEFORE ADVANCING and AFTER ADVANCING.   I
ran into a bugger of a problem trying to figure out why all 
my reports were coming out double spaced.  I had searched all of my program
code and everything looked to be using BEFORE.   Much later in the debugging
and code review, I found a single copy member that used an AFTER.
This one occurrence that was exercised twice in the entire run segment
was enough to cause the output to become double spaced.
Don't have a real good explanation of the why, just the when I matched all the
line/page advancement lines, the double spacing went away.

BTW, This was with MVS 5.2 and COBOL\370.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Really wierd problem!!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rgckv$du1$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7rci75$op$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <19990911113152.20930.00002421@ngol03.aol.com>`

```
The real problem with all this is defaults. Consider:
    write print-line.
    ? where am I?

Presumably, I am at the start of a new line, and the preceding line ends
with a <CR,LF>. What I really want is:

    move header to print-line.
    write print-line
        (after page AND before 1).

What I usually code is:

    move header to print-line.
    write print-line before page.
    move space to print-line.
    write print-line.

That will generally get things back into synch.


Sff5ky wrote in message <19990911113152.20930.00002421@ngol03.aol.com>...
>In article <7rci75$op$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>, "Frank Swarbrick"
><infocat@sprynet.com> writes:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

I
>ran into a bugger of a problem trying to figure out why all

debugging
>and code review, I found a single copy member that used an AFTER.

>was enough to cause the output to become double spaced.
>Don't have a real good explanation of the why, just the when I matched all
the
>line/page advancement lines, the double spacing went away.
>
>BTW, This was with MVS 5.2 and COBOL\370.

## Thread: Call for Papers: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2000) — 4 msgs

### Call for Papers: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2000)

- **From:** pazpax@clark.net (Peter Zuckerman)
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<pazpax-0909992006080001@pazpax-ppp.clark.net>`

```
      
                 CALL FOR PAPERS and PRESENTATIONS
                               for the

            17th International Conference and Exposition on

                  TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000)

           Theme: Testing Technology vs. Testers' Requirements
 
              June 12-16, 2000 * Washington, D.C. * USA
       (Conference: June 14-16; Workshops/Tutorials: June 12-13)

In cooperation with:

   Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) SIGSoft
   American Society for Quality (ASQ) Software Division
   IEEE Reliability Society
   Software Technology Support Center (STSC)

  You are invited to submit proposals for the TCS2000 Conference!

We are soliciting contributions for technical papers, tutorials and
workshops. The Conference will cover:

* The impact of increasingly complex software on testing
* Ways to improve the testing process     
* Practical experience with the various techniques     
* Which efforts have been most successful     
* Which efforts have been least successful

* Planned tracks:
  * Management
  * Technical
  * Automation
  * Advanced Topics (Refereed papers)
  * Case Studies

While presentations that directly relate to the theme are encouraged,
other topics relevant to improving the testing process are invited and
will be accepted.
                           
Submission guidelines are available at http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/

Downloadable/printable brochure is available at 
http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/tcs2000callpdf.html

GUIDELINES FOR REGULAR PAPERS:
http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/tcs2000callreg.html
Important dates:
Presentation proposals: October 15, 1999
   Notice of selection: December 15, 1999
    Materials deadline: March 15, 2000

GUIDELINES FOR REFEREED PAPERS:
http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/tcs2000callref.html
Important dates:
Presentation proposals: October 15, 1999
   Notice of selection: December 15, 1999
    Materials deadline: March 15, 2000

We are interested in hearing from you. If you want to submit a proposal
for a presentation, or explore an idea for a presentation, please contact
the Program Chair, Genevieve Houston-Ludlam, by:

       Phone: 410-266-8244; E-mail: ginger@fron-tech.com
     (preferred submission method, ascii text only please)

For refereed papers (Advanced Topics) please contact Roger Alexander, by
 E-mail: alexande@software.org; Phone: 703-742-8877; Fax: 703-742-7350

For more information on TCS2000 visit our web site at:

http://www.uspdi.org/conference/ or contact:

U.S. Professional Development Institute
E-mail: admin@uspdi.org; Phone: 301-270-1033; Fax: 301-270-1040

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

         One complimentary Conference registration is provided
                   for each accepted presentation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call for Papers: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2000)

- **From:** jvn@node14.unix.Virginia.EDU (Julian V. Noble)
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<7rbbah$oci$1@murdoch.acc.Virginia.EDU>`
- **References:** `<pazpax-0909992006080001@pazpax-ppp.clark.net>`

```
Someone should represent the Forth community here. Maybe Paul Bennet(t)
(1 t or 2?) who is concerned with safety issues.

To me the notion of "Testing Forth Code" is a sort of oxymoron, most of
the time--I test as I go, so the code works when it's done. Even long
assembler definitions can be done this way, as my paper in some future
FD (Marlin, are you there? I haven't received the double issue yet, and
I haven't heard from you about my paper either...) "A Call to Assembly"
will indicate.

A .ps version of said paper is available in .ZIPped form on my PHYS 551
web page

	http://Landau1.phys.virginia.edu/classes/551/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Call for Papers: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2000)

- **From:** peb@amleth.demon.co.uk ("Paul E. Bennett")
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<937000953snz@amleth.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<pazpax-0909992006080001@pazpax-ppp.clark.net> <7rbbah$oci$1@murdoch.acc.Virginia.EDU>`

```
In article <7rbbah$oci$1@murdoch.acc.Virginia.EDU>
           jvn@node14.unix.Virginia.EDU "Julian V. Noble" writes:

> Someone should represent the Forth community here. Maybe Paul Bennet(t)
> (1 t or 2?) who is concerned with safety issues.

If someone was paying I'd be happy to submit a paper and attend. However,
at present it is not in my budget to make the trip.
 
> To me the notion of "Testing Forth Code" is a sort of oxymoron, most of
> the time--I test as I go, so the code works when it's done. 

There is testing and testing. Naturally I would expect all programmers to 
test the functionality of their own code as they produce it. However, some
languages do not facilitate this as easily as Forth does. In my own book,
the inspection and testing that follows the submission of the code for 
inclusion in the product is where the trust in certification is built up.
Such testing also includes limits checks where a deliberate attempt at 
breaking the code is made. You should find the paper on the EuroForth 
site covers some of the basics and the form in there and in FD from a 
couple of years back will help keep track of the testing and certification 
effort.

> Even long
> assembler definitions can be done this way, as my paper in some future
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>         http://Landau1.phys.virginia.edu/classes/551/

My method works for Assembly within Forth as well. It could probably apply
to similar languages to Forth. Just one word of caution though. Ensure your
versioning and configuration management systems are well up to scratch so 
that you can be certain exactly what you have tested.
```

## Thread: Relevance — 9 msgs

### Relevance

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
I'm studying at the Chubb Institute in NYC.  We're still on an old curriculum
which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this.  Shouldn't I be
embarking on OO methodologies?

Will there ever be a growth market for converting legacy systems to OO ones?

TIA
RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: Relevance

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990909230447.01469.00000348@ng-cc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
Fifteen years ago, everbody told me to learn ADA, because COBOL was a dying
art.  Luckily, I didn't listen to them.

My prediction is that IBM is going to use Sun to eat Microsoft for lunch,
(having used MS to crush Tandem DEC and Wang) then pick their teeth with the
bones of Sun. 

So learn AS400 COBOL, and you'll probably be able to retire on it.

Another Data Point: I talked to a body snatcher last week who was looking for
people to convert online critical-mission systems from 'C' and OO C++ to COBOL.
She said her client was fed up with poor performance and unreliability of the
'C' and C++ code.  Client was early adopter of OOP methods....  

(By the way, I'm a Tandem specialist, and I don't particularly like The Armonk
Monster. But it's starting to look like a Blue World again...sigh.)





>I'm studying at the Chubb Institute in NYC.  We're still on an old curriculum
>which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this.  Shouldn't I
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

```

>which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this.  Shouldn't I be

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Relevance

- **From:** dnalor@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rapan$4al$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com> <37d87d40.2628824@news1.ibm.net>`

```

> >which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this.
Shouldn't I be
> >embarking on OO methodologies?
> >
> >Will there ever be a growth market for converting legacy systems to
OO ones?
> >
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is wrong to say "Great! Cobol ist it!"
It is wrong to say "Great! C++ is it!" too.
There are special languages for special problems.
So, go and learn at least C, it is a good base for using any procedural
language, and for some special things u need it anyway, even if u are
just using COBOL (e.g.: addressing dialog-managers, using interfaces to
UNIX-Services, and such things)

best regards,
Roland.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Relevance

- **From:** Headquarters <tf022724@psinet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37D8F520.8C4449DE@psinet.com>`
- **References:** `<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
I wondered the same thing in '92, when I started College.  Today, I find myself
sitting in a COBOL shop, collecting pretty good paychecks, thanking that curriculum
for teaching what many considered "outdated" curriculum.  While you should keep
abreast of those OO methodologies, it won't hurt to learn COBOL, either.  If
nothing else, it gives you a simple and easy way to learn the basics of programming
(not that I'm suggesting you need them) and a good, solid, ESTABLISHED programming
language to put on your resume'.

RoadRaat wrote:

> I'm studying at the Chubb Institute in NYC.  We're still on an old curriculum
> which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this.  Shouldn't I be
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: Relevance

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01befbd2$e7d432a0$cefbabc3@default>`
- **References:** `<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```


RoadRaat <roadraat@aol.com> wrote in article
<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com>...
> I'm studying at the Chubb Institute in NYC.  We're still on an old
curriculum
> which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this. 
Shouldn't I be
> embarking on OO methodologies?
> 
> Will there ever be a growth market for converting legacy systems to OO
ones?
> 
> TIA
> RoadRaat

If Cobol is your first language then you need to learn just Cobol, you can
then progress to OO Cobol, then learn C++ if you wish.
Also remember that not many shops use OO Cobol atm as it is reletivity new,
but there is a much more of a call for C++ as to just C.

As the old saying goes, "dont run before you can walk!"

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

#### ↳ Re: Relevance

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dwBC3.224$kL2.24128@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```

RoadRaat <roadraat@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990909225016.27481.00007034@ng-cr1.aol.com...
> I'm studying at the Chubb Institute in NYC.  We're still on an old
curriculum
> which teaches COBOL, but I wonder why I really need to do this.
Shouldn't I be
> embarking on OO methodologies?
>
1. It may be they are teaching "Programming," not COBOL. They
are just using COBOL as a means to an end.
2. It is now fairly common to convert legacy C++ and JAVA stuff
to COBOL.
3. We, who have been around a while, have seen them come
(and some go), languages, buzzwords, techniques. COBOL
abides.
```

<a id="t-66a4829486"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL UNDER SUN UNIX - 2 GIG FILE LIMIT — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL UNDER SUN UNIX - 2 GIG FILE LIMIT

- **From:** "Lou de Freitas" <Luiz.deFreitas@dol.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d907b6.0@news.dol.net>`

```
I would like to know what others have done to run MF cobol programs that
deal with files larger than the Sun-Unix 2 gig file size limit.

We have created called I/O sub programs that are executed in place of read
and write and these handle breaking up a file into parts that are each less
than two gig.

When files needs to be sorted each must be less than 500 meg or the files
will not be sorted correctly due to the sortwork file seeming to required
4-5 x the size of the input file.

Any comments would be great!

Lou d.
```

<a id="t-e4ea9b5742"></a>
## Thread: Truncatation of trailing spaces — 2 msgs

### Truncatation of trailing spaces

- **From:** Pam <pam.pollockNOkxSPAM@usa.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<02daf294.58c93b75@usw-ex0108-059.remarq.com>`

```
I have several programs which read in a variable length 
records and output fixed length records. My input and 
output files are accessed as line sequential. Several 
months ago, I had problems creating a fixed length record 
from variable length input. After researching it, I found 
the following solution. I created an external config file 
which set "stripspace off", and set the environment 
variable COBSW +L2. It may not be correct but it solved my 
problem. Until now... Now, I am combining (via a COPY 
command) several variable length files, executing a program 
that has the same above settings. The output is correct on 
my PC, but on any other PC, the output is still variable 
length. I have checked the PC environment variables, and 
have even deleted my project, recreated it without any 
settings (COBSW and external config file) and tried setting 
theses via a .BAT file. I still get the same results on my 
PC... a fixed length record. But I can not get the same 
results on any other PC.

Any ideas?

thanks
Pam

```

## Thread: Despate help needed with MF COBOL & Win98 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Despate help needed with MF COBOL & Win98

- **From:** "Richard Dixon" <rdixon@nildram.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37d95c2a@glitch.nildram.co.uk>`

```
I am using Microfocus Personal COBOL Student Edition (for DOS not Windows).
I was previously running MF COBOL under Windows 95 without any problems. I
have now upgraded to Windows 98 and cannot get it to run at all. I have
tried it in MS-DOS Prompt and properly in MS-DOS without success. I get the
message :- This Program tried to execute an invalid instruction .
Fault Location FFFF:3318
Interrupts In Service None.
I do not believe that this is a memory problem and I have I checked
CONFIG.SYS and AUTOEXEC.BAT which both seem OK. I have contacted the makers
of my new computer and they seem to think that everything is OK with DOS (of
course they would!). The MS-DOS is version WINDOWS 98 (4.10.1998).
I have also downloaded the fix from the Merant web site and used this
without success.

Does anyone have any ideas on how to get this working??/

If you can help would you mind e-mailing me direct rdixon@nildam.co.uk


Thanks in advance for any help
```

#### ↳ Re: Despate help needed with MF COBOL & Win98

- **From:** "TPF Moderator" <tpf_moderator@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rdsdb$mi7$1@linuxfr.org>`
- **References:** `<37d95c2a@glitch.nildram.co.uk>`

```
Howdy

You much check to see if the app is certified for Win98. Some bad code was
written to port 3.11 onto Win95 but Win98 ties the notch one more time and
maybe your version has tryied to access a deprecated API that returns NULL
thus causing the AV you described.

Regards
Andre

<a id="t-f923deb5a5"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Training needed - HELP! — 5 msgs

### Cobol Training needed - HELP!

- **From:** "r1michael" <r1michael@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O$pCSVJ$#GA.312@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
Hi My name is Rus. My employer is wanting me to learn COBOL as that is the
only language on our mainframe. None of the colleges in our area offer a
COBOL course. Well one does but it doesn't look like they will be offering
it until late next year, and that is a maybe. Anyway my other alternative is
a self train course on the web or something. My supervisor is the only
programmer at the office. Anyway, I need help finding a good course. If
anyone knows of any, I would greatly appreciate it if you could pass some
info to me. I need this info fast before they start thinking of hiring
someone. This is about the only way for me to move up and I want this bad.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Training needed - HELP!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37db0cf0.170510038@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<O$pCSVJ$#GA.312@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
On Sat, 11 Sep 1999 16:37:05 -0400, "r1michael"
<r1michael@email.msn.com> wrote:

>Hi My name is Rus. My employer is wanting me to learn COBOL as that is the
>only language on our mainframe. None of the colleges in our area offer a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>someone. This is about the only way for me to move up and I want this bad.

I'd like to recommend my book, Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours,
as a great intorductory text.... but I might not!

Tell us a little more about your mainframe?  What type?  Sperry?  IBM?
What Operating system and Version?

My book is a VERY GOOD book for teaching "modern" COBOL - which some
older systems, and small shops (sounds like yours is small) may not
have.  There are actually several things you need to learn - JCL - How
the mainframe works, the development environment on the mainframe, and
COBOL - I think the members of this group can come up with some good
ideas for you if we can get a few more details.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Training needed - HELP!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DB2E90.65C7902F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<O$pCSVJ$#GA.312@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
r1michael wrote:
> 
> Hi My name is Rus. My employer is wanting me to learn COBOL as that is the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

A lot depends on your approach to studying. Are you the sort of person
who is happier listening to an instructor in a class, or (like me), do
you pick stuff up by reading the theory and following through on the
examples.

If in the first category then you're stuck if COBOL isn't being taught
locally. Whether you do an online/correspondence course or read text
books amounts to the same exercise - you are self-pacing yourself.

For on-line - try objectz.com - I believe they offer some training.

For self-teaching texts :-

- Thane Hubbell's 'COBOL in 24 hours;' (that's before Thane makes his
sales pitch <G>)

- COBOL in 21 Days

- COBOL Unleashed

Whichever approach you take, having gone through the course or
material/texts - get your hands on other people's coding to get
examples. Go to Google.com and check sites using "COBOL". The name of
the game is 'steal' other peoples coding.

Good luck

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: un-COMP-ing a file — 5 msgs

### un-COMP-ing a file

- **From:** "Vickie Williams" <vickiew@accesscable.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rdhhj$r5m$1@nntp3.uunet.ca>`

```
I have a file full of COMP and non-COMP data. I just want to convert it to
all ascii for readability. I tried taking the original record layout as
input-rec, and stripped out the COMPs to create an output-rec. Then I read
with the input-rec and wrote with the output-rec from the input-rec. No
luck. Then I read with the input-rec, moved it to the output-rec, and wrote
the output-rec. Still no luck. In both cases, I got identical copies of the
original file. So, instead of
JOHN SMITH&^%%!*
I want
JOHN SMITH5551212090999
Needless to say, COBOL is not my native language...
Thanks for any help.
```

>

Ken

#### ↳ Re: un-COMP-ing a file

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37da8caa.137666619@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 11 Sep 1999 09:18:21 -0300, "Vickie Williams"
<vickiew@accesscable.net> wrote:

>I have a file full of COMP and non-COMP data. I just want to convert it to
>all ascii for readability. I tried taking the original record layout as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>original file. So, instead of

You can move the individual fields, field by field, or just make sure
all the names are the same except the 01 level, and do a 

Move Corresponding Old-Record to New-Record
```

#### ↳ Re: un-COMP-ing a file

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EF33BD439435526.52F838CA1162B779.68CE4C81C7460858@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7rdhhj$r5m$1@nntp3.uunet.ca>`

```
On Sat, 11 Sep 1999 09:18:21 -0300, "Vickie Williams"
<vickiew@accesscable.net> enlightened us:

>I have a file full of COMP and non-COMP data. I just want to convert it to
>all ascii for readability. I tried taking the original record layout as
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are on the right track.  But instead of moving the input record to
the output record, move each field of the input record to the
corresponding field in the output record.  That will allow Cobol to do
the data conversion.  Write your output record after all of the moves
are done.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I started out with nothing, I still have most of it.



 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: un-COMP-ing a file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7reah0$r79@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rdhhj$r5m$1@nntp3.uunet.ca> <3EF33BD439435526.52F838CA1162B779.68CE4C81C7460858@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Besides the (3 or 4) recommendations to use "MOVE CORRESPONDING" (or its
equivalent) ALSO think about defining your output numeric fields with "SIGN
IS LEADING SEPARATE".  This will probably help with what you are trying to
accomplish.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Expert systems

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01befc15$ac488860$01e8abc3@default>`
- **References:** `<01befbcf$397dcb60$cefbabc3@default> <37da8d01.137754294@news1.ibm.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<37da8d01.137754294@news1.ibm.net>...
> On 11 Sep 1999 12:59:16 GMT, "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >btw: I have built one and works great(very complex) and I have been
asked
> >to write another.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Expert System means a sort of AI solution where the program is smart
it produces complex results through simple answers from the user.

Described at a fundimental level, the system i developed writes script 
code(machine code) for an engineering plant
to produce 'parts' from a machine, all is required from the user is simple
information
and the program writes the code.

Like Prolog when rules are set, my Cobol solution works kinda like this
there are many paths in which the code will execute ==>(Prolog Rules)

Has anyone else done this type of work?

If you would like to know more post the request here.


Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

wrote:

>
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
