```
On the simplest level I did this as a "report generator" where it was
a point a shoot kind of thing, but I would not call it an expert
system.  I would like to discuss the subject further here though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Expert systems

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DB38B8.409E41CD@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<01befbcf$397dcb60$cefbabc3@default> <37da8d01.137754294@news1.ibm.net> <01befc15$ac488860$01e8abc3@default> <37db0db6.170708342@news1.ibm.net>`

```

The thing I find interesting is that an information system is
basically a tree of questions with intersecting branches.  The first
few are fairly standard point and shoot but you may go down a branch
and find that the likely cause is eliminated and the state of the
tests has totally shifted the percentages of the next test taking into
account the tests that have already been performed.  i.e.:  The next
test must eliminate the largest number of branches.

Was this actually done?

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Expert systems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01befd56$5477ba60$235cac3e@default>`
- **References:** `<01befbcf$397dcb60$cefbabc3@default> <37da8d01.137754294@news1.ibm.net> <01befc15$ac488860$01e8abc3@default> <37db0db6.170708342@news1.ibm.net> <37DB38B8.409E41CD@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in article
<37DB38B8.409E41CD@zip.com.au>...
> 
> The thing I find interesting is that an information system is
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

In a nutshell Yes, but once you have defined what the problem or user
is trying to achieve(coded) you then perform the processing and
calculation's
required.  There are millions of totaly different results in this case
2 files code file and information code file written to the server.

The idea or objective of the program is for the program to calculate
the code so there are no human interaction required aprt from which 
CAD/CAM drawing product they wish to produce.
 
I have included some code below, at this stage in the program(path)
we know which drawing,size,type,amount the user requires
this is a test based on data held in a conf file what needs to be written
to the code file.


       5mm-full-plus-half-repo-1.

           
           *> reposition procedure for channels that need
           *> to be repostioned full + %<whatever> times, based on
           *> the percentage of the total length in relation to
           *> that originX value.

          Move ws-5-round to g90-valuet
          perform varying Loop-CNT from 0 by 1 until Loop-cnt = 2
           if loop-CNT = 0
              move ws-chanhole-width to g90-valuey
                                        xyvaluey
           else
              move ws-outside-hole-dim to g90-valuey
                                          xyvaluey
           end-if

           move ws-initial5hole to g90-valuex
           move ws-5-round to g90-valuet
           invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using  g90-n-cnt g90
           move ws-g90 to outputtap-record
             write outputtap-record
           move zeros to g90-valuex, g90-valuey, g90-valuet
                                      *>400   = 250
           if Loop-CNT = 0
              subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-num-bend-a giving
              ws-punchable-a
           end-if
           if ws-punchable-a > ws-punchable-area
           *>first bend is greater than the origin will allow.
              Subtract ws-both-45 from ws-punchable-area
              giving ws-punchable-area2
              Subtract ws-punchable-area2 from ws-punchable-a
              giving ws-first-a
              if ws-first-a > ws-originxvalue
                 Add ws-chanpitchvalue to ws-both-45 giving
                 g90-valuex
                 if Loop-CNT = 0
                    Subtract ws-chanpitchvalue from ws-first-a
                 end-if
                 Invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using g90-n-cnt g90
                 Move ws-g90 to outputtap-record
                  write outputtap-record

                 *>test again to seee if we get more holes.
                 if ws-first-a > ws-chanpitchvalue
                 Compute ws-calc-k =
                         ( ws-first-a / ws-chanpitchvalue )
                 Move ws-calc-k to g282-valuek
                 Move 0.00 to g282-valuej
                 Move ws-chanpitchvalue to g282-valuei
                 Invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using g282-n-cnt g282
                 Move ws-g282 to outputtap-record
                  write outputtap-record
                  Move zeros to g282-valuei,
                                g282-valuek,
                 end-if
                 if Loop-CNT = 0
                    Subtract ws-first-a from ws-chan-num-bend-a
                 end-if
              end-if
              else

           if ws-punchable-a > ws-chanpitchvalue


              add ws-chanpitchvalue to ws-both-45 giving xyvaluex
              Invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using xy-n-cnt xy
              Move ws-xy to outputtap-record
               write outputtap-record
               Move zeros to xyvaluex
              if Loop-CNT = 0
                subtract ws-chanpitchvalue from ws-punchable-a
              end-if
              compute ws-sum =
                      ( ws-punchable-a + ws-chanpitchvalue ) +
                      ( ws-both-45 )
              if ws-sum > ws-originxvalue
                 if Loop-CNT = 0
                     Subtract ws-originxvalue from ws-punchable-a
                     giving ws-punchable-a2 *> <-means first origin area
                     subtract ws-punchable-a2 from ws-punchable-a
                 end-if
                 compute ws-calc-k =
                         ( ws-punchable-a2 / ws-chanpitchvalue )
                         move ws-calc-k to g282-valuek
                         move ws-chanpitchvalue to g282-valuei
                         move 0.00 to g282-valuej
                         invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using  g282-n-cnt
                                                           g282
                         move ws-g282 to outputtap-record
                          write outputtap-record


              else
                *>then the bend value is less than originX, so we
                *>can put all holes in this end
                 compute ws-calc-k =
                         ( ws-punchable-a / ws-chanpitchvalue )
                         move ws-calc-k to g282-valuek
                         move ws-chanpitchvalue to g282-valuei
                         move 0.00 to g282-valuej
                         invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using  g282-n-cnt
                                                           g282
                         move ws-g282 to outputtap-record
                          write outputtap-record
                         Move zeros to ws-repo-pos-A
                         if Loop-CNT = 0
                            subtract ws-chan-num-bend-a from
                                     ws-chan-num-length giving
                                     ws-chan-num-bend-b
                            subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-num-bend-b
                         end-if
                         Move zeros to ws-calc-k
                         compute ws-calc-k =
                             ( ws-chan-num-bend-b / ws-chanpitchvalue )
                         multiply ws-calc-k by ws-chanpitchvalue
                         giving ws-sum
                         if Loop-CNT = 0
                            compute ws-sum2 =
                                    ( ws-sum + ws-both-45 )
                            subtract ws-sum2 from ws-chan-num-length
                            giving ws-first-b-hole
                         end-if

      *--------------->this is to signal that we have finished
      *--------------->processing bend 'A'
                         Move zeros to ws-chan-num-bend-A

                         if ws-first-b-hole <= ws-punchable-range
                            move ws-first-b-hole to xvaluex
                            invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using x-n-cnt
                                                             xx
                            move ws-x to outputtap-record
                             write outputtap-record
                             move zeros to xvaluex

                            Move zeros to ws-left
                            subtract ws-first-b-hole from
                            ws-punchable-range giving ws-left

                            *>we test to see if we can get more holes
                            *>in the channel from this origin.
                            if ws-left > ws-chanpitchvalue
                               add ws-chanpitchvalue to ws-first-b-hole
                               giving xvaluex
                               invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using x-n-cnt
                                                                xx
                               move ws-x to outputtap-record
                                write outputtap-record
                               move zeros to xvaluex
                               move zeros to ws-calc-k
                               subtract ws-chanpitchvalue from ws-left
                               if ws-left > ws-chanpitchvalue
                                  compute ws-calc-k =
                                       ( ws-left / ws-chanpitchvalue )
                                       move ws-calc-k to g282-valuek
                                       move 0.00 to g282-valuej
                                       move ws-chanpitchvalue to
                                                            g282-valuei
                               end-if
                               *>calculate what punchable area is left
                               *>in the "B" section.
                               subtract ws-first-b-hole from
                               ws-chan-num-length
                               giving ws-repo-pos-B
                               subtract ws-both-45 from ws-repo-pos-B
                               if ws-calc-k <> zero
                                  compute ws-sum =
                                    ( ws-calc-k * ws-chanpitchvalue ) +
                                    ws-chanpitchvalue
                                  subtract ws-sum from ws-repo-pos-B
                               end-if
                            else
                               *>then only one extra hole is processed
                               *>in the B dimension.
                               subtract ws-first-b-hole from
                                         ws-chan-num-length giving
                                         ws-repo-pos-B
                               subtract ws-both-45 from ws-repo-pos-B
      *                        move 1 to Loop-CNT
                            end-if
                         else
      *                     Then the first hole in the extra flange
      *                     is greater than the originX value.

      *                     if Loop-CNT = 0
      *                       subtract ws-chan-num-bend-a from
      *                       ws-chan-num-length
      *                                  giving ws-chan-num-bend-b
      *                       move ws-chan-num-bend-b to ws-chan-left
      *                       subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-left
      *                     end-if
                            continue

                         end-if
           else
      *    No extra 5.0mm holes can be processed in the "A" dim.

           *>work out all holes backwards here:
           if Loop-CNT = 0
              subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-num-bend-b
           end-if
           compute ws-calc-k =
               ( ws-chan-num-bend-b / ws-chanpitchvalue )
           if Loop-CNT = 0
              multiply ws-calc-k by ws-chanpitchvalue
              giving ws-sum
              compute ws-sum2 =
                      ( ws-sum + ws-both-45 )
              subtract ws-sum2 from ws-chan-num-length giving
              ws-first-b-hole
           end-if
           if ws-first-b-hole < ws-punchable-range
              move ws-first-b-hole to xvaluex
              invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using x-n-cnt
                                               xx
              move ws-x to outputtap-record
               write outputtap-record
               move zeros to xvaluex

              subtract ws-first-b-hole from
              ws-punchable-range giving ws-left

              *>we test to see if we can get more holes
              *>in the channel from this origin.
              if ws-left > ws-chanpitchvalue
                 add ws-chanpitchvalue to ws-first-b-hole
                 giving xvaluex
                 invoke self "ComputeLnCNT" using x-n-cnt
                                                  xx
                 move ws-x to outputtap-record
                  write outputtap-record
                 move zeros to xvaluex
                 move zeros to ws-calc-k
                 subtract ws-chanpitchvalue from ws-left
                 if ws-left > ws-chanpitchvalue
                    compute ws-calc-k =
                         ( ws-left / ws-chanpitchvalue )
                         move ws-calc-k to g282-valuek
                         move 0.00 to g282-valuej
                         move ws-chanpitchvalue to
                                              g282-valuei
                 end-if
                 *>calculate what punchable area is left
                 *>in the "B" section.
                 subtract ws-first-b-hole from
                 ws-chan-num-length giving ws-chan-left
                 subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-left
                 if ws-calc-k <> zero
                    compute ws-sum =
                      ( ws-calc-k * ws-chanpitchvalue ) +
                      ws-chanpitchvalue
                    subtract ws-sum from ws-chan-left
                 end-if
      *          Move 1 to Loop-CNT

              else
                 *>then only one extra hole is processed
                 *>in the B dimension.
                 subtract ws-first-b-hole from
                           ws-chan-num-length giving
                           ws-chan-left
                 subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-left
              end-if
           else
      *       Then the first hole in the extra flange
      *       is greater than the originX value.
             if Loop-CNT = 0
              subtract ws-chan-num-bend-a from
              ws-chan-num-length
                         giving ws-chan-num-bend-b
              move ws-chan-num-bend-b to ws-chan-left
              subtract ws-both-45 from ws-chan-left
             end-if
           end-if
           end-perform

           *>set counter back to zero
           move 0 to Loop-CNT


Hence Expert systems
Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Expert systems

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DD30A0.A908A11D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<01befbcf$397dcb60$cefbabc3@default> <37da8d01.137754294@news1.ibm.net> <01befc15$ac488860$01e8abc3@default> <37db0db6.170708342@news1.ibm.net> <37DB38B8.409E41CD@zip.com.au> <01befd56$5477ba60$235cac3e@default>`

```
Simon R Hart wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in article
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the code file.

Simon,

Haven't studied your code in depth but it seems you've already drafted 
part of a message I was going to send to Pete Dashwood, (remember his
"Who cares ?"). His concept of computer-generated scripts can largely be
done using the OO technique you have shown - although I suspect he will
opt for another labngauge. 

Just one thing I'd like to see, (taking it beyond where you have already
gone)  - programs that generate additional new classes/methods as
required. No big deal to generate the source for the classes/methods -
but how do we get the system to automatically compile these new modules,
without exiting the application (???????).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Message if record is kept locked too long — 5 msgs

### Message if record is kept locked too long

- **From:** Vaclav Snajdr <snajdr.vaclav@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DB4BA5.5C87EB69@t-online.de>`

```
Situation:  one user keeps one record locked
                    the another users who need to work with the same
record, receive no access (Status 9D)
                    after some minutes they are going to be angry and
call the system administrator ...

Questin:    is possible to set one parameter to receive one message if
one record is kept longer
                   than the setted time period in the parameter   e.g. 5
minutes. ?
                   the message should be content the filename, the
primary key, the proces number,
                   the user device name (tty)

The MF-Cobol is used

Thanks for every answer

Vaclav
```

#### ↳ Re: Message if record is kept locked too long

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37dba74e.210034125@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37DB4BA5.5C87EB69@t-online.de>`

```
On Sun, 12 Sep 1999 08:43:49 +0200, Vaclav Snajdr
<snajdr.vaclav@t-online.de> wrote:

>Situation:  one user keeps one record locked
>                    the another users who need to work with the same
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Message if record is kept locked too long

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WeQC3.6344$ZR2.65755@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37DB4BA5.5C87EB69@t-online.de>`

```
The long-term solution, of course, is to change the program which is locking
the record for this inordinate amount of time. A single program should not,
for ordinary maintenance (add/change/delete), require a lock for more than
the time it takes to read the record into some working-storage area, then
immediately free, delete or rewrite it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fixed -Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rge5d$eqp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net> <Yk8C3.125$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37da9191.355700@news.indigo.ie> <YxqC3.15437$ei1.31103@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4KMC3.16029$ei1.31353@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Okay, here is the skinny, and why it worked.

The data that you are really writing is a stream of bytes consisting of four
types of byte.  You have printable bytes (letters spaces, etc.), plus three
distinct control characters ... CR, LF, and FF.

A carriage return <CR>(0Ah) causes the printer to start a line at the left
edge, but does NOT advance to a new line.  Used by itself at the end of a
line, it will cause the printer to overprint the next line. In Cobol you can
generally get it by using the "PRINT AFTER ZERO" statement. It can be used,
for example, for underlining or hashing out a password.

The <LF>(0Dh) will cause to printer to advance to a new line, but NOT
restart at the rightmost edge. The next line will start at the column that
the previous one ended at.

In general, a line sequential file printed without ADVANCING clauses will
terminate each line with a <CR,LF> pair.  This tends to be the same format
followed by standard programming editors, which makes the file format a nice
one to use when you want to see your output on the screen, or check it with
an editor.

To get to the top of a new page, however, you have to insert a <FF>
character. The <FF> character causes a printer to go to the top of the next
page, or with a laser, causes the page to be printed and ejected. (You can,
BTW, <HOME> the cursor on most lasers and overprint the entire page, but
that is a separate topic <G>).

Where do you insert it?  If you insert it AFTER the last line, then the last
line of the previous page will not have a <CR,LF> at the end, and this often
causes problems with a printer. For example, you might end up with your
header starting in column 80, because that is where the print head was after
the last line of the previous page was printed.

Printing it BEFORE printing the header leaves the header with no <CR,LF> at
its end.  Now you have problems with the second line of the header being
concatenated with the first line.

The solution which you found works quite well. Put the <FF> on a line by
itself.

Judy Shepherd wrote in message
<4KMC3.16029$ei1.31353@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>...

>As suggested I tried the 'before advancing' clause in a few places in the
>program and unfortunately it caused more problems than it solved....
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for all the suggestions....much appreciated....just wish that many
of
>them had not gone straight over my head!!.
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-0fbcfccc41"></a>
## Thread: COBOL v Other Languages — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL v Other Languages

- **From:** hancock4@bbs.cpcn.com (Lisa or Jeff)
- **Date:** 1999-09-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rglnb$pdk@netaxs.com>`

```
Per the posts of "TPF"...he is missing several important facts:

1) He suggested converting all the report programs to something else
   (IIRC it was "Cristal"?)

Sure, it's a lot easier to use a report generator to put out a simple
report rather than coding all the headers, page breaks and the like by
hand in COBOL.  Indeed, many mainframe shops have been using generators
(ie Easytrieve, SAS, DYL280, FOCUS) for years to do just that. 

But the big problem is that each generator is unique and not
static.  Years ago our organization had to change its Easytrieve
to Easytrieve Plus.  Our sister organization had to go from DYL260
to DYL280.  COBOL programs could be left alone.  Now that our
organizations are merged, management would like to save on license
fees and use only one generator.  But that would mean either all
the DYL280 programs or all the Easytreive programs would have to
be converted; plus all the staff retrained.  Very expensive and
time consuming.


Another big problem is that Client Server oriented products are far
from static and upgrades require staff retraining and rollouts.
My organization was suffering through a hodgepodge of PCs running
DOS 3.3 to Windows 97.  They spent a fortune in overtime to upgrade
everyone to Windows 95/97 even though the older 286/386s were doing just
fine where they were.  (A lot of people used them merely as a dumb
3270 terminal with simple word processing and their old 286 worked
just fine.)  Indeed, just as some of us got used to Windows 3.1 they
stuck Win 95 and now their's Win 97.

Likewise, we got used to tools like Word 6.0 and now there's Word 97
(with its %%#@!+*&^!! dancing paper clip).

It must be remembered that techno-geeks ENJOY upgrading their systems
and learning new products.  The magazine writers make their living off
of doing this so naturally they encourage it.  But for those of us in
the real world, supporter our clients who our serving our customers,
it is a big expense of time and money and inconvenience.



2) Staff experience:  Experienced COBOL programmers have been
around long enough now that they know how to write clear, concise,
and efficient code, and quickly identify bugs when they occur.
Our COBOL based applications today are extremely reliable and
machine efficient, processing thousands of online transactions
and millions of batch transactions.

In contrast, Client Server is new to everybody.  Systems crash--
is it the individual's PC, network, "switch", server, or what?
People have no experience and are figuring it all out.  This is
extremely costly to an organization in terms of both customer
dissatisfaction and employee time.

Training a large organization is costly too.  Not only taking
people away from their regular jobs, but buying a course/instructor,
and most importantly, the learning curve.



Being the "first on your block to own the newest..." is a bad 
strategy in data processing.


This of course is not to say we should frozen in place.  Of course
not.  But new products should roll out slowly AFTER they've been proven.

Many companies seem to want to put their most critical business
application on Client Server, without enough experience first.
I know of one outfit that tried to scrap their RPG S/36 with
Sun/Oracle and the new system is nowhere near ready to go up.
Every test fails miserably.  They just keep finding new problems.
(Now consultant-client finger pointing has begun.)
Since the S/36 isn't Y2k compatible, they're now making plans
(at considerable cost and inconvenience) to fake out the S/36
so it can keep it running.


Years ago, when IBM introduced its System/360, it knew it had to
provide an easy upgrade for existing users of 1401 and 7090 gear
and built in hardware emulation.  Until a few years ago, I knew of
shops STILL using a few 1401 Autocoder programs!  (I wonder if any
still are?)  The point is emulation allowed users to convert at
THEIR OWN PACE.

System/360, while a tremendous improvement over 1401/7090, had its
share of growing pains in its early years and had a long learning
curve.  Some users wisely took their time, retaining older systems
they knew well and were reliable and gradually evolved into COBOL
and online processing.
```

## Thread: Occurs depending on over linkage — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Occurs depending on over linkage

- **From:** kittu234@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We have a problem where we pass enormous amount of data back and forth
bertween CICS cobol applications and AIX based C++ applications which
Communicate using LU 6.2

Now 'all' the requests that emanate from C++ go through a set of Cobol
programs and then ultimately get directed to the actual Cobol program
that performs the requested task.

The problem is that the amount of data received/sent from  AIX to CICS
Cobol applications and back is Subjective, could be 20 bytes, could be
3 MB.

I want to define a field with 'occurs depending on' and pass only the
exact amount data to the programs down the call chain through linkage
by defining something like:
05  A-FIELD         PIC 9(9).
05 DATA-BYTE OCCURS  0 TO 3000000 TIMES DEPENDING ON  A-FIELD PIC X(01).

and setting the A-FIELD to the exact value of data being passed .

But  Cobol IBM documentation  seemed to say that when ever the OCCURS
DEPENDING ON  variables are passing over to another program thru
LINKAGE, the 'maximum length' (in this case 3 MB) is passed to the
called program( both with CMPR2 and NOCMPR2 compiler options) and not
that amount as is defined in the DEPENDING ON field!!.

  Is this true??

 If it is true is there any way for me to define storage dynamically???
At any point I want only that much memory defined as is required by the
program to be able to run maximum number of concurrent tasks in the
region.

 any help is greatly appreciated.
thank you.




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Occurs depending on over linkage

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rho0i$pcr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  kittu234@my-deja.com wrote:
> We have a problem where we pass enormous amount of data back and forth
> bertween CICS cobol applications and AIX based C++ applications which
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 05 DATA-BYTE OCCURS  0 TO 3000000 TIMES DEPENDING ON  A-FIELD PIC X
(01).
>
> and setting the A-FIELD to the exact value of data being passed .
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>  If it is true is there any way for me to define storage
dynamically???
> At any point I want only that much memory defined as is required by
the
> program to be able to run maximum number of concurrent tasks in the
> region.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Instead of using an OCCURS DEPENDING ON, why not just use A-FIELD as
you maximum, setting it to the actual data-length of A-DATA.

Assuming that you can pass two parameters, A-FIELD and A-DATA (for
example), define A-DATA as the maximum allowable in LINKAGE, which is
16MB-1. However, use A-FIELD as the LENGTH (as in reference
modification).

All subsequent programs must pass and use A-FIELD as the maximum
length. If you don't address A-DATA beyond the value found in A-FIELD,
you won't get an S0C4. By defining A-DATA as a maximum of 16777215, you
should never have to change the picture clause at all. The key is to
ALWAYS address A-DATA using reference modification.

Remember, when data is passed in LINKAGE, it is merely a series of
fullword addresses off R1. In your case, it will consist of two
addresses, one for A-LENGTH and one for A-DATA.

Basically, you've built your own 'DEPENDING ON' this way. If you think
about it, when passing a parameter to a BATCH program from the JCL,
you're doing the same thing as well.

Just my .02 cents....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Occurs depending on over linkage

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37dc6fb5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
My comments are as follows:

1.LINKAGE occupies NO storage at all. (It corresponds to an Assembler
DSECT). In effect it simply allows the storage of the CALLING program to be
addressed by the CALLED program. (Unlike C, which copies the passed
parameters to the called program, unless you pass a pointer. How many C
programmers have perished on that particular rock...?)

2. OCCURS DEPENDING ON is best avoided...If you MUST use it, ensure you set
the length into the target field, BEFORE you do the MOVE...(on some
platforms the effect is devastating if you don't do this...)

3. The 3MB area needs to be defined in the WORKING-STORAGE of the CALLING
(Main) program and in the LINKAGE  of the CALLED program. This raises
another problem in that the compiler will probably grizzle about a field
that large... (I can think of several ways to get round this, but they are
all inelegant, so I'll leave you to solve it yourself if the need
arises...:-) )

4. The answer to your question "Is this true?" is "Yes". There is no
standard COBOL construct which allows dynamic memory allocation. Most
Mainframers call an Assembler routine which issues a "GETMAIN" and similar
for "FREEMAIN", or write this stuff in PL/1 which DOES allow dynamic memory
allocation. This is not a criticism of COBOL; just a statement of fact.
Most of the time there is very little need (In commercial data processing)
for dynamic allocation of memory. You have come up with a possible one.

I believe you can solve your problem by ensuring that the Main program has a
3MB area and that the program which actually shovels it down the comm line
(the one which makes the LU 6.2 calls) is called by this program and has
addressability to its storage (via LINKAGE). You can then ensure that the
address and length of the message buffer (as required by LU 6.2) is provided
from the linkage section of the called program. It is not so silly for COBOL
to require an area equal to the maximum possible size; the message buffer
would need to be this big anyway... Of course your real concern is that you
are not actually TRANSMITTING 3MB each time. Provided you set the length of
the LU 6.2 message buffer, you won't be.

Pete.


kittu234@my-deja.com wrote in message <7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>We have a problem where we pass enormous amount of data back and forth
>bertween CICS cobol applications and AIX based C++ applications which
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
Some comments "interspersed"
```

- **From:** kittu234@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rir2l$h8u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37dc6fb5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
Your comment #3, says that I need to define the 3 MB in working storage
statically, which is basically what we do not want to do, because when
we do that we are essentially allocating that much memory to tasks that
use 20 bytes and also to tasks that use 3 MB.
It is a lot of memory wastage during the execution time of the former
which we are trying  to avoid. Is a there a way ( some one was talking
of hyperspace?? heard that??) to dynamically define storage in cobol
and pass it over in linkage??

thanks.
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37DCF932.E54D1864@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37dc6fb5@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7rir2l$h8u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
kittu234@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Your comment #3, says that I need to define the 3 MB in working
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


As bill said get main will do the trick of allocation.

In answer to your second part if you tell the program to grab the
linkage section variable the receiving program may act as if it has 3
meg but you are only allocating 20 bytes only a reference pointer is
passed.  The problem with this is if you make a programming mistake
and reference outside the allocated size in either program.

With the receive and transmit portion yes there might be a serious
issue here.  I can only guess at how this is achieved but typically
this would use the comarea between your program and the driver so you
actually know the size of the data being passed from the comarea
length.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs depending on over linkage

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rj2it$mup$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37dc6fb5@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7rir2l$h8u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7rir2l$h8u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  kittu234@my-deja.com wrote:
> Your comment #3, says that I need to define the 3 MB in working
storage
> statically, which is basically what we do not want to do, because when
> we do that we are essentially allocating that much memory to tasks
that
> use 20 bytes and also to tasks that use 3 MB.
> It is a lot of memory wastage during the execution time of the former
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In you're running AMODE 31, RMODE ANY together with COBOL2 or greater,
a 3MB WS Area should not be a huge overhead. If you were running the
old OS/VS COBOL, then I could see your cause for alarm. In COBOL2 or
greater, WS is essentially Dynamically Allocated from EDSA. EDSA is
normally around 2GIG for most CICS/ESA regions nowadays. Not knowing
your program flow, if the Front-End program that contains the potential
3MB WS area could be defined as a maximum 3MB in LINKAGE (as an option)
and you issue a GETMAIN on the length passed over (IE. A-LENGTH), then
you could pass A-LENGTH and the ADDRESS of the GETMAINED area in the
commarea downstream. However, note that passing of addresses could be a
potential problem if you decide to run a SYSPLEX environment and one of
the downstream modules is accessed via DPL.

Your PPT and PCT parameters regarding the allocation of data, namely
DATALOCATION and TASKDATALOC (respectively) must be set to ANY and the
GETMAIN must use the FLENGTH (Fullword Length) parameter to ensure 31-
Bit Storage Allocation.

If you're LU6.2 conversations are CONVERSATIONAL, then it would be best
to issue a FREEMAIN before your next RECEIVE (I'm assuming CICS is the
Back-End). If the sessions are dynamically allocated via an ALLOCATE,
then it doesn't matter.

As far as hyperspace, this would require all addressing of this area in
what's called 'AR' (Access Register) Mode and it must be done in
ASSEMBLER. Although I have not personally called an 'AR' mode ASSEMBLER
Sub-Program from CICS (and I don't know if it's possible), there may be
issues regarding STORAGE PROTECTION that would need to be addressed by
your SYSPROG. In any case, COBOL could NOT address any of this
hyperspace storage.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs depending on over linkage

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37de1e4c.33875430@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37dc6fb5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 1999 04:29:56 +0100, "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

>My comments are as follows:
and mine are intertwined with yours <g>

>
>1.LINKAGE occupies NO storage at all.
agreed
>
>2. OCCURS DEPENDING ON is best avoided
Why?  I have had no serious problems with ODO, even with socalled Complex ODOs
>
>3. The 3MB area needs to be defined in the WORKING-STORAGE of the CALLING
>(Main) program and in the LINKAGE  of the CALLED program. This raises
>another problem in that the compiler will probably grizzle about a field
>that large

Not sure:  The limits on the Visual Age COBOL for NT and OS/2 is 16MB for an elementary
item, none specified for group items
>>(I can think of several ways to get round this, but they are
>all inelegant, so I'll leave you to solve it yourself if the need
>arises...:-) )
That's what I use to tell my students when I am to lazy to spell out the solution for
them...but then, the next day, I present them a printed version of a real solution
nevertheless

>4. The answer to your question "Is this true?" is "Yes". There is no
>standard COBOL construct which allows dynamic memory allocation.

Except that the question was specifically for CICS, and CICS does have an EXEC CICS
GETMAIN  command to acquire storeage
> Most Mainframers call an Assembler routine which issues a "GETMAIN" and similar
>for "FREEMAIN", or write this stuff in PL/1 which DOES allow dynamic memory
>allocation.
Except, again, that an assembler GETMAIN is definitely a good idea running under CICS.
While CICS GETMAINs are automatically freed at task end, MVS GETMAINs are not, and the
CICS region is going to run out of virtual storage, with dire consequences to CICS
availability statistics

       |\      _,,,---,,_
 ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,    Some people know
      |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'  the price of everything,
     '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)       but the value of nothing

With kind Regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Occurs depending on over linkage

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ri7lv$fp7@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<kittu234@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7rhkre$n9p$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
  <much snippage>
>
> But  Cobol IBM documentation  seemed to say that when ever the OCCURS
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Is this true??

Yes, this is true (and the ANSI/ISO Standard *requires* it).   "Normally" in
a CICS environment when you want to pass a "dynamically altered size" amount
of storage, you would do a "EXEC CICS GETMAIN" and pass a pointer and a
length to a "receiving" program.  HOWEVER, I don't think this will work
across operating systems.

If none of the current CICS programmers in this NG come up with a "good
solution", I'll contact some of my IBM sources and see if they have a
suggestion.
```

<a id="t-967bf62846"></a>
## Thread: Data Validation — 2 msgs

### Data Validation

- **From:** ajandkris@aol.com (Ajandkris)
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990912225801.01554.00001107@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
I would like to know how to code a 60 day old check form the original date? 
Which is in MM/DD/YYYY format.  Please help!!!!!!!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Data Validation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ri6gt$f10@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990912225801.01554.00001107@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Assuming you have a "current" COBOL compiler (with Intrinsic Functions)

A) Move your input field to a YYYYMMDD field
B) Convert this date via FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE
C) Subtract 60 from that result
D) Convert this integer via FUNCTION DATE-OF-INTEGER

(Some of those steps can be "combined" in a single COMPUTE statement)
```

<a id="t-6a24ab2a84"></a>
## Thread: 60 day check — 3 msgs

### 60 day check

- **From:** ajandkris@aol.com (Ajandkris)
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990913162705.23620.00007671@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Help...
Earlier I sent a question regarding a 60 day check for data validation and
someone suggested that I use the Intrisic Function on the compiler that I am
working on, but this is my first Cobol mainframe class and I am a bit clueless
of where to start, but eager to learn.  I check with my Professor but she was
really no assistance.  Would someone please give me some desperatley needed
direction- that would greatly be appreciated.

     Format: mm-in pic 9(02), dd-in pic9(02), yy-in pic  9(04).                
                             I also have a WS setup for the current date.      
                            Thanks,                             
Arhtur
```

#### ↳ Re: 60 day check

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7rjoiq$s7u@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990913162705.23620.00007671@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Ajandkris <ajandkris@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990913162705.23620.00007671@ng-fx1.aol.com...
> Help...
> Earlier I sent a question regarding a 60 day check for data validation and
> someone suggested that I use the Intrisic Function on the compiler that I
am
> working on, but this is my first Cobol mainframe class and I am a bit
clueless
> of where to start, but eager to learn.  I check with my Professor but she
was
> really no assistance.  Would someone please give me some desperatley
needed
> direction- that would greatly be appreciated.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Arhtur

OK,  I'll do this homework assignment (because I have meant to code such an
example for the FAQ anyway).   As I understand the assignment, you are
supposed to verify that an "input date" is no more than 60 days "old".  (If
this isn't the exact assignment - then you will need to adjust the code
accordingly.)

NOTE WELL: Some code is omitted and some code could be combined, re-ordered,
or modified for "performance" reasons.  This also assumes that the "input
date" has already been validated to be a "good date" and as a date after
1600.

     ;****

 Working-Storage Section.
  01  Input-Date.
        05  MM-IN   Pic 9(02).
        05  DD-IN    Pic 9(02).
        05  Y4-IN     Pic 9(04).

  01  WS-Current-Date.
        05   Y4-CD   Pic 9(04).
        05    MM-CD Pic 9(02).
        05    DD-CD  Pic 9(02).

  01  Temp-Integer-fields.
        05   INT-Inp-DATE   Pic 9(10).
        05   INT-Cur-DATE  Pic 9(10).

  01  Reformatted-Input-Date.
        05  Y4-IN     Pic 9(04).
        05  MM-IN    Pic 9(02).
        05  DD-IN     Pic 9(02).
   01 Num-Input-Date redefines Reformatted-Input-Date
                             Pic 9(08).
                 ***
  Procedure Division.
 *   Do in initialization code
         Move Function Current-Date (1:8) to WS-Current-Date
         Compute INT-Cur-date = Function Integer-of-Date (
                                                      Function Numval
(WS-Current-Date)
                                                                            
                   )
          End-Compute
              ***
 *  Do in each validation loop
 *    Some programmers HATE "MOVE CORR" - but this is a reasonable place to
use it
 *        IMHO
        Move Corr Input-Date to Reformatted-Input-Date
        Compute INT-Inp-date = Function Integer-of-Date (Num-Input-Date)
        End-Compute
        If Int-Inp-Date  > Int-Cur-Date
                Display "Whatever happens when Input date is bigger than
today"
        Else
               If (Int-Inp-Date + 60) >= Int-Cur-Date
                     Display "Whatever happens when input date is OK"
               Else
                     Display "Whatever happens when input date is too old"
               End-IF
       End-IF
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 60 day check

- **From:** ajandkris@aol.com (Ajandkris)
- **Date:** 1999-09-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990913170848.23623.00007281@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7rjoiq$s7u@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Thanks so much, I really appreciate the help.
Thanks,
Arthur
```

<a id="t-496bbdf7d1"></a>
## Thread: I have a question: How can this work? — 7 msgs

### I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** "john smith" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
New to COBOL
I know this MUST happen everyday, but I'm lost as to an approach.
I designing a program that will take a file that has batches of records in
it.
There is 2 header records with each batch they help to define whats in the
batch, there is no trailer, I guess the 2nd header acts like a trailer.
Each Header has 80 bytes. the difference is the first three bytes are
either:
    '$NE' 1st header or '777' 2nd Header
The record types in the batch are all of the same type.
Each batch can have a different record type with a piecing of the record in.
    Here are the types with the length which IS the 1st 3 bytes.
             Type                 Lng   3-Bytes
          SpecialRec-Hdr  111  '111'
          SpecialRec-Dtl    087 '087'
          SpecialRec-Tlr    077 '077'

          NormalRec1-Hdr 114 '114'
          NormalRec1-Trl   090 '090'

there are two more normal recs with header and trailer that use codes in the
header2 to differentiate them with exactly the same length and 1st three
bytes as NormalRec1, ie.  NormalRec2, NormalRec3.

I cant understand how to process a batch that I wouldnt know how big it
could get, so I dont want to use a table of letsay 10000 occurs.
That would really eat up memory I think.

When I think this through, maybe my ignorance of how COBOL does things will
show through.
I define the records -ALL as TYPE  PIC X(03) and FILLER PIC X(111) to fit
all in one place with the largest size.

I will open the file
read the first record.
Run a routine  to check the first three bytes.
that sets a high-value of some constant, HEADER1-Y if 1st 3 bytes is $NE
Move that to working storage defined with Header1 and all its details.

that sets a high-value of some constant HEADER2-y if 1st 3 bytes is 777
Move that to working storage defined with Header2 and all its details.

that sets a high-value of some constant SpecialRecHdr-y if 1st 3 bytes is
111
Move that to working storage defined with SpecialRecHdr and all its details.

that sets a high-value of some constant SpecialRecDtl-y if 1st 3 bytes is
087
Move that to working storage defined with SpecialRecDtl1 and all its
details.

that sets a high-value of some constant SpecialRecTrl-y if 1st 3 bytes is
077
Move that to working storage defined with SpecialRecTrl and all its details.

that sets a high-value of some constant NormalRecHdr-y if 1st 3 bytes is 114
check working storage of header 2 for magic byte that is normalRec 1,2,3
Move that to working storage defined with NormalRecHdr1 2 or 3 as
appropriate and all its details.

that sets a high-value of some constant NormalRecTrl-y if 1st 3 bytes is 090
check working storage of header 2 for magic byte that is normalRec 1,2,3
Move that to working storage defined with NormalRecTlr1 2 or 3 as
appropriate and all its details.


If none of the above move it to an error-file as is.

I think that should work, however I get really baffled when I must DO
something with this batch.  I cant seem to figure out when to write the
record to my output.  I dont want to write the batch unless the header info
validates what is in the batch.  It could have gotten botched up in
transmission, and merged two batches together or something ugly like that.

I have seen reports that say batch so and so is invalid, using sums and
things from a header and trailer and puts the whole thing out in a report,
and file so it can be fixed and included in the next cycle. That is a smart
thing.
I just wonder how to find the whole batch if i've already read it and wrote
it out and changed it , without keeping the whole thing in memory somewhere.

oh i am so confused.
I dont want huge amounts of memory and I dont want bad batches.
any advice.
Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** "gee" <grant_englebrecht@nospam.compuware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e6c062@199.186.16.51>`
- **References:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
what is the sequence the records appear in
if you only have
hdr1
hdr2
btch dtl
btch dtl
btch dtl
hdr1
hdr2
btch dtl
btch dtl
btch dtl

then there is no need to store any records.
you only need to store the info from the hdrs to verify batch totals
eg
hdr1-flag = n
hdr2-flag = n

read rec
perform until eof
   if hdr1 then hdr1-flag = y
   elseif hdr2 then hdr2-flag = y
     elseif btch-dtl then
          if hdr1-flag = 'y' and hdr2-flag = y then
            perform process-batch until next hdr or eof
            if hdr1 or hdr2
               verify totals for batch
            endif
            hdr1-flag = n
            hdr2-flag = n
       else
         say rec seq error
        stop
       endif
   endif
 endif

if not eof then
   read rec
endif

sub process-batch
 verify batch rec
 store info
  read rec.
endsub


john smith wrote in message ...
>New to COBOL
>I know this MUST happen everyday, but I'm lost as to an approach.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

in.

the
```

#### ↳ Re: I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s85uq$e2l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
In article <uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>,
  "john smith" <someone@microsoft.com> wrote:
> New to COBOL
> I know this MUST happen everyday, but I'm lost as to an approach.
> I designing a program that will take a file that has batches of
records in
> it.
> There is 2 header records with each batch they help to define whats
in the
> batch, there is no trailer, I guess the 2nd header acts like a
trailer.

snipped for space reasons

I think I understand the problem - try something like this:
The key strategy is a double-pass of the input file.

A. Set up Working-Storage definitions for each type of record you might
encounter. Set up accumulators for detail-record numbers, etc. that
will be used to balance the batches.
B. Read Input file (File1).
   If record is a header, check to see if previous batch balanced.
        If balanced, write a record with the batch-number to a new
output file, File2. In any case clear accumulators, and start on the
next batch.
C. After all batches have been investigated, close File1 and File2.
Don't forget to investigate the last batch for balancing.
D. Open File1 and File2 for input. This starts them anew, from the top.
Open Output File3 (good batches) and Output File4 (rejects).
E. Now use File2 as a driver. Read a record from File2, then read File1
until you find the batch-number you got from File2.
If you encounter records for a different batch-number (on File1) write
them to File4 (the reject file). If you find records that match File2's
batch-number, write then to File3 (good records).

And so on. (Some details of the matching omitted above.)

John











Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sdv0n$ctv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
john smith <someone@microsoft.com> wrote:

: I think that should work, however I get really baffled when I must DO
: something with this batch.  I cant seem to figure out when to write the
: record to my output.  I dont want to write the batch unless the header info
: validates what is in the batch.  It could have gotten botched up in
: transmission, and merged two batches together or something ugly like that.

Just write out the batch records to a tempory file.  At start of
batch open output a relative or sequential file and write each
record.  At end of batch re open this input or open output to
delete the batch.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SMwG3.1949$2Q5.20628@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <7sdv0n$ctv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
>john smith <someone@microsoft.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>delete the batch.

I prefer another approach which is much faster; on the platforms I
have tried it on.  Open two sequential files input, both to the same
physical file.  Use one file to pass the batch for validation, then
use the other to fetch the batch for output.  Sequential can be much
faster than relative files for this type of operation; sometimes
many times as fast.  And always faster than reading, writing, then
reading again. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nHWH3.2252$j4.64545@viper>`
- **References:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
What I think you have here is what I think is called an EDI file (electronic
data interchange). What you typically have is a structure similar to a
report with several groupings:

report header
page header
column header
detail rec1
detail rec2
column trailer
page trailer
report trailer

And of course you have different names such as BATCH header and possibly
others.

What I have done when processing these types of files is to:
- define separate work areas for each record type, no redefinition
- define a generic WS group which has the max record length and the record
identifier, NOT the FD.
- define each record type as a conditional based on the record identifier of
the generic record
- 2000-read file
- 2100-check indicator
- 2200-move generic record to specific record type based on conditional
- 2300-do generic processing based on record type
- 2400-read file
- loop to 2100

The generic processing would set the flags you mentioned based on the record
type. To output the records you have already processed you could use 2
record identifiers pointing to the same DDNAME. Use one for validation and
the other for actual processing: Reading ahead with the validation pointer
to see if the batch is valid. Then use the generic pointer to do the
standard processing or exception processing based on the validation set by
the validation pointer.

If you have FILEAID using a copybook which contains the GENERIC record type
and all of the SPECIFIC rcord types. Then you can verify your record layouts
using the cross reference functions.

To think about it in another way, line up the record types left to right in
the HEADER1 HEADER2 HEADER3 DETAIL1 DETAIL2 DETAIL3 FOOTER3 FOOTER2 FOOTER1.
This composite record is typically what is fed into a COBOL program to
produce a CONTROL BREAK REPORT. In this case the CONTROL BREAK REPORT is
your data file.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I have a question: How can this work?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fW6I3.340$zc3.12434@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<uIPE3.54332$223.832212@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <nHWH3.2252$j4.64545@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote in message ...
>What I think you have here is what I think is called an EDI file
(electronic
>data interchange). What you typically have is a structure similar to a
>report with several groupings:


Um, I've been working with ANSI and EDIFACT EDI since 1983, and I still
don't buy that there is such a thing as a "generic EDI file." While EDIFACT
EDI "documents" do use 'groups' fairly liberally, ANSI EDI documents are
more often 'looped' than 'grouped.'


MCM
```

```
Hi out there.

I am sure that a lot of You do ftp against mainframes, I do as well.
But, there is a little problem I like to solve.

I am looking for a gui windows ftp client that can handle translate /
convert between the mainframe dataset name last part and the pc
extentions. Like this examples

Host PDS                                                  PC extention
tst1u.tu168.COB2SRC                              CBL
tst1u.tu168.COBCOPY                             CPY
etc....

Convertion so that the last part of the pds *COB2SRC* becomes the
extentions *CBL* in the pc directory, and so on.

I am sure a gui ftp client exists doing this, but I have not been able
to find them. Anyone know?


Thanks a lot.

Einar Clementz,  Oslo, Norway
email: eclementz@bigfoot.com
```

#### ↳ Re: ?FTP client against mainframe

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ySWH3.2258$j4.64826@viper>`
- **References:** `<37E3DE63.9C354C76@online.no>`

```
I was in a similar situation last year, but I simply wanted to be able to
process MVS file names through FTP. I was unable to find one, as you. So I
wrote my own. For lack of an actual programming language or real FTP client,
I used Microsoft Access VB and the FTP client which comes with Win9x.

What I did was use VB to create a BAT file to call FTP.EXE and route its FTP
directory listing to a text file. I then imported the text file into a MS
Access table. I then used the CLICK event to indicate which file I wanted to
download. Then simply use another BAT file to call FTP.EXE to download the
file. In your case you would then change the output filename based on the
last node of the file name. Further you could incorporate a file association
manually, or use EXPLORER and the windows built in associations established
by your applications to open the files with the appropriate application.
There is a way to use VB to access the registry to find which application is
associated to each extension and the application file path. But unless you
would have to talk to someone with more VB experience to figure that out.

The MS Access database I wrote is rather simple and not too big. I could
e-mail ot to you if you are interested.
```

---

<a id="t-0f46aebfd3"></a>
## Thread: Evaluate - The "definitive" example of what is different between '85 and draft (CD 1.6) COBOLs — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Evaluate - The "definitive" example of what is different between '85 and draft (CD 1.6) COBOLs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s18st$o9n@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Because of all the recent discussions (in comp.lang.cobol), I have created
(and tested) a sample COBOL program that demonstrates the ONLY (and QUITE
OBSCURE) difference between how the '85 Standard works and how the draft of
the next Standard works.

NOTE WELL:
  As discussed in previous parts of this discussion, it appears that most
(possibly ALL) current '85 compilers work in the "new" way and not according
to the "letter-of-the-law" of the '85 Standard.  Furthermore, I think that
MOST (possibly all) of the readers have indicated that they "like" the new
way rather than what the '85 Standard (erroneously?) required.  If, however,
you (for some really PERVERSE reason) have a need or desire to get a "fully
conforming" '85 Standard compiler (that will NOT work the same way in the
future), then you can use this sample code to contact the support group of
your compiler vendor.  (Although this is an issue with how the '85 Standard
defines "EVALUATE," the only time that it really shows up is with the
Intrinsic Function "RANDOM".  Therefore, if you have an '85 compiler without
intrinsic functions - e.g. VS COBOL II for IBM - I don't know of any way to
"legally" test this.)

So much for "preface" - code follows:

  Identification Division.
  Program-ID. EvalRand.
*  See "Substantive Change 27 in CD 1.6 of the latest draft of the next
COBOL
*  Standard to see what this tests and how it should work in the '85
Standard
*  versus how it should work in the next COBOL Standard.
  Data Division.
  Working-Storage Section.
  01  Num-Fields.
      05  Outside-Eval Pic S9V9(17).
      05  Test-Num  Pic S9V9(17) Value 1.
      05 Save-Num  Pic S9V9(17).
      05 Save-2nd-Random Pic S9V9(17).
      05 Save-4th-Random Pic S9V9(17).
  01 NumEd-Fields.
      05 Disp-Num1  Pic +9.9(17).
      05 Disp-Num2  Pic +9.9(17).
  Procedure Division.
      Perform Verify-Random-Order
      If Test-Num = Zero
          Perform Test-Evaluate
      End-If
      Stop Run
       .
  Verify-Random-Order.
      Compute Save-Num = Function Random (1)
      Compute Save-2nd-Random = Function Random
      Perform 2 times
         Compute Save-4th-Random = Function Random
      End-Perform
      Compute Save-4th-Random = Function Random
      If   (Save-Num Not = Save-2nd-Random)
       and (Save-Num Not = Save-4th-Random)
       and (Save-2nd-Random Not = Save-4th-Random)
          Display "Random works as expected"
          Move Zero to Test-Num
      Else
          Display "Random does NOT work as expected"
          Display "  The rest of this program won't work either"
          Display "Using another seed value MIGHT work - but I doubt it"
      End-If
       .
  Test-Evaluate.
      Compute Outside-Eval = Function Random (1)
      Move Outside-Eval to Save-Num
      Evaluate Test-Num
        When Zero
          Display "Get's correct EVALUATE response, but ..."
        When Function Random
          Display "Never"
        When Function Random
          Display "Never"
        When Other
          Display "Never"
        End-Evaluate
      Compute Outside-Eval = Function Random
      If Outside-Eval = Save-2nd-Random
          Display "Does not conform to ANSI'85 Standard - but to CD 1.6"
      Else
          If Outside-Eval = Save-4th-Random
              Display "Conforms completely to ANSI'85 Standard"
          Else
              Display "Doesn't conform to ANYTHING"
          End-IF
      End-IF
       .
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate - The "definitive" example of what is different between '85 and draft (CD 1.6) COBOLs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s5r27$sus@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7s18st$o9n@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" suggested the logic in the tiny program shown below
as a way to see if the IBM VS COBOL II ver. 4 compiler if '85
conforming. I'm willing to bet both Bills were surprised! This source
generated one IGY... warning (right before the Binder mao.
```

```
From one Bill to Another -
   (I think that this compiler message gives you a good clue of the answer,
but ...)

If you still want to test it without getting that message, change OPT to
NOOPT and it will leave the code in (and probably still not execute it).

FYI,
  If you didn't see it, Thane tried it with IBM COBOL for this-and-that
using FUNCTION RANDOM (not available with VS COBOL II) and did "prove" that
it was working according to the "new" (next Standard) rules and not to the
existing rules.
```

<a id="t-c90bc2fbe2"></a>
## Thread: Order of evaluating EVALUATE (wasRe: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Order of evaluating EVALUATE (wasRe: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s0c4m$rve@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <CnMD3.1757$eJ2.18573@news1.mia> <37dfa498@news3.prserv.net> <QnSD3.2465$eJ2.25322@news1.mia> <37e03c3a@news3.prserv.net> <37e03e80@news3.prserv.net> <7rq246$1em$11@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37e0e12d@news3.prserv.net> <37E0EF39.D8012591@zip.com.au> <37e18a86@news3.prserv.net> <7rsss3$kj0$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7ru7t7$4m2@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <7rvbsd$avk@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
OK - I'm cornfused here. Is (27) below the NEW rule or the OLD rule? 
Tell me it is the new rule, which would make sense. (It is a leadin 
to enumerated data types.) 

it is also the way most current EVALUATEs work. :) 

-Paul

William M. Klein (wmklein@nospam.netcom.com) wrote:
: OK,
:   I knew that I had seen this topic at J4.  I just found the following
: listed as a "Substantive changes potentially affecting existing programs" in
: CD 1.6 (the latest draft of the next Standard),

: "27) EVALUATE statement, sequence of execution. The sequence of evaluation
: of selection subjects and objects is now defined to be from left to right
: and selection objects are evaluated as each WHEN phrase is processed. When a
: WHEN phrase is selected, no more selection objects are evaluated.

: Justification:
: The previous COBOL standard did not define the sequence of evaluation of
: selection subjects and selection objects in an EVALUATE statement. It was as
: if they were evaluated all at once or in some undefined order. Also, the
: rules indicated that all selection objects were evaluated even if an earlier
: WHEN phrase was selected. The only place where the new rules would cause a
: difference is when a RANDOM function is specified in one of the selection
: objects that is no longer evaluated. In addition, some subscript bounds
: conditions or other errors might not occur with the current specifications.

: Because the new rules state that all selection subjects are evaluated at the
: start of execution of the EVALUATE statement, some subscript bounds
: conditions or other errors could occur during this evaluation that might not
: have occurred before.

: This change was made as a result of a request for an interpretation of the
: previous COBOL standard. It is believed that few, if any, programs will be
: affected by this change."

: --
: Bill Klein
:     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

#### ↳ Re: Order of evaluating EVALUATE (wasRe: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s0lcm$j04@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <CnMD3.1757$eJ2.18573@news1.mia> <37dfa498@news3.prserv.net> <QnSD3.2465$eJ2.25322@news1.mia> <37e03c3a@news3.prserv.net> <37e03e80@news3.prserv.net> <7rq246$1em$11@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37e0e12d@news3.prserv.net> <37E0EF39.D8012591@zip.com.au> <37e18a86@news3.prserv.net> <7rsss3$kj0$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7ru7t7$4m2@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <7rvbsd$avk@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <7s0c4m$rve@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr <paulr@bix.com> wrote in message news:7s0c4m$rve@lotho.delphi.com...
> OK - I'm cornfused here. Is (27) below the NEW rule or the OLD rule?
> Tell me it is the new rule, which would make sense. (It is a leadin
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sorry for the confusion.

It is the NEW (with the NEXT Standard) rule - with a "warning" that it
*MIGHT* impact some existing programs that followed the "letter of the law"
in the '85 Standard.

As others have pointed out, it is questionable that ANY existing
implementations actually do follow the '85 Standard rule.  Furthermore, if
you have such an implementation, then I think that you the user/customer
probably should contact the vendor and "request" that they provide you some
(currently NON-Standard - but soon to be STANDARD) option that works the
"new" way.

A way to test if your compiler works the "letter of the law" way (for Micro
Focus - for example) would be to run with +F (to get 163 run-time errors)
and have the following code:

01  Num-Fields.
   05   NF1  Pic 9.
   05   NF2  Pic 9.

    ....
move 3 to NF1
Move Spaces to Num-Fields (2:1)
Evaluate NF1
    When 3
       Display "Does not follow the 'letter of the law'"
    When NF2
       Display "You should get a 163 before getting here"
End-Evaluate
GoBack
..

If your program does the 1st Display and then ends successfully, your
compiler follows the NEW (not yet Standard) rule; if it gets a 163, then it
follows the strict '85 Standard rule.

One more example of how to test this (on an IBM mainframe compiler)

Set SSRANGE on (compile and run-time) and compile and run the following:

01  Num-Fields.
   05   NF1  Pic 9.
   05   Tabl occurs 3 times.
         10 Elem  Pic 9.
    ....
move 3 to NF1
Move Zero to Elem (3)
Evaluate NF1
    When 3
       Display "Does not follow the 'letter of the law'"
    When Elem (NF1 + 1)
       Display "You should get an SSRANGE error before getting here"
End-Evaluate
GoBack
..

If you get an SSRANGE error when you run the program, then IBM is following
the "letter of the law" - if not they are following the new rule.
```

```
I just wanted to say, that with the exception of the "attached" reply (which
I am WELL AWARE was intended as humor - and as such falls into another
category),  I have really appreciated all the explanations and responses
that I have gotten.

I hope that it hasn't "bothered" any of our "don't bother me with the
implementation" type C.L.C readers (or that they now know how to use the
"next" option of their NG-reader).

I am not positive that I have yet "internalized" it all (or really
understand the answer to such questions as how IBM implements recursion in
their latest on-line COBOLs; how RM does PERFORMs that aren't like IBM's or
stack-based; or whether IBM's PC/Unix COBOL is stack-based or not) - but I
do have several "saved" items to read the next time I try to understand this
all.
```

## Thread: Intrinsic Function 85 Standard (was:Date Validation (long)) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Intrinsic Function 85 Standard (was:Date Validation (long))

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s0kg8$iju@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990912225801.01554.00001107@ng-cf1.aol.com> <7ri6gt$f10@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37e03a9f.146828558@news.ntplx.net> <He6E3.27941$223.580463@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <7rr9ci$pbp@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37E1F693.667DA33E@IMN.nl> <KRvE3.34284$223.724628@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <37E29384.B12563BB@home.com> <37E33ABB.6A0ACA1C@att.net>`

```
William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote in message
news:37E33ABB.6A0ACA1C@att.net...
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
  <more snip>
>
> Bill (no relation) might be right, but his position is completely
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> options, e.g., initialize W-S or not.

Speaking of MVS (or more accurately OS/390) which is the area that I know
best ...
I am NOT saying that you should try to convince your company to upgrade
compilers every 6 months or so - but COBOL/370 (the 1st compiler with
Intrinsic Function Support) came out in the early 90's and I *do* think that
any shop that is still creating (and probably any that are maintaining)
COBOL code should be on one of the LE-enabled compilers by now.

In fact, IBM's Operating System support "policy" is that they are now ONLY
supporting the OS (where LE now lives) for 2 years.  Therefore, I think that
any shop that is NOT looking at a policy that lets them upgrade "safely" in
the 2 to 3 year range is asking for trouble.

P.S.  My comment about talking to the vendor was NOT aimed at IBM - where
each new compiler IS more efficient than the previous one.  However, there
have been reports (in this NG) about some of the '85 Standard compilers
having SIGNIFICANT overhead over the '74 compilers - for the same programs.
This is something that I think users should complain about.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7s0lt5$j7t@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <CnMD3.1757$eJ2.18573@news1.mia> <37dfa498@news3.prserv.net> <QnSD3.2465$eJ2.25322@news1.mia> <37e03c3a@news3.prserv.net> <37e03e80@news3.prserv.net> <7rq246$1em$11@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37e0e12d@news3.prserv.net> <37E0EF39.D8012591@zip.com.au> <37e18a86@news3.prserv.net> <37E34920.FA92BF20@att.net> <37e36c45@news3.prserv.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37e36c45@news3.prserv.net...
> so COBOL II was not conforming to the standard.
>

Leif,
  Actually with VS COBOL II, it WAS conforming - because (as the
"substantive change" text indicates in the next Standard), the ONLY
conforming source code that would ever have any different at run-time was
one which references the Intrinsic Fucntion "RANDOM" (because of
"side-effects" of each reference) and VS COBOL II doesn't support Intrinsic
Functions.  Although I have (in the other part of this thread) pointed out
ways to "test" various compilers using invalid data and subscript
violations, these really canNOT test whether a compiler is "conforming" or
not - because all of these "data problems" produce "unpredictable results"
in the '85 Standard - and anything that you do is OK.

THEREFORE, (with the VERY RARE case of someone who actually cares how far
along a RANDOM "chain" gets) there is NO WAY that you can call an '85
Standard "non-conforming" because it does the evaluation in the other
order - as the '85 Standard only tells you what results to get - not how to
get there.

HAVING SAID THAT,  I am glad that the next Standard will get rid of this
"problem" and would encourage people to get their current compiler vendors
to work the "new" way.
```

<a id="t-99ebda3012"></a>
## Thread: What does "stack based" mean? — 1 msgs

### Re: What does "stack based" mean?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s0pep$lmf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7rrb1p$q9b@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <OPgE3.5662$eJ2.59947@news1.mia> <7rs94r$ahe@lotho.delphi.com> <37e30a7d@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

: IBM claimed that their machine was better because it did fewer instructions
: and besides, they could actually "tailor" the instruction set by replacing
: mylar cards (ROS). Benchmarks were run against ICL 1900s which used a 6 bit
: "byte" (remember 24 bit registers above?) and a stack architecture. To IBM's

The 1900s did not use 'stack arhitecture', though the rest is right.

: consternation, the 1900s creamed them. Why? because although they were

Yes, that is true.  

: executing hundreds more instructions, they were executing them quicker. How
: did they do this? because they weren't carrying the overheads of a "COMPLEX"
: instruction set (like having to go off stepping through ROS before they
: could actually do anything.) Some of you will realise that this was where
: the idea of RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computing) originated.

The 1900 was quite a simple set of instructions and the architecture
catered for 'extracodes' where if it wasn't available as hardware
it was passed to Exec (the operating system) which could then do
the job using software.  This allowed the smaller machines to have
less hardware while still still being able to run programs
compiled for the larger machines.

The machines also used interrupt driven I-O which meant that the
CPU could carry on processing while peripherals used (what today
is called) DMA to access the buffers.  This gave a very efficient
multi-tasking.

I suppose in todays terms the 1900 was the Amiga and the 360/370/..
the Windows NT in that the 1900 was fast, efficient, multi-tasking
and dead.        ;-)

: Since then, huge advances in hardware have removed some of the negatives in
: both architectures and today we get machines of roughly equal power,
: notwithstanding their hardware architecture.
```
