<a id="t-f206a7a5d9"></a>
## Thread: Embedded Programming in COBOL (was: Re: What does "stack based" mean?) — 2 msgs

### Embedded Programming in COBOL (was: Re: What does "stack based" mean?)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s0r8u$27b9$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7rrb1p$q9b@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <OPgE3.5662$eJ2.59947@news1.mia> <7rs94r$ahe@lotho.delphi.com> <7rt1re$117t$1@news.hitter.net> <7ru7fg$pfu@lotho.delphi.com> <7ruejh$2igf$1@news.hitter.net> <7s0bn3$rve@lotho.delphi.com>`

```

paulr wrote in message <7s0bn3$rve@lotho.delphi.com>...
[...]
>The ATC system runs some (maybe most?) of it's non time critical
>software on COBOL based systems,  but the embedded systems are
>not COBOL; they are a mixture of various HOL and assembler.

What other HOLs? At the time the system was built, COBOL
was the only computer language approved for use by the
US government.

It seems you may be misinterpreting time critical as meaning
fast. In real-time programming, that is not the case. Different
parts of the system have different time requirements. One
routine may need to execute in about 5 ms; but the requirement
may be soft. Another routine may need to execute in 150 ms;
but the requirement may be hard. A COBOL routine might
meet the hard requirement of 150 ms by executing consistently
within 120 ms. An assembler routine might meet the soft 5ms
requirement by averaging less than 5 ms; but occasionally
using 5.5 ms. The routine with the hard real-time requirement
is the time critical routine despite its longer execution time.

I am not sure what you mean by "the embedded systems are
not COBOL." There is a mainframe computer connected to
radar, radio, displays, keyboards, etc. That computer is
dedicated to the task of controlling air traffic. It runs 24 hours
per day, 7 days per week, doing one thing. That computer
is embedded and the software running on that computer is
embedded even if it is written in COBOL.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: Embedded Programming in COBOL (was: Re: What does "stack based" mean?)

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s1fqm$j8s@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7rrb1p$q9b@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <OPgE3.5662$eJ2.59947@news1.mia> <7rs94r$ahe@lotho.delphi.com> <7rt1re$117t$1@news.hitter.net> <7ru7fg$pfu@lotho.delphi.com> <7ruejh$2igf$1@news.hitter.net> <7s0bn3$rve@lotho.delphi.com> <7s0r8u$27b9$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Sorry, I shoudl have been clearer. The computes running the RADAR
displays and such are not the IBM hunkers; they are embedded 
processors running a hodgepodge of things. Yes, they interface
to the 'mainframes' but they are running a real-time 
exec. 

The history of this is facinating, and probably inappropriate 
to post or write here. I worked for Systems Development Corp.
back in the 1980s, and they are the folks that actually built
and ran the first ATC's in the country. Listening to the old
codgers from Camarillo was a blast and a half. 

I believe, but won't swear to, some of the HOLs include Jovial 
and CMS-2x.  

-Paul


Rick Smith (ricksmith@aiservices.com) wrote:

: >not COBOL; they are a mixture of various HOL and assembler.

: It seems you may be misinterpreting time critical as meaning
: fast. In real-time programming, that is not the case. Different
: parts of the system have different time requirements. One
: routine may need to execute in about 5 ms; but the requirement
: may be soft. Another routine may need to execute in 150 ms;
: but the requirement may be hard. A COBOL routine might
: meet the hard requirement of 150 ms by executing consistently
: within 120 ms. An assembler routine might meet the soft 5ms
: requirement by averaging less than 5 ms; but occasionally
: using 5.5 ms. The routine with the hard real-time requirement
: is the time critical routine despite its longer execution time.

: I am not sure what you mean by "the embedded systems are
: not COBOL." There is a mainframe computer connected to
: radar, radio, displays, keyboards, etc. That computer is
```

```
    Bottom line - you have to pay attention.  I remember writing reports in
FORTRAN.  At that time we had to put the carriage control stuff in ourself.
And if we printed source code on the "other" Mainframes (Not IBM
compatable), we had to run a utility that shifted everyting over 1 char,
leaving a blank in col 01.  (They were control data machines).
```

```
    In addition to the problems that Bill Klein found, you seem to have at
least one go to out of a perform.  005-INPUT is a paragraph, not a section,
and not a thru range.  You should not go to out of a paragraph.  Perhaps it
used to be sectioned, but it does not seem to be sectioned now.

    This sort of thing can cause all sorts of odd behaviour.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux>`

```
"Right Justified" only impacts where the sending field is put in the
receiving field - not any "embedded" spaces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37e58f83@news3.prserv.net...
> JUST RIGHT must be THE most misunderstood construct in COBOL...
>

I thought I would start a new thread on this (semi-humorous) topic - and let
us all put in our nominations.

Personally, I can quickly think of:

 -  OCCURS DEPENDING ON in Working-Storage - "to save storage used"

 - CANCEL of a program has to free storage

 - USAGE COMP *has to be* the most efficient USAGE for numeric fields

 - (not really a feature) but WHY did ANSI/ISO take out the following
features when they changed from '74 to '85  (all from IBM mainframe
programmers)
    - Report-Writer
    - EXHIBIT NAMED
    - CALL USING file-name
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E63376.974A2FF3@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>     - CALL USING file-name

Or when I worked in a shop which had both ANSI 68 & ANSI 72 programs. 
Since both INSPECT & EXAMINE were being used to convert characters, I
thought I would simplify by using a command which was in both ANSI
standards, TRANSFORM.  Which went away in the next standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s5ppd$g1s@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37E63376.974A2FF3@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:37E63376.974A2FF3@NOSPAMhome.com...
> William M. Klein wrote:
   <snip>
> >
> >  - (not really a feature) but WHY did ANSI/ISO take out the following
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> standards, TRANSFORM.  Which went away in the next standard.

UNFORTUNATELY,

A) There never was a '72 Standard, but there was a '74 one
B) The '74 Standard did *not* include the TRANSFORM verb

I think you (like many IBM mainframers) are confusing LANGLVL(1) and
LANGLVL(2) with ANS'68 and ANS'74.  The relationship between those
variations is a bit complex (and I would need to start another thread to
explain it) but the bottom-line is that MANY features that IBM mainframe
programmers THINK were in the '74 Standard - weren't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s61nd$vpm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37E63376.974A2FF3@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
:>  - (not really a feature) but WHY did ANSI/ISO take out the following
:> features when they changed from '74 to '85  (all from IBM mainframe
:> programmers)
:>     - Report-Writer
:>     - EXHIBIT NAMED

ANSI/ISO never did take that out, it was never Cobol but was an IBM
extension.  I presume it was IBM that no longer included it when
they built a new compiler.

:>     - CALL USING file-name

Ditto.

Isn't is strange that IBM mainframers think that Cobol is defined
as 'what the compiler accepts'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ed9a50@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
There is NO doubt in my mind that the most misunderstood construct in COBOL
is the "CAME FROM" verb.

This was put forward at the last meeting of spaghetti standards, when the
boys realised that this new "structured" nonsense was going to prevent or
discourage the use of their favourite construct, "GO TO".

With the prospect of maintainable code (and the consequent diminution of job
security...) hanging over them, not to mention the demise of ALTER also on
the cards, the committee decided to get approval for "CAME FROM" as a "back
door" "GO TO"... It is buried in most compilers to this day but only the
most tenacious spaghetti coders can find it.

I reckon the second place goes to the "DONT DO THAT" clause, which can be
used both inside and outside complex PERFORM ...VARYING loops to ensure that
terminating conditions are ignored  and  processor cycles are consumed as
they were intended to be by the Great Hardware Vendor in the sky.

A tie for third place between the unconditional "SOMETIMES" clause (as in
"PERFORM this SOMETIMES") and the "STOP RUN" alternative, "HALT AND CATCH
FIRE".

Fourth place goes to the "SEARCH" enhancement clauses "HARD" and "REALLY
HARD", which are infinitely more efficient than the binary chop implemented
by "SEARCH ALL". ("SEARCH REALLY HARD" is implemented by a tertiary balanced
Fibonacci series, and is so dedicated that even when it finds what it is
looking for, it goes and does it again to be absolutely sure....)

Fifth on my list are the dyslexic arithmetic functions: DAD, SUBCART,
MILTIPLUY, DAVIDE, and COMPOST, with their exception detection "ON FULLUP".
Although they were intended to help a small minority of slightly disabled
programmers, they had to be withdrawn from the language because perfectly
normal programmers were using  them to get laughs and they were bringing
COBOL's reputation as a self-documenting language into disrepute.

Sixth would be the very rarely used "CLOSE DISK WITH REWIND". This is a
handy one when you need the afternoon off to attend a rock concert. It
causes an untraceable hardware error so the system is down for a few hours.
Of course, if it is overused they replace the hardware unit, so it is best
saved for times when Dire Straits, Oasis, or Pink Floyd are in town (Don't
waste it frivolously on Robbie Williams or Martine McClucheon...)

All of the above are very infrequently used because they are totally
misunderstood.

Pete.
```

```
peter dashwood wrote:
> 
> There is NO doubt in my mind that the most misunderstood construct in COBOL
> is the "CAME FROM" verb. <SNIP>

I wouldn't even attempt to match your humour, but here's one for real.
Was attending a disk course (not discourse !) given by ICL, I think it
was Disraeli's old home (Beaconsfield ?). We were split into teams to
design systems with as many uses for disks as possible - Money, Money,
Money - said the ICL salesmen gleefully.

Anyway, as we teams were discussing our designs, one guy calls out, "How
do you get the printer to do this ?". Some smart-ass from my team
replies, "Put in in reverse".

Jimmy, Calgary AB

> This was put forward at the last meeting of spaghetti standards, when the
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Now the CLOSE WITH REWIND is one that I have had problems with. In
particular, on the CYBER64, you need to issue one of those to make sure the
disk is rewound before you make use of a file for the second time.  The
machine has been obsolete for several years now,  so the number of people
looking for that particular command has lessened, but at one time there were
thousands of people wandering about trying to find that particular command.
```

```
>There is NO doubt in my mind that the most misunderstood construct in COBOL
>is the "CAME FROM" verb..[and others]...

You forgot the "DWIM" verb  - Do What I Mean.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37efa1c4.1251149@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37ed9a50@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <nzKH3.59$Cv4.5047@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 13:46:59 GMT, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>peter dashwood wrote in message <37ed9a50@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
>>There is NO doubt in my mind that the most misunderstood construct in COBOL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Wrong Newsgroup - DWIM is an ASSEMBLER instruction.  Also, it has been replaced a long
time ago by DMNS - Do what i Mean Not what i Say

The COBOL Equivalent of course is

	PERFORM WHAT I WANT  (with the optional phrase FOREVER)



     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         We are born naked, wet and hungry. Then things get worse.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kbLxN9SJXCR=u6uhTIqL9kbgQ6zT@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37ed9a50@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <nzKH3.59$Cv4.5047@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37efa1c4.1251149@news2.ibm.net>`

```
>>You forgot the "DWIM" verb  - Do What I Mean.

>Wrong Newsgroup - DWIM is an ASSEMBLER instruction.  Also, it has been replaced a long
>time ago by DMNS - Do what i Mean Not what i Say

Other operators in that family are DWMS (Do What i Meant to Say), DWSS
(Do What i Should have Said), DWSM (Do What i Should have Meant), DWMM
(Do What i Meant to Mean)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Most "misunderstood" construct in COBOL (was Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqd4p$g4q$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <937783000.970065@flux> <7s3tva$d6r@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e58f83@news3.prserv.net> <7s4im1$bt9@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com> <37ed9a50@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <nzKH3.59$Cv4.5047@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37efa1c4.1251149@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker, you may want to try the:

     PERFORM GENERAL-ONIPOTENCE-ROUTINE.

rather than the

     PERFORM WHAT-I-MEAN.

I have found it to me more transferable to other platforms, as well as being
aware of the changes to the standard regarding NEXT SENTENCE.  The
perform-what-i-mean, for example will quite happily
execute your periods, wheras the general-onipotence routine alters them to
end statements, recompiles, and thinks about it until the year 3000.  This
is much closer to the standards.

Volker Bandke wrote in message <37efa1c4.1251149@news2.ibm.net>...
>On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 13:46:59 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
>>peter dashwood wrote in message <37ed9a50@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
>>>There is NO doubt in my mind that the most misunderstood construct in
COBOL
>>>is the "CAME FROM" verb..[and others]...
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Wrong Newsgroup - DWIM is an ASSEMBLER instruction.  Also, it has been
replaced a long
>time ago by DMNS - Do what i Mean Not what i Say
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>         We are born naked, wet and hungry. Then things get worse.
```

<a id="t-4c4e38c179"></a>
## Thread: Opinion "Survey" - COBOL FAQ — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Opinion "Survey" - COBOL FAQ

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s3paq$p87@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
OK folks - it's time for an informal survey (which I may or may not pay
attention to):

The FAQ is STARTING to get "current."  (See the WEB version if you are
interested in what is there and/or what is new.)
     http://pweb.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

I have also "resurrected" my (COBOL) program to convert the HTML-ish version
to a relatively readable "text" version.

In the past, I put the *FULL* text version out in the "automated" FAQ
poster.  This meant that a fairly LONG (and often non-changing) news item
was posted to comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol every 15 days.

In most of the US, "reading in" this article into one's NG reader isn't too
bad (or expensive) - and anyone who isn't interested can just skip it or
"filter it out."  HOWEVER,  in some other places in the world (and possibly
with some ISPs in the US), there are "charges" for connect time and
downloading all of this "stuff".

I *could* just post (every 15 days) a "pointer" to the web version of the
FAQ.  HOWEVER, when I last tried this (and according to the reports of some
of the other FAQ readers), this is a problem for those who (still) don't
have access to the WEB - but do access the internet.  Furthermore, it means
that the latest text of the FAQ isn't "available" to those who browse the
Standard FAQ archives.

I am NOT interested in (or willing to) tell people that they can send me
private email to get the text version.  I get enough of these anyway - and
just do NOT want to worry about "timely replies" to this type of request.

  So ...

What do the NG readers think about this?  Should I keep posting the full
text version? or can I switch to just posting a "pointer" to the web
version?  If I did the latter, would it help any if I posted the full
table-of-contents and the "latest changes" sections? (but if I did this,
wouldn't I still get requests for the text version?)
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinion "Survey" - COBOL FAQ

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e56b29@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<7s3paq$p87@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote

As time goes by, more and more have access to the Web,
so I'll advocate the following compromise:

Post the URL every 2-4 weeks,
post the full text only if it has changed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinion "Survey" - COBOL FAQ

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990919213148.06333.00000064@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7s3paq$p87@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:

>
>What do the NG readers think about this?  Should I keep posting the full
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>-

I would think that the periodic newsgroup postings should be fine with
a pointer to the Web page.  What about an FTP site for the TXT version?
In my case, the newsgroup posting never made it thru anyway because
the file was too large and got chopped off at 2K bytes.
The idea of having a 'whats new' section in the semi-monthly posting
would be handy for knowing whether there is anything of particular interest.
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinion "Survey" - COBOL FAQ

- **From:** Steve Burns <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E646E7.3975EAAD@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<7s3paq$p87@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
You could do both...  That is, post a primary note with the url and a
message that says your next subsidiary note contains the text.  That way
people who don't want it don't get (as long as they bother to read your
message).  But people who do want to see the text version will be able
to get it easily.

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> OK folks - it's time for an informal survey (which I may or may not pay
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

#### ↳ Re: Opinion "Survey" - COBOL FAQ

- **From:** Paul Galbraith <pdg@interlog.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

```
For me personally, I always want to be able to connect the the newsgroup and
find the FAQ at any given time.  Since I have web access, that means that either
a periodic complete posting, or a periodic posting of the web reference are
fine.

For people without web access, however, I think it would be frustrating to have
to wait (potentially) several weeks for the next full posting of the FAQ.  So,
my suggestion is to continue making the full post.

Paul
```

<a id="t-d33b36ca16"></a>
## Thread: Student - Perform varying after problem — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student - Perform varying after problem

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf02d4$400b7c00$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<37e2a147.3528523@nntp.xsite.net>`

```
zennmaster <zenn@master.net> wrote in article
<37e2a147.3528523@nntp.xsite.net>...
> After two days of numerous variations I still can't find the correct
> logic to pull values using perform-varying-after statements and move
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 UNTIL SUB-P2 > 3.

One of your problems is probably that the period is terminating the perform
here.

>   ADD RATE (SUB-GENDER SUB-RISK SUB-POLICY) TO WS-PREM.
>   ADD WS-PREM TO WS-PREM-TOT.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>     MOVE INS-SUB (SUB-P1,SUB-P2) TO SUB-RISK.

Another problem is probably that you move the same value to all variables.

>                      
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I see no point in varying SUB-P2, as all MOVEs in 200-INS-SUB move the same
value, and you end up with INS-SUB(SUB-P1,3) in all variables.

You may be better off with an inline perform:

PERFORM VARYING SUB-P1 FROM 1 BY 1
  UNTIL SUB-P1 > NUM-INS-CODES-IN
ADD RATE (INS-SUB(SUB-P1,1)
             INS-SUB(SUB-P1,2)
             INS-SUB(SUB-P1,3) ) TO WS-PREM
ADD WS-PREM TO WS-PREM-TOT
MOVE WS-PREM TO PREM-OUT
WRITE SALES-REC FROM SALES-REPORT AFTER 1
END-PERFORM

Note: all periods removed.  Those are old-style programming.  You only need
periods (in procedure division) before and after labels.

Gunnar.
```

```
Rats, you got it already.....

IN-S
:
 05 INS-CODES-IN.
       10 INS-CODES OCCURS 5 TIMES.
                 15 INS-SUB OCCURS 3 TIMES       PIC 9.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
:
150-SALES-LOOP.
:
  PERFORM 200-INS-SUB VARYING SUB-P1 FROM 1 BY 1
        UNTIL SUB-P1 > NUM-INS-CODES-IN
         AFTER SUB-P2 FROM 1 BY 1
                UNTIL SUB-P2 > 3.
:
(This perform isn't exactly what I want...obviously I get the same
values)
200-INS-SUB.
    MOVE INS-SUB (SUB-P1,SUB-P2) TO SUB-GENDER.
    MOVE INS-SUB (SUB-P1,SUB-P2) TO SUB-POLICY.
    MOVE INS-SUB (SUB-P1,SUB-P2) TO SUB-RISK.
    ADD RATE (SUB-GENDER SUB-RISK SUB-POLICY) TO WS-PREM.
    ADD WS-PREM TO WS-PREM-TOT.
    MOVE WS-PREM TO PREM-OUT.
    WRITE SALES-REC FROM SALES-REPORT AFTER 1.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student - Perform varying after problem

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf048a$b7303110$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<37e2a147.3528523@nntp.xsite.net> <01bf02d4$400b7c00$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <7s8gq7$mor$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7s8vt4$kmo$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken Mullins <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote in article
<7s8vt4$kmo$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>...
> > Note: all periods removed.  Those are old-style programming.  You
> only need  periods (in procedure division) before and after labels.
> 
> This is a matter of opinion I think...personally, I think periods make
cobol
> more readable and help eliminate possibly falling thru to code in
> error...IMHO...

What style you choose to use is a matter of opinion, I'll give you that.
I still think the use of periods are old-style.  In the old days you didn't
have any choice.  Now the programs may have a better structure using
if/then/else/end-if and evaluate/when/end-evaluate.  The period enforces
multiple closure of all open ifs and make some program constructs very
difficult.  As an example, how would you implement the following construct
using period and no end-if (I know how to do it but it's not nice)?

if condition-1
   statement-1
   if condition-2
      statement-2
   end-if
   statement-3
end-if

Gunnar.
```

```
    At last - someone I agree with.  This was a LONG time ago, but I believe
that an old version of RMCOBOL (early 80's?) only updated certain program
counters (ie address of abend) at every period.  If you printed the compiler
listing, the hex address would be blank until it found a period.

    Of course that was a long time ago.
```

## Thread: Need help with COBOL CASE statement — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need help with COBOL CASE statement

- **From:** "Christie Sachetti" <sachetti@pathway.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11>`

```
I am new to COBOL.  Is there such a thing as a CASE statement in COBOL?  I
can't seem to get it to work.  My professor suggested using this instead of
a nested IF statement.

DO CASE
        CASE VARIABLE-IN <= 100
                MOVE 0 TO DISCOUNT.
        CASE VARIABLE-IN > 101 AND <= 500
                MOVE 10 TO DISCOUNT.
END CASE

FRUSTRATED STUDENT
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with COBOL CASE statement

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e4d716@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11>`

```
EVALUATE TRUE
     WHEN VARIABLE-IN NOT > 100
                  MOVE ...
     WHEN VARIABLE-IN NOT > 500
                  MOVE ...
END-EVALUATE

HAPPY STUDENT(?)
```

```
Christie Sachetti <sachetti@pathway.net> wrote:
: I am new to COBOL.  Is there such a thing as a CASE statement in COBOL?  I
: can't seem to get it to work.  My professor suggested using this instead of
: a nested IF statement.

: DO CASE
D
  EVALUATE TRUED
    
:         CASE VARIABLE-IN <= 100
:                 MOVE 0 TO DISCOUNT.
                                    ^ stop ends _all_ statements
          WHEN Ariable-In <= 100
                  MOVE ZERO  TO Discount    <<--- NO full stop

:         CASE VARIABLE-IN > 101 AND <= 500
:                 MOVE 10 TO DISCOUNT.
          WHEN ....

: END CASE

  END-EVALUATE

: FRUSTRATED STUDENT

RTFM
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with COBOL CASE statement

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e592f5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11>`

```
Christie,

There is NO "CASE" statement in COBOL.

The equivalent is "EVALUATE" as follows:
[stuff in square brackets are comments and mustn't be coded...]
...
Working-Storage Section.
        01  Variable-in      pic 999.
               88 Gets-nothing       value 1 thru 100.
               88 Gets-10-PC        value 101 thru 500.
...
Procedure Division.
...
            evaluate TRUE
                when gets-nothing
                     move zero to discount
                when gets-10-PC
                     move 10 to discount

[If Variable-in was only allowed to have values in the range 1 - 500, you
could validate this at the same time by using the WHEN...OTHER clause of
EVALUATE. Like this...

                when other
               [carry out exception notification]
]
            end-evaluate

[As the range of values you are interested in is suitable, and COBOL is
intended to be a self-documenting language, it makes sense to use 88 levels
as described above. If the ranges were not so convenient you could test
Variable-in directly with a WHEN clause (when Variable-in NOT > 100...etc.).

You are probably wondering why Variable-in is tested with WHEN instead of
with EVALUATE. Why not write "Evaluate Variable-in"...?

The answer is because, if you Evaluate a variable (dataname in COBOLspeak),
you cannot provide a relational operator (like >, = , etc.). You are only
allowed to provide tests for specific values of the variable. Let's say
Variable-in actually contains a "10" for 10%, a "20" for 20%, a zero if
there is to be no discount, and all other values are invalid. Now we can
evaluate the variable directly, like this...

           evaluate Variable-in
                 when zero
                         move zero to discount
                 when 10
                         move 10 to discount
                 when  20
                         move 20 to discount
                 when other
                 [Do the exception for invalid input...]
           end-evaluate

Summary:

Use "EVALUATE TRUE" when you want to test relations with a variable.
Use "EVALUATE dataname" when you want to test specific instances of the
variable.

Once you feel comfortable with all this, read the manual for the use of ALSO
which allows several variables to be included in the EVALUATE.

Pete.

Christie Sachetti wrote in message <7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11>...
>I am new to COBOL.  Is there such a thing as a CASE statement in COBOL?  I
>can't seem to get it to work.  My professor suggested using this instead of
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with COBOL CASE statement

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E5B7C7.3BB7CA1D@att.net>`
- **References:** `<7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11> <37e592f5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> You are probably wondering why Variable-in is tested with WHEN instead of
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>            end-evaluate
 
What Peter's discussing here is that COBOL insists that you evaluate &
test (WHEN) items of the same class. An 88-level is basically a
TRUE/FALSE situation, whereas ZERO, +989, etc., are not. So, if you want
to evaluate a data field you must code values for the field in the same
class. If you want to use 88-levels, start with EVALUATE TRUE... Note
that if you EVALUATE a data name, that's the only thing (for lack of a
better word) considered, whereas, if you code EVALUATE TRUE you can code
tests of various fields, 88-levels of different fields, etc.

I think Peter's mistaken when he states "You are only allowed to provide
tests for specific values of the variable". We may code WHEN's with the
THRU option, e.g.:

EVALUATE  VARIABLE-IN
WHEN  +10  THRU  +99
	imperative statement(s)
WHEN  ZERO
	something else
WHEN  +100
	...
WHEN  OTHER
	...
END-EVALUATE

Bill Lynch
```

- **References:** `<7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11> <37e592f5@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37E5B7C7.3BB7CA1D@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <37E5B7C7.3BB7CA1D@att.net>...

>
>I think Peter's mistaken when he states "You are only allowed to provide
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-EVALUATE


You are absolutely right, Bill. 

I was trying to keep it all very simple for someone new to it. 

Thanks for pointing out the bit I missed.

Pete.
```

```
>
> William Lynch wrote in message <37E5B7C7.3BB7CA1D@att.net>...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Interesting "problem" with using the THRU option (in EVALUATE - or almost
anywhere that COBOL supports it) is:

05  Alph-Field  Pic X.

Evaluate Alph-Field
   When "6" thru "M"
        Display "Does this include 7 an A - or Z and 5"
End-Evaluate

Gets different "evaluations" depending on whether you are using an ASCII or
EBCDIC run-time (or collating system).
```

```
Wow Bill!

That's mind-blowing...

I knew there was a reason I avoided it, just couldn't remember why....<g>

Getting different results on different collating sequences would be a very
good reason why someone like myself (who's really into platform
independence) wouldn't want to use it. (My actual reason for not using it is
because I consider it "ugly" but that is a subjective viewpoint and
completely debatable...)

Having said that,  the collating sequence problem wouldn't have occurred to
me in a million years...thanks very much for pointing it out.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with COBOL CASE statement

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 1999 02:31:11 +0100 "peter dashwood"
<dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

:>Getting different results on different collating sequences would be a very
:>good reason why someone like myself (who's really into platform
:>independence) wouldn't want to use it. (My actual reason for not using it is
:>because I consider it "ugly" but that is a subjective viewpoint and
:>completely debatable...)

You can always specify a collating sequence in the Environment Division and
thus maintaining platform independence even using THRU.

:>Having said that,  the collating sequence problem wouldn't have occurred to
:>me in a million years...thanks very much for pointing it out.

:>William M. Klein wrote in message <7s5q0j$g7b@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>...

:>>End-Evaluate
```

```
ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>...
:>>  (see bottom)

   [ snipped ]

:>>Interesting "problem" with using the THRU option (in EVALUATE - or
almost
:>>anywhere that COBOL supports it) is:

:>>05  Alph-Field  Pic X.

:>>Evaluate Alph-Field
:>>   When "6" thru "M"
:>>        Display "Does this include 7 an A - or Z and 5"
:>>End-Evaluate

:>>Gets different "evaluations" depending on whether you are using an
ASCII or
:>>EBCDIC run-time (or collating system).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with COBOL CASE statement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s4i6c$bjv@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7s2kdi$snb$0@208.231.48.11> <37e592f5@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote in message
  <much snippage>
>
> You are probably wondering why Variable-in is tested with WHEN instead of
> with EVALUATE. Why not write "Evaluate Variable-in"...?
>
> The answer is because, if you Evaluate a variable (dataname in
COBOLspeak),
> you cannot provide a relational operator (like >, = , etc.). You are only
> allowed to provide tests for specific values of the variable. Let's say
> Variable-in actually contains a "10" for 10%, a "20" for 20%, a zero if
> there is to be no discount, and all other values are invalid. Now we can
> evaluate the variable directly, like this...

Pete is 100% correct - as far as ANS'85 conforming compilers go.  HOWEVER,

A) The draft of the next Standard does explicitly allow you to put the "to
be tested" variable in the EVALUATE line, and the rest of the conditional
(starting with the relational operator) in the WHEN clause.

B) Check YOUR specific compiler.  Several compiler vendors have already
introduced this (oft requested) feature as an extension to their '85
Standard compilers.
```

## Thread: EXEC CICS DOCUMENT INSERT 'AT' parameter — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### EXEC CICS DOCUMENT INSERT 'AT' parameter

- **From:** "Ed" <edieder1@twcny.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol,ibm.software.webrunner,ibmpub.java.cics
- **Message-ID:** `<71aF3.13312$r6.351979@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```

Help please...

I'm running COBOL CICS code to interact with a web browser using CICS TS
1.3. I've got the HTML 'dynamic' code with data in a table to post at the
end of a web page created with a static template, but I need to insert it
into a particular spot on the page. My code below won't work with the AT
parm included. I assume I'm setting up the parameter wrong... can someone
clue me in on how to setup this value?

003379     EXEC CICS DOCUMENT INSERT
003380               DOCTOKEN     (WEB-DOCTOKEN)
003381               TEXT         (DETAIL-TABLE)
003382               LENGTH       (LENGTH OF DETAIL-TABLE)
003383*** WAIT       AT           (BKMDETAILS)              <--- What should
this look like?
003384               RESP         (CICS-RESP)
003385     END-EXEC
003386

The template (static HTML page) has the bookmark #bkmDetails.

I tried various AT parameters options, such as the following that's
highlighted in the above code:

001019     05  BKMDETAILS   PIC X(016) VALUE X'7B829294C485A3818993A2'.
001020***       NEEDS AT LEAST 16 BYTES LONG, ELSE STORAGE FOLLOWING THIS
001021***       VALUE GETS PULLED IN...

Any help please - the IBM CICS Web Interface book is very vague.

Thanks in advance - Ed
```

#### ↳ Re: EXEC CICS DOCUMENT INSERT 'AT' parameter

- **From:** "Ed" <edieder1@twcny.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol,ibm.software.webrunner,ibmpub.java.cics
- **Message-ID:** `<kYVF3.85$56.9025@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<71aF3.13312$r6.351979@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I found the answer... I expected it to search on HTML bookmarks, but it's
really searching on IBM CICS formatted bookmarks. You can insert one of
these as:

<!--DFHBOOKMARK=BKMDETAILS      -->

Post note that the bookmark value has to be 16 bytes long with trailing
spaces. You can use the web "EXEC CICS" API (I forgot to write this down,
but refer to the redbook hyperlink below) to insert a "DFH"bookmark or use
an editor to insert it manually. Then the CICS DOCUMENT INSERT command will
work.

The IBM redbook is at http://www.redbooks.ibm.com/SG245480/sg245480.pdf -
it's about 3.5meg.
Ed

Ed <edieder1@twcny.rr.com> wrote in message
news:71aF3.13312$r6.351979@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com...
>
> Help please...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

should

> 003384               RESP         (CICS-RESP)
```

## Thread: Interfacing with AutoCAD — 6 msgs

### Interfacing with AutoCAD

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf02f8$449dcb00$d43763c3@default>`

```
Has anyone ever done the above, or at least
read a drawing file into a Cobol program as data.
I'd be very suprised if anyone can answer this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Interfacing with AutoCAD

- **From:** john.e.freeman@attcanada.net
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e566a6.21403931@news.attcanada.net>`
- **References:** `<01bf02f8$449dcb00$d43763c3@default>`

```
On 19 Sep 1999 15:41:22 GMT, "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
wrote:

>Has anyone ever done the above, or at least
>read a drawing file into a Cobol program as data.
>I'd be very suprised if anyone can answer this.


Yes.  Done much the same way as in Basic or C, using binary mode file
IO.  Since you mentioned AutoCad, I'm assumimng a PC based COBOL
compiler with a set of OS routines that ships with it, such as with
Microfocus COBOL or Realia COBOL, for example.  This is just like the
low level routines which ship with PC based Basic or C.

If you're wondering about potentially enormous and variable file sizes
in COBOL, note that this method bypasses the standard COBOL file
system - which is designed to handle business data, not binary or
graphics data.  I'm sure someone out there could update this, as its
been a while since I did this, however, here is a solution I used some
years ago using MF version 2.6 (I think).

1) Define a linkage section variable in the MAIN program which is one
bye long: PIC X.   

2)  Call an external memory allocation function.  You might want to
find the file size first, then allocate enough blocks of memory to
hold it. 

3)  Change the address of the linkage byte to the address returned by
the memory function using the SET statement.

4)  Using the binary file IO routines, read bytes from the file
specifying the linkage section byte as the target buffer.  The binary
routine will load offsetting from this address.  You will probably
have to load in chunks.  (This may have changed by now)

By the way, the usage of the SET statement, as well as the binary file
and the memory function, are documented in the MF COBOL manuals.

No, this isn't native COBOL, however, a so-called native C memory
function on a PC is very different in behavoir from a C memory
function on a VAX 4300.  The point is, can you do it?  Yes you can.
Cool eh?  

EF.
```

#### ↳ Re: Interfacing with AutoCAD

- **From:** maxomtech@wiscasset.net
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00

```
Yes, sort-of, in AutoCAD (at least thru version 13) you have the ability
to define a Material List. Thru an 'AutoLisp' program (I think it is
built into to AutoCAD), you can (and I have several clients that do),
export this material list to a "LINE SEQUENTAIL" file.  We use this
facility to import Part Information and Bills of Material directly into
our COBOL apps.

Hope it helps

Chris Loy
Maxom Holdings, Inc.
http://www.maxom.com
```

## Thread: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s) — 1 msgs

### Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf02d0$c2065030$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <CnMD3.1757$eJ2.18573@news1.mia> <37dfa498@news3.prserv.net> <QnSD3.2465$eJ2.25322@news1.mia> <37e03c3a@news3.prserv.net> <37e03e80@news3.prserv.net> <7rq246$1em$11@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37e0e12d@news3.prserv.net> <37E0EF39.D8012591@zip.com.au> <37e18a86@news3.prserv.net> <37E2447F.815DC1D9@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in article
<37E2447F.815DC1D9@zip.com.au>...
> Firstly by my understanding of the evaluate EVALUATE TRUE must
> (should?) be equivalent to the IF THEN ELSE chain.  This is certainly
> that way that Fujitsu has implemented it.  It is a little difficult to
> test on MVS but a suspect a quick look at the assembler will prove to
> be the same.

It is.  I've checked the output from the IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1
compiler.
That is

EVALUATE A
  WHEN 'x'
    ...
  WHEN 'y'
    ...
END-EVALUATE

generates exactly the same code as

IF A = 'x'
  ...
ELSE IF A = 'y'
  ...
END-IF
END-IF

Also the same with a period as multiple closure.

But the following generates several unneccessary branches:

EVALUATE TRUE
  WHEN A = 'x'
    ...
  WHEN B = 'y'
    ...
END-EVALUATE

In all cases the comparisons stop when a match is found.

Gunnar.
```

<a id="t-4cd08d6152"></a>
## Thread: MAPPING ASSEMLER DSECT TO COBOL??? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: MAPPING ASSEMLER DSECT TO COBOL???

- **From:** "Dave Shufon" <dshufon1@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kA%E3.12379$r6.373375@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5F5E3.584$MG.7152@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
I've got to disagree with what some of the others are saying how the equates
would be converted over to COBOL.  I work with both Assembler and COBOL and
I see equates like this coded in a LOT of the Assembler programs I maintain.
One person here said that equates are done by assembler programs so they can
call the registers by something other than just a number.  That's correct
(in fact where I work we use a canned initialization macro that among other
things, equates all the registers to R0, R1, R2, . . . R15), but that's not
all equates are used for.

It's also used to set up constants.  For instance we have a lot of one-byte
binary fields in our files, and if you wanted to check one for a particular
numeric value - say 37 - you could do this a few ways:
1)         CLI   INBYTE,X'25'

2)         CLC   INBYTE,COMPBYTE
        . . . . .
        . . . . .
    COMPBYTE DC    X'25'

3)         CLI   INBYTE,COMPBYTE
        . . . . .
        . . . . .
    COMPBYTE EQU   37

Using the equate you can still use the CLI instead of the CLC and you don't
have to figure out what the hex equivalent of 37 is (I know some folks have
been doing this so long they can do the conversions in their heads, but for
newbies it doesn't come naturally).

It can also be used to load a specific value into a register.  For instance
in your example you could load the value of 4 into register 15 (say for
setting a return code) by saying:
          LA    R15,BACK

If your assembler program is doing things like this, then the previous
responses were wrong.  MAIN, SCND and BACK are not level88 elements (which
would mean that they describe states that COMPUSE could be in), but rather
they are constants by themselves.  So the COBOL equivalent would be:

       01  ERIC.
              03  COMPID01            PIC X(8).
              03  COMPUSE            PIC X(1).
              03  MAIN                       PIC X(1) VALUE X'01'.
              03  SCND                     PIC X(1) VALUE X'02'.
              03  BACK                     PIC X(1) VALUE X'04'.

Eric Bass <ebass@tcpip4vse.com> wrote in message
news:5F5E3.584$MG.7152@newsfeed.slurp.net...
> Hello all.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> ERIC BASS
```

```
Cheers,

WOB
```

```
> <Major Snippage>

I know, I know.  I tend to get wordy sometimes.  Sorry.

> > they are constants by themselves.  So the COBOL equivalent would be:
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> <Remainder Snipped>

So you're saying the equates don't generate those three bytes in the
assembler DSECT?  I thought they would have, which is why I thought he'd
need to include them as elements themselves.  I'll have to check out a
compile listing at work tomorrow and see.

Also, how can he use the '88' level names in the same manner that he can use
the equates in the assembler program?  In the assembler program MAIN, SCND
and BACK become literals and retain their values no matter what is in
COMPUSE.  But I've only seen '88' levels used to evaluate what's in the data
element they're under - so if COMPUSE has a value of 1 then MAIN is true,
but SCND and BACK are false.  Are you saying that you can use the names of
the '88' level fields as literals regardless of what's in COMPUSE? (Like:
IF OTHER-FIELD = MAIN . . . ?)  If that's true, then it's something I didn't
realize we could do.
```

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>

```
>

> 
> If Eric's intentions (which I believe) were to mimic his existing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ASSEMBLER Version of the COBOL instructions.

/nit mode on/
I'm pretty certain COBOL will generate CLC's with a length of 1 & a 2nd
operand = a one-byte constant containing the value of the EQU. This is
what I meant about how COBOL can drive long time Assembler people nuts.
/nit mode off/

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: MAPPING ASSEMLER DSECT TO COBOL???

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E5B4F7.6D215B27@att.net>`
- **References:** `<5F5E3.584$MG.7152@newsfeed.slurp.net> <kA%E3.12379$r6.373375@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Dave Shufon wrote:
> 
> I've got to disagree with what some of the others are saying how the equates
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2)         CLC   INBYTE,COMPBYTE

Not meant as a flame, but this is a crude use of the EQU. If you want a
symbol to represent the length of a field, than use the length
attribute, e.g.:

fielda_length  EQU  L'fielda

Let the Assembler calculate these things and you can change the data
structure without having to change your EQU's. If you code:

SOMETHING  EQU  37  or

SOMETHING  EQU  X'25'

these are had coded values which won't change if the field whose length
they represent is changed. In cse anyone's wondering, the length
attribute (L') may be used in open code. It's very powerful and I was
very happy to see COBOL II offer the LENGTH OF special register.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: MAPPING ASSEMLER DSECT TO COBOL???

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<937814792.25369.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<5F5E3.584$MG.7152@newsfeed.slurp.net> <kA%E3.12379$r6.373375@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Things are getting out of hand here.   Everything's been snipped so much
that the original question has been lost.   If I remember correctly, the
original question was how would he should define the DSECT hex field COMPUSE
in COBOL.  My original answer, amongst others,  was just to define as a
character, and then we all gave examples, including 88 levels, of how he
could use this field.   Equates didn't come into the question (although
there was one in the DSECT).  MAIN, SCND, BACK were not in the original
DSECT and were 88 levels in somebody's example of possible usage.

So basically the original answers gave examples and were correct and now
every ones arguing over a mistaken view of part of an example.

Head of a needle and angels comes to mind here.

Rick

Dave Shufon <dshufon1@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message
news:kA%E3.12379$r6.373375@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com...
> I've got to disagree with what some of the others are saying how the
equates
> would be converted over to COBOL.  I work with both Assembler and COBOL
and
> I see equates like this coded in a LOT of the Assembler programs I
maintain.
> One person here said that equates are done by assembler programs so they
can
> call the registers by something other than just a number.  That's correct
> (in fact where I work we use a canned initialization macro that among
other
> things, equates all the registers to R0, R1, R2, . . . R15), but that's
not
> all equates are used for.
>
> It's also used to set up constants.  For instance we have a lot of
one-byte
> binary fields in our files, and if you wanted to check one for a
particular
> numeric value - say 37 - you could do this a few ways:
> 1)         CLI   INBYTE,X'25'
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Using the equate you can still use the CLI instead of the CLC and you
don't
> have to figure out what the hex equivalent of 37 is (I know some folks
have
> been doing this so long they can do the conversions in their heads, but
for
> newbies it doesn't come naturally).
>
> It can also be used to load a specific value into a register.  For
instance
> in your example you could load the value of 4 into register 15 (say for
> setting a return code) by saying:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-cb450843bb"></a>
## Thread: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s) — 1 msgs

### Re: Sample "solutions" to the "Right Justify" Question(s)

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e4d11f@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<7rnb8v$l32@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <7s1rif$6b3@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```

Charla J. Williams <charlajw@netcom10.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7s1rif$6b3@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com...
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> passes the compiler but gets ignored in execution.

JUST does NOT right-justify the data within a field. It right-justifies a
field when moved to a larger field.
02 F1 PIC X(10) JUST.
02 F2 PIC X(05).

MOVE "AB" TO F2           F2="ABbbb"
MOVE F2 TO F1               F1="bbbbbABbb"

what we want is F1="bbbbbbbbAB". This cannot be done
with just a JUST.
```

<a id="t-293d07c142"></a>
## Thread: What does "stack based" mean? — 1 msgs

### Re: What does "stack based" mean?

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s2jmj$141i$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7rrb1p$q9b@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7rrb1p$q9b@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>...
>(Somewhat a follow-up on the "recursion in COBOL" thread.)
>
>For "years," I have heard that IBM (at least in their Mainframe COBOLs) is
>unusual (or possibly "unique") in NOT using "stack based PERFORMs".  In
>fact, I once had the "odious" task of documenting (for Micro Focus)
examples
>of what PERFORM-TYPE (IBM) versus PERFORM-TYPE(RM) versus PERFORM-TYPE(MF)
>meant.  However, I have never really "internalized" what this term means,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Although this is (a little) off-topic and is targeted mostly at those in
the
>NG who LIKE "tuning" the assembler/object code that is generated by their
>compilers, I was wondering if there were some readers who would be willing
>to explain this in (relatively) easy-to-understand terminology.  (I might
>also be interested in anyone who can explain what RM does that is neither
>"stack-based" nor "IBM-like".)

I might be able to explain the differences, in COBOL, by taking
some liberties with the COBOL language.

Each paragraph/section has, associated with it, an active next
address and a default next address. At program start-up these
addresses are the same.

IBM (see the comments, below) and RM appear to be similar
in using memory locations to hold the active next address.
Given the statement:

Perform para-A thru para-C.

Something like the following will take place at the Perform.

    move address of ra-000 to active-next-address of para-C.
    go to para-A.
ra-000.

At the ends of para-A thru para-C, the following will occur.

para-A.
    ...
    move active-next-address of para-A to next-address.
    move address of para-B to active-next-address of para-A
    go to next-address.
para-B.
    ...
    move active-next-address of para-B to next-address.
    move address of para-C to active-next-address of para-B
    go to next-address.
para-C.
    ...
    move active-next-address of para-C to next-address.
        *> at this point next-address has the return address, ra-000
    move address of para-D to active-next-address of para-C
    go to next-address. *> return from perform
para-D.

Since the active next addresses for paragraphs A and B were
unchanged from there initial values, they essentially "fall-thru"
to the next paragraph.

For a stack-based implementation, the following may be done.

At the Perform:

    move address of para-A to next-address.
    perform
        call next-address *> leaves return address on stack
        if next-address not equal default-next-address of para-C
            exit perform cycle
                *> repeatly call the next address until addresses match
        end-if
    end-perform.

At the ends of para-A thru para-C, the following will occur.

para-A.
    ...
    move default-next-address of para-A to next-address.
        *> default-next-address of para-A is address of para-B
    exit paragraph. *> essentially return
para-B.
    ...
    move default-next-address of para-B to next-address.
        *> default-next-address of para-B is address of para-C
    exit paragraph. *> essentially return
para-C.
    ...
    move default-next-address of para-C to next-address.
        *> default-next-address of para-C is address of para-D
    exit paragraph. *> return with matching address
para-D.

The example given as IBM is more like COBOL F, not VS
and later. As I understand it, VS and later has two active next
addresses available.

One difference between IBM and some others, is that IBM
treats the end of a section as the same location as the end
of the last paragraph of that section. RM and MF treat the
end of a section as occurring separately and after the end
of the last paragraph of that section.

As I mentioned elsewhere, both RM and MF have options,
for well-structured programs, to eliminate the normal
"fall-thru" processing replacing it with simple CALL/RET.
This improves performance.

For a given computer, a stack-based implementation will
give better performance than non-stack methods when
performing single paragraphs. This is because the
stack-based solution need not check for the end of a perform
range for that single paragraph. A non-stack method must
always work with its active and default next addresses even
if the perform was of a single paragraph. (Whether the
implementation gives better overall performance is debatable;
but the performs will be faster.)

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-1972a7a58e"></a>
## Thread: Visual JCL? — 29 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Visual JCL?

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
I'm studying for my JCL final, and it makes sense now after a month of
head-scratching.  Having to write all that code has been useful in helping me
understand how it works, but now that I get it, aren't there graphic
environments for creating JCL code akin to the way HTML editors generate HTML
code?  In actual commercial situations wouldn't it be a great tool to have
something that resembles early Windows, i.e. a graphic shell that runs over the
underlying code?

Maybe current versions of ISPF are much friendlier than the PDF I work with now
(I can practically hear the cogs mesh).

Signed,
Curious at Chubb
RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual JCL?

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s72gr$r1n$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
Hi RoadRaat,

You know, you are right.  Before I retired (1981) my employer had built a
shell
over JCL so that application job control was generated by an English
Language
we called Chore (for chain of runs executive). Also, we built another shell
we
called Schedule Register which created all of the instructions for the
operator(s),
tape handling, printing, and mailing.  I believe some of today's job control
reflects
some of that effort, even if on other vendors hardware more than IBM.

Warren Simmons
```

```
> environments for creating JCL code akin to the way HTML editors generate
HTML
> code?  In actual commercial situations wouldn't it be a great tool to have
> something that resembles early Windows, i.e. a graphic shell that runs
over the
> underlying code?
>
> Maybe current versions of ISPF are much friendlier than the PDF I work
with now
> (I can practically hear the cogs mesh).
>
> Signed,
> Curious at Chubb
> RoadRaat

As with programs, most JCL is "cloned" (copy a working set - and modify it
for the new system). When it comes to things like running existing systems,
you probably will use lots AND LOTS of PROCs.  If you know how to override
PROCs, this will get you thru much of what you need to do. (and if you don't
know how to do this yet, this is something that you really should study)

If you are talking about IBM utilities (which are often taught in JCL
courses - even though strictly speaking it isn't JCL), then it is nice to
know how to do this yourself,  but you probably will find interactive
"tools" to do most of what these can do for you.  I know that I almost
always had to "pull down the manual" whenever I had to do much beyond an
IEBCOPY PDS compress.

Finally, the latest PDF (and most 3rd party products) DO have "JCL
generators" for many of their facilities.  How much these are used and how
useful they are depend entirely on the site that you end up working at.
```

```
    I have been told that there were 2 patches to IEFBR14.  I do not know
what the other one is.  The one I was talking about was a failure to zero
the return code before exit.  If it came in with a bad return code, it went
out with a bad return code.

    I am having trouble imagining a 2cd problem.

news:7s79n4$l84$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IEFBR14 (Was Re: Visual JCL?)

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AB4G3.4959$I6.213114@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com> <7s79n4$l84$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7s9gjq$mtt$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```

Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> wrote in message
news:7s9gjq$mtt$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com...
>     I have been told that there were 2 patches to IEFBR14.  I do not know
> what the other one is.  The one I was talking about was a failure to zero
> the return code before exit.  If it came in with a bad return code, it
went
> out with a bad return code.
>

If I remember my IBM days, reg 15 was the entry point address coming into a
program and the return code coming out.  To fix that problem they had to
double the size of the program, inserting SR 15,15 as the top line.

>     I am having trouble imagining a 2cd problem.
> >     My favorite JCL/IBM utility story is the time they found a bug in
> > IEFBR14.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual JCL?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e863e8.1644174@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com> <7s79n4$l84$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7s9gjq$mtt$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 1999 22:58:01 -0400, "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> wrote:

>    I have been told that there were 2 patches to IEFBR14.  I do not know
>what the other one is.  The one I was talking about was a failure to zero
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The second problem was far more involved than a simple SR 15,15 missing from the source.

IEFBR14 was initially not linked as RENT, and thus there were problems of placing it into
the system where reentrant modules were required, e.g. as a dummy exit routine etc.
To fix this problem changes had to be made to the MVS SYSGEN process, which really is not
a trivial and/or trifle thing to do


Nad, on this note, I still have a printed dump in my closet where IEFBR14 abended with an
S0C4 .... anybody willing to guess what was the reason there?

And no, we did not modify IEFBR14, it was its standard two line variation that bombed out!

g
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Assassins do it from behind.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual JCL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7se5gd$qis$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com> <7s79n4$l84$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7s9gjq$mtt$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37e863e8.1644174@news2.ibm.net>`

```
On Wed, 22 Sep 1999 16:30:20 GMT vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>Nad, on this note, I still have a printed dump in my closet where
IEFBR14 abended with an
:>S0C4 .... anybody willing to guess what was the reason there?

:>And no, we did not modify IEFBR14, it was its standard two line
variation that bombed out!

As its two instructions are  SR  and  BR, neither of one makes a storage
reference, the only place an 0C4 could occur is in fetch of the
instructions, which would be possible if the program was LINKed to by
an RB running in a non-zero key which wasn't the TCB key, i.e., in a
TCB with KEY8 was running an RB that was in KEY1 and that routine did a
LINK to a non-reentrent IEFBR14.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual JCL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Je7pN8BuN94lcfpMwOMu1vVAzGt+@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com> <7s79n4$l84$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7s9gjq$mtt$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <37e863e8.1644174@news2.ibm.net>`

```
On Wed, 22 Sep 1999 16:30:20 GMT vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>Nad, on this note, I still have a printed dump in my closet where IEFBR14 abended with an
:>S0C4 .... anybody willing to guess what was the reason there?

:>And no, we did not modify IEFBR14, it was its standard two line variation that bombed out!

As its two instructions are  SR  and  BR, neither of one makes a storage
reference, the only place an 0C4 could occur is in fetch of the instructions,
which would be possible if the program was LINKed to by an RB running in a
non-zero key which wasn't the TCB key, i.e., in a TCB with KEY8 was running an
RB that was in KEY1 and that routine did a LINK to a non-reentrent IEFBR14.
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual JCL?

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sas7a$d2o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990921000710.21824.00000387@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
(SNIP)
, aren't there graphic
> environments for creating JCL code akin to the way HTML editors
generate HTML> code?
> Signed,
> Curious at Chubb
> RoadRaat
>

This sounds nice on the surface, and I've seen (cumbersome, inhouse)
tools for just that. However, a  problem is that it's difficult to
bring in contactors or new employees, and then have them struggle thru
a new tool for something they can already do swiftly and correctly by
hand (cloning - as Mr. Klein mentioned). If there were some tool that
was the 'industry standard', then maybe you could find people who knew
it, but there is no such tool.

In terms of the web world it would be roughly like requiring everyone
who writes Java to use Visual Cafe to edit Java, instead of letting
them pick their own editor. I.e., so if a new guy doesn't know Visual
Cafe you need to teach him that first.

IMHO, JCL ends up being a small side-endeavor of the total job, so it's
best not to get too fired up into trying to avoid doing it 'natively'.

There are JCL-checker softwares that you probably don't have at school.
These will point out most of the doo-doo induced by flub-fingered
typing. And some will check DISP parameters vs. catalog, etc. So
cloning can be real quick and accurate. And we can spend time on the
real work - monitoring the NGs. :)

John




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-56524fe3c4"></a>
## Thread: COBOL DECLGENs for MS-ACCESS — 2 this month / 3 total

### COBOL DECLGENs for MS-ACCESS

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e63fff@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
I have become tired of manually converting MS-ACCESS table definitions into
COBOL, so they can be DECLARED for access in SQL via ODBC.

Mainframe COBOL provides a facility called "DECLGEN" (Declaration Generator)
which accesses the structure of a DB2 database and builds COBOL definitions
for the tables in it.

I have written the same software for the PC and am prepared to give it away
to members of this forum.

Basically, you design and build your database in MS-ACCESS (using ONLY the
database (RDB) facilities - no macros or ACCESS specific code - pure
DEFINITIONS of tables, relationships, indexes, etc.). The principle is not
to PROGRAM in ACCESS; just use it as the excellent PC RDB repository it is,
and do all the programming in COBOL. I have found this leads to excellent
performance on the network and, despite horror stories you may have heard
about ACCESS, it actually performs very well if it is left to just managing
the RDB.

Using ACCESS in this way also provides a ready growth path for your clients.
When the volumes outgrow ACCESS, you simply define exactly the same table
structures to INGRES or ORACLE (or DB2 or UDB on the mainframe), do a data
load and away you go, with no re-programming thanks to ODBC.

You will need to have the MS-ACCESS Documenter facility installed with
ACCESS. You run the Documenter, which produces a standard PC file with all
the ACCESS definitions in it (the settings you require to do this are
documented with DECLGEN - it takes about 2 minutes to set it up the first
time you do it...). You then run DECLGEN, pointing it at the Documenter
output. It produces a correct COBOL definition for every table you have
documented.  Two COBOL definitions are produced:

1. The standard COBOL record structure at an 01 level with the various
fields defined beneath it. (Useful if you want to separate and isolate your
SQL access to specific modules of code which are not "visible" to the rest
of the system. You communicate with them by passing a standard COBOL record
structure and the action you want carried out.) This definition would be
used in WORKING-STORAGE and LINKAGE sections.

2. The "host variable definition" form of the table. This definition can
then be included into the  SQL DECLARE section of your COBOL program. In
this version ALL the "columns" of the original table are defined as 01
levels within BEGIN and END DECLARE statements, and entries are also
provided (optionally) for declarations of SQLCODE and SQLMSG, so that
replies from ODBC can be passed back to the programs.

In my company we have found that doing this gives us a platform independent
data repository which can easily be grown as required. We no longer use
COBOL specific access methods (Indexed and Relative files) and have no
problems with having to convert data across platforms or new releases of
COBOL.

The other major spin off from this is that we can use Crystal Reports (this
fantastic piece of software is also independent across RDB platforms) to
generate all the reports and train the Users to generate their own. (We put
an ActiveX control which links to the Crystal build functions on some of our
application screens, and another similar control allows access to the
Crystal run-time. A table of available reports is maintained easily by the
users and as they develop new reports they simply update this table. Our
applications allow them to select the report they want and invoke the
Crystal run time via the ActiveX control, using the report name and location
from the table. They can view reports on screen, transmit them via e-mail or
fax, or print them to hard copy, all selectable when they click the Crystal
control. COBOL listings using Report Writer or WRITE BEFORE/AFTER ADVANCING
are obsolete for us.

If anyone wants DECLGEN please mail me: pete_dashwood@compuserve.com

It is FREE, with the usual provisos...(no consequential damage, no selling
it for profit, etc.) Also, you should be a reader of this forum. (I'll take
your word for it...<g>)

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DECLGENs for MS-ACCESS

- **From:** "Ed Milne" <emilne@attcanada.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AmyH3.2264$5c3.305@tundra.ops.attcanada.net>`
- **References:** `<37e63fff@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```

peter dashwood wrote in message <37e63fff@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
>I have become tired of manually converting MS-ACCESS table definitions into
>COBOL, so they can be DECLARED for access in SQL via ODBC.
>
>Mainframe COBOL provides a facility called "DECLGEN" (Declaration
Generator)
>which accesses the structure of a DB2 database and builds COBOL definitions
>for the tables in it.


I have written an Access Basic routine that does what Peter described.  To
use this routine, create a command button on a form.  Use the supplied code
as the "On Click" method for the command button.  When you open the form and
click on the button, Access will generate COBOL descriptions for all the
tables in the Access database.

Notes

1.  This is rather rough and ready and I have only defined some field types.
I would welcome any suggestions to make the code better or more complete.
2. I am not positive of the COBOL format of the field types and I have not
tested the generated code.
3. The code produces two files for each table - <table name>1.cbl contains a
record
layout and <table name>2.cbl is the list of 01 level fields.
4. The code produces code for all tables except those beginning with "MSys"
(Access internal tables) .
5. Access creates the files in the last directory that you accessed through
Access.

Ed Milne

-------The Code-------
On Error GoTo declgen_Error

  Dim db As Database
  Dim con As Container
  Dim tdf As TableDef
  Dim i As Integer
  Dim fld As Field
  Dim outfile1, outfile2 As String
  Static prefix00 As String * 8
  Static prefix01 As String * 11
  Static prefix05 As String * 14

  prefix00 = "      * "
  prefix01 = "       01  "
  prefix05 = "           05 "
  Set db = DBEngine(0)(0)
  For Each tdf In db.TableDefs
    With tdf
      If Left(.Name, 4) <> "MSys" Then
        outfile1 = .Name & "1.cbl"
        outfile2 = .Name & "2.cbl"
        Open outfile1 For Output As #1
        Open outfile2 For Output As #2
        Print #1, prefix01; .Name; "."
        For Each fld In .Fields
          Select Case fld.Type
          Case dbLong
            Print #1, prefix05; fld.Name; Tab(52); " COMP   PIC S9(9)."
            Print #2, prefix01; fld.Name; Tab(52); " COMP   PIC S9(9)."
          Case dbCurrency
            Print #1, prefix05; fld.Name; Tab(52); " PIC 9(9)V99."
            Print #2, prefix01; fld.Name; Tab(52); " PIC 9(9)V99."
          Case dbText 'text
            Print #1, prefix05; fld.Name; Tab(59); " PIC X(";
Trim$(Str$(fld.Size)); ")."
            Print #2, prefix01; fld.Name; Tab(59); " PIC X(";
Trim$(Str$(fld.Size)); ")."
          Case dbMemo 'memo
            Print #1, prefix05; fld.Name; Tab(59); " PIC X(256)."
            Print #2, prefix01; fld.Name; Tab(59); " PIC X(256)."
          Case dbDate
            Print #1, prefix05; fld.Name; Tab(59); " PIC 9(8)."
            Print #2, prefix01; fld.Name; Tab(59); " PIC 9(8)."
          Case Else
            Print #1, prefix00; fld.Name; " undefined data type"
            Print #2, prefix00; fld.Name; " undefined data type"
          End Select
        Next fld
        Close #1
        Close #2
      End If
    End With
  Next tdf

Exit_declgen:
  Exit Sub

declgen_Error:
  MsgBox Error$
  MsgBox "Error #" & Err & " " & Error & "in procedure declgen."
  Resume Next
```

```
>    Hi, Can somebody send me the source of a program (in cobol)  that
simply
>writes "hello" on the screen ?
>
>Thanks;
>
>

Here it is:


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  HELLO.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       A.
             DISPLAY "Hello".
             STOP RUN.
```

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 1999 03:08:22 GMT "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

:>Didier Fauconnier wrote:
 
:>>     Hi, Can somebody send me the source of a program (in cobol)  that simply
:>> writes "hello" on the screen ?
 
:>> Thanks;

:>Homework obviously, but I'm not giving too much away - this one-liner
:>works with MircroFocus :-

:>	display "Hello".

Wouldn't the missing Identification Division be a fatal error?

Do you mean that the Microfocus compiler will:

1. Insert required Identification Division
2. Insert required Program-id
3. Insert a Procedure Division statement 
4. Generate object code

for the above one line program?

Quite interesting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NEWBIE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
  <snip>
>
> :>Homework obviously, but I'm not giving too much away - this one-liner
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Strange but true, but YES, MF does all that you have suggested.

(They always allow the Identification Division header to be omitted, - which
will be allowed in STANDARD COBOL with the next Standard.  However, they
will also make some "assumptions" if you omit the Program-ID paragraph and
the Procedure Division header - which is "less common".)
```
