## Thread: Fujitsu WARNING message qeustion — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu WARNING message qeustion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s6e23$e84@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(I finally have my V4.0 - not yet V4.2 - Fujitsu compiler "up and running")

I am trying to test (and verify) the examples that I am putting in the FAQ.
Using Fujitsu V4.0 compiler, I am getting a W-level message that makes no
sense to me.

Given:

 Working-Storage Section.
 01  NumField   Pic 9(08).
 01 WS-Current-Date.
     05 CD-Y4     Pic 9(04).
     05 CD-MM    Pic 9(02).
     05 CD-DD    Pic 9(02).

 Procedure Division.
     Compute NumField = Function NumVal (WS-Current-Date)
     End-Compute

   ****

I am getting the following W-level message (and yes, I do understand that it
is only a warning message - but I don't understand it anyway).

JMN3384I-W ARGUMENT OF FUNCTION NUMVAL MUST BE NONNUMERIC LITERAL OR
ALPHANUMERIC ITEM. ARGUMENT ALPHANUMERIC ITEM ASSUMED"

  ****

As we have recently discussed in C.L.C, a group item is (almost always)
treated as Alphanumeric - and is certainly a valid input argument to any
Intrinsic Function that requires such.

QUESTIONS:

1) Can someone with V4.2 check this out and see if this same message is
being issued there?

2) Can anyone figure out what this message is trying to "warn" about - or is
it just a compiler "bug"?

  ***

P.S.  I had to re-type the entire "error message" into this note.  Does any
experienced Fujitsu user know how to "cut and paste" an error message out of
the "Console" display that you get when you compile a program?  Nothing that
USUALLY works for me, worked with this text.
```

```
about - or is

Your code generates the same message in 4.2. HOWEVER, changing
the definitions to:

 Working-Storage Section.
01  NumField   Pic 9(08).
01 WS-Current-Date   PIC X(8).
01 FILLER REDEFINES WS-CURRENT-DATE.
     05 CD-Y4     Pic 9(04).
     05 CD-MM    Pic 9(02).
     05 CD-DD    Pic 9(02).

The result compiles without error.


> P.S.  I had to re-type the entire "error message" into this note.
Does any
> experienced Fujitsu user know how to "cut and paste" an error
message out of
> the "Console" display that you get when you compile a program?

If you find out, let me know.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu WARNING message qeustion

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e83717@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7s6e23$e84@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <37e6d4c1.113645619@news1.ibm.net> <37e6f32a.121432139@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Comments interspersed below. I agonised long over whether to respond to this
as I think it is a complete waste of time. It's a warning....who cares? But,
evidently there are people who do, and I was persuaded when I read that you
are trying to validate examples on the FAQ, so here goes...

>>On Mon, 20 Sep 1999 17:55:17 -0500, "William M. Klein"
>><wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>>
>>>(I finally have my V4.0 - not yet V4.2 - Fujitsu compiler "up and
running")
>>>
>>>I am trying to test (and verify) the examples that I am putting in the
FAQ.
>>>Using Fujitsu V4.0 compiler, I am getting a W-level message that makes no
>>>sense to me.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>I am getting the following W-level message (and yes, I do understand that
it
>>>is only a warning message - but I don't understand it anyway).
>>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>Intrinsic Function that requires such.

Yes, and the compiler has accepted it as such. All it has done is tell you
that it was not 100% certain that this is alphanumeric, (Thane should be
ecstactic...<g>) or at least, it is not as certain as it would be if you had
given the function an elementary item.

>>>
>>>QUESTIONS:
>>>
>>>1) Can someone with V4.2 check this out and see if this same message is
>>>being issued there?

>>>
OK, I did and it IS issued by 4.2.

>>>2) Can anyone figure out what this message is trying to "warn" about - or
is
>>>it just a compiler "bug"?
>>>
What it is warning about is that the function requires either a numeric
string (literal) or an alphanumeric data item. As a group item has no
picture, the compiler cannot be absolutely sure how the user intends to use
it. However, COBOL convention since time immemorial has ordained that group
items are treated as alphanumeric (except for Thane...<g>), so the compiler
writer has taken it as such, and allowed it to be passed to the function.
That's why it is a WARNING. Just in case he made an assumption that was not
what was intended.

(Having worked on some COBOL compilers in my younger days, I can assure you
that it is quite a task interpreting what users really mean when they write
COBOL. I am also of the school which says: When in doubt, communicate! A
Warning message ensures that the user's attention is drawn to the area in
question...(It certainly got your attention...and mine.)

There are programmers who don't feel comfortable about seeing ANY
diagnostics come out of a compilation. If you are in this category and the
message bothers you, define the field with a picture (to suppress the
diagnostic) then redefine it for the breakdown. (Yes, I know it's inelegant,
but I'M not the one who's bothered by Warnings...).

>>>  ***
>>>
>>>P.S.  I had to re-type the entire "error message" into this note.  Does
any
>>>experienced Fujitsu user know how to "cut and paste" an error message out
of
>>>the "Console" display that you get when you compile a program?  Nothing
that
>>>USUALLY works for me, worked with this text.
>>>
Yes, I had the same problem. All the things you would expect to function,
don't. I think it is because the diagnostic is linked back to your source
code. (Double clicking on it throws you into the editor with the cursor on
the line which caused the error)

It certainly is tiresome, if you are in the habit of cutting and pasting all
your diagnostics into a scrapbook  for future reference...<g>

Regards,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu WARNING message qeustion

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sak0i$jcu$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7s6e23$e84@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7s6e23$e84@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>...
>(I finally have my V4.0 - not yet V4.2 - Fujitsu compiler "up and running")
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am getting the following W-level message (and yes, I do understand that
it
>is only a warning message - but I don't understand it anyway).
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>2) Can anyone figure out what this message is trying to "warn" about - or
is
>it just a compiler "bug"?
>

Hi Bill,

My 'guess', is the compiler is warning about the fact that you are using a
group and the problem, the whole group, although alphanumeric (even although
it contains individual numeric items), as a whole may not contain a numeric
value which makes sence.

For example:

01 ALPHANUM-GROUP.
   03 MY-AGE PIC 99 VALUE 21.
   03 MY-IQ  PIC 99 VALUE 21.
   03 MY-DOOR-NUM PIC 99 VALUE 21.

COMPUTE NUMFIELD = FUNCTION NUMVAL (ALPHANUM-GROUP)
the value NumField will contain a numeric number which has no meaning,
perhaps.

What does the compiler say about:
01 ALPHANUM-GROUP.
    03 MY-AGE PIC 99 VALUE 21.
COMPUTE NUMFIELD = FUNCTION NUMVAL (ALPHANUM-GROUP)

Given that another example in this thread says the warning goes away when
the group is redefined and used in the COMPUTE statement instead, my guess
the compiler is making a distinction between an alphanumeric item which is a
group and an alphanumeric item which is elementary.

Regards,
Steven Lalewicz.

>  ***
>
>P.S.  I had to re-type the entire "error message" into this note.  Does any
>experienced Fujitsu user know how to "cut and paste" an error message out
of
>the "Console" display that you get when you compile a program?  Nothing
that
>USUALLY works for me, worked with this text.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu WARNING message qeustion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sbi2t$87d@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7s6e23$e84@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <7sak0i$jcu$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Up till now, I hadn't thought it worth making public - but because this
thread has continued, I thought I would post the message that I sent to
Fujitsu Support related to the warning message when a group item was used as
the argument to an Intrinsic Function requiring an "alphanumeric argument",

"... in researching the V4.0 documentation, I did find a couple of
"problems" in your documentation - that may relate to this problem.

1) In the LRM, Page 749,
  The item called "2" starts,
"When the argument type is alphabetic,"

I think that this is just a "typo" - as item 1 talks about alphabetic
arguments - and I think that this item is SUPPOSED to handle "alphanumeric"
items.

2) In the same bulleted item, you state (in part),
"the argument must be an alphabetic data item, ..."

This is CLOSE but not exactly the same as what the Intrinsic Function
Amendment says (See page A-28) - which is,
"A data item of *CLASS* alphanumeric"

This is important because there are both "class" and "category" of
alphanumeric.  Given what your LRM states, it is VAGUELY possible that group
items might not be allowed as alphanumeric arguments.  However, with the
correct (ANSI/ISO) wording and if you look at page IV-15 or the "base"
ANS'85 Standard, you will see that it is QUITE explicit that group items are
"data items of *CLASS* alphanumeric" - and those MUST be accepted as an
alphanumeric argument to functions which require such.

  ***

Bottom-Line (unless already done in V4.2)

 - LRM needs both typo and wording fixed
 - "Warning" message should either be removed or reworded"
```

<a id="t-61378f7da0"></a>
## Thread: Checking File Creation Date — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** Doug.Blaze@merchants-fla.com (Doug Blaze)
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>`

```
Hello All,

I have an application that uses several ISAM files.  The application
is distributed on a couple of hundred customer machines and has been
in production for about 8 months.  

Changes are being made to the app that requires I add a couple fields
to a couple of the ISAM files.  Because of this, I will need to
convert the files to the new format.  The app is written in MF 4.0.32
WorkBench.  My first thought was to use their CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST
routine which says it returns the date the file was created.  Not
true.  It returns the date/time the file was last updated, not
necessarily the date created.  Since I distribute a null ISAM file
with the app, I know the date the null file was created.  If I attempt
to open the file in the app using the new FD, I get a FileStatus error
#39.  I can use this to determine when I need to rebuild the file for
this particular upgrade, but if I have changes in the future, I may
have a couple different formats out there and I won't be able to tell
which format the current file is using.  The change I'm putting in
doesn't have to affect all users.

I'd like to know if anyone has a better way of knowing which
version/format a particular ISAM file is in or a definitive way of
getting the file creation date.  

Any ideas would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Doug Blaze
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s629v$vpm$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>`

```
Doug Blaze <Doug.Blaze@merchants-fla.com> wrote:
: Hello All,

: Changes are being made to the app that requires I add a couple fields
: to a couple of the ISAM files.  Because of this, I will need to
: convert the files to the new format.  The app is written in MF 4.0.32
  [...]
: to open the file in the app using the new FD, I get a FileStatus error
: #39.  I can use this to determine when I need to rebuild the file for
: this particular upgrade, but if I have changes in the future, I may
: have a couple different formats out there and I won't be able to tell
: which format the current file is using.  The change I'm putting in
: doesn't have to affect all users.

I have a system that has had a couple of updates of file structure.
You need to have a program, or a series of tiny programs, that
determine the structure and rebuild appropriately.  So, when
the program starts it (for example) calls a program that just
tries to open the file (or files) in the latest format.  If
this fails it passes it to a program that tries to open the 
previous format, failure (ie 39 status) goes to the even older.
When one of these finds it then it can reformat to the latest
format.

As the latest format is opened first there is no significant
overhead at the start of running the system.

You could also have a control file that maintains file versions
in a record (or records).  Just check this at start of run and
apply rebuilds appropriately.
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>`

```

You could try the Win32 API "FindFirstFile" according to the documentation this
returns the file creation date and time.  Let me know if you would like a
sample.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** joarmc@linux1.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn7ud4al.55g.joarmc@linux1.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 1999 14:59:37 GMT, Doug Blaze <Doug.Blaze@merchants-fla.com> 
wrote:
>
>I'd like to know if anyone has a better way of knowing which
>version/format a particular ISAM file is in or a definitive way of
>getting the file creation date.  
>
Just an idea for the future. Why not put a key of, say, low-values in the
file the information in this key would be the file version number. You
can then read this key upon start-up. If the file is back-level, then
upgrade it. You might want to ask the end-user first since they might
want to do a backup (just in case). Or you might just tell them to please
run the file update utility to change the file format. Some message like:

Your data file is not the most current version.
Please run the "updatebd" program to convert it to the current version.
Rerun this program after your have done this.
Exiting.

You could also check to see if they are running an updated file with an
older release and abort.

Hope this was of some use to you.

John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

```
John McKown <joarmc@linux1.johnmckown.net> wrote:
:>I'd like to know if anyone has a better way of knowing which
:>version/format a particular ISAM file is in or a definitive way of
:>getting the file creation date.  
:>
: Just an idea for the future. Why not put a key of, say, low-values in the
: file the information in this key would be the file version number. You
: can then read this key upon start-up. If the file is back-level, then

Except, of course, the problem is that the program gets a '39' 
status error and the file doesn't get opened so no read of
the version number can be done.  Of course if the version is
held in a separate control file then it can be read (but may
not get reset if the main file is recovered from a backup).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** joarmc@linux1.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn7udm3c.6a5.joarmc@linux1.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net> <slrn7ud4al.55g.joarmc@linux1.johnmckown.net> <7s6nnq$3g2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
On 21 Sep 1999 01:41:14 GMT, Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote:
>
>Except, of course, the problem is that the program gets a '39' 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Surely there is a way around this? Of course, I'm not using ISAM where I
work. I'm on an S/390 running OS/390 with VSAM. Using that access method,
I can create an FD which will "read anything" so long as the record key
length does not change. Basically it defines multiple records within
the FD. One has a length of keylen+1, the other has a maximal record length, 
and a third has the "control record" defined, plus whatever else is needed
for the application. I can use a keyed read to then read this file.

This may not be portable. I'm not familiar with other COBOL implementations.

John
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E6EBF4.3B8FBFA1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>`

```
Doug Blaze wrote:
> 
> Hello All,
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Doug Blaze

I think Richard's approach is quite a good one. I know I'm going to hit
the same problem you are describing - but as I'm using OO I only have
one class (Class MyFile) directly accessing the file - so I'm only
concerned with one program going belly-up for a particular file, even
though 20 or more programs may be accessing Class MyFile.

As a suggestion - and assuming you are starting your application from a
Main Menu, why not hold file "Format Update" dates as a table in your
start-up App - whether you will be using all files or not in a
particular run, check any relevant files against this file table - using
the file information from CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST to give you the "latest"
date of the file ?

Surely, it really is the "latest" date you want and your 'rebuild'
program, if incremental, could handle all changes, even if there were 6
versions of the file format.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s7ekr$b4h$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>`

```
The description of File Formats has a layout of the variable
length header used for MF files. This header is the first 128 bytes
of the data file. According to my manuals for WB 3.2, the file
creation date and time is at offset 8 with length 14 within that
header. The following program accesses that date.

       program-id. ism-date.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select ism-file
               assign to "ism.dat"
               organization is record sequential.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd ism-file.
       01 ism-record pic x(128).
       working-storage section.
       01 file-creation-date pic x(14).
       procedure division.
           open input ism-file
           read ism-file
           move ism-record (9:14) to file-creation-date
           close ism-file
           display file-creation-date
           stop run
           .

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Doug Blaze wrote in message <37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net>...
[...]
>I'd like to know if anyone has a better way of knowing which
>version/format a particular ISAM file is in or a definitive way of
>getting the file creation date.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Checking File Creation Date

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s8nvo$ju8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37e6489f.8985451@news.icix.net> <7s7ekr$b4h$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
: of the data file. According to my manuals for WB 3.2, the file
: creation date and time is at offset 8 with length 14 within that
: header. The following program accesses that date.

Of course the creation date may not be especially useful.  If
a REBUILD was done to copy the data file as well as rebuild
the index then it will have the date of the rebuild and not
the original creation date of the initial file.

However, if the header contains the record size that is 
probably exactly what is required.  It seems to me though
that he is better having a number of FDs each being one
of the older formats.  When one open cleanly this file is
copied to the latest format and the files are then renamed
with the original being sent to a backup directory.

This is then independant of variations in manufacturers 
formats.  I still use LevelII format is some systems, while
it is slightly slower than 'current' formats it is a lot
easier to debug out in the field.
```

<a id="t-cadead5f68"></a>
## Thread: RMCOBOL under UNIX — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: RMCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E61FBA.918D5C9D@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<7rtsi1$hde$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Tom Wymer wrote:
> 
>  Anybody familiar with running  an RMCOBOL program under UNIX?  I need
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

Your distribution diskettes should include a program call
"rmmapinx.cob", which lets you look at the index file structure: keys,
offsets, record lengths, etc. With that information, you could write a
program to show you the contents of the file.
As for locking, it seems that RM/COBOL uses the Unix operating system
locks, one for every opened file. You almost allways have to reconfigure
the OS to add more locks to the kernel if you have more than a few
users. But that's about all I can tell. Why worry about locks?

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

#### ↳ Re: RMCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s860a$e3v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7rtsi1$hde$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37E61FBA.918D5C9D@conexion.com.py>`

```
In article <37E61FBA.918D5C9D@conexion.com.py>,
  "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py> wrote:
> Tom Wymer wrote:
> >
> >  Anybody familiar with running  an RMCOBOL program under UNIX?  I
need
> > help understanding how ISAM files ure locked in a multi-user/multi-
site
> > environment and if anybody knows of a way to view ISAM files?
> > Pleas email me at twymer@hotmail.com
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> program to show you the contents of the file.
------------------this is essentially correct but if I recall there are
2 utilities for this purpose and the dependency is file size(?)
> As for locking, it seems that RM/COBOL uses the Unix operating system
> locks, one for every opened file. You almost allways have to
reconfigure
> the OS to add more locks to the kernel if you have more than a few
Suggest you read the RM documentation regarding file handling to
understand record vs file level locking. It might also be covered under
the "Open" verb. Either is available and each has a specific purpose.
While it is true that the kernel level lock resources may need to be
increased it is just as possible that they can be decreased -- this is
a matter of standard UNIX system tuning and really has nothing to do
with whether you are talking about RM or any other language used for
development.
> users. But that's about all I can tell. Why worry about locks?
>
Let's hope that when developing applications for a multiuser/site
environment that he *does* "worry about locks" regardless of the
language used.  Failure to do so *can be* and *usually is* a formula
for disaster.  This is particularly true when dealing with critical
information.

> Regards
> Albert Ratzlaff
>

Cordially,
trblshtr
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RMCOBOL under UNIX

- **From:** "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E800B8.C4CD48BB@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<7rtsi1$hde$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37E61FBA.918D5C9D@conexion.com.py> <7s860a$e3v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
trblshtr wrote:
> 
> In article <37E61FBA.918D5C9D@conexion.com.py>,
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2 utilities for this purpose and the dependency is file size(?)

In my distribution(s) there is only one utility for looking at indexed
file structures. There are 2 utilities for converting relative files
from previous versiones, depending on record size.

> > As for locking, it seems that RM/COBOL uses the Unix operating system
> > locks, one for every opened file. You almost allways have to reconfigure
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> development.

When responding to the original message I had the impresion the sender
was looking for operating specific information on how locks are
implemented. After re-reading the post, I'm not so sure. But of course
you are right about worrying about file and record locking in general.

> > users. But that's about all I can tell. Why worry about locks?
> >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

## Thread: cob: errors in assembling — 2 msgs

### cob: errors in assembling

- **From:** "Geert Soenen" <gsoenen@infosoft.be>
- **Date:** 1999-09-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s59g6$d7m$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```
Hello;

We have MF-Cobol V3.2 running on AIX V4.3. When compiling our cobol programs
with the '-c' option to product object (.O) modules, we always get the
following strange error message:

$ cob -cx INTERF.cbl
* Micro Focus COBOL for UNIX         V3.2 revision 020 Compiler
* Copyright (C) 1984-1994 Micro Focus Ltd.     URN 2XCLY/ZZ0/00000C
* Accepted - verbose
* Accepted - osvs
* Compiling INTERF.cbl
* Total Messages:     0
* Data:        1744     Code:        1442
Assembler:
/tmp/cob19794/INTERF.s: line 52: 1252-142 Syntax error.
cob: error(s) in assembling /tmp/cob19794/INTERF.s

Can anyone help me on this???

Bye
Geert Soenen
```

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s874l$f1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7s59g6$d7m$1@news3.Belgium.EU.net>`

```

  "Geert Soenen" <gsoenen@infosoft.be> wrote:
> Hello;
>
> We have MF-Cobol V3.2 running on AIX V4.3. When compiling our cobol
programs
> with the '-c' option to product object (.O) modules, we always get the
> following strange error message:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
(Just a guess as I don't have access to the documentation anymore).
There is a reference w/in the program which is valid within COBOL (for
example: CALL x) through compilation but which is unresolved at the
point of producing the .O  Been a while but check "Dynamic"(?) in your
MF Doc index.
-trblshtr-
```

## Thread: 60 day check — 6 msgs

### Re: 60 day check

- **From:** "M. Lenz" <Mogens.Lentz@sas.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sfe2g$vv$1@news.dknet.dk>`
- **References:** `<19990918094116.02283.00001683@ng-fv1.aol.com>`

```
If you want one numeric field to contain yyyymmdd then yes:

01     filler.
    05    field1 pic 9(8).
    05    filler redefines field1.
        10 yr-in        pic 9999.
        10 mo-in     pic 99.
        10 dy-in      pic 99.

explain your problem in more details!
```

```
Lawrence Allen Free wrote:

> I'm not familiar with the function.  The usual method for checking
> days differences is to convert dates to a julian format, i.e.  year,
> day of year, or is this for output to another language or database?

So how many days are there between today and last Christmas?  IBM Julian
isn't sufficient (true Julian is).  So you either have to write your own
code or you use a function which will remain debugged after maintenance
programmers play with it.
```

```
> days differences is to convert dates to a julian format, i.e.  year,
> day of year, or is this for output to another language or database?

You came in when the movie was almost over.  The technique is to convert a
date into a count of days since a certain baseline date, subtract (or add as
the case may be) 60 to(from) it, and convert the resulting number back into
a date.  Ways of doing so were discussed in this thread and also "Data
validation".
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol compiler

```
Didier Fauconnier wrote:
>    Where can I find a free cobol compiler ?

You can download Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 free from www.adtools.com.

But a better option is to buy the Sams book "Teach Yourself COBOL
in 24 Hours" by Thane Hubbell, which includes a CD with both 16 and
32 bit versions of the same compiler.

Or, if you would like a more advanced COBOL book, buy Sams "COBOL
Unleashed" by Jon Wessler, et al., which also comes with the same
compiler.

IDG Books "COBOL for Dummies" by Arthur Griffith comes with a CD
including a number of COBOL compilers and interpreters, including
Fujitsu 3.0.  But the book is not as

Both books are readily available at good book stores and online.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol compiler

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GRQF3.3337$LX1.17663@news3.mco>`
- **References:** `<7s8i5g$nm9$1@news0.skynet.be> <PtQF3.3281$LX1.16986@news3.mco>`

```
Didier Fauconnier wrote:
>    Where can I find a free cobol compiler ?

Sorry, I got a telephone call while I was finishing the above post
and sent it unfinished.

You can download Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 free from www.adtools.com.

But a better option is to buy the Sams book "Teach Yourself COBOL
in 24 Hours" by Thane Hubbell, which includes a CD with both 16 and
32 bit versions of the same compiler.

Or, if you would like a more advanced COBOL book, buy Sams "COBOL
Unleashed" by Jon Wessler, et al., which also comes with the same
compiler.

IDG Books "COBOL for Dummies" by Arthur Griffith comes with an
excellent CD that contains a number of COBOL compilers and
interpreters, including Fujitsu 3.0.  But "COBOL for Dummies"
is not as detailed and thorough as either of the above books.
Not a criticism; the 'for Dummies' books are supposed to be
light and easy introductions.

Both books are readily available at good book stores and online.
```

<a id="t-155745e473"></a>
## Thread: Collaborative Article on COBOL — 6 msgs

### Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990921111703.18987.00000501@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
I've just been approached by an editor from IEEE Software to write an article
on converting legacy COBOL code to new COBOL code, specifically for the IBM
COBOL compilers.

This would include 
   * reasons for converting
     - supported compilers
     - access to new features
     - interoperability with other compilers
   * sample code fragments, showing how using new features produces
     better code
   * discussion of conversion techniques, including the IBM products
   * discussion of COBOL in the WebSphere environment

I said I would look into this but I really don't know any thing about WebSphere
and little about the conversion products. The editor suggested we work with the
COBOL language newsgroup, to draw on their expertise and insights. I liked the
idea so this post is to announce this project to see if we can get some real
world perspectives.

The article is due in to the editor by Ocotber 11 or so, for publication in the
March, 2000 issue of IEEE Software. Anyone who contributes material that is
used will be recognized as a co-author or contributor. (There is no
renumeration for me or anyone else, which is not all bad.)

So let me know, send me your ideas. You can post them on the newsgroup for
group discussion or you can email me. (Probably best not to post long pieces on
the newsgroup).

Thanks for your help, let's see what we can do.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sagp0$42k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990921111703.18987.00000501@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19990921111703.18987.00000501@ng-fe1.aol.com>,
  scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote:
> I've just been approached by an editor from IEEE Software to write an
article
> on converting legacy COBOL code to new COBOL code, specifically for
the IBM
> COBOL compilers.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> I said I would look into this but I really don't know any thing about
WebSphere
> and little about the conversion products. The editor suggested we work
with the
> COBOL language newsgroup, to draw on their expertise and insights.

Your list doesn't include the inadvertent problems that occur on
coversions and the costs they entail. It sounds like your article is
entended to sell the concept of upgrading to the latest COBOL regardless
of the cost.

To show the other side of the story consider the following list of
conversion traps T made up when converting IBM VS/COBOL (ANSI 74) to
COBOL II (ANSI 84).


COMP Fields

Problem

VS/COBOL ignores the picture for COMP fields except for determining the
size of the field. For example, a field defined as
"COMP PIC S9" is identical to "COMP PIC S9(4)". However, COBOL II
truncates the results of any calculation to a COMP
field to match the picture.

For example, suppose a program has a field defined as "COMP PIC S9" and
the field contains a value of 9. If you increment
the field in VS/COBOL, you get 10 (9 + 1 = 10). If you increment the
field in COBOL II, you get 0 (9 + 1 = 10 truncated to 1
digit gives zero).

Since programs often use COMP fields as subscripts and zero can be a
valid subscript value, this difference can produce interesting and
exciting results.

Solution

Identify all COMP fields in working storage and give them a picture of
at least S9(4). This is their effective picture under
VS/COBOL.


COMP-3 Fields

Problem

The version of VS/COBOL used before August 1996 used the full physical
size of a COMP-3 field regardless of the picture.
If a picture only defined an even number of digits, an extra digit was
automatically used. For example, the compiler treated
"COMP-3 PIC S9(4)" as if it were "COMP-3 PIC S9(5)". However, COBOL II
truncates any values moved to such fields to
the number of digits actually defined.

For example, suppose a program contains a field defined as "COMP-3 PIC
S9(4)" and moves 12345 to that field. Under the old VS/COBOL compiler
the result is 12345. Under the new VS/COBOL compiler and COBOL II, the
result is 2345.

Programs recompiled since August 1996 should have been changed to
correct this problem. However, any reengineering project may recompile
programs that have not been changed in many years.

Solution

Change the pictures for all COMP-3 fields so they define an odd number
of digits unless they should only contain an even number of digits. Most
fields should have an odd number of digits to take full advantage of the
physical size of the space used. However, some fields, such as dates,
should only have an even number of digits.


NEXT SENTENCE

Problem

After converting a program to COBOL II, it is natural to use the COBOL
II structures for ending statements rather than
periods for new code. This mix of old and new code can cause problems
with the NEXT SENTENCE clause in existing
statements.

For example, in the following code

    IF <test> THEN
      NEXT SENTENCE
    ELSE
      <move statement>
    END-IF
    <add statement>.
    <subtract statement>

if the test is true, the next statement executed is the <subtract
statement> rather than the expected <add statement>. This happens
because a "NEXT SENTENCE" clause continues following the next period
rather than with the next statement.

Solution

Make a mass change of all occurrences of "NEXT SENTENCE" to "CONTINUE".
The new CONTINUE clause does nothing. It just continues with the next
statement, which is the real intent of the "NEXT SENTENCE" clause.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf0617$aee7e230$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<19990921111703.18987.00000501@ng-fe1.aol.com> <7sagp0$42k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote in article
<7sagp0$42k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
> In article <19990921111703.18987.00000501@ng-fe1.aol.com>,
>   scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The described outcome of the examples does not correspond to my experience.
(I do agree, though, that there exists compatibility problems in general.)
I have compared the generated code from three compilers:

IBM OS/VS COBOL RELEASE 2.4
IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1

1. For the problem with COMP field the outcome depends on the TRUNC option.
With TRUNC(STD) (TRUNC for OS/VS COBOL) the effective outcome is identical
for all three compilers: the result is truncated so that only the last
digit (when interpreted as decimal) is kept.
With TRUNC(OPT) (NOTRUNC for OS/VS COBOL) the generated code is identical
for all three compilers, the variable is treated as a halfword, no
truncation takes place.
With TRUNC(BIN) (no equivalent for OS/VS COBOL) the newer two compilers
truncates the result at the capacity of a halfword, that is: no truncation
for the the value used.

2. For the problem with COMP-3 field the outcome is identical for all three
compilers: 2345.  The constant generated in the literal pool is X'02345C'
in all cases.  I don't have access to a very old OS/VS compiler, so you may
well be right there.

3. For the problem with NEXT SENTENCE the effective outcome is identical
for all three compilers: NEXT SENTENCE is interpreted as after next period
(by definition).  I don't see what the problem is, how should OS/VS COBOL
have behaved different?

See compiler generated code below (syntax modified for clarity).

-----

Problem 1: COMP fields.

01  A PIC S9 COMP.
    ADD 1 TO A

IBM OS/VS COBOL RELEASE 2.4 with TRUNC

LH   3,=H'1'      
AH   3,A       
CVD  3,WORK      
XC   WORK(7),WORK
CVB  3,WORK      
STH  3,A       

IBM OS/VS COBOL RELEASE 2.4 with NOTRUNC

LH   3,=H'1'
AH   3,A
STH  3,A

IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0 with TRUNC(STD)

LH   3,=H'1'
AH   3,A 
LR   2,3      
SRDA 2,32(0)  
D    2,=F'10'
STH  2,A 

IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0 with TRUNC(OPT)

LH   2,=H'1'
AH   2,A 
STH  2,A 

IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0 with TRUNC(BIN)

LH   2,A          
CVD  2,WORK       
AP   WORK+4(4,13),=X'1F'
CVB  WORK       
STH  2,A         

IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1 with TRUNC(STD)

LH   3,=H'1'
AH   3,A  
LR   2,3       
SRDA 2,32(0)   
D    2,=F'10' 
STH  2,A  

IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1 with TRUNC(OPT)

LH   2,=H'1'
AH   2,A  
STH  2,A  

IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1 with TRUNC(BIN)

LH   2,A          
CVD  2,WORK       
AP   WORK+4(4,13),=X'1F'
CVB  2,WORK       
STH  2,A          

-----

Problem 2: COMP-3 fields.

01  B PIC S9(4) COMP-3.
    MOVE 12345 TO B

IBM OS/VS COBOL RELEASE 2.4

MVC  B,=PL3(02345)

IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0

MVC  B,=PL3(02345)

IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1

MVC  B,=PL3(02345)

-----

Problem 3: NEXT SENTENCE.

IF A = 1 THEN  
  NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE           
  statement1
END-IF         
statement2.   
statement3    

IBM OS/VS COBOL RELEASE 2.4

         LH    3,A
         CH    3,=H'1'
         BNE   GN1
         B     GN2
GN1      EQU   *                          
         statement1
         statement2
GN2      EQU   *                          
         statement3
IKF3001I-E     END-IF NOT DEFINED. DISCARDED.

IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0
                                          
         CLC  A,=H'1'
         BNE  GN1
         B    GN2
         B    GN3
GN1      EQU  *                           
         statement1
GN3      EQU  *                           
         statement2
GN2      EQU  *                           
         statement3

IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.1.1

         CLC  A,=A'1'
         BNE  GN1
         B    GN2
         B    GN3
GN1      EQU  *                           
         statement1
GN3      EQU  *                           
         statement2
GN2      EQU  *                           
         statement3

Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: Locking Problem on NT

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s9bhf$esq$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1248d2c9ac20f585989694@news> <7ro25f$nma$2@news.igs.net> <4bTfNxQjjK4K6f5p=Yh59q+5JBdp@4ax.com> <37e06ed6.21162684@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.124b5298277b4887989695@news> <7rs95h$5nq$1@news.igs.net> <MPG.12516be7734f8817989696@news>`

```
Is this using MF NetExpress Cobol...If so, they had a problem with their
file handling not too long ago...See following note from one of their
fixpacks - caused improper file locks


IMPORTANT INFORMATION
   =====================
   NOTE: V1.0.001 AND ABOVE OF THE FILE HANDLER FIXPACK IS DEPENDENT ON
   V1.0.003 OF THE RUN-TIME SYSTEM FIXPACK. YOU MUST HAVE BOTH OF THESE
   FIXPACKS INSTALLED TOGETHER.  IF YOU UNINSTALL ONE OF THESE FIXPACKS YOU
   SHOULD THEN UNINSTALL THE OTHER.

   This section describes changes to the underlying locking mechanism which
   may cause incompatibilities with applications using versions of the
   file handler from the GA release of Net Express 3.0.

   In the GA version of Net Express 3.0, the FILEMAXSIZE configuration
option
   defaulted to 8 which was incompatible with earlier products.

   From File Handler Fixpack v1.0.001 onwards, FILEMAXSIZE defaults to 4,
   limiting file addressibility to 32 bit values which is compatible with
   earlier versions of Micro Focus products.

   Because the default setting has changed, you may have applications linked
   under the GA version of Net Express that have a different default setting
to
   applications linked under newer versions of Net Express. Therefore, if
you
   install the File Handler Fixpack, you must make all the FILEMAXSIZE
settings
   consistent across all your applications.

   If applications that share files use different FILEMAXSIZE settings, file
   corruption and/or locking errors can occur. Setting FILEMAXSIZE to 8
allows
   up to 64 bit values for file addressing on Windows NT platforms when
   accessing the NTFS file system, but changes the underlying record locking
   mechanism. If you are sharing very large files, that is files greater
than
   1 Gb in size, all programs accessing the file must use FILEMAXSIZE set to
8
   to avoid possible corruption problems.

   Do not set FILEMAXSIZE to 8 under any of the following circumstances:

    o  You are running applications on either Windows 95 or Windows 98.
       (Under this circumstance, if you do set FILEMAXSIZE to 8, the File
       Handler ignores this setting and behaves as though FILEMAXSIZE was
       set to 4.)

    o  You are running applications that access FAT file systems, since such
       file systems do not support file addressibility greater than 32 bit
       file addressing. (Under this circumstance, if you do set FILEMAXSIZE
       to 8, the File Handler ignores this setting and behaves as though
       FILEMAXSIZE was set to 4.)

    o  You are sharing files between applications running on Windows NT and
       applications running on either Windows 95 or Windows 98

    o  You are sharing files with applications using earlier versions of the
       Micro Focus Cobol product that only supported 32 bit file access

   The FILEMAXSIZE option specifies the number of bytes used internally to
   store file offsets. This also affects internal locking mechanisms.
   Programs sharing the same file all need to use the same FILEMAXSIZE
   settings to ensure semaphores and record locks are handled correctly,
   thereby avoiding potential file corruption in shared files.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Locking Problem on NT

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1252c98e4be37d83989698@news>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1248d2c9ac20f585989694@news> <7ro25f$nma$2@news.igs.net> <4bTfNxQjjK4K6f5p=Yh59q+5JBdp@4ax.com> <37e06ed6.21162684@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.124b5298277b4887989695@news> <7rs95h$5nq$1@news.igs.net> <MPG.12516be7734f8817989696@news> <7s9bhf$esq$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`


### Cobol and PL/1

- **From:** "goosse" <p.barendregt@hetnet.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eye0SKHB$GA.93@net025s>`

```
I 'm having trouble calling a pl/1 program dynamic from a cobol program 390
program. W're using the latest LE environment and when i do a static call
their is no problem but a dymanic call will force a s0c1.
Does anyone know the solution ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and PL/1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s8sc4$18a@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<eye0SKHB$GA.93@net025s>`

```
The 2 "obvious" questions (which may or may not help)

1) Do you have the correct version of SCEELKED in your LKED step (SYSLIB)
2) Have you checked the LE ILC manual?    I would look particularly at:

"10.2.2.1 COBOL Dynamically Calling PL/I"

which can be seen on the web at:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA406/10%2e2%2e2%2e1
?SHELF=
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and PL/1

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990921185747.20297.00000627@ng-bg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<eye0SKHB$GA.93@net025s>`

```
"goosse" <p.barendregt@hetnet.nl> writes ...

>I 'm having trouble calling a pl/1 program dynamic from a cobol program 390
>program. W're using the latest LE environment and when i do a static call
>their is no problem but a dymanic call will force a s0c1.
>Does anyone know the solution ?

Well, without any more information, I would check that the subroutine is linked
with the entry point being the name of the module, not CEESTART.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
```

## Thread: Avoid Error 114 with DOS? — 1 msgs

### Avoid Error 114 with DOS?

- **From:** "Enric Masdeu" <e.masdeu@atr3s.es>
- **Date:** 1999-09-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s8rkq$eaa5@esiami.tsai.es>`

```
        I have an application compiled in NetExpress3. Some of my clients
are getting Error 114 (Attempt to acces item beyond bounds of memory) in a
certain module (a C++ dll called from various parts of my program).
     Previous experiences with this error showed me this should be a program
error (non initialized linkage variables,..). But in this case there's
something that makes me think the cause is different:
        They only get the Error when opening the program from a Windows
icon. If they open a DOS session (or  use a .PIF) they don't get the Error
114.
        Does anybody know why this happens?

Thank you.
```

## Thread: Net Express / Dialog System context help — 16 this month / 20 total

### Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```

With a right mouse click I'd like to put up a contxt menu. This should also
include a "help" choice linked to a help text specific for the field that
was clicked on.

Has anyone come up with a high-level solution for this? i.e: I would like to
code this exit at window- or even screenset level. Not for each and every
field again. But how then am I to know which field was clicked, and how to
return after coming back from the help routine?

Anyone ?



 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E96EE3.2C806169@home.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
Peter van Zeeland wrote:
> 
> With a right mouse click I'd like to put up a contxt menu. This should also
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone ?

To supplement what Simon has already given you, you might find the 
following useful :-

      *>------------------vestype.cbl --------------------------------

       Class-id.          VesselTypes
                          inherits from Base.

       Class-control.     VesselTypes          is class "vestype"

                          Characterarray       is class "chararry"
                          EntryCallBack        is class "entrycll"
                          PopupMenu            is class "popupmnu"
                          .

       *> I've deleted other methods which are not relevant to the PopUp

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "new".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-self               object reference.
       Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
         invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
       End Method "new".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       copy "p2cevent.cpy".
       copy "vesmax.cpy".

       01 ws-x                                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ws-y                                 pic x(4) comp-5.

       *> Vessel Type descriptions are shown without ampersand
       *> as method "get-typename-text" uses wt-descrip for printing

       78 MaxVesType                                value 14.
       01 wt1.
          05 pic x(30) value "01,Aerial Cooler         ,01,A". *> A
          05 pic x(30) value "02,Exchanger/Reboiler    ,01,E". *> E
          05 pic x(30) value "03,Glycol Reboiler       ,01,G". *> G
          05 pic x(30) value "04,Line Heater/Furnace   ,06,H". *> H
          05 pic x(30) value "05,Piping/Pipelines      ,01,P". *> P
          05 pic x(30) value "06,Plate Exchanger       ,02,L". *> l
          05 pic x(30) value "07,Pumps                 ,02,U". *> u
          05 pic x(30) value "08,Sphere                ,05,r". *> r
          05 pic x(30) value "09,Spherical             ,01,S". *> S
          05 pic x(30) value "10,Tanks                 ,01,T". *> T
          05 pic x(30) value "11,Tower with Transition ,05,O". *> r
          05 pic x(30) value "12,Vessel                ,01,V". *> V
          05 pic x(30) value "13,Vessel with One Boot  ,13,N". *> O
          05 pic x(30) value "14,Vessel with Two Boots ,14,W". *> w
       01 wt2 redefines wt1.
          05 wt3 occurs MaxVesType.
             10 wt-code             pic 9(02).
             10                     pic x(01).
             10 wt-descrip          pic x(22).
             10                     pic x(01).
             10 wt-ampersand        pic 9(02).
             10                     pic x(01).
             10 wt-oldcode          pic x(01).

          *> wt-oldcode refers to codes used in RM/COBOL V2

       01 VESSEL-record.
          copy "vessels2.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==VESSEL==.

       01 ws-FacilitySweetSour      pic 9(01).

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 os-Callback                     object reference.
       01 os-Dialog                       object reference.
       01 os-Parent                       object reference.
       01 os-Popup                        object reference.

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "check-menuitem".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-results.
          05 ls-choice                      pic 9(02).
          05 ls-string                      object reference.
       Linkage section.
       01 p1                                pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using p1.
         move p1 to ls-choice
         invoke self "get-typename-string" using     ls-choice
                                           returning ls-string
         invoke os-Parent "return-vestype" using ls-results

         *> The ls-string (Vessel Type name) is displayed in the Dialog

       End Method "check-menuitem".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "create-popup".
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 n                                     pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-event                              pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-item                               object reference.
       01 ls-length                             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-MethodName                         pic x(14).
       01 ls-string                             object reference.
       01 ls-text                               pic x(22).

       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-Parent                            object reference.
       01 lnk-Dialog                            object reference.
       01 lnk-x                                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-y                                 pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using lnk-Parent
                                lnk-Dialog
                                lnk-x
                                lnk-y.

         set os-Parent  to lnk-parent
         set os-Dialog  to lnk-Dialog
         move lnk-x     to ws-x
         move lnk-y     to ws-y
         invoke PopupMenu "new" using os-Dialog returning os-Popup
         invoke os-Popup "create"
         move p2ce-menuSelect  to ls-event
    *>   move p2ce-button3down to ls-event
    *>   move "check-menuitem" to ls-MethodName
    *>   invoke EntryCallback "new" using ls-MethodName
    *>       returning os-Callback
         move length of ls-text to ls-length

         perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxVesType
            invoke self "get-menu-line" using     n
                                        returning ls-text
            invoke characterArray "withLengthValue"
                using ls-length ls-text returning ls-string
            invoke os-Popup "AddItem"
                   using ls-string returning ls-item
         *> invoke ls-item "setEventTo"
         *>        using ls-event, os-Callback, n

         *> Instead of Callback - you could use :-

            invoke ls-item "setEventto"
                 using ls-event, self, "check-menuitem ", n

            Note : Make sure you allow for a null terminator
            (x"00") after "check-menuitem  before closing the quotes


         End-perform
       End Method "create-popup".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "finalize-objects".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Procedure Division.
          if os-Popup <> null
             invoke os-Popup "finalize" returning os-PopUp
          End-if
          invoke self "finalize" returning self
       End Method "finalize-objects".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "get-max-vestype".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-max                       pic x(4) comp-5.
       Procedure Division  returning lnk-max.
          move MaxVesType to lnk-max
       End Method "get-max-vestype".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "get-menu-line".
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       78 ampersandChar                 value "&".
       01 ampersandCount                pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 name1                         pic x(22).
       01 name2                         pic x(22).
       01 p1                            pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 p2                            pic x(4) comp-5.

*         05 pic x(30) value "04,Line Heater/Furnace   ,06".
*         name1         "Line Heater/Furnace   "
*         name2         "Line &Heater/Furnace  "
*                        0........1.........2..
*                        1...567..0.........0.2

       Linkage section.
       01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-text                      pic x(22).

       Procedure Division using     n
                          returning lnk-text.

        move wt-descrip(n)   to name1
        move wt-ampersand(n) to ampersandCount
        evaluate true
           when ampersandCount = 1
                move ampersandChar to name2(1:1)
                move name1         to name2(2: )
           when other
                subtract 1 from ampersandCount giving p1
                add 1      to   ampersandCount giving p2
                move name1(1:p1)            to name2
                move ampersandChar          to name2(ampersandCount:1)
                move name1(ampersandCount:) to name2(p2: )
        End-evaluate
        move name2 to lnk-text
       End Method "get-menu-line".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "get-typename-text".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-code                          pic 9(02).
       01 lnk-name                          pic x(22).
       Procedure Division using     lnk-code
                          returning lnk-name.
         move wt-descrip(lnk-code) to lnk-name
       End Method "get-typename-text".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "get-typename-string".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-length                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-text                           pic x(22).
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-code                          pic 9(02).
       01 lnk-string                        object reference.

       Procedure Division using     lnk-code
                          returning lnk-string.
         move length of ls-text to ls-length
         move wt-descrip(lnk-code) to ls-text
         invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
            using ls-length, ls-text returning lnk-string
       End Method "get-typename-string".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "show-popup".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Procedure Division.
         invoke os-Popup "showat" using ws-x ws-y
       End Method "show-popup".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

       End OBJECT.
       End CLASS VesselTypes.

*>----------------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<37E99805.A67D4D37@home.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <37E96EE3.2C806169@home.com> <7sbvgp$so7$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> number and type of the products dealt with change ?

No; it so happens that this is a static list - it aint gonna grow -
there are only a finite number of shapes for vessels in an oil/gas
plant.

Originally I had the Vessel Types in a fixed length Description File,
(which I still use for many other descriptions records - last count
about 20 types), identified by a record-code. From that I could access
the Ves Types and generate the Popup.

What the class also gives me apart from the PopUp is the names of Ves
Types to display in Windows dialogs (I can put 'dialogue' for you can't
I ? <G>) and the 'straight' text-name for printing purposes. (Plus the
table above also allows for my conversion from RM/COBOL to N/E data
files).

What you didn't also see are the methods associated with the same table
of Vessel Types, varied info by Type, used to generate/check Vessel
records, e.g. :-

*----------------------------------------------------------------------
*                     VESSEL TYPE - DEFAULTS
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
*    Type                Coating Insulated  Manway    Service    X-ray
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
*Aerial Cooler            n/a(4)   n/a(4)   n/a(4)   Default(3)  Full(1)
*Exchanger/Reboiler       n/a(4)   Yes(1)   n/a(4)   Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Glycol Reboiler          n/a(4)   Yes(1)   n/a(4)   Sour(2)     None(3)
*Line Heater/Furnace      n/a(4)   Yes(1)   n/a(4)   Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Piping/Pipelines         No(2)    Y/N(5)   n/a(4)   Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Plate Exchanger          n/a(4)   n/a(4)   n/a(4)   Default(3)  n/a(4)
*Pumps                    n/a(4)   n/a(4)   n/a(4)   Default(3)  n/a(4)
*Sphere                   No(2)    No(2)    Y/N(5)   Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Spherical                No(2)    No(2)    No(2)    Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Tanks                    No(2)    No(2)    Yes(1)   Default(3)  None(3)
*Tower with Transition    No(2)    Yes(1)   Yes(1)   Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Vessel                   No(2)    No(2)    No(2)    Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Vessel with one boot     No(2)    No(2)    No(2)    Default(3)  F/P(5)
*Vessel with two boots    No(2)    No(2)    No(2)    Default(3)  F/P(5)
*
* Where value = 5 prompt user to key-in field/button
*
*----------------------------------------------------------------------
*
*                                Pressure/Temp        Thicknesses
*                                 ( 4 lines )           ( 9 lines )
*                                -------------        -----------
*
*  01 Aerial Cooler              01 - Shell           12 - Wrapper
*
*  02 Exchanger/Reboiler         01 - Shell           01 - Shell
*                                                     02 - Head
*                                                     09 - Trans/Cone
*                                02 - Tube            07 - Channel Shell
*                                                     08 - Channel Head
*
	etc..........

*-----------------------------------------------------------------------*                    
RADIO BUTTON inter-action
*
* Activity  - Yes(1)                         "enable"  Activity
*             Shutin(2)                      "enable"  Activity
*             Abandoned(3)                   "enable"  Activity
*
* Coated    - Yes(1)                         "enable"  Coating
*           - No(2)                          "hide"    Coating
*           - Partial(3)                     "enable"  Coating
*           - n/a(4)                         "hide"    Coating
*
* Insulated - Yes(1)    if Temp >= 200       "enable"  Insulation
*           - No(2)                          "hide"    Insulation
*           - Partial(3)                     "enable"  Insulation
*           - n/a(4)                         "hide"    Insulation
*
	etc.......
*-----------------------------------------------------------------------

........and it goes on and on.

This OO Class gives me complete Validity checking plus REUSABILITY.
(Check out Bill Klein's currtent message about copy files - I don't need
them to cover procedures).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf0572$985cea60$0ee5abc3@default>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```


Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl> wrote in article
<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01>...
> 
> With a right mouse click I'd like to put up a contxt menu. This should
also
> include a "help" choice linked to a help text specific for the field that
> was clicked on.
> Has anyone come up with a high-level solution for this? i.e: I would like
to
> code this exit at window- or even screenset level. Not for each and every
> field again. But how then am I to know which field was clicked, and how
to
> return after coming back from the help routine?
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

Peter,

You need to use the "popupmnu" GUI class for the popup menu
then, when your end user right clicks of left clicks the text field
a callback is invoked to execute the relevent commands held within the
popup menu.


You need to use the window event      p2ce-button3down
to trigger the event. for example:

          move p2ce-button3down to EventValue
          move "mouseclickedC " to methodName
          invoke EntryCallback "new" using methodname
              returning aCallback
          invoke oTextEditField "setEvent" using EventValue aCallback

You would then have a method called "mouseclickedC "
which would create and show the popup menu.


To add items after you have created an instance use:

           move "Display Setup" to ws-text
           move length of ws-text to StringLength

           invoke characterArray "WithLengthValue" using StringLength
                                              ws-text
                                              returning aString


           invoke opopup "addItem" using aString returning bString

Simon R Hart
Eaton
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1259e387b5c6b0a0989699@news>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
Without going to OO, you can achieve the result by placing the following 
code in each window's dialog
 MOUSE-EVENT
     IFNOT= $EVENT-DATA 7 QUIT
     INVOKE-CONTEXT-MENU $WINDOW CTX-MENU $NULL
   @CTX-FIELD-HELP
     MOVE "HF" COMMAND-FLAG
     RETC
(ctx-field-help is assigned to the popup item "Field Help")

and placing code such as this in your event loop back in your program
           EVALUATE COMMAND-FLAG
               WHEN "HF"
                    PERFORM 80-HELP-FIELD
           END-EVALUATE

and the help-field paragraph
       80-HELP-FIELD.
           MOVE 0 TO ON-LINE-CONTEXT-NO
           EVALUATE DS-OBJECT-NAME
               WHEN "EF-ID"
                    MOVE 1 TO ON-LINE-CONTEXT-NO
           END-EVALUATE
           IF ON-LINE-CONTEXT-NO NOT = 0
              CALL "ON-LINE" USING ON-LINE-PB
                                   ON-LINE-TOPIC
              END-CALL
              IF ON-LINE-RETURN NOT = 0
                 DISPLAY "on-line error: " ON-LINE-RETURN
              END-IF
           END-IF

If you need a special menu for a field, just put the mouse-event in that 
field's dialog but let field-help roll-up to the ctx-field-help in the 
window's dialog.

Hope this helps. I tried to keep it short. If it was too short, email me 
at rayleech1@home.com without the number 1.

In article <6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01>, 
peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl says...
> 
> With a right mouse click I'd like to put up a contxt menu. This should also
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> return after coming back from the help routine?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985122D7@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <MPG.1259e387b5c6b0a0989699@news> <37f04709.3738134@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.125b343ef65e453d98969a@news> <37f2d759.171780090@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane,
Yes, I missed the sarcasm but I'm on the same page now. I was pretty 
sure you used DS and _didn't_ like it :)

Since we're ranting, let me add my .02

Prior to this year's user conference, MF was considering changing the 
scripting language to COBOL (mainly because of Fujitsu). I talked at 
length to Wayne and followed up with a letter explaining why I thought 
that would be a perfect solution. In my case it would ease the training 
issues getting new developers on-line. I think it would lower the 
resistance of some people not currently using DS to give it a shot (I 
heard this comment for the um-teenth time from a potential client on 
Tuesday: 'Dont mind COBOL but don't want to learn scripting lang..."). 

I've already learned the syntax and quarks so to me its not as big a 
deal. That said, I would in a heartbeat 'unlearn' that and go back to 
COBOL, even if there would be the inevitable new verbs or constructs.

One suggestion that was made to me at the conference was to use the 
Vocabulary -- with the caveat that everything is not in the vocab (kinda 
useless then I thought). I haven't worked with the DS Vocab since the 
initial early-release so I cant vouch for that statement. Anyone have 
any knowledge??

Maybe if enough people told MF (specifically Wayne) their feelings on 
the subject, they may rethink the whole thing. 

ray

In article <37f2d759.171780090@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net says...
> >> Perfect illustration.  What language is that? 
>[snip] 
> Sorry, my sarcasm didn't make it through.   Why in the world did they
> not make it COBOL?
```

```
Mike Madara
Prince Frederick, MD
```

```
Simon R Hart wrote:
> 

> IMHO, the D/S sucks, thats why we -now- have the new OO features
> of COBOL to enjoy, adding scripting to a lang just complicates things.

No ambiguity in what you think about Dialog System is there <G>

But to be fair, Ray used it in DOS. When VISOC arrived without D/S he
'put it in the drawer', and jumped at it again when it was reintroduced
in NetExpress - and his application is highlighted by Merant.

Dialog System is a good tool - and had it been part of the package when
Visoc was issued I would DEFINITELY have gone that route, (and then
found I couldn't do my 10 x 24 entry-table). But by default, I fell into
OO/GUIs, gritted my teeth and carried on. On reflection I'm glad it
worked out that way, because it introduced me to OO, (which I probably
would have totally ignored had I been using Dialog System).

But Dialog System is a good tool - albeit you can't quite do everything
that we do with OO/GUI. As a generalization, I would say that all
packaged 'pre-canned' tools probably have some limitation - as against
say, the OO/GUI or Win API calls approach.

Curious - Thane, or our good friend Bob - can I produce a Progress Bar
in SP2/Flexus ?????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-35ef5edc94"></a>
## Thread: Collaborative Article on COBOL — 25 msgs

### Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Ed Milne writes ...

[snip my orignal post]

>Your list doesn't include the inadvertent problems that occur on
>coversions and the costs they entail. It sounds like your article is
>entended to sell the concept of upgrading to the latest COBOL regardless
>of the cost.

Well, I wouldn't put it exactly like that. But I do believe in keeping my tools
up to date. 

>To show the other side of the story consider the following list of
>conversion traps T 

(typo for "I"?)

>made up when converting IBM VS/COBOL (ANSI 74) to
>COBOL II (ANSI 84).

Well, ANSI 85

>
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>VS/COBOL.

What COBOL II did was enforce the rules better than OS/VS COBOL. The programmer
using a picture of S9 is telling the compiler that only values -9 to +9 are
valid. COBOL II says, "OK, I'll take you at your word because you wouldn't lie
to me."

The cause here was really not paying attention to details in the first place. A
good programmer would not have been so sloppy in the first place. The
conversion was good because it caught these kinds of errors in addition to
bringing you the benefits of the new compiler.


>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>physical size of the space used. 
I have always taught that packed fields shoud be defined with an odd number of
digits because that's how the hardware stores packed data. Again, this problem
has to do with careless programming, not any change in the language or
compiler.

>However, some fields, such as dates,
>should only have an even number of digits.
>

Not necessarily. There are lots of ways to store dates. In Julian formats, for
example (yyddd or yyyyddd or dddyy or dddyyyy) there are an odd number of
digits. You can store year, month, and day in separate binary, packed, or
display fields. I have become convinced that the right way to store dates is as
eight bytes of display data in yyyymmdd order.



>
>NEXT SENTENCE
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

But you don't have to do this as part of the conversion. The old code would
have worked fine. You chose to use the new features because you get some
benefit (easier to code, read, maintain). But you have to pay attention to
details.

Yes, I guess overall I would say the conversion was good for you because it
caught careless practices and therefore made your code more robust. This is
truly a benefit, even if one unlooked for.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
:>NEXT SENTENCE
:>After converting a program to COBOL II, it is natural to use the COBOL
:>II structures for ending statements rather than
:>periods for new code. This mix of old and new code can cause problems
:>with the NEXT SENTENCE clause in existing
:>statements.
:>
:>For example, in the following code
:>
:>    IF <test> THEN
:>      NEXT SENTENCE
:>    ELSE
:>      <move statement>
:>    END-IF
:>    <add statement>.
:>    <subtract statement>
:>
:>if the test is true, the next statement executed is the <subtract
:>statement> rather than the expected <add statement>. This happens
:>because a "NEXT SENTENCE" clause continues following the next period
:>rather than with the next statement.

NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF are invalid in the same statement.  Your
compiler may allow this but the standard doesn't.  The code above
should be rejected.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sbjkh$944@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
  <snip>
>
> NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF are invalid in the same statement.  Your
> compiler may allow this but the standard doesn't.  The code above
> should be rejected.
>

1) It is true that this is illegal according to the Standard.

2) (As far as I know) More compilers allow it (as an extension) than
prohibit it.

3) This "restriction" in the Standard is actually a totally USELESS
restriction, because the Standard does allow the following code:

NextSentence is the name of a "copy member" and it includes

   If A = A
       If A not= A
            Continue
       Else
             Next Sentence
   Else
            Continue
   End-IF

    ***
 then wherever you want to use NEXT SENTENCE in an "IF" that is terminated
with END-IF, you can (add a WS item called "A" with any old value) and code
the ANSI CONFORMING

   IF   whatever
          copy NextSentence.
   Else
         do something else
   End-IF


   ***

The reason that this is conforming - but using a NEXT SENTENCE instead of
the COPY statement, is fairly "obscure" but has been conformed by J4 and WG4
as "true".
```
