- **Message-ID:** `<37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF are invalid in the same statement.  

Why???  (why doesn't the standard allow it?)

> Your compiler may allow this but the standard doesn't.  

I bet most compilers allow it.

> The code above should be rejected.

Unless you have good reason for allowing it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:

:> NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF are invalid in the same statement.  

: Why???  (why doesn't the standard allow it?)

Because a mix of NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF is a sure-fire
bug generator.  If you were to write code that mixed these
then you may well know to avoid creating bugs around those
areas, but the _next_ programmer that follows on these is
bound to fall into your traps.

Consider AMSI-74:

       IF ( something )
           NEXT SENTENCE
       ELSE
           staements
           .

The NEXT SENTENCE is defined as, and acts as, a GO TO to the
anonymous label formed by the full stop.  The placement of
this label is forced by the syntax to be the termination of
the IF (or of all active IFs).

When ANSI-85 END-IF is added:

         IF ( something )
             NEXT SENTENCE
         ELSE
             statements
          END-IF
         .    <- full stop 1
         lines of statements
         .    <- full stop 2

The IF ... END-IF is a complete statement and does not require
full stop 1.  The only requirement for a full-stop is at the
end of the paragraph.  If the NEXT SENTENCE had been used
deliberately (maliciously) or naievely to go to a full stop
and full stop 1 was removed, or lines were added between the
END-IF and full-stop 1, then the effect of the NEXT SENTENCE
is to go to the wrong place.

This means that changes _outside_ the IF statement affect the
action of that IF statement.  This makes the mix of NEXT 
SENTENCE and END-IF in the same category as ALTERed GO TOs.

If fact it makes it worse because the full stop is anonymous
and may usually be considered a matter of style when outside
a conditional statement.

If, for example, full stop 1 was ommitted to deliberately
cause the NEXT SENTENCE to GO TO full stop 2 and an extra
line was added in 'lines of statements' then the effect of
this addition would be different if it was done by an 'always
add a full-stop' programmer or a 'only at end of paragraph'
programmer.  And this is _outside_ a conditional.

:> Your compiler may allow this but the standard doesn't.  

: I bet most compilers allow it.

Microfocus seems unable to detect the problem because it
has NEXT SENTENCE as a staement in its own right rather than
a clause of the IF statement (or as well as).  But whether
the compiler allows it, or more specifically does not
flag it as an error, is irrelevant.  There are many things
that compilers do not give compiler errors on that are
disallowed by the standard.

:> The code above should be rejected.

: Unless you have good reason for allowing it.

There no _good_ reasons.  There may be _bad_ reasons (such
as maliciously wanting to trap the next programmer or ensuring
full employment in fixing ever more obscure bugs).

Of course, if you can think of a _good_ reason (laziness
doesn't count) then please do state what it might be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sgomk$f46@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:
>
  <snip>
>
> : I bet most compilers allow it.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is simply "hogwash".

There is a SIGNIFICANT difference between extensions and errors.

Micro Focus has many *documented* EXTENSIONS - and UNDER NO CIRCUMSTANCES
should a documented extension get an error.

Micro Focus (like *ALL* ANSI/ISO/FIPS conforming compilers) provides a
FLAGGING (not error) method for detecting "NEXT SENTENCE" in an IF
terminated with an End-IF (or in other cases where the Standard doesn't
allow them).

It is true that some compilers do "miss" some errors - but most of them will
take "error/bug reports" on such situations - but please, PLEASE, don't
confuse what the Standard allows with what is required to get "error"
messages.

If you don't know how to get MF (or Fujitsu, or IBM, or any other compiler)
to issue "non-conformance" messages, then check their documentation.

(I will respond to some of the other parts of this "I hate NEXT SENTENCE"
diatribe in a separate note, but this just needed its own reply - because it
is so off base.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Next Sentence, etc (was: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sha5q$rid@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sgomk$f46@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7sh6t9$7hj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7sh6t9$7hj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...

>
> : Micro Focus has many *documented* EXTENSIONS - and UNDER NO
CIRCUMSTANCES
> : should a documented extension get an error.
>
> They do in the circumstance of having a NOMF directive.

Have you ever read the MF documentation?  It has been quite a while since I
had to explain what the various compiler directives do in MF.  (These are
all well documented in the MF manuals - but you seem unable to understand
what they say.)

MF, OSVS, VSC2, etc are a set of directives which ONLY determine which
Reserved Words are "turned on" for a specific compilation.  They do not
(directly) impact what syntax is or is not supported.  Therefore, if a
specific "syntax construct" does not require any reserved words outside of
the set supported by the language directives selected, that syntax is
supported.  (For example, the words "NEXT" and "SENTENCE" are included with
EVERY language dialect possible - including NOMF.)

ON THE OTHER HAND,  there are also a set of "flagging" directives which are
aimed at determining whether a certain piece of syntax is or is not
supported in the dialect chosen.  For example, FLAG(ANS85) will detect ALL
"extensions" which are not part of the ANSI Standard.  (There is a slight
difference betweent the FLAG(ANS85) and FLAGSTD directives - which is best
documented by MF.  But the bottom-line is that the former is for ANSI
conformance while the latter is follows the FIPS rules - the 2 are quite
similar but the format of messages differs.)

Similarly FLAG(OSVS), FLAG(VSC2), etc check for syntax outside the language
definitions of those products.

There are a couple of other options that MF offers that can "modify" the
"severity level" of flagging messages (either on a universal level - or
message-by-message).

   ***

Each compiler (that I know of) has various types of messages and methods of
selecting "dialects".  Compare the OS/VS COBOL "LANGLVL(1)" and "LANGLVL(2)"
options - which are often misunderstood as ANS74 and ANS68.

Bottom-Line: (as I said originally) - If a vendor documents something as an
extension and you have selected the options that specify for ALLOWING their
extensions, then the extensions should not (EVER) get an error message.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Next Sentence, etc (was: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sojb9$cku@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sgomk$f46@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7sh6t9$7hj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sha5q$rid@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Given all the discussion on the merits, sins, or whatever of "Next
Sentence," I thought that I should be "fair" and mention the (so far
unmentioned) fact that

"Next Sentence" (*regardless* of in what construct) is being added to the
new (with the next Standard) category called "archaic".

This means that it is NOT "obsolete" (which would mean - theoretically at
least - that it would be removed in the NEXT Standard after this draft one),
but rather that it is a technique that when/if it ever becomes "little used"
(replaced by another construct), will THEN be placed in the OBSOLETE
category and removed in the Standard after that.

What this means (sort-of) is that it is guaranteed to be included in both
the next Standard and the Standard after that (2100???) but might be removed
at some Standard after that.  (FYI, it does NOT mean that the feature will
not/never be "enhanced" - i.e. some future Standard *might* allow it with
END-IF - but I would say that this is unlikely.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> :> The code above should be rejected.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't count) then please do state what it might be.

I work in an IDMS shop with a pre-compiler putting in IF code with NEXT
SENTENCE.  I always terminate their code with a period as the surest way
of making sure the IF logic is finished.  I also use NEXT SENTENCE
within those sentences to make sure I know where the logic will drop
through.  Yes, I am knowledgeable enough to know what code the
pre-compiler will make, but I agree that the next programmer may not be.

This doesn't quite answer your question because I can avoid having
pre-compiler statements within nested IF's, but your reasoning for
disallowing mixing NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF, basically said "NEXT
SENTENCE is bad, since END IF makes NEXT SENTENCE unnecessary, they
should not be allowed together".   Explaining why NEXT SENTENCE is bad
doesn't explain why it should only be disallowed if the IF ends with an
END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:
:> 
:> Of course, if you can think of a _good_ reason (laziness
:> doesn't count) then please do state what it might be.

: This doesn't quite answer your question because I can avoid having
: pre-compiler statements within nested IF's, but your reasoning for
: disallowing mixing NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF, basically said "NEXT
: SENTENCE is bad, since END IF makes NEXT SENTENCE unnecessary, they

I think that you would have trouble finding any words from me
that actually got close to your interpretation.

It is CONTINUE that make NEXT SENTENCE unnecessary and this is
quite independant of whether END-IF is used.  If an IF or nested
IF without END-IFs the NEXT SENTENCEs can be replaced by
CONTINUE without change of effect even though the two have
different meaning.

When an END-IF is introduced the NEXT SENTENCE becomes invalid
Cobol according to the standard (though an extension may allow
it to be used with varying actual effects).

: should not be allowed together".   Explaining why NEXT SENTENCE is bad
: doesn't explain why it should only be disallowed if the IF ends with an
: END-IF.

As I have specifically stated that NEXT SENTENCE is not bad when
not used in conjunction with END-IF, I find your statement
puzzling.  I have quite clearly stated in all my messages that
it is 'NEXT SENTENCE in conjunction with END-IF' that is
bad.

It is not me that disallows it, it is the standard that does so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> different meaning.

I understand this, but the standard apparently didn't make it illegal to
have CONTINUE and NEXT SENTENCE in the same IF structure, or at least
that wasn't part of the discussion.

It very well could be that I missed the point - but I still have no idea
why they made this combination illegal.
 
> When an END-IF is introduced the NEXT SENTENCE becomes invalid
> Cobol according to the standard (though an extension may allow
> it to be used with varying actual effects).

Yes, but why did they decide to make this rule?  What harm would have
been done if they had left it alone?


> : should not be allowed together".   Explaining why NEXT SENTENCE is bad
> : doesn't explain why it should only be disallowed if the IF ends with an
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> bad.

I thought you said that it "against standards", in that case.  I asked
why the standards committee decided to make it against the standards.  I
thought you were describing why NEXT SENTENCE was undesirable (bad), and
because of this they could outlaw it in the case an END-IF were used. 
Apparently I missed your real reason given as to why they outlawed it,
which doesn't surprise me, as the reason I inferred is completely
bogus.  I still have no idea why it was outlawed.
 
> It is not me that disallows it, it is the standard that does so.

Understood.  I hope someone could supply me with the answer as to why
the standards committee chose to do so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Next Sentence - "Why?" (was: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sooth$ogb@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com...
> Richard Plinston wrote:
   <snip>
>
> It very well could be that I missed the point - but I still have no idea
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <various snippage>

> > It is not me that disallows it, it is the standard that does so.
>
> Understood.  I hope someone could supply me with the answer as to why
> the standards committee chose to do so.

OK, let's try and answer this.  (By way of "preface," The Standards
committees "hate" when they get "why did you do this" questions - especially
when they come in years after the "decision" was made.  Therefore, this is
definitely MY understanding and not necessarily "truth" on their reasoning.)

A) The JOD (Journal of Development) which was (while the '85 Standard was
being developed) the "source" of new enhancements to COBOL - *DID* allow a
NEXT SENTENCE to be in an IF statement that was terminated by an END-IF
clause.

B) During development of the '85 Standard, there were some (but definitely
not all) of the developers who agreed with Richard that mixing the "new"
CONTINUE statement with the old NEXT SENTENCE clause would lead to
maintenance problems.

C) If you put in enough "switches," then every NEXT SENTENCE's "logic" CAN
be re-coded with a CONTINUE - but the reverse is NOT true.  Therefore, the
more "general" syntax, CONTINUE, was seen as the "syntax of the future".

D) Therefore, (I think) they decided to remove the JOD facility and stay
with a more "restricted" syntax of allowing CONTINUE with END-IF, to keep
"upward compatibility" of NEXT SENTENCE withOUT END-IF, but to disallow the
"old" NEXT SENTENCE with the new END-IF (if they were both at the same level
of "nesting").

   ***

Because some users and vendors have found the ANSI/ISO restriction
counter-productive and/or counter-intuitive, many (but definitely not all)
vendors have explicitly allowed (as a documented extension), the old NEXT
SENTENCE clause to be used with the new END-IF (and END-SEARCH) feature.

Does this help (at all)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Next Sentence - "Why?" (was: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F0C011.126EE9EC@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sooth$ogb@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> > Understood.  I hope someone could supply me with the answer as to why
> > the standards committee chose to do so.
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

Yes.  It sounds to me that they're saying "NEXT SENTENCE" is bad coding,
but we can't disallow it because it is used so much, so we're
disallowing it when it is used with obviously new code.  Nothing
particular about END-IF which makes NEXT SENTENCE worse, except that
END-IF is obviously newer code, giving them the opportunity to force us
to code "correctly".

Trouble is, there are cases (with some pre-compilers), where we still
need to use NEXT SENTENCE and full stop periods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf0940$ec4a4330$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in article
<37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com>...
> It very well could be that I missed the point - but I still have no idea
> why they made this combination illegal.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > it to be used with varying actual effects).

I agree with you.  Why this fuzz about NEXT SENTENCE?  After all it works
as designed.  It is by definition a GO TO a virtual label after the next
period, the period is the sentence terminator.  When used in an IF
construct it works exactly that way.  I can even see a sensible use for it.
 Imagine a lot of code interspersed in nested IF/ELSE/END-IFs, and a period
at some point after the outermost IF/END-IF.  Then NEXT SENTENCE at any
place within this mess will bring you directly after the period.  That can
be used as error exits.  Of course GO TO and a real label will do the same
thing, but as I understand it is not comme-il-faut to use GO TO nowadays
(or has that changed).

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7srla6$h2f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com> <01bf0940$ec4a4330$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
Gunnar Opheim <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote:
:> > When an END-IF is introduced the NEXT SENTENCE becomes invalid
:> > Cobol according to the standard (though an extension may allow
:> > it to be used with varying actual effects).

: I agree with you.  Why this fuzz about NEXT SENTENCE?  A
: After all it works
: as designed.  

It was designed to work without END-IFs.  Yes it always
works exactly as this design would imply.

A
: It is by definition a GO TO a virtual label after the next
: period, the period is the sentence terminator.  When used in an IF
: construct it works exactly that way.  I can even see a sensible use for it.

Exactly, no problem, use it myself. (without END-IF).

:  Imagine a lot of code interspersed in nested IF/ELSE/END-IFs, and a period
: at some point after the outermost IF/END-IF.  Then NEXT SENTENCE at any
: place within this mess will bring you directly after the period.  That can
: be used as error exits.  Of course GO TO and a real label will do the same
: thing, but as I understand it is not comme-il-faut to use GO TO nowadays
: (or has that changed).

It is specified that way in current MF compilers as an extension.
Older compilers or use of OLDNEXTSENTENCE have it as equivalent
to a CONTINUE, ie they go to the end of the outermost IF, the
point at which the full stop _should_ be.

Other compilers may have either of these behaviours as an
extension or they may disallow NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF entirely.

The Fujitsu wording expects a full stop terminating the IF in
that it goes to the sentence following the IF statement.

That is it expects the next sentence to follow on immediately
after the IF statement, which would be true where a full stop
is used to terminate the IF.

The problem is, however, little to do with the IF or the NEXT
SENTENCE but is entirely to do with full stops.  The target
of the NEXT SENTENCE, when abused, is an anonymous label
and thus is a potential bug-nest.

Where OO is a mechanism to improve reliability by encapsulating
and localising control and other effects, this misuse of
NEXT SENTENCE is the opposite in that the effect of the NEXT
SENTENCE depends on code that is outside the statement it is
in (ie the adding or removing of full stops in the rest of
the paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oIryNwtOS00QwvsjuPJD3HfX=x=8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com> <01bf0940$ec4a4330$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <7srla6$h2f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ssv6h$h04$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Wed, 29 Sep 1999 07:54:39 -0400, "donald tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>Richard Plinston wrote in message <7srla6$h2f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>>Gunnar Opheim <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>> etc.

Now Randall is going to write.

The semantic problem stems from the fact that NEXT SENTENCE is NEXT
SENTENCE.  If NEXT SENTENCE had been NEXT STATEMENT then it would have
meant "next statement" which is what CONTINUE means.  Unfortunately,
NEXT SENTENCE is NEXT SENTENCE and is defined as "next sentence".
This leads to the following counter-intuitive behavior:

IF  COND-A
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
    PERFORM PARA-A  
END-IF
PERFORM PARA-B.

If you implement NEXT SENTENCE as "next statement", PARA-B is
performed unconditionally.  If you implement NEXT SENTENCE as "next
sentence", PARA-B is performed only when COND-A is false.  AFAIK, JOD
was never clear on this, but J4 has clearly ruled that NEXT SENTENCE
means "next sentence", and therefore PARA-B is dependent on COND-A.  

But J4 has admitted that using NEXT SENTENCE this way is illogical and
destined to confuse maintenance programmers.  Furthermore, there are
compilers which have always implemented NEXT SENTENCE as "next
statement".  I believe all the major ones have a compile time option
to make NEXT SENTENCE mean "next sentence", but many are hesitant to
turn on an option to get non-intuitive behavior.

Therefore, J4 has banned the mixing of NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF, and
designated NEXT SENTENCE archaic.  All good Cobol programmers should
use CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE.  CONTINUE is easier to type,
and where the two constructs differ, NEXT SENTENCE is illogical and
possibly inconsistently implemented.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F3646B.E01839E9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com> <01bf0940$ec4a4330$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <7srla6$h2f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7ssv6h$h04$1@news.igs.net> <oIryNwtOS00QwvsjuPJD3HfX=x=8@4ax.com>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:

> Therefore, J4 has banned the mixing of NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF, and
> designated NEXT SENTENCE archaic.  All good Cobol programmers should
> use CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE.  CONTINUE is easier to type,
> and where the two constructs differ, NEXT SENTENCE is illogical and
> possibly inconsistently implemented.

If they have a pre-compiler putting in IF logic, good programmers should
code unambiguous code which will be clear whether or not the
pre-compiler inserted code is visible.  This usually means putting in
full stop periods and often means putting in NEXT SENTENCE to get around
the unseen code.

Other than that, I do not see any reason to use NEXT SENTENCE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F20F92.48BEEE44@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sb9nn$nen$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EBB00E.C6BE3B28@NOSPAMhome.com> <7sgn1g$3rq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EF732D.913E7BBA@NOSPAMhome.com> <7soi09$nvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <37EFD991.4532D422@NOSPAMhome.com> <01bf0940$ec4a4330$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <7srla6$h2f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Gunnar Opheim <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> works exactly as this design would imply.

But so was ELSE (designed to work without END-IFs).  Adding the END-IF
in no way changed the advantage or disadvantage of using ELSE or of
using NEXT SENTENCE.   It simply allowed the compiler to determine
whether the code is legacy  (pre END-IF) or not.

If the standards committee is going to use that enforce their coding
styles, why not work on something which really makes a difference?
```

#### ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sd2kn$vgb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
[snip]
> >COMP-3 Fields
> >
> >Problem
> >
> >The version of VS/COBOL used before August 1996 used the full
physical
> >size of a COMP-3 field regardless of the picture.
> >If a picture only defined an even number of digits, an extra digit
was
> >automatically used. For example, the compiler treated
> >"COMP-3 PIC S9(4)" as if it were "COMP-3 PIC S9(5)". However, COBOL
II
> >truncates any values moved to such fields to
> >the number of digits actually defined.
> >
> >For example, suppose a program contains a field defined as "COMP-3
PIC
> >S9(4)" and moves 12345 to that field. Under the old VS/COBOL compiler
> >the result is 12345. Under the new VS/COBOL compiler and COBOL II,
the
> >result is 2345.
> >
> >Programs recompiled since August 1996 should have been changed to
> >correct this problem. However, any reengineering project may
recompile
> >programs that have not been changed in many years.
> >
> >Solution
> >
> >Change the pictures for all COMP-3 fields so they define an odd
number
> >of digits unless they should only contain an even number of digits.
Most
> >fields should have an odd number of digits to take full advantage of
the
> >physical size of the space used.
> I have always taught that packed fields shoud be defined with an odd
number of
> digits because that's how the hardware stores packed data. Again, this
problem
> has to do with careless programming, not any change in the language or
> compiler.

Yes, it is better programming practice to use an odd number of digits
with COMP-3 fields.  But the point is that an upgrade to a new version
of a COBOL compiler from the same manufacturer destabilized a previously
working program.  Because of this possibility, you have to treat an
upgrade as a mass change to all the programs in a system with all the
appropriate cost and timing implications.

>
> >However, some fields, such as dates,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not necessarily. There are lots of ways to store dates. In Julian
formats, for
> example (yyddd or yyyyddd or dddyy or dddyyyy) there are an odd number
of
> digits. You can store year, month, and day in separate binary, packed,
or
> display fields. I have become convinced that the right way to store
dates is as
> eight bytes of display data in yyyymmdd order.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >After converting a program to COBOL II, it is natural to use the
COBOL
> >II structures for ending statements rather than
> >periods for new code. This mix of old and new code can cause problems
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Make a mass change of all occurrences of "NEXT SENTENCE" to
"CONTINUE".
> >The new CONTINUE clause does nothing. It just continues with the next
> >statement, which is the real intent of the "NEXT SENTENCE" clause.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But you don't have to do this as part of the conversion. The old code
would
> have worked fine. You chose to use the new features because you get
some
> benefit (easier to code, read, maintain). But you have to pay
attention to
> details.

Your original post stated that one of the justifications upgrades was
the ability to use new features.  However, you have to weigh that
against the cost of destabilizing the programs.

>
> Yes, I guess overall I would say the conversion was good for you
because it
> caught careless practices and therefore made your code more robust.
This is
> truly a benefit, even if one unlooked for.

The people that you are addressing and this newsgroup and IEEE are all
techies who recognize the personal benefits of learning the latest and
greatest software.  We benefit regardless of what happens to the systems
we convert.  So your proposed article is preaching to the converted.

However, the user department who paid for the upgrade still feel that
they got no benefit from the project.  BTW, the project was to modernize
 a legacy payroll system that was bad code to start and then was hacked
over for 30 years.  The programmers who maintain the system love the
version.  The users don't see any difference.  That is the real problem
with upgrades.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Collaborative Article on COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sd61d$md3@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990922120110.00284.00000917@ng-fs1.aol.com> <7sd2kn$vgb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:7sd2kn$vgb$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> [snip]
  <much nore snippage>
> >
> > But you don't have to do this as part of the conversion. The old code
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Probably the reason why (at least in the case of IBM - OS/VS COBOL to VS
COBOL II *or* the more resent COBOL for this-and-that) made it perfectly
clear that you did NOT need to "upgrade" or change - much less "destabilize"
existing code.  The old code continues to work as it did then and can even
be called from new programs.  The only time that things CHANGE are when you
actually *do* write new code or maintain the old code (or - because it is
cost-justified based on other benefits) decide to re-compile existing code
with the new compiler.

As a reminder both OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II *object* code is FULLY
support today, after March 2001 (the drop date for VS COBOL II), and for the
foreseeable future - if you are using the LE run-time.
```

- **From:** "Nik Billings" <nikb@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2tZF3.1$Rb1.60@news1.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
I'm using Win98 and Deskware Cobol 0.015

the line:
 ACCEPT Val1.

<a id="t-a13f473377"></a>
## Thread: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING? — 19 msgs

### From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I know that the PARM= data is available to the executed program via the
USING on the PROCEDURE DIVISION statement and a LINKAGE SECTION
defination.

I just can't seem to find it in the manuals for COBOL for MVS and VM on
IBM's Bookmanager site.

Where is it documented?

TIA,
Bill
```

#### ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I *never* can find this in the manual when I look for it.  It is there (and
I usually find it 2 or 3 days later) but it can't be found by any "logical"
search.  HOWEVER, the answer is:

1) In your linkage section code:

   01  Passed-Data.
          05  Parm-Len   Pic S9(04) Comp.
          05  Real-Data.
              10  Each-Byte Occurs 1 to 100 times depending on Parm-Len
                                   Pic X.

   ****
or better yet (as you are using an LE-enabled COBOL) look for CEE3PRM which
does it for you. See
  http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA306/3%2e5%2e12
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

- **From:** Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sdsai$jak$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com> wrote:
> I *never* can find this in the manual when I look for it.  It is
there (and
> I usually find it 2 or 3 days later) but it can't be found by any
"logical"
> search.  HOWEVER, the answer is:
<snip>

I was trying to figure this out a few months ago.  I am convinced that
IBM COBOL II and IBM COBOL for MVS handle this differently, and here is
my evidence:

1.  My program worked when compiled with teh COBOL II compiler and it
didn't with the COBOL for MVS compiler.
2.  Section 7.2, "Run-Time Options", in the "VS COBOL II Application
Programming Guide" (which can be found at
http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYA1101/7%
2e2?SHELF=IGYSH007) describes exactly what you say.
3.  As the original poster said, the COBOL for MVS/VM manual has no
reference to this process.

Call me crazy, but I can't get it to work.

-Art


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

Art Perry wrote:
> 
> my evidence:
> 
> 1.  My program worked when compiled with teh COBOL II compiler and it
> didn't with the COBOL for MVS compiler.

Did you compile with SSRANGE in the new release?

Did you get a S0C4 that was hidden by a bad implementation in Cobol
II?

Are you trying to use more than 100 bytes?


The code supplied was exactly right.  Please post enough of your code
for us to help to work it out.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sh8q8$t98$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7sdsai$jak$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37EB7F7C.C06B9404@zip.com.au>`

```
I just wanted to note that I tried this today using COBOL II on VSE/ESA and
it worked perfectly.  I've been trying for ages to figure out how to do
this, too!  I had it almost right, but I had the length as PIC S9(8) COMP
instead of S9(4) COMP, and I had the length and the parm as two separate 01
levels instead of both under one 01.  Ah well, so close...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqlk2$9ap$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7sdsai$jak$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37EB7F7C.C06B9404@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <37EB7F7C.C06B9404@zip.com.au>,
  Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
> Did you compile with SSRANGE in the new release?
No.  I compiled it with the default (NOSSRANGE)
> Did you get a S0C4 that was hidden by a bad implementation in Cobol
> II?
No.  The program ended with a zero return code.
> Are you trying to use more than 100 bytes?
No.
> The code supplied was exactly right.  Please post enough of your code
> for us to help to work it out.
Here is the code.  The output (SYSOUT) is garbage, so I won't post it
here, unless you think it will help.

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200   PROGRAM-ID. PARMTEST.
000300   AUTHOR. ART PERRY.
000400 DATA DIVISION.
000500* WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000600  LINKAGE SECTION.
000700   01  LINK-PARM.
000800       05 LINK-PARM-LENGTH             PIC 9(04) BINARY.
000900       05 LINK-PARM-DATA               PIC X(100).
001000
001100 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
001200     DISPLAY LINK-PARM-DATA (1:LINK-PARM-LENGTH).
001300     GOBACK
001400     .

Here is the compiler warning I get:

LineID  Message code  Message Text
     7  IGYSC2025-W   "LINK-PARM" or one of its subordinates was
                      referenced, but "LINK-PARM" was a "LINKAGE
                      SECTION" item that did not have addressability.
                      This reference will not be resolved successfully
                      at execution.

Do I need a set statement or something?

Thanks,

-Art


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f3eef6.49250448@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7sdsai$jak$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37EB7F7C.C06B9404@zip.com.au> <7sqlk2$9ap$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 1999 15:07:58 GMT, Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com> wrote:

>In article <37EB7F7C.C06B9404@zip.com.au>,
>  Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The first Commandment: Thou Shallst NEVER ignore warnings!

The second Commandment:  NEVER,  no exceptions


The warning you get is actually a very severe error indicator. The compiler (and thus your
program) has no way of addressing the areas described in the Linkage Section.   On your
PROCEDURE DIvisision you would have to provide this information:

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Link-Parm.

That should make a lot of a difference

     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         A confident manner is important; computers can sense this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990928133136.08803.00001028@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7sqlk2$9ap$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7sqlk2$9ap$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com>
writes:

>001000
>001100 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It looks like you need to add USING LINK-PARM to your Procedure division
so it know to pick up something from the PARMS and put its address into 
your program at LINK-PARM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqmjv$9uo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7sdsai$jak$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37EB7F7C.C06B9404@zip.com.au> <7sqlk2$9ap$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7sqlk2$9ap$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Art Perry <eowynfuzz@my-deja.com> wrote:

> 000600  LINKAGE SECTION.
> 000700   01  LINK-PARM.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 001100 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

***RED FACE ALERT***
In reviewing the code AFTER I posted the code, I realized I forgot the
USING clause of the PROCEDURE DIVISION statement.  OOPS.

I added it (PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK-PARM) and guess what?  It
worked.

Sorry for the screw-up, and I humbly retract any "Attitude"  I may have
displayed in the previous posts.  Thanks for all your help!

-Art


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

> 
> I *never* can find this in the manual when I look for it.  It is there (and
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                    Pic X.

I never thought to do an occurs depending on here.  I always expect to
know exactly what was passed (the parm length can show how many parms
were passed from JCL).  But anyway, I always have a program example to
cut and paste so I never had to look it up.
 
>    ****
> or better yet (as you are using an LE-enabled COBOL) look for CEE3PRM which
> does it for you. See
>   http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA306/3%2e5%2e12

Just looked this up.  I am trying to figure why a Cobol program would
use this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F0C28A.3FDC5C8C@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sbkut$9ue@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <37EF7D9A.217317D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > 1) In your linkage section code:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> cut and paste so I never had to look it up.

Traditionally we tend to code just the variables like:

	05   FILLER pic x(02).
	05   DEBUG-FLAG X(01).

This is actually an error but since the JCL is controlled the likely
hood of it falling over is pretty slim.

Correct version:
	05  parm-len  s9(04)  comp.
	05  DEBUG-FLAG
...
	if parm-len < 1
		display 'invalid parameter expect debug flag'
		move 15 to return-code
		stop run
	end-if

Some things are not 'really' required when you code in a controlled
environment.

Ken
```

    05  PARM-TEXT           PIC X(whatever).

JCL:
    EXEC PGM=FOO,PARM='HELLO WORLD'

#### ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sd3jr$2s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7sbc2u$pm3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com> wrote:
> I know that the PARM= data is available to the executed program via
the
> USING on the PROCEDURE DIVISION statement and a LINKAGE SECTION
> defination.

This is the code that I use to process parameters, where PARM1 is just a
switch that is present or not and PARM2 has a related value - i.e.
PARM2=value.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WS-PARMS.
    05 WS-PARMS-DATA  OCCURS 10 VALUE SPACE         PIC X(20).

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  LINK-PARMS.
    05 LINK-PARM-LENGTH                       COMP  PIC 9999.
    05 LINK-PARM-DATA                               PIC X(200).

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK-PARMS.
UNSTRING LINK-PARM-DATA(1:LINK-PARM-LENGTH)
  DELIMITED BY ',' OR '='
  INTO WS-PARMS-DATA(1)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(2)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(3)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(4)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(5)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(6)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(7)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(8)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(9)
       WS-PARMS-DATA(10)
PERFORM
  VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
  UNTIL SUB1 > 9
  EVALUATE WS-PARMS-DATA(SUB1)
  WHEN 'PARM1'
    SET PARM1-SET               TO TRUE
  WHEN 'PARM2'
    MOVE WS-PARMS-DATA(SUB1 + 1)
                                TO PARM2-VALUE
    ADD  1                      TO SUB1
  WHEN OTHER
    DISPLAY 'UNKNOWN PARAMETER '
            WS-PARMS-DATA(SUB1)
    STOP RUN
  END-EVALUATE
END-PERFORM
```

#### ↳ Re: From PARM= to PROC DIV USING?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>

Bill Thompson wrote:
> 
> I know that the PARM= data is available to the executed program via the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Where is it documented?

'Don't know, but this is how it's done:

LINKAGE SECTION.
1	PARMS.
	5	PARM-LENGTH	PIC S9999 BINARY SYNC.
	5	PARM-DATA	PIC X(?).

PROCEDURE DIVISION  USING  PARMS.

That's it. Good technique involves checking the length to see if the
PARM=... was actually coded.

Bill Lynch

Note: IBM Mainframe only - can't speak for other flavours.
```

<a id="t-8f8723b0ea"></a>
## Thread: COPY in Procedure Division — 18 this month / 23 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).

I won't quote the full text, but this person thought that this was an
"awful" idea (my word, not his/her).  Although I don't use this technique
all that often, I certainly have used on multiple occasions.  I find it
particularly useful when coding I/O logic, exception processing, and other
common (across program/application) logic.

I am wondering how other feel about this as a "general" technique - good,
bad, indifferent?
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e984cb@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
If you have common code to put in a COPY statement in the procedure
division, I would consider putting that instead in a subprogram you would
call.

Too many COPY books are something to be avoided.
I have seen programs like:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG1.
COPY CCC111.
COPY CCC112.
COPY DDD333.
COPY CCC115.
PROCEDURE DIVISION
COPY PPP111.
COPY QQQ222.
COPY AAA333.
IF AA > BB
   COPY BBB555.        <== NOTE REQUIRED POINT
ELSE
   COPY XXX777.
.
COPY VVV666.
COPY BBB999.
STOP RUN.

news:7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com...

> Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EA245F.F6A5A4FD@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

I have seen copybooks used well.  For example in a personnel
application called CHRIS that I worked on it was non platform specific
and hid the CICS stuff in standard copybooks and subroutines.  The
main driver was copied in and as Volker said overridden:

   COPY STDPROC  replacing ==standard-verification== with
			 ==special-verification==

This is effectively macro processing.   OK but i would prefer easier
macro methods.


I have seen the result of putting copy book code into place and then
changing from sections to paragraph (for standards) causing very
interesting results.

In most cases it should be subroutines.  You can get better
reusability from subroutines than performs e.g.:

	Call 'chknum' using number1 num1rslt.
	call 'chknum' using number2 num2rslt.

instead of;
	move number1 to called-number
	perform ..
	move number2....

The only problem I have with calls in cobol is that you cannot put a
definition into place on them, as the above sample:

	call 'chknum'  using number3.

will probably bomb with a protection error rather than the compiler
picking it up.

The advantages of calls over copybooks is that you can save time
recompiling if you use make on PC or Unix and SCLM (which has a make
built in) on MVS.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<56qG3.1085$2Q5.10452@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
>Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).
>...
>I am wondering how other feel about this as a "general" technique - good,
>bad, indifferent?

One of the prime tenets of good structured design is that procedures
should be self contained with minimal linkage.  By their very nature,
Procedure Division COPY files are conceptually a type of 'procedure',
whether the resultant code is actually structured as such.  To include
straight line code in a COPY statement increases at least the implied,
or potential, linkage, and strike me as a bad idea.  I prefer to make
all COPY files to be free standing paragraphs, or sets of paragraphs,
placing necessary Working Storage fields in an associated COPY, with
well defined input, output, and function.  Of course, making the COPY
a callable subprogram would also be fine.  My $.02  :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E991FD.D961B016@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> bad, indifferent?

Simply - it is a a good idea if it does what you want. Given a
compiler that triggers off re-compiles where copy files are changed,
you've got the best of both worlds.

You'll appreeciate that with OO I don't need to do it as I can call
methods which would be those copy file procedures. (Reusability !) 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e9a353.171144403@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I maintain a system that uses this MESS.  I think it might have
STARTED as a fairly good idea, but it grows into a gordian knot.

Let's say there is a file called HISTORY we have

HISTORYFS
HISTORYFD
HISTORYDS
HISTORYWS

And so on - the DS is the declaratives.  Now, they found some
commonality in the declaratives, so the HISTORYDS has MORE in it than
just the history file - requireing the inclusion of other FD's and
WS's because the fields are referred to but you might not even be
using that file!

Also, we use them in the prodedure division.  And I HATE HATE HATE it.
It's such a TERRIBLE idea I can't imagine why anyone ever considered
it.  We have one that has different sections with different
paragraphs.  Some of these are performed in some programs, but few use
all of them - but you have to include all the WS related to all fields
and FILES referenced in that procedure copy member.

I'm sure it can be done correctly, and be useful.  But it wasn't where
I am and I have to fight with it DAILY and I DO NOT LIKE IT.



On Wed, 22 Sep 1999 20:25:09 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
>Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EA6458.A999A157@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e9a353.171144403@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> I maintain a system that uses this MESS.  I think it might have
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

OK, OK. So Thane has illustrated a real stinker. The originator had to
have been a bozo to copy all that FD stuff. When I said it was OK for
copies, I was thinking along the lines of Judson, (including date
routines).

Want to take the concept to extremes ? So let's design an Accounts
Receivable system :-

	1. Main Menu
	2. Edit Customers
	3. Print Customers
	4. Enter transactions
	5......
	30..........

So 30 programs if I do it in 0O. Nah ! Let's write one class called
'Doit' and it has the following methods :- 

	method-id. Main Menu
	method-id. Edit Customers
	method-id. Print Customers
	method-id. Enter transactions
	5......
	method-id. 30..........

It is possible AND it might work - but I think the general consensus
would be that I was a candidate for the Funny Farm !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ebb900.2896374@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e9a353.171144403@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Wed, 22 Sep 1999 20:25:09 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
>I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am wondering how other feel about this as a "general" technique - good,
>bad, indifferent?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I do use COPY in the procedure division for repetetive things. An Example is while
processing input variables from a CICS map, I just code

	COPY MRGFIELD REPLACING ==(FIELD)==  WITH This-Screen-Var

where MRGFIELD contains

           MOVE Z(FIELD)F TO DFHBMFLG
           IF DFHCURSR
              SET cursor-on-(FIELD) TO TRUE
           END-IF
           IF Z(FIELD)L > 0
              MOVE Z(FIELD)I TO CA-Z(FIELD)I
           ELSE
              IF DFHERASE
                 MOVE LOW-VALUES TO CA-Z(FIELD)I
              END-IF
           END-IF

Of course, I would LOVE to have a decent macro preprocessor, like the one provided with
PL/I


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         WinErr: 00C Memory hog error - More Ram needed. More! More! More!
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sckc1$br8$2@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
    The major complaint that I have is the need to use two copys files, in
different places in the program if you need additional working storage.

    Managing "work" data names is one of the weaknesses of cobol in my
opinion.  Some other languages (example pascal) allow you to define a data
name in the "paragraph" (forget what pascal calls it) that does not exist
outside of that small area.  The best you can do with Cobol is another
program.  Even entry's use a common working storage.

    This sounds off topic, but is not if you think about it a little.  When
I write a general purpose procedure copy, and I need a temp data name,
creating a data name that I can count on NOT existing anywhere else can be a
problem.  Even if I remember the extra WS copy.



William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com...
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
> Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kmqG3.1113$2Q5.10638@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7sckc1$br8$2@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
>    The major complaint that I have is the need to use two copys files, in
>different places in the program if you need additional working storage.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem.  Even if I remember the extra WS copy.

COBOL simply requires more programmer discipline to maintain proper data
segregation.  The data naming method I use has served me well for several
decades.  I use carefully named 01 entries for each COPY procedure, then
create a 3-6 character acronym from that name.  Then I append the acronym
to each data name under the 01.

For example, for a COPY paragraph named "DATE-EDIT", the associated 01
entry might be "DATE-EDIT-WORK", and all the subordinate entries would be
prefixed with "DEW-" or "DATWRK-".  When I develop systems, I maintain a
data name dictionary and abbreviation dictionary for that system so every
field is named exactly the same thing everywhere it is defined or used,
except for the unique prefix of the particular 01 record it is in (COPY
file or no).  Each file, of course, has its own unique prefix.  Print
file, temporary work file, and Working Storage 01 record prefixes need
only be unique within the program.  Using "WS-" as a prefix for all
Working Storage entries is pointless, because some programs have hundreds,
perhaps thousands, of such entries.  Unique prefixes tell you which 01
record is involved at every reference.  Plus, because the prefixes are
unique, the rest of the data name can be consistent throughout the whole
system.
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Boris Nashtut" <altcom@san.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4rTG3.97$e5.4459@newsr1.san.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
There are 2 considerations for me in using COPY in Procedure Division.
Development:
When I develop modifications for the system the COPY statement provides ease
of implementation. I can change one copybook and it would eventually apply
to many load modules in many libraries. I can make last minute change and
start mass recompile.
Debugging:
When I work on production support and get calls in the middle of the night
because system is down/abend, the COPY statements caused me allot of
trouble. I have to constantly switch from 'source' library to 'copy' library
just to read code. This is the time when I HATE COPY statement.

Called program would be preferred method for me in both of the above
instances.

P.S. I work in mainframe environment with many production libraries and
often provide support for programs I have never  seen.
I read this group periodically and find this group very helpful - first time
posting.
Thank you, Boris

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com...
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
> Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sg7rg$9kd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Sir:

IMHO

It is just another possible tool to be used.  Like any other it can be
misused and abused or used correctly for specific benefit. Others in
this thread have pointed out some of the (potential) pitfalls and some
of the (potential) advantages.  There are many other issues that have
not been pointed out by either side.  Bottom line:  use the proper tool
for the task at hand and use it properly.  To reject the tool out-of-
hand is just as invalid as insisting that it be used when it shouldn't
be.
For those who prefer to view it from the perspective of the results (as
"building material" rather than as a "tool") a similar case exists.
Again, it is up to the "builder" to use the correct "material" for the
task at hand. To reject "wood" simply because "plastic" may may be more
durable is just as invalid as using "wood" when "plastic" is called for.

In article <7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com> wrote:
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the
Procedure
> Division (particularly in the middle of a paragraph).
>
> I won't quote the full text, but this person thought that this was an
> "awful" idea (my word, not his/her).  Although I don't use this
technique
> all that often, I certainly have used on multiple occasions.  I find
it
> particularly useful when coding I/O logic, exception processing, and
other
> common (across program/application) logic.
>
> I am wondering how other feel about this as a "general" technique -
good,
> bad, indifferent?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You asked!

Cordially,
-trblshtr-
```

#### ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EC6B1D.FBE8A4C9@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
A someone who originally worked with assembler where the use of
macros is rampant and essential I think this is a good idea in
principle. Macros are used in C a lot as well.

Basically they reduce the amount of code you have to write. One
program I was working on we replaced multiple invocations of
various routines by copy verbs and reduced line of code by 50%.
At no loss on meaningfulness (if the copy expansion is concerning
you, have a look at the cmpilationm listing - you do keep the
compilation listings for your live code, don't you?). We also
fixed a lot of bugs. Previously, the programmmer had to do
something like

Move '12' to parm1

move 'a' to option-setting-a

move infile-data to parm-3

set parm-orig to true.

(etc)

call 'mangeleit' using parm1 option-setting-a parm-3 did-it-work

(The programmers were supposed to use standard parms and a
separate paragraph to call the subroutine, but that was too
boring for some of them).


This turned into 

copy "mangleitpd" replacing
	==function== by ==update==
        ==option== by ==speed==
        ==data== by ==infile-data==.        


If you forgot a parameter the copy was set up to generate a
syntax error.

Of course you can overuse them, use them in tricky ways, and use
meaningless names and thereby turn a good think into a bad idea.


The copy verb lacks a lot of power (eg nested copy replace) and
the inability to have data local to a paragraph does create
problems. But used with discipline it is very useful.


Tim Josling

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> I received a "private email" about using COPY statements in the Procedure
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> bad, indifferent?

I have seen also this used quite a bit for Y2K logic, but as you say,
mainly for I/O (mainly database) and exception processing.

But I would like to see PROCEDURE DIVISION copy members go through an
approval process.  If it is common enough to be useful as a copy member,
it has to be stable enough that one doesn't need to re-compile
everything when it gets changed.  Called programs are pretty efficient
now and can be used for most logic.
```

IMHO From the standpoint of machine resource utilization they are no
more "efficient" than they ever were and in some cases; less. Systems
upon which they run are simply more powerful. Hence, called programs
may be useful, effective, beneficial, easier to maintain, etc, etc,
but, so too, can be the copybook which is, still, more "efficient".

## Thread: Comma Problem (this should be way easy) — 19 msgs

### Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

- **From:** "john smith" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
I am trying to put a comma out to a file.   With EBCDIC it is a hex 6B with
Ascii it is a Hex 2C, thats what I want a 2C in a 1 byte field.
I tried:

FILLER  PIC   X(01)                                    VALUE ','.
FILLER  PIC S9(01) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE +2.
FILLER  PIC   9(01) BINARY                     VALUE +44.
FILLER1 PIC   X(01)                                    VALUE ','.
FILLER1 PIC S9(01) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE +2.
FILLER1 PIC   9(01) BINARY                     VALUE +44.

All I get is 00  when viewing in hex.
The whole point is I have to export some data to a comma-delimited field.
There has to be an easier way.

Thanks for any help I just want to send out this file in the morning.
and get it out of my hair.
```

#### ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
In article <kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>, "john smith"
<someone@microsoft.com> writes:

>I am trying to put a comma out to a file.   With EBCDIC it is a hex 6B with
>Ascii it is a Hex 2C, thats what I want a 2C in a 1 byte field.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you are issuing an INITIALIZE statement against the 01 level,
all fields are set to low values for the definition (zero for numeric fields
and spaces for character fields).   With fields declared as FILLER,
you will get x'00' from a INITIALIZE of a character field.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7se7ge$jk8@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com...
> In article <kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>, "john smith"
> <someone@microsoft.com> writes:
>
   <snip>
>
> If you are issuing an INITIALIZE statement against the 01 level,
> all fields are set to low values for the definition (zero for numeric
fields
> and spaces for character fields).   With fields declared as FILLER,
> you will get x'00' from a INITIALIZE of a character field.
>
>

Not  true (or at least it isn't supposed to be).  INITIALIZE should IGNORE
elementary FILLER fields, not change them at all.   Do you have a compiler
that actually does set them to low-values (or X'00')?
```

>Not  true (or at least it isn't supposed to be).  INITIALIZE should IGNORE

I am reasonably certain that prior when we were under MVS vs 5.2
using AD\Cycle 370 COBOL for MVS,  any sections of a record
defined as FILLER PIC X(...) became x'00' after an INITIALIZE.

We are now on OS390 v 2.6 and I have not tested this little gotcha 
since we converted.   I usually try to stay away from things that have
burned me in the past.
```

compiler

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

My guess (and it is only that) is that they were X'00' *BEFORE* the
INITIALIZE and that INITIALIZE didn't do it.  (C.f. VS COBOL II WSCLEAR or
COBOL for this-and-that STORAGE(00) run-time options).

If you do an INITIALIZE of a group item with a FILLER elementary item (with
a VALUE clause), then I really, REALLY, don't think it will get changed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3PKG3.192$MQ.3862@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com> <7se7ge$jk8@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7se7ge$jk8@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>...
>Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
>news:19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I just cheat. Whenever I INITIALIZE group-item-name, I use
  INITIALIZE group-item-name
    REPLACING ALPHANUMERIC DATA BY SPACE
                        NUMERIC DATA BY ZERO


Works really cool, all the time; sets DISPLAY numerics to '0'  and BCD and
BINARY numerics to the correct format.

(OK, I know, "what if a field is redefined, and the same storage is both
alpha and numeric?"  Well, I know there is something in the manual about
that, but my answer is, "don't use INITIALIZE in that case!!!")

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
The views and opinions expressed herein are my own.
As I am self-employed, they also express the views of my employer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

Michael, there is nothing wrong with what you are doing - it is just
"redundant." (Some of the COBOL-bashers say that almost all of COBOL is
redundant, but ...) What you have coded is exactly what the compiler should
be doing if you just say

    INITIALIZE group-name
```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:wHOG3.460$MQ.11483@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> But Bill, "which" compiler am I working with this week?  AcuCOBOL on Unix,
> COBOL for MVS/390, Microfocus Personal COBOL for DOS (yeah, I still use
that
> at home for testing), and who knows what other compilers have pulled a
> 'gotcha' on me?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

I am the LAST person to argue with this statement.  Having said this, the
question (or what I was responding to) was comparing

   Initialize Group-Item

        versus

  Initialize Group-Item
      Replacing Alphanumeric by Spaces
                        Numeric          By Zeroes

   ****

If you find any compiler ANYWHERE that isn't treating these two identically,
then the compiler just has a BUG.  These are *SUPPOSED* to work identically
(even on how they handle filler elementary items.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EBDE99.7DDFF89B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com> <7se7ge$jk8@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <3PKG3.192$MQ.3862@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7sg03v$csc@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <wHOG3.460$MQ.11483@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7sghil$qj5@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Michael Mattias wrote :

<snip.....>

I know this topic has diverted to the "INITIALIZE" verb, but geting back
to the original query, commas in ascii-delimited - "INITIALIZE" wouldn't
have become a problem if the enquirer had taken the following approach:-

01 myascii-record.
   05			pic x(01) value '"'.
   05 A-PartName	pic x(30).
   05			pic x(02) value '",'.
   05 A-PartNumber	pic 9(06).
   05			pic x value ','.

	move File-PartName   to A-PartName
	move File-PartNumber to A-PartNumber
	write myascii-record

OK - so you could string, but it's not efficient with large
ascii-records, (and also confusing); I have some which are 700
characters in length. While the intent is to generate asciis as input to
oil company DBs - it is also a useful tool for my user to edit with
Global search/replace and then recreate a data-file from the edited
ascii. 

And BEFORE somebody explodes in horror at me letting the user 'edit'
datafiles - this is not accounting with a required audit trail; it is
done for practical reasons. With the 'musical chairs' going on in the
oiipatch, buy-outs/take-overs etc., facilities or vessels "move" between
corporations. Editing allows the user to transfer relevant history data
to new or existing data-files, (e.g. Vessel XYZ is sold by Amoco to
Chevron). 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Michael Mattias wrote:

> I just cheat. Whenever I INITIALIZE group-item-name, I use
>   INITIALIZE group-item-name
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> that, but my answer is, "don't use INITIALIZE in that case!!!")

Agreed.

The advantage of using an INITIALIZE command is not great enough to be
worth confusing some future maintenance programmer about what you wanted
to accomplish - nor to force him while tracking down a bug in the middle
of the night to pull out the manual to find out how a REDEFINES is
INITIALIZED.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF9C45.7D813369@home.com>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <19990923093140.11196.00000278@ngol03.aol.com> <7se7ge$jk8@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <3PKG3.192$MQ.3862@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37EF7430.6B06B2FF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Michael Mattias wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> INITIALIZED.

Even with REDEFINES you can get INITIALIZE to work properly :-

	01 ClientRecord.
	   05 PrimeKey.
	      10 Rectype	pic 9.
	      10 ClientNumber   pic x(03).
	      10 FacilityNumber pic 9(02).
	   05 Name
	   05 AddressData.
	      10..... etc
	   05 ClientData.
	      10		pic 9(03) comp-3.
	      10 ....
	      10 etc....
	      10 filler		pic x(15)
	   05 FacilityData.
	      10 		pic x.
	      10		pic x.
	      10        	pic 9(02) comp-3.
	      10 ....
              10 filler		pic x(05).

	if new record
	   initialize ClientRecord

		if ws-ClientRectype
		   initialize ClientData
		   move 1 to Rectype
		   move ws-ClientNumber to ClientNumber

		else initialize FacilityData
		     move 2 to Rectype
		     move ws-CliewntNumber to ClientNumber
		     move ws-FacilityNumber to FacilityNumber
		End-if
	End-if

OK it wont work for all redefinitions - but it does for the above.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9uqG3.226$J72.6622@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
john smith wrote in message ...
>I am trying to put a comma out to a file...
>The whole point is I have to export some data to a comma-delimited field.
>There has to be an easier way.
>

You can use:

01  OUTPUT-BUFFER     PIC X(120)
...

    STRING
       DATA-1   DELIMITED BY SIZE
       COMMA  DELIMITED BY SIZE
       SPACE   DELIMITED BY SIZE
       DATA-2   DELIMITED BY SPACE
       ...
    INTO  OUTPUT-BUFFER
   WRITE OUTPUT-FILE FROM OUTPUT-BUFFER
.....

If your compiler does not accept COMMA as a symbolic literal, just define it
in working-storage, PIC X(01) VALUE ','.


Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
The views and opinions expressed herein are my own.
As I am self-employed, they also express the views of my employer.
```

```

: ...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comma Problem (this should be way easy)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "john smith" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AWzG3.13620$I6.372926@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<kigG3.6705$I6.277182@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <9uqG3.226$J72.6622@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7sdvfe$ctv$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <Y6zG3.752$J72.31865@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Thanks all of you for your help!

What I did was think that because I said
FILLER PIC X(01) VALUE ','.
I assumed it would be.
Unfortunately as someone pointed out it initialized to all 00000's or
spaces.
This is on an ibm390 thing.
so I left it as a
FILLER1 PIC X(01) VALUE SPACE.
MOVE ',' TO FILLER1.
That seemed to work fine.
and I found out
after doing this
FILLER1 PIC S9(01) PACKED-DECIMAL.
MOVE +2 TO FILLER1.
That gave me a 2C in a 1 byte field, but when I FTP'd it out the computer
converted it to an ASCII equivalent of 1A?
So I had to go back and change everything.

The coolest part of all is I'm no longer afraid of this endeavor or writing
JCL and PROC and PROGRAMs, I may not be so good but once one starts working
Im hooked.

Thanks Again,
I'm sure I'll have lots of questions as they give me more things to do and
play with.
Next thing to tackle is tables *wince*

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:Y6zG3.752$J72.31865@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
