<a id="t-415732bcbc"></a>
## Thread: Varying screen Size — 8 msgs

### Varying screen Size

- **From:** "Jonathan Roberts" <snarfle99@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Greetings,

I use AcuCOBOL 4.2 runtime and compiler in an NT environment.
My development is done on a 17" monitor, for my users who use
15" / 14" monitors...

How can I make my applications scale to the screen size that  they are
running on?

The only work around I can currently find is to redesign my screens!
If this is the only way I fear we will part ways with this vendor quite
soon!

Anyone out there have other suggestions?

JR.
```

#### ↳ Re: Varying screen Size

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oX8G3.4660$zl1.61126@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
I'll assume this is a serious question rather than the flame bait it appears
to be.

Resizing is based on screen resolution, not monitor size. An application
sized for a 800x600 resolution (SVGA) will run on any montor/video adapter
combination that supports the same resolution.

If you have designed your application to require higher resolutions than the
current user configuration then you will need to make sure that all the user
monitor/video adapters are configured to the same resolution, assuming they
can support the higher resolution. Failing that, you will have to redesign
your application with the hardware limitation in mind.

If the application is character-based then it may be possible to select a
different font. Usually font selection is handled by the command shell
rather than your application. If it is graphical then you may choose to
dynamically resize the windows, fonts, etc. based on the size of the
available desktop or rearrange your interface for a smaller resolution.

What I know about AcuCOBOL can be written upon the head of a pin with a
magic marker but blaming then for your poor user interface design is hardly
fair.

Jonathan Roberts <snarfle99@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net...
> Greetings,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Varying screen Size

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37e91a99.20489393@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
"Jonathan Roberts" <snarfle99@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Greetings,
>
>I use AcuCOBOL 4.2 runtime and compiler in an NT environment.
>My development is done on a 17" monitor, for my users who use
>15" / 14" monitors...

It's not actually "screen size" that is the problem.  The problem is
actually "screen resolution."  The three most frequestly used screen
resolution settings are 640 x 480, 800 x 600 and 1024 x 768.
Essentially these number represent the horizontal  and vertical pixel
density supported by the monitor and video card on the specific
machine.

The problem occurs when you go from machine 1 which may be running at
800 x 600 to machine 2 which may be running at 640 x 480.

The screen size doesn't actually change...it's just that if the screen
was designed to be "full screen" on an 800 x 600 resolution monitor,
it will be too large to fit on a monitor with a 640 x 480 resolution.
Most GUI tools allow for automatic screen scrolling to accomodate the
lower pixel density.

>How can I make my applications scale to the screen size that  they are
>running on?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>soon!

Don't switch compilers....COBOL sp2 provides support for automatic
resolution switching through the use of a simple environment variable.

If you design at 800 x 600 and have set the environment variable
properly, the GUI screens will automatically resize downward in a
proportional fashion for 640 x 480 resolution and automatically resize
upward in a proportional fashion for 1024 x 768 resolution monitors.

COBOL sp2 supports Acucobol very well.

>Anyone out there have other suggestions?
>
>JR.
>
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying screen Size

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sh1gb$h95$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net> <7sb962$8k2$1@news.cerf.net> <37e93485$0$21154@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Jonathan Roberts wrote in message
<37e93485$0$21154@fountain.mindlink.net>...
>Entirely graphical controls...

Well, what you have to do is:

Make sure your window is resizeable.

Make sure you use a font that is freely resizeable

Make all controls occur using dynamic settings, which means: size, lines,
cols in variables.

First of all, I presume you do program event oriented, in that case, you
must take action on the event:

    ntf-resized

    With this event you will get line and size in respectively event-data-1
and event-data-2 (pixels)

    When this occur, you will have to resize all your controls relative to
this. This is accomplished
    by modify control and respective attributes. Remember to use a relative
factor to give the right
    proportions against the window.

Here is a very small sample of a context:

    display standard window,
        title "Scaleable window"
        lines current-lines, size current-size,
        resizable
        background-low.
    display screen-1.
    accept screen-1.
    evaluate key-status
    when event-occurred
        if event-type = ntf-resized
            divide event-data-1 by 100 giving current-lines
            divide event-data-2 by 100 giving current-size
            modify mycontrol
                lines current-lines - 9
                size current-size - 8
            end-if
    end-evaluate.

As you see, it is fully possible to resize a window and its controls in
Acucobol, so there should not be any need for replacing Acucobol.

Hope this helps.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Varying screen Size

- **From:** "Ted Spayde" <tspayde@caltype.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5qtG3.6332$QJ.364775@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```


Jonathan Roberts <snarfle99@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:37e91068$0$20089@fountain.mindlink.net...
> Greetings,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone out there have other suggestions?

The easiest way to accomplish this is to adjust the size of the font to the
resolution of the screen. This means that the window must be based on the
font as follows:
           display standard graphical window
               size   80.00,
               lines  28.00,
               screen line 1
               screen col 1
               control font fixed-font,
               cell size = label font fixed-font
               modeless
               erase,
               resizable
               background-low.

To get the right font size, first ... accept terminal-abilities from
terminal-info.
This accept will give you the number of pixels available on the screen
(horizontally and vertically).

Next, pick a font size (i.e. 5) and set fixed-font to this size with the
W$FONT routine. Then, use W$TEXTSIZE to get the size of some character (i.e.
X). Divide the horizontal text size into the number of horizontal pixels. If
it is greater than 80, set the font size up by one and set and check the
size again. Do this until you can't get 80 characters on the screen, then
set the font size down by 1.

NOTE: You need to do this before you display the main window. Also, it only
works really well if you use a fixed font (i.e. Courier New).

Hope this helps.
Ted
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SynKronix PERSCOBOL COBOL to JAVA converter?

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wbxG3.18$TA.1730@wdc-read-01.qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<19990922150207.08822.00000109@ngol02.aol.com> <37e965e7@news3.prserv.net>`

```
If you tried the PERCobol Educational version, it does not have the speed
enhancements that the Enterprise version has.  Also, the PERCobol runtimes
are about 500x faster than they were last year around this time.  Be aware
also that PERCobol programs are limited to the speed of the JVM, unless you
compile them natively.

We think of PERCobol programs as "Cobol that runs on a Java Virtual
Machine."  Since PERCobol does output Java source/bytecode, you can use it
to convert your Cobol to Java.  But it wouldn't be much different than using
other Cobol compilers to convert Cobol to Assembler or Machine Language.
Even though they do, it isn't very practical.

The great thing about PERCobol is that it provides platform independence, a
modern GUI, and access to the latest Java technology.  You can code
Servlets, HTML, TCP/IP, E-Mail, JavaBeans, and more right from Cobol.  Your
program will run as an applet or an application, right out of the box.

David Sanders
LegacyJ, Corp.  (was Synkronix, Inc.)

----

in a ComboList.  I have read and reread the definitions of the Intrinsic
Functions "Random" and "MOD" - and I know that I must be doing something
wrong - as I am getting a few - but definitely NOT an "even distribution"
from my list using the following logic. (This list should never hold more
than 999 items - usually a lot less - but it is variable.)  (For those
without Fujitsu or GUI background who don't "know" ComboLists - the only
thing that you need to know about this is that

  Move "ListCount" of CallName to Num-in-List

puts the number of items in the list into the variable Num-In-List

Can someone hit me over the head with what I am doing wrong?

Sample code follows:

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  SeedNum  Pic 9(04) Value Zero.
 01  Random-Num Pic 9(05) Comp-5.
 01  Num-in-List Pic 9(03).
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
* This randomizes each "run" of the application
   If SeedNum Zero
     Compute SeedNum = Function NumVal (Function Current-Date (11:4))
     Compute Random-Num = Function Integer (Function Random (SeedNum)) * 10)
   End-IF
* This gets the total of number of items in the list
   Move "ListCount" of CallName to Num-in-List
   If Num-In-List > 0
      Compute Random-Num =  Function Integer ((Function Random) * 10000)
      Compute Random-Num = (Function Mod (Random-Num Num-In-List)) + 1
*  This *should* highlight a RANDOM entry in the list
      Move Random-Num to "ListIndex" of CallName
   End-If
     .
```

#### ↳ Re: Help me find my logic error (Fujitsu PowerCOBOL)

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sdlui$e5t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7scbo5$dfi@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```
How often does position 11 for 4 of the current date change/increment?
If your looking for ten thousand different seeds and the low order
bytes are obly zeros, then you have fewer seeds....

Could the clock always being started at a round value at program start
and with so few instructions to the get clock, usually the same time
has elapsed?  This kind of behavior I've noticed in CICS in the past...

(SeedNum)) * 10)
>    End-IF

>    Move "ListCount" of CallName to Num-in-List
>    If Num-In-List > 0
>       Compute Random-Num =  Function Integer ((Function Random) *
10000)
>       Compute Random-Num = (Function Mod (Random-Num Num-In-List)) + 1
> *  This *should* highlight a RANDOM entry in the list
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help me find my logic error (Fujitsu PowerCOBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7se8de$k92@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7scbo5$dfi@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <7sdlui$e5t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:7sdlui$e5t$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> How often does position 11 for 4 of the current date change/increment?
> If your looking for ten thousand different seeds and the low order
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I did post another reply related to the "solution" to my problem.

1)  It turns out that Fujitsu (at least at V4.0) takes

   01  RandomNum   Pic 9(03).
   Compute RandomNum = Function Integer ((Function Random) * 1000)

and for some (unpleasant - but legal) reason figures that you only want ONE
significant digit from RANDOM *before* multiplying it by 1000.  This meant
that the MOD that I did was  always against a number divisible by 1000 -
which gave a VERY limited selection.

2)  I still need to rework my code to get rid of the "slight" bias that
occurs when you mod a COBOL decimal field to get a random value.  For
example, if you  do

  01  BigField   Pic 9.
  Compute NumField = Function Mod (BigField 4)

if Big field can have any of the values 0 thru 9 (in an even distribution),
the result is SLIGHTHLY biased to 0, 1, 2 - and against 3 (The first 3 can
occur 3 times, while the last can only occur 2 times.)

I have figured out the code to "solve" this - but still need to implement
it.
```

## Thread: Scrolling records — 4 msgs

### Scrolling records

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37E85E50.C7403A8E@home.com>`

```
Movie scenario : Our erstwhile hero/heroine breaks into a highly secure
classified installation, and typing at 1,200 words p.m., breaks the
security code - and Voila! - streams of records scroll for him/her -
you get the idea.

Now the real world - In a PC environment I can set up scroll tables,
(list boxes, dropdown lists), subject to memory constraints. For info
just how do you do it on the big-iron or multi-user systems with many
users accessing the same tables. How would corporations the size of say,
Amex or Visa do it. Logically you have to set some size limitation, and
restart a fresh scroll with Start Myfile Key > xxxx  or < xxxx(?) -
depending upon whether you want to scroll forward/backwards.

A brief description or pseudo code would be informative.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Scrolling records

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zz6G3.133$9r3.10246@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37E85E50.C7403A8E@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37E85E50.C7403A8E@home.com>...
>... Voila! - streams of records scroll for him/her -....
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>users accessing the same tables....

Why not just capture it all to disk and query it later using START etc as
you have suggested, if your data capture file is indexed..

But if you capture the data to a non-indexed file, instead of keeping the
data
in a memory-based table, why not just keep the key and a relative record
number? Then you can just table up something like...

01 FILLER
    05 Scroll-Key OCCURS A LOT indexed by sk-index
         10  KeyValue                 PIC X(12)
         10  RRN                        PIC S9(07) COMP.

.. and when you need a record

     SEARCH Scroll-Key
      WHEN KeyValue (SK-Index) EQUAL Value-I-am-desparately-seeking
         MOVE RRN(Sk-index) TO Relative-key
         READ relative-file...


Just a thought.

MCM






just fetch the records as needed.



. How would corporations the size of say,
>Amex or Visa do it. Logically you have to set some size limitation, and
>restart a fresh scroll with Start Myfile Key > xxxx  or < xxxx(?) -
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Scrolling records

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90C968BF72979FD6.36517D9BADFF92CE.C060A6501B04F29A@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<37E85E50.C7403A8E@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 22 Sep 1999 04:34:54 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
enlightened us:

>Movie scenario : Our erstwhile hero/heroine breaks into a highly secure
>classified installation, and typing at 1,200 words p.m., breaks the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

I've done something like you're describing on the Mainframe in a CICS
environment.  The file that was being displayed was an ESDS file.  The
program was pseudo-conversational.  The screen was defined as 22
lines, thus you would display 22 lines of data.  The other two were
used for heading information and PF Key display.  Basically, the first
key displayed on the screen and the last key displayed on the screen
were saved in a temp storage record.  If a user pressed the FORWARD PF
Key, I'd take the last key displayed, read the file, read next, save
the key as the first key, build the display line, read 21 more records
building display lines, save the last key; rewrite the temp storage
record.

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Laughing stock: cattle with a sense of humor.

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

## Thread: Locking Problem on NT — 1 msgs

### Re: Locking Problem on NT

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1251ee02bb5afb0c989697@news>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1248d2c9ac20f585989694@news> <7ro25f$nma$2@news.igs.net> <4bTfNxQjjK4K6f5p=Yh59q+5JBdp@4ax.com> <37e06ed6.21162684@news1.ibm.net> <MPG.124b5298277b4887989695@news> <7rs95h$5nq$1@news.igs.net>`

```
A netadmin friend ran across a similar sounding problem with a borland 
app. He suggested setting two registry keys on the NT server. One 
disables files cached after the close, the other disables oportunistic 
locks. The MS Knowledgebase articles were Q102967, Q1260626 and some 
related articles.

These made the problem much less noticable but (unfortunately) not 
completely gone. On the bright side, at least files are not kept open on 
the server now after a user has closed the application.

In article <7rs95h$5nq$1@news.igs.net>, donald@willmack.com says...
> Hope that it works for you.
```

---

<a id="t-39bea3c0fa"></a>
## Thread: Recursion in Cobol — 1 msgs

### Re: Recursion in Cobol

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sak0l$6ht$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37dc677b.259238160@news1.ibm.net> <19990914031833.29168.00006225@ng-ff1.aol.com> <37def9de.158071504@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In article <37def9de.158071504@news1.ibm.net>,
  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> On 14 Sep 1999 07:18:33 GMT, frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY) wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I don't track you.  It sounds like you return control upwards first
it's not a
> >recursive call by the Realia program; or is it indirect recursion?
> >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thane,

When we added full support for UPS's new communications capabilities
(UPSLink) to our product, we had to use the "is recursive" clause on a
Ca-Realia program to support a callback function for displaying
communication progress while our program was waiting for an API
function call to complete.

Using a supplied API call, we first passed a memory pointer for the
callback entry point in our program to UPSLink and told it to send us
progress status updates.

Then we called the API function to transmit a file via dial-up tcp/ip.
This function would not return control to our program until the file
transfer was completed.

UPSLink then began the file transfer and periodically called our entry
point to report progress.

CA-Realia runtime created another instance of our program to handle the
callback processing. We displayed an SP2 popup window showing the
current status of the file transfer.

When we received a completion status via the callback, we exited the
callback process and CA-Realia runtime cancelled the second instance of
our program and returned to the API call statement that was waiting for
completion in the original instance of our program.

I thought this was a very effective approach for providing callback
functionality in a COBOL program.

John Chafin

P.S. We had a serious problem when we first tried to implement this
and, to make a long story short, CA-Realia support was absolutely
fantastic.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

---

<a id="t-449d5b7e9c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL & Java — 7 msgs

### COBOL & Java

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EA983C.5BAF16C5@home.com>`

```
Lief Svalgaard wrote a short time back that he found Java slow - having
tried it about a year ago.

Just got Doke & Hardgrave "Java for the COBOL Pogrammer". Naturally,
flipped to back of book for conclusions, where they say - three-tier 
design (business logic, GUI, DB interface) comes at a cost in
performance, plus interpreted code using bytecode and Java Virtual
Machine - as against putting the Java code through a native language
compiler; of course the latter makes it platform specific.

Always curious about this platform specific stuff - I'd be pleasantly
surprised if I could use Windows style GUIs on a Linux platform.

Leif, or anybody else, any comments about the above topics ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL & Java

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37eb82c3.16817007@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<37EA983C.5BAF16C5@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>Lief Svalgaard wrote a short time back that he found Java slow - having
>tried it about a year ago.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

Jimmy:

sp2 supported the Motif interface on Linux for quite some time, but
very few people wanted it, therefore customer demand tends to dictate
which of our versions get attentiona dn which do not.

I believe that Micro Focus dropped support for their Motif version of
Dialog System years ago.  I don't think that Acu supports Motif with
their GUI extensions.  I'm also quite sure that none of the compiler
companies support Motif with their GUI solution.  Perhaps my friends
at the various compiler companies can verify this or refute my
statements if I am incorrect.

You probably don't want to write the low level X-Window routines
because:

A.  They are a pain in the rear.
B.  There are some GUI features in Windows which are not supported by
Motif...parent/child windows and combo boxes to name two of them.
This means if you wanted to maintain 1 set of code for both, you would
have to do a lot of extra programming to accomodate GUI screens which
have a "one for one" relationship in both Motif and Windows.

There is a possible solution for you.  I'm not sure if it is a good
solution or not, but there is a Linux "user supported" emulator called
WINE which allows Windows programs to run in Linux Motif.

If you go to the official WINE web site, you will see that there are
quite a few Windows applications which have been certified to run in
the WINE environment.

More information on the WINE emulator can be found at:

WINE Headquarters

http://www.winehq.com/

WINE FAQ

ftp://rtfm.mit.edu/pub/usenet-by-hierarchy/comp/os/linux/answers/windows-emulation/wine-faq

There is also a USENET Newsgroup for WINE at:

comp.emulators.ms-windows.wine

Hope this is helpful.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL & Java

- **From:** Paul Galbraith <pdg@interlog.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ECFF93.93C31B14@interlog.com>`
- **References:** `<37EA983C.5BAF16C5@home.com>`

```
James,

I'm not sure if you're interested in writing native Java, or Cobol that
does GUI via a Java toolkit of somekind?

Java Runtimes are now coming out (especially from IBM, try
www.ibm.com/java) that feature JIT (just in time) compilers.  These compile
Java bytecode into native machine code at runtime, giving a (sometimes)
huge performance boost over the Sun's basic JRE.  Still, Java performance
is considerably slower than code compiled with a good optimized Cobol
compiler--at least with the Java apps that I've seen (take a look at DB2
6.1).  Whether that's because of JRE performance, or generally inefficient
code, I don't know.

I must say, however, that the portability of Java is fantastic.  DB2 6.1 is
a great example of this as well.  IBM has rolled out DB2 6.1 across several
platforms almost simultaneously, and I'm sure that the Java front end is
largely responsible for this possibility.

Paul

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Lief Svalgaard wrote a short time back that he found Java slow - having
> tried it about a year ago.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL & Java

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ED4B8F.7852DE1F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37EA983C.5BAF16C5@home.com> <37ECFF93.93C31B14@interlog.com>`

```
Paul Galbraith wrote:
> 
> James,
> 
> I'm not sure if you're interested in writing native Java, or Cobol that
> does GUI via a Java toolkit of somekind?

Paul,

Many thanks for the information. Currently I'm using NetExpress for
developing, using OO and GUI classes and visually I find the performance
excellent. The only slight delay is at startup when dynamically creating
a fairly large Main Menu, and the program is also doing file checks to
ensure necessary files exist and don't have corrupted indices.

Other than that I find the performance excellent taking the three-tier
approach for OO COBOL design, referred to.

If I can do it in COBOL why re-invent the wheel ? I was more curious
that a couple of COBOLers, Doke and Hardgrave, who wrote "An
Introduction to Object COBOL" in 1998, were now promoting that COBOLers
should learn Java - and at the same time noting Java's performance
defects.

The interesting thing I find in your comments is the reference to using
JAVA for GUI portability - if they can eventually meet COBOL performance
by fine-tuning the code or JVM - then JAVA for GUIs becomes an
interesting possibility as a 'universal' GUI-maker for COBOL developers
- re-usable across platforms (????). And of course a combination of OO
COBOL and Java sharing objects sits just fine.

NetExpress has a module 'Forms' that I haven't gotten into - but with
COBOL and Java Script provides the tools for accessing the Web.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Pete.

<a id="t-db176e2b0c"></a>
## Thread: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question. — 2 msgs

### LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Consider this a philosophical question:  If there are two supported
ways to do something, a LE runtime service and a COBOL intrinsic
function, and though both work differently, if either provided the
result needed, which one should be used?

Consider this also:  If there are two supported ways to do something, a
LE runtime service and a COBOL extended function, and though both work
differently, if either provided the result needed, which one should be
used?

Would the answers be different if platform independence was a desired
goal?

Would the answers be different if upward release independence was a
desired goal?

TIA
	Bill
```

#### ↳ Re: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ed9a4e@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
(Dons saffron robe and sips herb tea as he formulates response to Honourable
Tommo's question)

Comments interspersed...

Bill Thompson wrote in message <7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>result needed, which one should be used?

As a philosopher, I can state without hesitation, use the COBOL intrinsic
function.
(Platform independence is a goal which is highly under recognised and
undervalued by COBOL programmers at the moment. If events unfold in the way
us philosophers predict they will, this skill will be critical to the
careers of many of today's programmers. Start thinking Universal COBOL
across platforms; don't be seduced by extensions, environments, and promises
of "much greater efficency" into staying on one vendor's platform. Get in
the habit of writing code which requires the minimum amount of change to run
on anything. Ask yourself, if your career image is to be a "390 programmer",
a "DEC VAX programmer",  a "Bendix 200 programmer, or a "computer
programmer". Philosophically, which answer shows the most vision?)


>
>Consider this also:  If there are two supported ways to do something, a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Considered it at length. Don't use either. Invoke the old "imagination" or
"lateral thinking" function inherent in the carbon based processor you have
inside your head, and find a solution in standard COBOL, which can run on
anything...

>Would the answers be different if platform independence was a desired
>goal?
>
What do you mean, "IF"? Platform independendence HAS to be a desired goal...

>Would the answers be different if upward release independence was a
>desired goal?
>
Using standard COBOL does not obviate upward release independence. (Vendors
would go broke if it did...)

So, now you're thinking "Pete's certainly got a bee in his bonnet about
platform independence."

I'll tell you why in non-philosophical terms. Machines and environments come
and go. The heart of the language has to remain stable to accommodate this
change in the real world. If you intend to make programming your life's work
(and I wouldn't advise it if you think you are likely to live longer than
the next 15 years...) then you better learn to ignore the BS from vendors
and get comfortable in ANY environment.

Secondly, and much more importantly, every time you code a module you are
producing a solution to a specific business problem. When you move on from
your current employer (or he politely asks you to do so because the cost of
maintaining the computer system has bankrupt the company...) you will move
to another business which may well ask you to solve the same (or very
similar) problem. You could start over and rebuild the solution for the new
platform, some people are happy to do this.

Us lazy philosophers like to take the line of least resistance and provide
solutions which are general and re-usable so we don't have to do them over
again. This gives us much more time to philosophize about really important
things, like the taste of beer, and where your lap goes when you stand up...

The point is, that code which is standard and platform independent has at
least a fighting chance of running on any platform which your career changes
may require it to. If you DO have to change it, the changes are likely to be
minimal and you can soon get back to philosophizing... Maybe, you can spend
the extra time thinking about some design changes to the next system you are
going to develop...???

Seriously, I have had numerous occasions in my career where I was able to
utilize code I had written somewhere else (especially if you ascribe to the
philosophy of keeping things small and modular). After a while I learned to
think about the actual problem (rather than just do what the spec. said) and
predict what enhancements they were likely to want. Ensure the hooks are in
there and when they come back crying you can tell them to dry their eyes
because you're going to make it all better by Friday...

The business have their requirements; we, as programmers, have some of our
own. Platform independence is right at the top of mine. (I also require that
it will not be a boring job and will not cut into my drinking time and I
will be a hero if I finish it by Friday...)

Truly, the frog feels only the moonlight as he protects the lotus blossom.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-5256e5a514"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 11 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Denis" <grosnou@antispam.skynet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
Hello,
I'm trying to compare the COBOL & PL/I languages in order to write
recommendations for our developers. Both language are available. Thousands
of COBOL modules as thousands of PL/I modules are currently used in
production with language environment.
The comparison must be based on COBOL for OS/390 v 2.1 and PL/I for OS/390 v
1.1.1 running on OS/390 v1.3 with language environment v1.7 The OO COBOL
extensions must not be considered, only the 'standard' COBOL statements are
used.
The main criteria are:
- Readability of the sources
- Maintenance of the source
- Difficulty to find specialized developers
- Quality of support from IBM
- Language advantages/drawbacks/flaws of the language (built-in
functions...)
- Limits in the management of  memory (Limits in working-storage size, size
of arrays...)
- Arithmetic limits & precisions (integers, floating points, packed-decimal
variables...)
- Performance at execution (Quality of the compiler & optimizer)
- ILC with COBOL, PL/I, Assembler (For example PL/I fetch limitations).
- ...?

I've already gathered many information from the Language Reference
appendices sections for of each language but there are still large gaps.

Many thanks in advance
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990923100034.18987.00001368@ng-fe1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
Denis writes...

>Hello,
>I'm trying to compare the COBOL & PL/I languages in order to write
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Perhaps you could help us by telling us what you consider to be the "large
gaps"; we don't want to cover ground you've already been over.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sdktd$1vu$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
Like most such comparisons, the problem is subjective and not amenable to an
objective analysis. Especially for such criteria as "readability or
maintainability", where every fan will tell you that their favourite
solution is the best solution.

Leave your PL/I / COBOL programmers to program PL/I / COBOL. Don't force
them to move - they won't like it, and the code quality will be inversely
proportional to their level of annoyance.

Leave code written in PL/I / COBOL as it is. It's cheaper. You're already on
LE, so your ILC issues are resolved.

There is no such product as PL/I for OS/390 v1.1.1, You probably mean PL/I
for MVS and VM 1.1.1. PL/I for OS/390 is actually VisualAge PL/I for OS/390
V2.2, and it requires OS/390 V2.8. The upgrade is worth it, but you appear
to be using an old OS/390 (OTOH, OS/390 v2.3 (I know you wrote 1.3) is not
Y2K conformat IIRC, so you may be facing a very fast update anyway). VA PL/I
also removes all the restrictions mentioned lower down in your list - see
the announcement letter for details.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** giprice@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TDCG3.20463$1E2.156010@ozemail.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com> <7sdktd$1vu$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
In <7sdktd$1vu$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>, "Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com> writes:

8<-----snipped----->8
>There is no such product as PL/I for OS/390 v1.1.1, You probably mean PL/I

By the by, the last release of Version 1 of OS/390 was Release 3.
(I had to install it.)  The first release of OS/390 Version 2 was Release 4.
Hence, OS/390 1.3 cf. OS/390 2.4.
MVS/SP Version 5 Release 2 is the oldest release of MVS which can be
expected to work next year, I believe.  (Possibly SP5.2.2.  Check this
stuff out if it is important to you!)

Cheers, Greg

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Denis" <grosnou@antispam.skynet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sf3i9$rak$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com> <7sdktd$1vu$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Thank you (all) for your answers
Yes it is PL/I for MVS 1.1.1 (and not PL/I for OS/390) and it is really
OS/390 v 1.3. I've checked with the MVS team and "our OS/390 version is Y2K
compliant. OS390 v2r6 is in test and will be in production 'just after 1900'
(ooops I mean 2000 :-)". (BTW MVS 5.2.2 is also Y2K compliant, but this is
not the goal of this thread). PL/I for OS/390 v2.2 is just announced and
will be in production not before 1 year at our site (after 2000, after
OS/390 v2r6, after OS/390v2r8 then PL/I v 2.2). We must live with the
current version of PL/I in the meantime.

What we are looking for, is experience from companies that use PL/I and
COBOL (C/C++ or other language does not play in the game here) and have
people that knows PL/I & COBOL: What do they give has recommendations to
their developers when they ask 'which language should I use?' 99.99% of our
developments is for business applications and not  system-type applications.
For example, in the context of business applications, when would you
recommend to use COBOL and not PL/I and the reverse. The idea is not to
force anybody to use this or that language on subjective criterias, but when
a new project starts, to recommend this or that language based on
"emotional-neutral" advises in order to choose the "best" language for the
project. Here "best" means maintenance, readability, language limits,
etc,etc...

The goal is not to (re-)activate a religion war to get the advice of other
companies that are in the same configuration.

Once again, thank you to everybody for your answers

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EB76B7.772C@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com> <7sdktd$1vu$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7sf3i9$rak$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
Denis wrote:
> What we are looking for, is experience from companies that use PL/I and
> COBOL (C/C++ or other language does not play in the game here) and have
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> recommend to use COBOL and not PL/I and the reverse. 

Much as I like PL/I, I would recommend COBOL for your garden-variety
business application, unless you're already a PL/I shop.

PL/I really shines in other types of applications that require a lot of
bit-twiddling, floating-point math, character string manipulation,
dynamic storage allocation, etc.  Processing records with fixed fields
and fixed-point numbers is what COBOL was designed for.

I use PL/I as a substitute for assembler in applications where ease of
coding, readability, and maintainability is more important than
squeezing out the last few machine cycles. I.E system programming, image
manipulation, Printer-dependent print datastreams, text processing,
etc.  If I was oing to do a payroll, I'd start with COBOL. (well, *I'd*
use PL/I, but that's a subjective choice).
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7se77i$jab@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
The following is the reply that I got from one of my "usually reliable
sources" (who has a slight COBOL slant),

"> The main criteria are:
> - Readability of the sources

COBOL is much more English like, so if the programmers know English
than COBOL would be more readable.  (If the programmers do not know
English, then PL/I might be more readable)  You can't "hide" tricks
in COBOL like you could in PL/I.  PL/I has more flexibility to do
things like use the same name for a variable as for a function, which
is hard to read and maintain.

> - Maintenance of the source

COBOL is harder to be "tricky" with, so it can be easier to maintain.
See also the readability answer above.  In addition, there are many
more 3rd party vendor tools available to work with COBOL than PL/I

> - Difficulty to find specialized developers

There are more COBOL programmers in the world than for any other language,
including Java and C/C++.  Of course, that means more than PL/I programmers
too.

> - Quality of support from IBM

COBOL and PL/I are equal here

> - Language advantages/drawbacks/flaws of the language (built-in
> functions...)

Too hard to answer.  COBOL has built-in functions now, but PL/I might have
more.  This is a very subjective question.  What I might see as a flaw in
one language, another might see as an advantage.  Is the language to be
used for business applications or system-type code?  COBOL is the best
language
in the world for implementing business rules, while PL/I is better for
system
programming.

> - Limits in the management of  memory (Limits in working-storage size,
size
> of arrays...)

COBOL and PL/I are equal here

> - Arithmetic limits & precisions (integers, floating points,
packed-decimal
> variables...)

PL/I has the advantage here, though both languages support all of the most
commonly used data types.

> - Performance at execution (Quality of the compiler & optimizer)

The COBOL optimizer has many patents and is well known as very good, but
resulting code depends on the skill of the programmer, and I would say that
COBOL and PL/I are probably equal here.

> - ILC with COBOL, PL/I, Assembler (For example PL/I fetch limitations).

COBOL does not have the fetch from fetch restriction, and any COBOL program
can
be MAIN or SUB at any time.  COBOL can be made to look like C/C++, and can
be shared easily as common code with dynamic CALLs, common linkage
conventions, automatic start up of run-time library, 'normal' naming of
entry points (Entry point of program SUB1 is SUB1, not CEESTART like PL/I
and C)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990923211148.03116.00001593@ng-fz1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7se77i$jab@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
>Subject: Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison
>From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@nospam.netcom.com 
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
>    wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

While not PL/I vs COBOL, there's a real good PL/I vs C comparison that might
help you out as far as understanding what PL/I has to offer.  

It's called "Power vs. Adventure - PL/I and C" and can be found at:
 http://www.uni-muenster.de/ZIV/Mitarbeiter/EberhardSturm/PL1andC.html

While (IMHO) COBOL can't hold a candle to PL/I, PL/I did make big mistakes by
omitting built-in SORT and SEARCH verbs (SORT's are done via CALL's and
SEARCHE's are done via DO loops). 

PL/I supplies a much more robust programming language with features COBOL
should not be lacking at this late date - a "real" MACRO preprocessor,
user-definable functions, useful POINTERs, bit manipulation, STREAM and STRING
I-O, dynamic memory allocation, array functions, local variables, true
INITIALIZATION capability, default processing (e.g., files automatically OPENed
and CLOSEd in not done explicitly), etc.

BTW, the latest implementation eliminates the FETCH limitation noted above and
also provides a way for the more anal-retentive to reimpose COBOL-like
"features" such as reserved keywords.
```

## Thread: cobol .exe file — 2 msgs

### cobol .exe file

- **From:** CrazedHope <rkowalc@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sdsg9$jl0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 I'm trying to make an .exe file using microfocus cobol.  Does anyone
know how to do that or give a reference for it?
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol .exe file

- **From:** JohndeV <johndevNOhwSPAM@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<026faffc.190c4409@usw-ex0108-057.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<7sdsg9$jl0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You did not say what version you have of Micro Focus.and on
what platform you were running.

But anyway, here goes.

First compile your program;

COBOL programname.CBL omf"obj";

If you use an old 16bit version just do

XM COBOL programname.CBL omf"obj";

Then link your program.  With recent versions of Micro Focus
you can do the following;

For a Static linked EXE; CBLLINK -T programname
For a Shared runtime EXE; CBLLINK programname

If you want to know what other parameters you can use with
CBLLINK, just type CBLLINK at a command prompt and press 
ENTER.

For linking with older versions of MF I suggest that you
have a look at your documentation.

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

<a id="t-65b92e7058"></a>
## Thread: (Semi-Long) Re: Help me find my logic error (Fujitsu PowerCOBOL) — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### (Semi-Long) Re: Help me find my logic error (Fujitsu PowerCOBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sd5ar$m3j@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I found my "logic" error (in my program using FUNCTION MOD and RANDOM) and I
am *not* a happy camper.  I don't know what other vendor's compilers will do
with this (or even what Fujitsu V4.2 would do - but it turns out that V4.0
is doing some "odd" things with their intermediate results and compute.
(All perfectly "legal" but still not pretty).  If you are interested (and
I'll let the Fujitsu-believers see it - to believe it), try compiling and
running the following (non-GUI) program, and I think you will see what is
(and is not) happening:

 @OPTIONS MAIN
 Identification Division.
  Program-ID. TestRand.
 Data Division.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  01  Random-Num  Pic 9(05) Comp-5.
 01  Random-Num2  Pic 9(05)v9(05) Comp-5.
 01  Num-in-List  Pic 9(03).
 01 Table-of-Results.
   05  Each-approach
     occurs 4 times
     indexed by Ind1.
      10 Each-Num
         occurs 10 times
         indexed by Ind2
             Pic 9(03).
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
* Setup RANDOM series
  Compute Random-Num = Function Random (1)
      Move 45 to Num-in-List
*
      Set Ind1 to 1
      Perform
        Varying Ind2 from 1 By 1
        until Ind2 > 10
         Compute Random-Num = Function Integer ((Function Random) * 10000)
         Compute Random-Num = (Function Mod (Random-Num Num-In-List)) + 1
          Move Random-Num to Each-Num (Ind1 Ind2)
      End-Perform
*
      Set Ind1 to 2
      Compute Random-Num = Function Random (1)
      Perform
        Varying Ind2 from 1 by 1
        until Ind2 > 10
         Compute Random-Num = Function Integer ((Function Random) *
10000.000)
         Compute Random-Num = (Function Mod (Random-Num Num-In-List)) + 1
         Move Random-Num to Each-Num (Ind1 Ind2)
      End-Perform
*
      Set Ind1 to 3
      Compute Random-Num = Function Random (1)
      Perform
        Varying Ind2 from 1 by 1
        until Ind2 > 10
          Compute Random-Num2 = ((Function Random) * 10000)
         Compute Random-Num = Function Integer (Random-Num2)
         Compute Random-Num = (Function Mod (Random-Num Num-In-List)) + 1
         Move Random-Num to Each-Num (Ind1 Ind2)
      End-Perform
     Perform
       Varying Ind2 from 1 by 1 until Ind2 > 10
           Display "1:" Each-Num (1 ind2)
                " 2:" Each-Num (2 Ind2)
                " 3:" Each-Num (3 Ind2)
     End-Perform
     .
```

<a id="t-df1083da05"></a>
## Thread: Collaborative COBOL Article — 1 msgs

### Collaborative COBOL Article

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990923102134.18991.00001308@ng-fe1.aol.com>`

```
Ed Milne writes...

[snip]

>Yes, it is better programming practice to use an odd number of digits
>with COMP-3 fields.  But the point is that an upgrade to a new version
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>appropriate cost and timing implications.

But that was, at least partially, my point. The program was not really working
before. It may have been just luck of the draw that the existing, underlying
bug never surfaced. For example, suppose you use PIC S9 for a page counter. If
your report never goes over 9 pages, then you consider the program to be
working. If the report grows to 10 pages what happens depends on several
variables but there are these possibilities in the end: 

* the program will abend (the bug is now drawn to your attention) 

* the page number wraps to zero (subpossibilities: a) no one notices; is this
then a bug or not? and b) a user notices but decides to ignore it; is this a
bug or not? and c) a user notices and requests a fix; this is a bug)

* the page number shows up as 10; the program is now not performing according
to the rules of the language; is this a bug?

>Your original post stated that one of the justifications upgrades was
>the ability to use new features.  However, you have to weigh that
>against the cost of destabilizing the programs.
>
>

Point taken. But you have to also consider the benefits of uncovering hidden
bugs that would have cost the customer later.

>The people that you are addressing and this newsgroup and IEEE are all
>techies who recognize the personal benefits of learning the latest and
>greatest software.  We benefit regardless of what happens to the systems
>we convert.  So your proposed article is preaching to the converted.
>

Well, the orientation is to try and make the business case for doing the
conversion. We need to point out factors that non-technical people may not see,
in a way that doesn't require them to be technically competent (just literate,
I hope).



>However, the user department who paid for the upgrade still feel that
>they got no benefit from the project. 

Someone has not been doing their marketing, then. When I worked for IBM over 25
years ago, they taught us "everyone sells". This was an uncomfortable message
for most of us techies, but over time I have come to believe it. It is OUR
responsibilty to ge the users excited about updates and aware of the costs of
doing thing poorly. And we need to do it on their terms. That's the way things
work and how we make life better for ourselves. We cannot just stay in our
cubicles and bemoan the shortsightedness of our users. We must show them the
big picture.


> BTW, the project was to modernize
> a legacy payroll system that was bad code to start and then was hacked
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

But see previous comment.

Thanks for your thoughts on all of this. It really has been helpful.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-7599c5ef05"></a>

## Thread: S/390 Load Modules — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### S/390 Load Modules

- **From:** "Steve Higgins" <steve@higgins99.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7se58j$tjg$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
This isn't strictly a COMP.LANG.COBOL question I suppose, although I am
attempting to write the program in COBOL ...

Does anyone know where I can locate a document that will describe the
internal format of an MVS load module? All I'm trying to do is extract the
CSECT and User information (ie stuff from the IDENTIFY linkedit parm and the
compile date & time), although the full format would be useful for later
development.

This stuff must be documented somewhere but Bookmangler won't give it up.

Thanks in advance

Steve Higgins

(Yes, I know COBOL isn't very suitable to the task but I don't have the time
to learn Assembler or relearn PL/1)
```

#### ↳ Re: S/390 Load Modules

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990923172547.20302.00001324@ng-bg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7se58j$tjg$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Steve Higgins writes ..

>This isn't strictly a COMP.LANG.COBOL question I suppose, although I am
>attempting to write the program in COBOL ...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks in advance

Actually, load module layout is deliberately not a documented interface and you
will not find the details in the non-licensed documentation (and maybe not even
in the licensed documentaion).

But it's not clear how you are trying to access this data: programmatically, or
from ISPF, or what? There are utilities that can pull this kind of stuff off in
batch (I think AMBLIST but I'm not sure).

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **From:** George Young <gmyoung@ibm.net>

Appendix H.  Linkage Editor Load Module Formats

DFSMS/MVS Version 1 Release 4 
Program Management 
Document Number SC26-4916-03 
Program Number
5695-DF1
5647-A01
 
I think the binder has an API that might be useful for querying this
info (and I suspect has to be used if you are using a Program Object in
a PDSE rather than a load module in a PDS). 

You might also be able to parse the output of AMBLIST, though it may be
subject to change overtime.

George

OS/390
MVS Diagnosis:
Tools and Service Aids 
Document Number SY28-1085-05 
Program Number
5647-A01

(the same manual that describes AMASPZAP). For MVS I think it was just
called 'Service Aids'.

George

##### ↳ ↳ Re: S/390 Load Modules

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7se8q0$kir@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7se58j$tjg$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37EA9EDF.6A7A88F6@ibm.net>`

```
Included in the "attached" note is an important ASSUMPTION that you need to
think about (depending on exactly what you intend to do).  Load Modules in
PDS (or PDSE's?) and Program Objects (only in PDSEs) have VERY different
formats.  If you are creating an application that "assumes" Load Modules, at
least be aware that at some time they may change to Program Objects.
```

<a id="t-0164600b86"></a>
## Thread: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question. — 1 msgs

### Re: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7seah6$lml@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7sdha5$akr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
(Cross posting to comp.lang.cobol and IBM-MAIN - as part, but not all, of
the thread is in both - and it really relates to both.  Semi-long reply to
question included in the end of the post.)

I think that the most important question (and there are some others) that
you need to ask - is:

Which is more important -
   portability across languages (but all on OS/390 - or at least S/390)
      or
   portability across platforms (including IBM OS, such as OS/2 - but also
all Unix and other proprietary OS).

If you are interested in "cross-language - but always on S/390" portability,
then I would (probably) go with the LE-services.   This would, for example,
get around the problem of COBOL (according to ANSI/ISO) using a different
"Lillian" date than IBM does (which is controllable by a COBOL compiler
option, but ...).  Furthermore, there are LOTS and lots of LE callable
services that have no corresponding COBOL features.  Therefore, if your
programmers could "get used to" using services, their training/maintenance
should be (?) easier.

If, on the other hand, you think your application will EVER go to any other
platform (including IBM's own OS/2 - much less AIX or Windows - and who
knows about USS), then you should avoid the LE callable services like the
"plague".  I once (in a former life) wrote a medium complete set of
LE-callable service routine emulators for Micro Focus - and it certainly CAN
BE DONE ... but there is no indication that IBM ever intends to provide a
complete set across their own platforms - much less in "open source code"
for you to port to other systems.  (Note initially - in the days of SAA and
AD/Cycle - there was supposed to be a difference between CEE3xxxx routines
and CEExxxx routines - but this is a "promise" that never came to fruition.)

    ***

It should be noted that as far as "different code" - "same results",  I
think it is (relatively) fair to assume that GENERALLY, you will (should?)
get the same results from using Intrinsic Functions and "native language"
and "callable services" - because the compiler (on OS/390) usually uses the
callable service in the object code that it produces.  Back in the days of
VS COBOL II (as compared to OS/VS COBOL) one of the big "selling points" was
that DB2, Fortran, *and* COBOL were finally set to all get the same results
from the same arithmetic expressions.  I think that today, this is something
that is FAIRLY safe to assume in whichever COBOL solution you go with.

   ***

One other issue to think about is the fact that the draft of the next COBOL
Standard includes support for USER-WRITTEN functions (and access to
non-COBOL functions).  Although IBM doesn't support these yet (as an
extension) and may or may not introduce them in the short-term, there are
many other COBOL vendors that already do support them.  My guess (crystal
ball) is that you will see them in COBOL sooner rather than later.  This
would be another reason to "stay with" functions rather than callable
services - as it provides a "standard" coding technique that can be
used/extended in the future (where the callable services could even be put
in a user-written/platform-dependent function).
```

<a id="t-febcccbf44"></a>
## Thread: Problem with Microfocus COBOL Ver 3.1 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Problem with Microfocus COBOL Ver 3.1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7seera$5bs@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7sduos$2eaj$1@news.inc.net>`

```
(In the future, you might find better replies in comp.lang.cobol - rather
than alt.cobol), but

If you print out the REDEFINED filed (the one that's all PIC X), then I
would be very surprised if you actually got commas and periods in the
output.  However, when you print out the numeric-edited version, you have
"incompatible data" - (because the actual storage doesn't match the PIC),
therefore, the compiler can do whatever it wants.  In this case, the
compiler is BELIEVING the PIC rather than the  storage - and not even
"looking at" what's in storage for those bytes, but printing out what SHOULD
be there.

Besides the solution of printing the all PIC X version - AND depending on
what you really want to do, another possible solution would be to look at
adding the "BLANK WHEN ZERO" clause to your numeric-edited definition.  I
think (but haven't tried it) that this might also solve your problem.
```

## Thread: COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000

- **From:** coboljit@aol.com (COBOLJIT)
- **Date:** 1999-09-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990923020524.09091.00000226@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000 is the only COBOL text written specifically to
deal with year 2000 issues.

This comprehensive technical handbook contains information on all aspects of a
COBOL Year 2000 project, and includes detailed technical solutions, including
source code and routines  for locating dates, doing date calculations and date
handling.  

Topics include :
Basic approaches to handling date problems
Dates in files and on screens
Gregorian and Julian Dates
Routines for Date entry, validation, conversion and system dates
Fiscal date handling routines
Common date pitfalls
Common Date handling routines for day differences, adding days to dates, dates
of the week etc.
Testing.

Written by Mo Budlong, author of the best selling textbook, Teach Yourself
COBOL in 21 Days.

Available from
The other is a book " COBOL Dates in the Year 2000", which describes the
problem, the various ways of handling.  Topics include :
Basic approaches to handling date problems
Dates in files and on screens
Gregorian and Julian Dates
Routines for Date entry, validation, conversion and system dates
Fiscal date handling routines
Common date pitfalls
Common Date handling routines dor day differences, adding days to dates, dates
of the week, comparing dates and dates in words
Testing.

Source code for routines for use in your programs also available.

Helen

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
King Computer Services, Inc.
COBOL Training for the Year 2000
"COBOL, Just in Time" Crash Course
"COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000" Technical Reference.
818-951-5240
```

"available" (thanks to me upgrading my ISP service).  I'll see how this goes
(for a month or two) and decide "whither the FAQ" in a little while.

If you haven't been there recently, see

   http://pweb.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

for the latest version.

The following is the summary text from "What's new" (from the "olde" text
version)

Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 - Updated some old (bad) web links
 - Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed
to - by frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 - Added information about Synkronix (PERCobol)
 - Updated information for changes in product and company names and
addresses where available
 - Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 - Started the Samples/Example Section

I have gotten so much spam since I started posting on newsgroups that I set
up a different account so I could filter the whole mess into it's own
folder. I'm not really hiding, just cranky about all the unsolicited stuff
that comes my way. In fact, one of the unsolicited things that came my way
was the offer to contribute to 'COBOL Unleashed'.

As for the rest of the posters to this thread who object to the use of
aliases, I respect your opinion but do not agree with it. I have no trouble
with docdwarf, redsky, or any of the other aliases as long as they are
consistently used. Now, if I was to start changing my alias every other day
or using somebody else's name (like maybe Leif Svalgaard) that would be poor
netiquette. Besides, using an alias also puts some distance between me and
the company for which I work.


Now admit it, did anybody think Oscar T. Grouch was actually somebody's real
name? Maybe I should have used 'Cookie Monster'?  ;-).

Karl Wagner

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards Committees - was How many authors ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7stcjo$nba$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<yvKG3.28132$I6.454617@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <37ed2b95.75600316@news1.ibm.net> <37ED4E60.FC4F6410@home.com> <7smpt8$5fq$3@news.igs.net>`

```
When was the first Cobol standards committee set up?

When I started as an operator in early 64, most of the large(ish)
applications were written in COBOL.   This was on an ICT1500 (actually a
rebadged RCA301 if I remember correctly) and COBOL compiles took so long
that bugs weren't fixed unless they were unpatchable.   Each application had
a patch list and when a problem arose, the operators had to manually patch
the programs entering octal values as bit patterns via coloured buttons on
the  console panel.

Was there already a standards committee then?

Rick

donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:7smpt8$5fq$3@news.igs.net...
> I served on the 74 standards group as a student, way back in the very late
> 60's or early seventies.  Even got to meet Grace and take a course from
her
> ... that was when she was working out of Marlbourgh Mass., with the Dec
> group.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standards Committees - was How many authors ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F2924B.1BCADBA1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<yvKG3.28132$I6.454617@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <37ed2b95.75600316@news1.ibm.net> <37ED4E60.FC4F6410@home.com> <7smpt8$5fq$3@news.igs.net> <7stcjo$nba$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Rick Price wrote:
> 
> When was the first Cobol standards committee set up?
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
Rick,

Started before you and I got involved with ICT 1500s. I think first
moves were about 1959. The one to really fill us in is Warren Simmons if
he will jump in.

Interesting you had COBOL on your machine in '65. I was designing, but
our programmers were using machine code and FAS(1500 Assembler). Not
surprising COBOL didn't come up as a topic (Unigate, Trowbridge,
Wiltshire), we were using that 'rebadged' RCA machine together with
their MCF (Magnetic Card File), so obviously the software must have been
'RCA' based so the 1500 and MCF could 'talk' to one another. I left in
'67 and they got rid of the MCF - lots of support problems and
phone-calls to Cherry Hill, California - and Sprint and Candice Bergen
weren't around in those days offering cheap telephone calls.

My own first contact with COBOL was when my Chief Programmer,
(Debenhams, Taunton, Somerset), approx '69, recommended we go COBOL on
our ICL 1900 as our standard language - as all the recruits he was
interviewing, had all learned COBOL. 

Grin-time. Our chief Unigate honchos along with the company secretary
did an RCA tour taking in the factory. "What's that ?", they said,
pointing to something under a dusty cover "Oh that's Joe's machine, to
play with" - and we were paying a king's ransom for the same thing. Part
of the tour, they took in US and Canadian supermarkets. One manager
proudly pointed out to them "we have Ilchester Beer Cheese".
(Combination of cheddar and beer, made just outside Yeovilton RN Fleet
Air Arm base). As the company secretary said to me, "Our hosts were so
gracious, and knowing their paranoia about hygiene - I didn't want to
tell them the 'Ilchester' was mixed in a bath-tub".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Randall Bart wrote:
>

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> cheese than could be mentioned in one Monty Python sketch.

Trust you to know Randall - but you are right, (Still gotta get back
about the bearoids - and I gave you a whole daffy of clues on the Movies
to help you on your way - "Get thee to a nunnery" should have clicked
with you. Any luck on the catfish ).

I can cheat a bit, having worked for Unigate - but off the top of my
head, and not an exhaustive list :-

       Ayrshire, Caerphilly, Cheddar, Cheshire, Derby, Derby (Sage),
       Dorset Blue Vinney, Double Gloucester, Leicester, St.Ivel Lactic,
       Stilton (Blue), Stilton (White), Wensleydale

Best Cheddar you could buy back then - Canadian Black Diamond, allowed
to age more than its brethren. And don't go looking for cheddar in
Cheddar Gorge, Somerset - it aint made there !

That Ilchester - Have no idea if it is still made - it was a small
independent company, nothing to do with Unigate. Could tell you a couple
of amusing tales about Blue Stilton and Dorset Blue Vinney, (the latter
being very rare). I even have a funny (computer) tale about Green Giant!

Jimmy, Calgary AB

<a id="t-ede4e4e875"></a>
## Thread: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone" — 22 msgs

### Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(A new thread - obviously as a follow-up to another in C.L.C.)
   (Sorry for sounding annoyed, but the "tone" of the previous thread does
have me annoyed.)

As most of those who read C.L.C. for any time know, I usually try to stay
out of the "religious wars" on what is or is not "error-prone" or "good
programming." Except to either "raise the question" or to report (often as a
possible source for the FAQ) on a summary of what I hear as all the sides of
the issue.  (Compare for example the previous SECTIONS/PARAGRAPH debates,
Efficiency/Readability debates, numbered-prefixes-or-not debates, and even
the previous Extensions/Portability discussions)

I do, however, have a STRONG bias against anyone who thinks that the method
that they know/like (are used to) is the ONLY way to avoid errors or to do
"good programming".  (I wasn't the author of the recent paint-brush/painter
discussion, but that's a great summary of my personal philosophy.)

IMHO,

Yes, there are some techniques that are more "prone" to be used to create
difficult to maintain source code. However, all of them can be used in
easy-to-maintain manners as well.

Yes, there are some techniques that are often "more efficient" on "more
platforms" than others - while other techniques (sometimes overlapping with
the previous - sometimes not) that are easier to maintain over the long
run - or to be "self-documenting" even in the short-run.

Yes, there are a variety of other issues that impact each programmer's
"selection" of the coding techniques that they use regularly or most
comfortably (or in code intended to be used more than once).

   BUT ...

To say (or imply) that some VALID (for *some* implementation) syntax,
standard, or other "coding technique" (or maintenance methodology or design
methodology, etc) is WRONG or awful or a "bug bomb" or other pejorative is
simply a matter of PREJUDICE - not a matter of God-Given-Truth.

For example, my experience has been that using a "bad technique" in a new
program - but where that technique is widely used and understood in a large
shop is a whole lot BETTER than using a "good technique" that no other
programmer will be able to maintain (or understand).  Clearly, if you are
developing code that will never be seen by any other programmer, this
doesn't apply - but you still shouldn't (IMHO) use techniques that you will
have trouble understanding when you get back to the problem in months or
years.

   ***

I *do* (obviously) have an opinion about "Next Sentence" (with or without
END-IF).

Given that COBOL
 - Does have sentences
 - Does support Next Sentence
 - Does even allow (in portable code) a Next Sentence in an IF terminated by
an END-IF (if you use the COPY technique that I documented elsewhere) - and
many/most implementations allow it without the COPY statement
 - Does CLEARLY define the difference between what a NEXT SENTENCE (clause)
and a CONTINUE (statement) does
 - Does FULLY support NEXT SENTENCE working identically in ANS'74 and ANS'85
(in either old or new code)

Then, if you, your programmers, or whoever doesn't know how to read,
maintain, and understand such code, I suggest that they learn how to.

I can't see that using "NEXT SENTENCE" as an EXPLICIT GO TO (which CONTINUE
is not) is any worse than using any other "GO TO" (of a exit that is defined
in the language construct).

Certainly there are people who think that any type of GO TO (including the
new "EXIT PERFORM" and "EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION" statements) are wrong,
error-prone, and hard to maintain.   They certainly are and always will be
"hard to understand" for those who haven't been trained in what they do.  As
someone who is visually impaired, I just find it hard (with today's tools)
to believe that the "deciding factor" should be how big a period (or
full-stop) is.  OTOH, if that really is what you (and your shop) is worried
about (on whether you can or cannot see periods), then "fine," let that be
your shop Standard - but don't tell me that the language is "flawed" because
it has a construct that uses it.

There are constructs in current COBOL Standard that are marked as "obsolete"
and I probably would encourage people to "flag" for these (occasionally) and
try and develop (new) code that avoids them. NEXT SENTENCE isn't in that
category.  (GO TO DEPENDING ON is - for those who wonder how I feel about
it. Although even it can be used in a maintainable manner in programs where
EVALUATE is not available.)

Bottom-Line:
   Do try and use the constructs in the COBOL language in a way that is
"clear" and "maintainable."  Please do not try and tell others that your
method is the only way of doing so.
```

#### ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

brush/painter

>

> difficult to maintain source code. However, all of them can be used in

"more
> platforms" than others - while other techniques (sometimes
overlapping with
> the previous - sometimes not) that are easier to maintain over the
long
> run - or to be "self-documenting" even in the short-run.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard, or other "coding technique" (or maintenance methodology or
design
> methodology, etc) is WRONG or awful or a "bug bomb" or other
pejorative is

>

new
> program - but where that technique is widely used and understood in a

> shop is a whole lot BETTER than using a "good technique" that no other

are

you will

or

ANS'85

>

that
