##### ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com> wrote:
:>
:> To say (or imply) that some VALID (for *some* implementation) syntax,
:> standard, or other "coding technique" (or maintenance methodology or
: design
:> methodology, etc) is WRONG or awful or a "bug bomb" or other
: pejorative is
:> simply a matter of PREJUDICE - not a matter of God-Given-Truth.

It is the Cobol standard that says that mixing NEXT SENTENCE
and END-IF is WRONG.  This is not an issue of prejudice but
of Committee-Given-Truth.

:>
:> For example, my experience has been that using a "bad technique" in a
: new
:> program - but where that technique is widely used and understood in a
: large
:> shop is a whole lot BETTER than using a "good technique" that no other
:> programmer will be able to maintain (or understand).  Clearly, if you

Exactly.  If they are used to NEXT SENTENCE and full-stops then
they should not be introduced to END-IF because they won't
be able to maintain or understand it </sarcasm>.

There is _nothing_ wrong with leaving NEXT SENTENCE in programs
as long as they are not in combination with END-IF (as I had
previously stated).  This is not a "bad technique".  However,
mixing NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF is disallowed by the standard
for good reasons and techniques to defeat this (and to hide
the deviousness in a COPY) can be interpreted as malicious.

:> Given that COBOL
:>  - Does have sentences
:>  - Does support Next Sentence
:>  - Does even allow (in portable code) a Next Sentence in an IF
: terminated by
:> an END-IF (if you use the COPY technique that I documented elsewhere)
: - and

No, it does not allow a NEXT SENTENCE in an IF terminated by an
END-IF.  In your example the outer IF was terminated by an
END-IF, the inner IF that contained the NEXT SENTENCE was
terminated by the ELSE of the outer IF.

:> many/most implementations allow it without the COPY statement
:>  - Does CLEARLY define the difference between what a NEXT SENTENCE
: (clause)
:> and a CONTINUE (statement) does
:>  - Does FULLY support NEXT SENTENCE working identically in ANS'74 and
: ANS'85
:> (in either old or new code)
:>
:> Then, if you, your programmers, or whoever doesn't know how to read,
:> maintain, and understand such code, I suggest that they learn how to.

Oh I certainly know how to read and understand such code.  I also
know how to maintain it - making it fit the intent of the
standard for the language.

But anyone used to _STANDARD_ COBOL will have problems with
your code.


:> I can't see that using "NEXT SENTENCE" as an EXPLICIT GO TO (which
: CONTINUE
:> is not) is any worse than using any other "GO TO" (of a exit that is
: defined
:> in the language construct).

Your lack of being able to see why it is worse is beside the point.

The problem is that you are concentrating on the NEXT SENTENCE
clause and the GO TO aspects of it and are missing completely
the implications of the target of these.

There is nothing wrong with a GO TO or a NEXT SENTENCE (when
not mixed with END-IF) or any other explicit control where
the target of these is _also_ explicit.

That is, if I see a label then I am alerted that this is a 
place to which transfer of control is made.  If I remove
that label then the compiler gives me an error if it was
used.  The label itself is sufficient to trigger the care
that is required in dealing with the code around it.

In the case of NEXT SENTENCE without END-IF the terminating
full stop is part of the IF statement and thus there is
a contained area of logic flow.

Use of NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF uses an anonymous label
(the next ful stop somewhere in the code) that is not
part of the IF statement.

Adding and removing full stops, deliberately or naievely,
will cahnge the effect of code that is not part of what
is being changed.

Similarly changing the END-IF back to a full stop will aslo
change (add bugs) when it alters the flow outside the IF
statement.

:> your shop Standard - but don't tell me that the language is "flawed"
: because
:> it has a construct that uses it.

While it may be that the standards committe were less vigilant
than those that want to abuse the standard, it is not the
language that is flawed - the standard prohibits this use 
for good reason.  It is your wanting to abuse the language
that is the flaw in your argument.

:> Bottom-Line:
:>    Do try and use the constructs in the COBOL language in a way that
: is
:> "clear" and "maintainable."  Please do not try and tell others that
: your
:> method is the only way of doing so.

I entirely agree with you, but you are using constructs that
are not in the Cobol language (or only so due to extreme
misinterpretation of the intent), are _not_ clear and are
_not_ "maintainable".

While you have continued to tell others "the way to do so",
I have not even disclosed 'my method', let alone claimed
it as 'the only way'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sk2fd$hkq@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
>
> There is nothing wrong with a GO TO or a NEXT SENTENCE (when
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that is required in dealing with the code around it.

I assume that this means that you don't like the EXIT
PERFORM/PARAGRAPH/SECTION enhancements that are in the draft of the next
COBOL Standard.  This NG has already discussed them in detail, so I won't go
thru them again - but all of them very definitely "go  to an unlabeled"
point in the program.  If you strongly disagree with this "functionality"
(now not only being enhanced - but actually be added as a new feature - for
the next Standard)  I would suggest that you contact J4/WG4 "quickly" so
that your views can be considered during -or before - the next (quite
possibly final) public review period for the upcoming Standard.

Note:  In some ways, it always struck me that when the committees decided to
add the EXIT options, that they should have added a (synonym) for NEXT
SENTENCE of "EXIT SENTENCE". This would be "symetrical" with what each of
the new (not old) variations of EXIT does.  On the other hand, as the
current "NEXT SENTENCE" is a clause and the new EXIT variations are
statements, I don't really think this is needed.
```

```
should be more or less trouble free.

mayby you should consider a perform thru for clarity.  The only time that I
normally get in real trouble is when I copy a set of paragraphs,  make small
changes, and forget to change an internal go to.  Why can't the compiler
flag that sort of thing.  Also performing a paragraph that is part of a
perform range.  The closest MF comes is a warning about inefficient code,
and you get that only when you generate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s1MH3.578$X71.5692@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sk2fd$hkq@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7sklfa$2j8$3@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
>
>    But if your code depends on the puesdo go to formed by a next sentence,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and you get that only when you generate.

This is one of the advantages of using paragraph numbers and a source
code checker.  When you move the paragraphs, you do a search/replace
on the paragraph numbers (e.g. replace " 123???-" by " 456???-"), and
it also fixes any GOTO or PERFORM statements that refer to them.  The
code checker catches all PERFORM THRU wrong exits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<7sl8l6$kci$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
In article <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
  Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote:
> trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com> wrote:
> :>
> :> To say (or imply) that some VALID (for *some* implementation)
syntax,
<snipped a lot>

Actually Mr. Plinston, Mr. Klein wrote that which you erroneously
attributed to myself.  I do applaud Mr. Klein's attempt to point out
that "my way..your way" *arguments* are often counterproductive.  As
regards the specific argument concerning 'next sentence' with end-if:
1) I, generally, avoid use of next sentence.
2) next sentence's action is, however, clearly defined.
3) You have been arguing in the initial thread that it "should" not be
used with end-if.
4) Mr. Klein has been arguing that it, like any other COBOL tool, "can"
be used.
I happen to agree that it "should not" but I also agree with Mr.
Klein's indicated position that if it is *needed* to accomplish a
specific action it's use is just as valid as the use of any other tool
in the kit.  Accomplishing the task at hand, at least sometimes,
supercedes whether something "should", or "should not", be done.

Cordially,
-trblshtr-
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7spqpd$2h8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sl8l6$kci$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com> wrote:

: 2) next sentence's action is, however, clearly defined.

I am not sure that this is actually the case when used as
Klein suggested.

NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF are disallowed in the same IF
statement, however if NEXT SENTENCE is used in an inner
IF that does not have an END-IF but relies on being 
terminated by an outer IF's ELSE, then it may not
technically be disallowed (IMHO because the committee 
was not vigilant enough in eliminating misinterpretations).

In the case of MF they define the action of this inner
NEXT SENTENCE as being equivalent to CONTINUE (in some
earlier compilers or when OLDNEXTSENTENCE is specified)
or to go to the end of the current sentence indicated by
the next full stop even if this is outside the scope of 
the IF.

Fujitsu, however, do not word it this way.  They have:

 "When NEXT SENTENCE is written, control passes to the
execution sentence [sic] following the IF statement".

and

"The END-IF statement delimits the scope of an IF statement".

Now I doubt that Fujitsu considered the mix of NEXT SENTENCE
and END-IF as they are clearly disallowed, but these two
rules seem to imply that Klein's example would have
NEXT SENTENCE operate as MF's OLDNEXTSENTENCE in that it
would go to the end of the outer IF.

In fact it seems to go to the next full stop, but I 
certainly wouldn't rely on it in any compiler except
MF, and would not do so there because the behaviour 
may be changed.

Do you still think that NEXT SENTENCE is clearly defined
in this case ?  (It certainly is clear, unambiguous, and
useful where END-IF is not used).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sq62j$6do@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sl8l6$kci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7spqpd$2h8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Yes,
  "Next Sentence" is clearly defined.  Please notice that your "sic" was
required to even make the Fujitsu situation ambiguous.  In COBOL a statement
is NOT the same thing as a sentence.  Both of these are clearly defined in
COBOL and are not the same thing.  NEXT SENTENCE goes to the "next
sentence" - NOT the "next statement".

It is true that with the MF "OLDNEXTSENTENCE" directive, you can change
that.  But you can also change the meaning of the MOVE statement with almost
every compiler that I know by using the "TRUNC" (or equivalent) directive or
compiler option.

Part of the vendor documentation is what impact directives have on
semantics.  This doesn't make it less well defined - or change what the
"base" meaning is, it just says that the vendor/compiler gives you one or
more options for OTHER *well defined* behavior.

NOTE:
  I am quite familiar with a BUNCH of MF directives that start "OLD" that
are for compatibility with behavior of previous compilers that MAY have been
"bugs" or may have been something else.  When MF did have a compiler that
treated "NEXT SENTENCE" like a CONTINUE, it was simply a bug - and I don't
think you will find any current MF documentation that says anything more
positive about it.  They could have just "fixed the bug" (and changed
program behavior - for existing customers - as some vendors did/do.)
However, they provided an option for continuing this behavior - and when you
select it you are running in non-Standard conforming mode - just as you are
when you use the IBM NOTRUNC/TRUNC(BIN) compiler option - which is
"required" when interacting with some other software.  So what does that
"prove"? Nothing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sr3v6$cmc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sl8l6$kci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7spqpd$2h8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sq62j$6do@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
: Yes,
:   "Next Sentence" is clearly defined.  Please notice that your "sic" was
: required to even make the Fujitsu situation ambiguous.  In COBOL a statement
: is NOT the same thing as a sentence.  Both of these are clearly defined in
: COBOL and are not the same thing.  NEXT SENTENCE goes to the "next
: sentence" - NOT the "next statement".

When NEXT SENTENCE is used according to the standard the next sentence
_is_ the next statement after the IF because there must be a
terminating period for the IF statement.

MF think this is the case because an ANS85 directive will flag
your code as non-standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>

```
"The NEXT SENTENCE verb transfers control to the next COBOL sentence, that
is, following the next period. It does not transfer control to the logically
next COBOL verb as occurs with the  CONTINUE verb. "

"When OLDNEXTSENTENCE is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE statement behaves like
a CONTINUE statement."

As taken directly from the online help for netexpress...

statement

in
> : COBOL and are not the same thing.  NEXT SENTENCE goes to the "next
> : sentence" - NOT the "next statement".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sr9hr$242@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sl8l6$kci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7spqpd$2h8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sq62j$6do@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7sr3v6$cmc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
I really (well almost) want to drop this whole conversation - because I have
lost any concept of what Richard is really trying to say (and I am getting
so that I don't believe that anyone who reads this NG via Deja.Com or online
will care much either), but ... in terms of "next sentence" really means
"next statement" - if you are ANSI/ISO conforming, it seems silly that
anyone would really suggest this. (Certainly the Standard has never said
it - and no vendor's documentation that I have read says this. Unless you
are using a "syntax modifying directive like OLDNEXTSENTENCE - but I think
that just as NOTRUNC changes MOVE, we can drop that irrelevance.) However,
just to make it clear that this isn't true (never was, never will be)
consider the following ANSI/ISO conforming (and not particularly obscure or
unusual situation) code:

If SomeField = "XYZ
   Search aTable
       When anItem (ind) = "ABC"
             Next Sentence
Else
    Perform Whatever
End-If
Display "In next Statement but, Still in same sentence"
  .
Display "In next Sentence"
 .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Philosophical "Wars" and what is or isn't "error-prone"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sr9ja$p5e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7shciv$sbd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7silov$uh4$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7sjfbu$rka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <7sl8l6$kci$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7spqpd$2h8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
In article <7spqpd$2h8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
  Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote:
> trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com> wrote:
>
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Mr. Plinston,
You have taken that piece of my prior post somewhat out of the overall
context of that post(i.e. "can" vs "should").  However, because you
asked:  It *is* "difficult" to surmise the exact action which will
occur given "just" the documentation and, within *that* context, I
would agree that it is *not* "clearly defined".  That said: IMHO *some*
action will occur.  Whatever that action is will, usually, be
consistent given the specific compiler/system in use.  Once that action
has been determined, and verified as to consistency, one could then
state that the action is "clearly defined".  That being the case it is
valid to also state that, like any other language tool, it *can* be
used.  More to the point: it comes back to programming "style".  One
style is just as valid as any other and, while we all tend to judge
style, in the final analysis it should not be the final determinant as
to what can, or cannot, be accomplished with COBOL; or any other
programming language.  As to "style": I happen to dislike the use of
next sentence with, or without, end-if.
-trblshtr-
```

```
Not to worry. The expression "To skin a cat" refers to removing
the hide from a catfish (which has no scales). The expression
does not refer to adorable little kitten-kittens. Oooo, kootchie-
coo. Meow.
```

### COBOL CGI

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w_zG3.313$EO6.1222@news.flash.net>`

```
Can anyone provide me with a generic Cobol cgi program?  I am trying to use
this on a MS Personal Web Server running on Win95...  I found a Cobol
program that was supposed to work...  but it doesn't...  and I really don't
know much about using cgi with Cobol.. (the system variables etc.)

Also would there be any differences to the program in a WinNT environment?

Thanks in advance

-Steve K.
```

```

news:w_zG3.313$EO6.1222@news.flash.net...
> Can anyone provide me with a generic Cobol cgi program?  I am trying to
use
> this on a MS Personal Web Server running on Win95...  I found a Cobol

don't

>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

What do you mean when you say "it doesn't work"? Does it

a) fail to compile?
b) fail to link?
c) fail to execute from a command line or the debugger?
d) fail to run when invoked by the web server?

Sending another sample might help solve (a) but may not do anything for (b)
(c) or (d).

Perhaps if you provide a few more details I could assist you in getting a
CGI program running under PWS.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL CGI

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g3fI3.955$p05.1559@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<w_zG3.313$EO6.1222@news.flash.net> <bxLG3.9439$zl1.176822@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
First of all, let me clear some things up....

With my compiler, i received a copy of England Tech. Services' Cobol-CGI.
It included Source, Object, and EXE. for 2 programs.  The EXEs were created
with the cobol object, the standard libraries and a special C program which
is called to "read" and to "write" the cgi.  The source for the C prog was
however not provided.

I put the HTML doc into the wwwroot folder and the EXEs into the scripts
folder.  And corrected the path to the programs in the HTML doc.

When i load the page, it returns:

"JMP00261-W NO 'ON EXCEPTION' STATEMENT EXISTS, WHEN EXCEPTION OCCURRED ON
'ACCEPT ARGUMENT-VALUE' STATEMENT"

and the browser returns:

"CGI Error
The specified CGI application misbehaved by not returning a complete set of
HTTP headers. The headers it did return are"

This is the majority of one of the programs.  If you need me to post the
rest of it I will upon request.  It is supposed to accept env-variables from
the client, send them to the server, and be sent back to the client in a
text format (not HTML) on the browser.

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. SHOWENV.
*
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SPECIAL-NAMES.
     ARGUMENT-VALUE IS COMMAND-LINE.
*
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 FILE-NAME            PIC X(80).
01 WEB-DATA.
     05 REQUEST-PROTOCOL PIC X(20)
                         VALUE SPACES.
     05 FILLER           PIC X
                         VALUE LOW-VALUES.
     05 REQUEST-METHOD   PIC X(20)
                         VALUE SPACES.
     05 FILLER           PIC X
                         VALUE LOW-VALUES.
     05 EXECUTABLE-PATH  PIC X(80)
                         VALUE SPACES.
     05 FILLER           PIC X
                         VALUE LOW-VALUES.
     05 LOGICAL-PATH     PIC X(80)      VALUE SPACE.
     .
     .

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 0000-MAINLINE.
     ACCEPT FILE-NAME FROM COMMAND-LINE.
     CALL "CGISETUP" USING FILE-NAME.
     PERFORM 1000-GET-ENV-VALUES.
     PERFORM 2000-SEND-REPLY.
     GOBACK.
 1000-GET-ENV-VALUES.
     CALL "CGIREAD" USING
         BY CONTENT "REQUEST PROTOCOL" & X"00"
         BY REFERENCE REQUEST-PROTOCOL.
     CALL "CGIREAD" USING
         BY CONTENT "REQUEST METHOD" & X"00"
         BY REFERENCE REQUEST-METHOD.
     .
     .

 2000-SEND-REPLY.
     PERFORM 2100-SET-PLAIN-TEXT.
     PERFORM 2200-WRITE-HEADING.
     PERFORM 2300-WRITE-ENV-VALUES.
 2100-SET-PLAIN-TEXT.
     CALL "CGIWRITE" USING
         BY CONTENT "Content-type: text/plain" & X"0D0A" & X"00".
 2200-WRITE-HEADING.
     CALL "CGIWRITER" USING
         BY CONTENT "Here are the values of the CGI items " & X"00".
     CALL "CGIWRITE" USING
         BY CONTENT "returned as a plain text file:" & X"0A" & X"00".
 2300-WRITE-ENV-VALUES.
     CALL "CGIWRITER" USING
         BY CONTENT "REQUEST PROTOCOL: " & X"00".
     CALL "CGIWRITE" USING REQUEST-PROTOCOL.
     CALL "CGIWRITER" USING
         BY CONTENT "REQUEST METHOD: " & X"00".
       .
       .
```

- **References:** `<7rkibh$5hu$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7rv4h8$lj2$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <+NfjN993qwWEBGS5WQiqCLWGcnyT@4ax.com> <_DNG3.384$MQ.9634@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37f0c079.4342271@news.enter.net>`

```
Ok, the answer is:

Robert Ludlum, in his novel, "The Icarus Agenda."

I just went looking for my paperback copy so I could give a verbatim quote,
but it looks like that is one of the books which found its way to the Racine
Rescue Mission when I was getting rid of dead weight prior to moving last
year.

Working only from memory, it is the "Paddy" character who says, "but if it
looks like a duck and sounds like a duck and walks like a duck, it's usually
a duck."

While Richard Darman was not the source of the phrase, the reason I
remembered this arcana is because of him. About a month after Mr. Darman got
a few minutes in the limelight for his "Darman Duck" comments (which, for
the record, he was applying to government-imposed  "user fees" in some
Federal budget proposal, trying to make the point that there is no
difference between a "user fee" and a tax), I read Mr. Ludlum's "Icarus
Agenda". The "duck phrase" struck me as incredibly timely - until I realized
that I was reading a paperback, and there was no way that Mr. Ludlum could
have "borrowed" from Mr. Darman's comments. So I checked the copyright page,
and yup, the hardcover version of "Icarus Agenda" had been published the
year before.

So that's the story.

No winners. Maybe you'll all do better when we return after this message for
"Double Jeopardy."
```

---

<a id="t-b83141d8c9"></a>
## Thread: OO COBOL for NetExpress 3.0 — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO COBOL for NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eZOXaCwB$GA.284@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Does anyone use the class libraries with NetExpress?  We are using
NetExpress 3.0/Dialog and are trying to use some of the class libraries
(creating a progress bar, generic message box, etc) and are having a tough
go of it.  The documentation is good in that it does show all of the
available methods, but there are very few examples.  I checked with
MicroFocus and they have no training available to use these.  Does anyone
know of a book that explains them?  Would these libraries be consistent
across all COBOL vendors or are they proprietary?  Any help would be
appreciated.

bill
```

#### ↳ Re: OO COBOL for NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EC6A4A.A8B9DD@home.com>`
- **References:** `<eZOXaCwB$GA.284@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Bill Wood wrote:
> 
> Does anyone use the class libraries with NetExpress?  We are using
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> bill

Your points well taken. Don't want to blow the horn, but IMHO opinion
NetExpress is a great product - but with serious shortcomings when it
comes to GUI design. So to answer your questions :-

- Yes the class libraries are proprietary - and that concerns me - if
each compiler vendor writes different sorts of classes, different
method-names to do the same thing - then we the developers are screwed -
we wont be able to move from compiler to compiler with reusable code. I
feel VERY STRONGLY that this is something the COBOL Standards should
address before COBOL-XXX is given the green light. 

- Dialog System only applies to NetExpress - no good even thinking of
using it with Fujitsu or Liant(Ryan McFarland) - similarly no point in
trying to use their 'window builders' with NetExpress. (There are
generic GUI builders out there like SP2 from Flexus - that's before Bob
Wolfe makes his pitch <G>)

- There are no books available on N/E class usage, in the context you
are asking - building windows - if there was such a tome I would be
reading it avidly. And this is the area where N/E has its shortcomings.
Don't get me wrong, they have totally embraced OO as a tool, whereas
other compilers, as a generalization, for the moment have assiduously
avoided the full OO issue - I think based on the failure of Visoc
(precursor to N/E) - the others are playing a wait-and-see game.

It's ironic they (Merant) build a great product, with a tremendous
amount of pizazz and a lot of OO technique built into the IDE that you
stare at each time you load their development package, but only give you
fairly basic examples. It has been a long learning-curve to get a handle
on just some of the GUI class information. (It is rather like buying
that glossy new auto with the crisp driver's manual - but you have to go
to one of the auto/book-store chains to get a manual giving you the full
McCoy - as yet for N/E GUI classes you can't go to that bookstore).

I haven't done any work yet designing a progress-bar, but over the
weekend, I'll hash something together showing how I build what I call an
InfoBox, (displays records instead of the progress-bar). How it is
constructed might help you to do a progress bar and the dialog you want
for general messaging.  If when you say 'general messaging' you mean the
standard Windows messagebox with 'OK, Retry, Cancel' - that's dead easy
and I'll include some examples. (When Simon Hart reads this, he may also
offer some help).

PS: You have to use the Progress-Bar class, as it isn't possible in 
Dialog System, but I would have thought you could build your general
messagebox from within Dialog System. (I don't use D/S).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: OO COBOL for NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf0795$5682d6c0$dd648cd4@default>`
- **References:** `<eZOXaCwB$GA.284@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Bill,

Using a progress bar is good for an application when procesing lots of
data.
So I use this object quite often.  The example im about to give is a
progress loaded
into a basic dialog box which pops up with no caption to show the user that
data is processing.

Most recently i have added the progress to the status bar, then when needed
i have displayed it
then processed it as below, only problem with this is you have to code
where to put it,
but i will give a simple example here.

       copy "guibase.if".



       Class-Id.   ProgramProgress
                   data is private
                   inherits from Modal.

       Object Section.

       Class-Control.
           Modal is class "modal"
           ProgramProgress is class "progind"
           .

       Working-Storage Section.

       copy "logevnt.cpy".
      *--> Copy files
       copy "mfres.cpy".    *> Symbol nos to identify screen controls
       copy "p2cevent.cpy". *> 78s used to identify physical events

       78  wsNullTerminator        value x'00'.
       78  wsNewLine               value x'0D0A'.


       Object.

       Object-Storage Section.
       01 oProgressIND          usage object reference.

       method-id. "create".
       local-storage section.
       01 lsResourceId                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnkStep     pic 9(9) comp-5.
       procedure division.
           invoke super "create"

           move ID-PROGINDICATOR to lsResourceID
           invoke self "GetObjectFromId" using lsResourceID
                                       returning oProgressIND
           move zeros to lnkstep
           move 100 to lnkstep
           invoke oProgressIND "setStepSize"

           invoke oProgressIND "step"


       Exit Method.
       End Method "Create".
       

 This method is invoked from the calling class.

       method-id. "killProgress".

       procedure division.

           Invoke oProgressIND "hide"
           Invoke oProgressIND "finalize" returning oProgressIND
       exit method.
       end method "killProgress".

This method is invoked from the calling class
Note: you can set up the step value using method "SetStepSize"
once you have calculated how much data needs to be processed.

       method-id. "stepprog".

       local-storage section.
       01 lsResourceId                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnkStep     pic 9(9) comp-5.


       procedure division.


          invoke oProgressIND "step"

       exit method.
       end method "stepprog".

       Method-Id. "Show".
       working-storage section.

       78  ws-X         value 400 .
       78  ws-Y         value 200.

       Local-Storage Section.
       01 aNewProgDialogBox     object reference.
       01 ResourceId               pic x(04) comp-5.
       Linkage Section.

       01 lsX                      pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lsY                      pic x(04) comp-5.


       Procedure Division using lsX, lsY.

           compute lsX = (lsX - ws-X) / 2
           compute lsY = (lsY - ws-Y) / 2

           invoke self "setxy" using lsX lsY

ShowNoFocus is used here so that the program
will continue to execute.

           invoke super "showNoFocus"

       Exit Method.
       End Method "Show".

       End Object.

       End Class ProgramProgress.

If you would like an example of the status bar then mail me(little more
complex)

Message Boxes are very common, this is nice all we have to do is invoke the
class
with a few parameters of course.

The example i will show is the most complex you will ever need.  It is
someone trying
to write to a network server when the server has gone down, so the
retry/cancel
msg class is used:.

Note: The reference MessageBox is class "msgbox"

             Perform Until ws-EOL = 'y'
             Move zeros to status-code
             call "CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST" using totalPath
                                               file-details
                                               returning status-code

             If status-code = 14625 or status-code = 14601
             *>cant read from device
                 invoke MessageBox "new" using self
                                           returning lsMessageBox
                 invoke lsMessageBox "setTitleZ" using wsHardWare-Fault
                 invoke lsMessageBox "RetryCancel"
                 string 'Cannot read from device - ' lnkserver-path
                      wsNewLine
                      'Please contact Administrator on Ext. 6561'
                      wsnullterminator
                      delimited by size
                      into wsTotalMSG
                 end-string

                 Invoke lsMessageBox "SetTypeCritical"
                 Invoke lsMessageBox "setUnmoveable"

A beep sound :)
                 Invoke Alarm "warning"

                 invoke lsMessageBox "setMessageZ"
                                        using wsTotalMSG
                 invoke lsMessageBox "show" returning lsId
                 invoke lsMessageBox "finalize" returning lsMessageBox

                 If  lsId = mb-return-retry
                   *>lets try to access the server again:.

                     Move 0 to lnk-HardWare-Code
                 Else
                   *>cancel was choosen
                     Move 'y' to ws-EOL
                     Move 1 to lnk-HardWare-Code

                 End-If

                 Move spaces to wsTotalMSG

             else

             Move 'y' to ws-EOL
             If status-code = zeros *>file already exists.

      *      If File-Size <> zeros *>file already exists
                 invoke MessageBox "new" using self
                                           returning lsMessageBox
                 invoke lsMessageBox "setTitleZ" using wsOverWrite
                 invoke lsMessageBox "yesno"
                 string 'Overwrite file? - '
                      totalPath
      *               delimited by x"00"
                      into wsTotalMSG
                 end-string


The below settypequestion puts the little question
bitmap into the msgbox

                 Invoke lsMessageBox "SetTypeQuestion"
                 invoke lsMessageBox "setMessageZ"
                                        using wsTotalMSG

                 Invoke Alarm "warning"
                 invoke lsMessageBox "show" returning lsId
                 invoke lsMessageBox "finalize" returning lsMessageBox
                 *>test outcome
                 If lsId = mb-return-yes
                    Move 0 to lnk-Filecode
                 Else
                    Move 1 to lnk-Filecode
                 end-if
                 Move 'y' to ws-EOL
             end-if
             end-if
             end-perform


Simon R Hart
Eaton.

> go of it.  The documentation is good in that it does show all of the
> available methods, but there are very few examples.  I checked with

>
```

```
This is machine-specific.  On 3090 mainframe, do NOT use comp as these are
converted to Comp-3 by the compiler anyway!

Phil
Lisa or Jeff wrote in message <7rut5q$ivi@netaxs.com>...
>Per the question on performance, if you are processing a high volume
>of records and/or doing a lot of CPU intensive work with each
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>handling.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL performance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sfvtt$cr8@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rut5q$ivi@netaxs.com> <7sftk7$jka$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
M. Lenz <Mogens.Lentz@sas.dk> wrote in message
news:7sftk7$jka$1@news.dknet.dk...
>
> This is machine-specific.  On 3090 mainframe, do NOT use comp as these are
> converted to Comp-3 by the compiler anyway!
>
> Phil

Used to be true for S/390 (S/370)  - not true with latest COBOL for
this-and-that compilers (if you use the correct compiler options).

Still a good warning that what happens with COMP is
compiler/operating-system dependent.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL performance

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F00394.18D7E6B0@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7rut5q$ivi@netaxs.com> <7sftk7$jka$1@news.dknet.dk> <7sfvtt$cr8@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Huh: Going back to COBOL 63 on DOS releases 18 - 26 and COBOL F on OS360
MFT/MVT COMP was binary and SYNC was advisable when using COMP.  I used
those compilers and those statements hold true for all of the MVS
compilers I have used since then.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical programming Services morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca	

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> M. Lenz <Mogens.Lentz@sas.dk> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL performance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sp43a$kmo@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7rut5q$ivi@netaxs.com> <7sftk7$jka$1@news.dknet.dk> <7sfvtt$cr8@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <37F00394.18D7E6B0@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
I *think* that this was referring to the fact that (historically) IBM
mainframe COBOLs have converted binary (USAGE COMP/COMP-4) items to
"packed-decimal" INTERNALLY before doing many operations on them -
especially when comparing/working with other fields that may be other
USAGEs.  In other words, the compiler generated the packed-decimal
instructions - rather than the binary ones (in many/some cases).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL performance

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EB7DAA.27EEAC12@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7rut5q$ivi@netaxs.com> <7sftk7$jka$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
"M. Lenz" wrote:
> 
> This is machine-specific.  On 3090 mainframe, do NOT use comp as these
> are converted to Comp-3 by the compiler anyway!
> 

Phil

This is wrong.  Integer maths should be done with COMP.  If you mix
COMP and COMP-3 you will get conversion (don't know if one wins, it
might be COMP-3).

If you use subscripts then use COMP.  This is stated in the MVS
programmers guide on efficiency.

If you are adding small integers together then use COMP it will be
handled in direct machine instructions and not handed to the maths
unit.

The main problem is when you convert from one to another.  I have
often seen WS variables defined as display (not COMP or COMP-3) that
were never written to a terminal, whenever an ADD was done it was
converted into some internal representation and then back out again.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL performance

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sglt6$ksk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7rut5q$ivi@netaxs.com> <7sftk7$jka$1@news.dknet.dk> <37EB7DAA.27EEAC12@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <37EB7DAA.27EEAC12@zip.com.au>,
  Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
> "M. Lenz" wrote:
> >
> > This is machine-specific.  On 3090 mainframe, do NOT use comp as
these
> > are converted to Comp-3 by the compiler anyway!
> >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In general you are correct.  However, in my experience this is a
machine, compiler, and OS specific issue. Therefore, if "efficiency" is
truly an issue I think it behooves the developer to understand the
relationship. For example: is sign an overpunch or separate?, does the
processor require conversion or not?, do the OS components care?.  Good
of you to point out that it is worthwhile to check documentation for
pointers on efficiency, or lack thereof.

As for the WS variables of which you speak...I recently worked on a
contract where management (VP software dev) *insisted* that *all* WS
numerics be pictured *display*.  Go figure...
-trblshtr-
```

## Thread: Using Learn Cobol 24 Hours by Thane Hubbell — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Using Learn Cobol 24 Hours by Thane Hubbell

- **From:** "COLLINSJ" <COLLINSJ@xtra.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sescl$8a5k2$1@titan.xtra.co.nz>`

```
I am new to Cobol and struck a problem in Chapter 17 of this book,the only
one to date.  Both Chapt 17a and d have the same error code as follows :
JMP0613-U SORT MERGE LIBARY CANNOT BE LOADED .
FB3BESORT,DLL  CODE = 1157 = CHAPT17A  ADR=00401233.

If any one has any idea please let me know
Regards John
collinsj@xtra.co.nz
```

```
wrote:

>I am new to Cobol and struck a problem in Chapter 17 of this book,the only
>one to date.  Both Chapt 17a and d have the same error code as follows :
>JMP0613-U SORT MERGE LIBARY CANNOT BE LOADED .
>FB3BESORT,DLL  CODE = 1157 = CHAPT17A  ADR=00401233.

We are talking in e-mal now about this problem - but no solution yet.
Anyone have any ideas?  This might be just a v4 over v3 install
problem.  He is now using v4.
```

#### ↳ Re: Using Learn Cobol 24 Hours by Thane Hubbell

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ed36d3.78478852@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7sescl$8a5k2$1@titan.xtra.co.nz>`

```
On Sat, 25 Sep 1999 15:47:43 +1200, "COLLINSJ" <COLLINSJ@xtra.co.nz>
wrote:

>I am new to Cobol and struck a problem in Chapter 17 of this book,the only
>one to date.  Both Chapt 17a and d have the same error code as follows :
>JMP0613-U SORT MERGE LIBARY CANNOT BE LOADED .
>FB3BESORT,DLL  CODE = 1157 = CHAPT17A  ADR=00401233.

Problem Solved!

It was a version over version install problem that had it confused.

Solution: Uninstall all Fujitsu products from the Add/Remove programs.
DELETE the \FSC directory and all subdirectories.
Run REGEDIT and search for Fujitsu removing all entries.
Re-install v4.  Everything works!
```

```
I think this is a real bloody pain. Mr. Microsoft again. Same headache

junk really screws things up. By comparison the old DOS RM/COBOL V2
re-installs just over-writing what exists - but even there, thanks to

#". So you have to do SETVER - but Windows 98 no longer gives you SETVER
automatically - you now have to search for it.

Hhha ! And Windows was supposed to make life easier.

PS. You can put your marketing hat back on <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
implied (said) AT&T bought IBM.  AT&T bought IBM Global services, also
known as Advantis, also know as IBM Internet services etc.  They did
this last year and promised no e-mail changes (to me when I asked) but

books and I'm not really happy about the whole thing.  So I have
registered a domain name (that I will use for my independant software
sales efforts), and I'm looking for web hosting and e-mail forwarding
that has the features I want.  Once I find it, I'll become someone
else. AT&T is nice enough to forward ibm.net traffic for a year, but
that's it.
```

## Thread: Variable Select/Assign? — 7 msgs

### Variable Select/Assign?

- **From:** "Brian Robinson" <brobin12@csc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sgbh2$bti$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Is it possible to use a variable name in the Select/Assign statement using
the Micro Focus compiler?
e.g. SELECT filename ASSIGN TO variablename

I am asking for a Cobol-ignorant coworker who is converting a system with a
large number of Cobol programs from a DOS platform to Unix.

TIA,

Brian Robinson
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Select/Assign?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EBD61E.DF02A88F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sgbh2$bti$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Brian Robinson wrote:
> 
> Is it possible to use a variable name in the Select/Assign statement using
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Brian Robinson

	input-output section.

            select                 Data-File
            assign                 Data-Filename
            organization           indexed
            access                 dynamic
            record key             Data-primeKey
            file status            ws-fileStatus.

       File Section.
       FD  Data-File.
       01  Data-record.
	   05 Data-primekey
           05 other..........       

	Working-storage section.
	01 Data-Filename			pic x(100).

	Procedure Division.
	move "c:\mydirectory\customers_company1.dat" to Data-Filename
	open input Data-File
	if ws-filestatus <> "00"
	  ....then goto error routine
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Select/Assign?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sh72m$7hj$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7sgbh2$bti$1@lore.csc.com> <37EBD61E.DF02A88F@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

: 	input-output section.
:             select                 Data-File
:             assign                 Data-Filename

: 	Working-storage section.
: 	01 Data-Filename			pic x(100).

It is optional whether the 'Data-Filename' is defined in
Working-Storage, it can be left to the compiler to create
an automatic variable for the actual filename.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EC363B.4A9222FF@home.com>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Semantics, semantics mon Anglais. I was giving the guy a quick answer on
how to do it - it's up to him to read the manuals/on-line. I didn't
propose to send him a White Paper on the merits of going from
command-line, hard-coded, variable names etc... I'll leave that to you -
although as you already pointed out - the poor wretch shouldn't even
have been given the assignment.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Select/Assign?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sgntc$3rq$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7sgbh2$bti$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Brian Robinson <brobin12@csc.com> wrote:
: Is it possible to use a variable name in the Select/Assign statement using
: the Micro Focus compiler?
: e.g. SELECT filename ASSIGN TO variablename

: I am asking for a Cobol-ignorant coworker who is converting a system with a
: large number of Cobol programs from a DOS platform to Unix.

Why has your company given this task to someone who is 'COBOL-ignorant'
and who (apparently) hasn't, can't, or won't read the manuals ?

And also (apparently) hasn't the initiative to write a small
program (or compile one of the ones he has) to see what happens.

I would say that the project has a bleak outlook.

But, yes it will take a variablename on the select .. assign, and
will probably also allow a VALUE OF FILE-ID variablename in the FD.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Select/Assign?

- **From:** "Brian Robinson" <brobin12@csc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7snojt$nma$1@lore.csc.com>`
- **References:** `<7sgbh2$bti$1@lore.csc.com>`

```
Thanks for all your replies.

"Cobol-ignorant" was a bad choice of words on my part.  My co-worker is
certainly capable in Cobol, but does not have the experience of those who
frequent this newsgroup.  I have many years, but not on the PC platform or
with Micro Focus.

The task-at-hand is a Dos to Unix conversion of a smaller installation,
requiring extensive scripting to adapt not just Cobol, but C and other
language programs to the new environment.  Therefore the project was
assigned to someone capable of analyzing and performing the wide range of
programming involved.  It was to her credit that she thought to inquire if a
variable was allowed in the Select/Assign, rather than convert the identical
program six times, with six separate input files, as the legacy system did.

Brian Robinson
```

## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35UG3.24789$ei1.46241@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> writes: > Denis wrote:
> > What we are looking for, is experience from companies that use PL/I and
> > COBOL (C/C++ or other language does not play in the game here) and have
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> and fixed-point numbers is what COBOL was designed for.

But so was PL/I !  PL/I has excellent fixed-point facilities,
both for arithmetic and for input/output,
and, of course, records with fixed fields is just one of
several types of fields that PL/I can process.

> I use PL/I as a substitute for assembler in applications where ease of
> coding, readability, and maintainability is more important than
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> use PL/I, but that's a subjective choice).
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ec2ce3@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<35UG3.24789$ei1.46241@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
> But so was PL/I !  PL/I has excellent fixed-point facilities,
> both for arithmetic and for input/output,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > use PL/I, but that's a subjective choice).

PL/I was just fine in its time.
I have used it extensively about 25 years ago.

I see some problems with PL/!:

1) it ain't available today as a viable platform on most systems
2) therefore hard to find skilled PL/I programmers, especially
the young (and therefore cheap) labor with "3-5" years of experience
that the industry seems to crave.
3) PL/I was too "permissive". Almost everything was allowed.
If you used the "wrong" type  variable, PL/I would assume some
suitable default behavior. Not at all strongly typed as a good HHL
should be.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ec3030@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<35UG3.24789$ei1.46241@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37ec2ce3@news3.prserv.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37ec2ce3@news3.prserv.net...
>Not at all strongly typed as a good HHL
> should be.


needless to say that should be HLL. This post is save nit-pickers out there
the
trouble of jumping on that...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF6C8A.9C5@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>`
- **References:** `<35UG3.24789$ei1.46241@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37ec2ce3@news3.prserv.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> I see some problems with PL/!:
> ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> should be.

Agreed.  This was a "good idea" that didn't work out.  Back when
compiles took a loooong time (anyone else remember one-day turnaround on
compiles?) it made sense to try and turn anything into a runnable
program, now it encourages lousy coding, but it's to imbedded in the
language to change.
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EC96DA.3C03E830@att.net>`
- **References:** `<35UG3.24789$ei1.46241@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
robin wrote:
>
I worked for IBM in the 1956-70 time frame and have always thought PL/I
had two major stumbling blocks:

1. it was a proprietary, i.e., IBM, language (talking 1965-70 here);
2. most big IBM mainframe shops had just converted from Autocoder to
COBOL, and not even IBM expected them to do it twice for such a small
payback (in most cases, anyway).

Personally, I liked PL/I; but have never worked in an installation which
uses it.

Bill Lynch
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

```
Arelem wrote:

> EXHIBIT NAMED was considered a debugging extension.  Now you are
> expected to use (Licensed Product) CODE/370 or other debugging
> facility.

I don't though.  I just code around to get the same effect as EXHIBIT
NAMED & EXHIBIT CHANGED.  Debugging tools are a real pain, especially if
I use pre-compilers to do database access.  A simple EXHIBIT NAMED is a
big time saver.

I don't mind too much if they force us to pay for their debugging
tools.  But just don't force us to USE them.
```

```
programmers in the area with the primary focus on COBOL, CICS, DB2, and MVS
(systems programming).  We have limited C++, HTML, and SMALLTALK.  I've been
with the company since college (yes 10 years ago) and really enjoy the work
we do.  I am not a professional headhunter, consultant, etc., but am an
employee trying to help with recruiting.  If you are interested, and have
one or more years of programming experience, please email me your resume at
kansascityjobs@kc.rr.com  If you have a background in finance or business it
would add another plus to your resume.

Kathy
```

## Thread: Evaluators sought for COBOL development tool — 1 msgs

### Evaluators sought for COBOL development tool

- **From:** bsisa@us.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sgc71$d1o$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In order to help us design user-friendly products with functions and
features customers want, designers and developers at IBM follow
User-Centered Design (UCD), an approach involving input from users.
Specifically, we are looking for COBOL developers who have experience
with MVS or S/390, and are familiar with Windows based Integrated
Development Environments (IDE�s), e.g., Micro Focus, ACUCOBOL, Visual
Basic, Visual C++, VA Java.  If interested in participating in a
usability test of a COBOL development tool, please send email to
STLUCD@US.IBM.COM.  Once your email is received, you will be sent a
questionnaire to see if you meet the criteria we are looking for in a
participant for this project.

The usability test will take place in October in San Jose, California.
For those who are selected, the honorarium will be $150 for three hours,
and we will work around your availability, including evenings.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Series 600 and 1200,, XA/R series, Compaq, DOS.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> should be.

If you want strong typing, you can have it.
```

```
robin  <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote:
>"Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> writes: 

>> I see some problems with PL/!:
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Series 600 and 1200,, XA/R series, Compaq, DOS.

But not on Linux and not at a reasonable price.  I can get C for Windows
for under $100 and I got C for CP/M many years ago for under $30.
I have the IBM OS/2 personal edition, unfortunatly, I don't have an
OS/2 system for it anymore and I do't think the version I have runs on
windows, but I'm only doing Linux developement anyway.  I've looked at
Liant (is this now open?) and as an individual who hates C and prefers
PL/1, I just could not justify spending that much money for a compiler.

>> 2) therefore hard to find skilled PL/I programmers, especially

my 3-5 years experience expires on Multics PL/1 and IBM PL/1F.

>> 3) PL/I was too "permissive". Almost everything was allowed.

>> suitable default behavior. Not at all strongly typed as a good HHL
>> should be.

I seem to remember warnings about all the things that could be a problem.
I've had more problems with C not reporting errors.
```

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Date Validation (long)

- **From:** Barticus@usa.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ec4621.37293925@netnews.att.net>`
- **References:** `<19990912225801.01554.00001107@ng-cf1.aol.com> <7ri6gt$f10@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37e03a9f.146828558@news.ntplx.net> <He6E3.27941$223.580463@typ12.nn.bcandid.com> <7rr9ci$pbp@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Thu, 16 Sep 1999 12:28:19 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Should anyone ask, I have *NO* intention of posting pre-Intrinsic Function
>code in the FAQ.  I know this shows my "prejudice" - but as it has been a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>function"

I just came off a job at a site using Unisys V Series.  On V there are
no intrinsic functions, there is no COBOL-85, there is only COBOL-74
and wishing will not make it different.  Unisys has declared the V to
be dead, an as of 2000-04-01 they will provide no support for it
whatsoever.  Someone doing a Y2K conversion on such a site might well
be in need of such routines.

The site in question already had a very old and well written date
conversion copybook, but some sites may not be so fortunate.  
```

### Printer Drivers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EC1440.60682496@home.com>`

```
Not strictly COBOL, but it is associated. I have NetExpress and use
PC_PRINT with a dot-matrix, (plenty good enough for what I do); user has
an HP laser.

I'm doing a test report and want to send the user a copy to review;
options - (a) motor across town and back - 2 hours, (b) mail him a
package - umpteen pages, and (c) which I prefer - zip him a printfile
via the Web, (having selected from Windows, 'write to disk').

PC_PRINT will recognize the selected default printer. If I get a copy of
my user's printer driver for HP laser, can I install it on my machine
without the actual device being attached ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>

```
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

Jimmy,

Yes you can. What i sugest is to simply install the HP driver from the
Windows
database of drivers(as HP are very common). Then send the output to a file
as you rightly said, then you can email him/her.

 Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

<a id="t-36df53ef27"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and WINSOCK — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL and WINSOCK

- **From:** Paul Galbraith <pdg@interlog.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ECEC4B.A8CD2130@interlog.com>`
- **References:** `<7rltgu$b3r$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37def94e.157928142@news1.ibm.net> <7rn7s5$q7h$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
You can definitely do this.  We have a working application that communicates
from a PC client to AS/400 server.  Both client and server are Cobol and do
direct socket communications.

For the PC side, check out Microsoft's sockets API documentation.  You should be
able to call all the relavent functions from MF Cobol.  The MF Cobol
documentation provides information on how to interface to the Windows API
functions.

The Windows Sockets API documentation is at
http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/sdkdoc/winsock/apistart_520i.htm.

Good luck!

Paul

Edwin O'Farrell wrote:

> WIN32 NT/IIS
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

---

<a id="t-271ba418c0"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and WINSOCK — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL and WINSOCK

- **From:** Paul Galbraith <pdg@interlog.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ECF52A.55F53A9A@interlog.com>`
- **References:** `<7rltgu$b3r$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37def94e.157928142@news1.ibm.net> <37df07eb.5739816@news.idola.net.id>`

```
You can do this from Cobol using the AS/400 sockets API.  Take a look at the
_Sockets Programming_ book at
http://publib.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/QB3ANN03/CCONTENTS.  This
book is for V4R4 but there should be publications for earlier OS/400 releases as
well.  Good luck.

Paul

Yatno wrote:

> i have same q,
> and in AS400 platform
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
    The university of GA used PL/I heavily in 72-76, and they (system staff)
were VERY fond of it.  However I remember someone bragging about how much
they could cram into one line.  How readable is PL/I on average (I have
never used PL/I myself, and have seen some cobol that would make your brain
bleed).  Remember what punch cards did to neatness?



robin <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF6DC7.4D7F@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7sklf9$2j8$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> How readable is PL/I on average 

(IMHO) PL/I is more readable than COBOL, or at least used to be, because
COBOL had to resort to tricks to do things like bit-twiddling that were
intrinsic to PL/I.  As to C/C++, PL/I:C :: Rexx:Perl.
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f0c05e$1$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37EEB49A.E85E534E@att.net> <37ef2e26.1973620@enews.newsguy.com>`

```
In <37ef2e26.1973620@enews.newsguy.com>, on 27 Sep 1999 at 09:07, cmcgann
at connect stop ie (Clement McGann) said:

>>Who else offered it then? IIRC IBM didn't get a working compiler
>>to market until 1967.

>The Burroughs big mainframe of the banking world - back in 1972 
>(if not before) had an excellent PLI compiler - the B6700
>It also used virtual storage long before IBM ever heard of it

When did the B6700 support virtual storage?

Virtual Memory was invented in the late 1950s in Manchester (England)
under the name One-level store (OLS).  IBM worked on virtual memory and
virtual machine concepts in the lab in 1964 (perhaps even before).  The
first IBM computer using virtual storage outside of an IBM lab was a
modified 360/40 running CP/40 in Cambridge (Massachusetts) in 1965 or
1966.  Then the 360/67 and CP/67 came along and the rest is history . . .

Gilbert Saint-flour
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00

```
Actually, the B*5500* supported virtual storage long before the 6700.  I
won't argue with your chronology, but my recollection is that Burroughs
was the first to commercially market a virtual storage system on any
scale. These systems came with head-per-track disk for better swapping
performance. (They used segmented, not paged, memory).  I can't say I
remember PL/I on these systems.  The MCP was written entirely in
ALGOL-60.
Definitely ahead of their time. 

Gilbert Saint-flour wrote:
> >The Burroughs big mainframe of the banking world - back in 1972
> >(if not before) had an excellent PLI compiler - the B6700
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gilbert Saint-flour
```

```
The B5000 which was in use by 1962 had virtual memory.  I may still have
a Burroughs manual.  
The Honeywell 800 FACT compiler had automatic segmentation where the
program segment would be paged in from tape and a segment was flagged as
not in memory by having the word that pointed to it set to bad parity
(they took advantage of a peculiarity of the binary multiply
instruction). The performance was less than optimum.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services  morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca  

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Peter Flass wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> virtual memory.  The laughers needed to have better marketing.
```

```
Peter Flass wrote:
> 
> Actually, the B*5500* supported virtual storage long before the 6700. 

True, but then the B5000 supported it before the B5500 ;-)

> won't argue with your chronology, but my recollection is that Burroughs

> scale. 

My recollection was that Ferranti didn't have a lot of sales for the
Atlas and the Titan. OTOH, I believe that Ferranti published first, and
they had paging rather than just segmentation.

> These systems came with head-per-track disk for better swapping
> performance. 

Yeah, and IBM pooh-poohed the idea of fixed head disks until they
announced the 2305, at which point it became respectable <g>.

> I can't say I
> remember PL/I on these systems. 

Well, Burroughs did claim that their machines were the only ones
properly designed for PL/I, and there can be no doubt that the cactus
stack made things easier.

> ALGOL-60.

Can you say ESPOL? DC ALGOL? Neither of those was ALGOL 60, although
they were derived from them.

> Definitely ahead of their time.

In some ways. But the B6500 had a problem state instruction that let you
circumvent security.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZajyNy2pwIzlt2NUs+CJQetYa287@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37EEB49A.E85E534E@att.net> <37ef2e26.1973620@enews.newsguy.com> <37f0c05e$1$tfs$mr2ice@news> <37F0E952.7E1F@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> <37F27EB1.6A074BF2@nsf.gov>`

```
>> Actually, the B*5500* supported virtual storage long before the 6700. 
>True, but then the B5000 supported it before the B5500 ;-)

For the record, the very first customer shipment of a virtual storage
computer was a Burroughs B5000, delivered in late 1961.  The B5500 was
a later enhancement of the same architecture, and the B5700 was a
B5500 after marketing started changing 5s to 7s.  The B6500 was a new
architecture similar to the B5000.  Less than 100 B6500s shipped
before marketing changed it to B6700.  Direct descendants of the B6700
were the B7700, B6800, B7800, B6900, B5900, B7900, all the A Series
models and the modern Unisys Clearpath HMP NX Series.

The B5000 never had an assembler -- from the very beginning all
software was written in ALGOL.  The first B5000 program was the ALGOL
compiler, which was compiled manually, and went thru weeks of
debugging and patching before it self-compiled. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 7)_

```
news:ZajyNy2pwIzlt2NUs+CJQetYa287@4ax.com...
> >> Actually, the B*5500* supported virtual storage long before the 6700.
> >True, but then the B5000 supported it before the B5500 ;-)

> For the record, the very first customer shipment of a virtual storage
> computer was a Burroughs B5000, delivered in late 1961

I think the very first was the ATLAS computer designed in Manchester,
England, and shipped in 1961. The virtual memory model used in IBM's
AS/400 (the famous 'single-level store') is still based on the ATLAS
design.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t0p5p$k1t$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37EEB49A.E85E534E@att.net> <37ef2e26.1973620@enews.newsguy.com> <37f0c05e$1$tfs$mr2ice@news> <37F0E952.7E1F@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> <37F27EB1.6A074BF2@nsf.gov> <ZajyNy2pwIzlt2NUs+CJQetYa287@4ax.com>`

```
Some clarifications, provided by thanks to a friend and coworker who was on
the team at the time:

Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote in message
news:ZajyNy2pwIzlt2NUs+CJQetYa287@4ax.com...

> The B5000 never had an assembler -- from the very beginning all
> software was written in ALGOL.  The first B5000 program was the ALGOL
> compiler,

... all true so far ...  with the single, *possible* exception of the
original version of the ALGOL compiler.

>which was compiled manually,

Not quite.  Two compilers were written at the same time.  The "father of
them all" was written in a language called OSIL which produced B5000 object
code on a B220 (first generation) system.  The process of building the very
first B5000-resident ALGOL compiler was a long and arduous one; after the
first "assembly" of the object code, which required twelve straight hours of
machine time and a whole lot of manual tweaks in the process, the
participants agreed that they wouldn't do *that* again, and from that point
all revisions to the *original* compiler were through patches to the object
code.  There was only one assembly/bind of that original compiler.

>and went thru weeks of debugging and patching before it self-compiled.

Yes, both the original version and its updated-in-parallel ALGOL-language
version were tweaked until the original version could produce an ALGOL
compiler from the ALGOL source that would in turn compile a working ALGOL
compiler from the same source.    That process began on a B5000 simulator
running on a Philco 2000 system, because the hardware was not quite ready
for testing at that point.  But the developers had not actually gotten the
ALGOL-symbol version to compile and run on the simulator by the time the
B5000 hardware was ready for their use, so the original "handcrafted"
compiler did indeed run for a time on the B5000 system itself.

Once the "handcrafted" compiler successfully compiled a new ALGOL compiler
using the ALGOL symbol file, and *that* compiler in turn compiled a *second*
ALGOL compiler that seemed to work, the developers thought they were done,
but unfortunately, that *third* compiler produced from the same symbol file
failed because of an obscure problem in the original handcrafted compiler.

But once the ALGOL-language version of the ALGOL compiler successfully
"triple-compiled",  the original compiler produced on the B220 was rendered
obsolete.   I have been told that, from the very beginning, the code
produced by the ALGOL-language ALGOL compiler was always much more
efficient, both in processor and memory utilization, than its "handcrafted"
predecessor.

From that day to this, updated ALGOL compilers shipped to Unisys customers
are three generations removed from their previous versions; that is, the
patched ALGOL compiler is always compiled with a patched ALGOL compiler that
itself was compiled with a patched ALGOL compiler.   Having a compiler that
will compile itself proved not to be quite enough.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** multics@wizvax.wizvax.net (Richard Shetron)
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00

```
Shmuel (Seymour J.) Metz <smetz@nsf.gov> wrote:
>Peter Flass wrote:
>> 
>> Actually, the B*5500* supported virtual storage long before the 6700. 
>
>True, but then the B5000 supported it before the B5500 ;-)

IIRC I thought the Bourroughs machines did segmentation but did not
do paging.  The entire segment was swapped in and out.

Multics did both segmentation and pageing.  There were no 'files',
everything on disk was a segment and mapped into virtual memory when
first refrenced.
```

## Thread: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes — 3 msgs

### Re: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes

- **From:** "J. Keith Jackson" <jkeithj@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cdCH3.8333$E85.102162@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<nauH3.9069$OU1.73830@news4.mia> <01bf08ad$f7c96a80$e13563c3@default>`

```
Simon:

Thanks for the reply.  In the interim I discovered the problem.  Apparently
to run this program, SHARE.EXE must be loaded.  I had failed to include that
in my AUTOEXEC.BAT on the notebook.

But, for the sake of completeness:

>Where the data files are when you try running on the laptop, where they
>are when you run them on the desktop

Laptop:  Program in C:\CMSPRG, data in C:\CMSFIL.
Original Desktop:  C:\CMSPRG and D:\CMSFIL.
Win98 Desktop:  D:\CMSPRG and D:\CMSFIL.

The appropriate file was edited to reflect the location of the data files.
I've done this before with no problem.

>What the data files do, and their names

The data files are databases, with various names too numerous to include.

>Are the data files in the same folder as the executing program file(s)?

No, they are in a different one as noted above.

>The error codes are compiler specific, you need to find out which compiler
>or "flavour" you are using.

I have no clue about that.

But as I said, I discovered that apparently SHARE.EXE must be loaded in
order for this app to function.  I still don't understand why "recovering"
them would change this, but...as long as I have it working I won't ask.

Again, thank you for your response.

J. Keith Jackson
jkeithj@bellsouth.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KzKH3.18$iG2.1314@client>`
- **References:** `<nauH3.9069$OU1.73830@news4.mia> <01bf08ad$f7c96a80$e13563c3@default> <cdCH3.8333$E85.102162@news1.mia>`

```
J. Keith Jackson <jkeithj@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:cdCH3.8333$E85.102162@news1.mia...
[snip]
> But as I said, I discovered that apparently SHARE.EXE must be loaded in
> order for this app to function.  I still don't understand why "recovering"
> them would change this, but...as long as I have it working I won't ask.

Mr. Jackson:

I cannot speak for GEAC, but I think I have some insight into this
particular situation.

This is a multiuser accounting package that you are moving from one machine
to another.  And, therein lies the source of the symptoms you are
experiencing.

The multiuser file system used by the package, in order to communicate with
other (potential) users on the same network, must record in each file
overhead that the particular file is open in a shared mode by a user.  This
is really not an issue on the network since it is, after all, a multiuser
file system.  This is just a means to communicate with other cooperative
processes (in this case, other users attached to the network at your office)
that the files are being used by more than one user, and that certain steps
must be taken by each cooperating process to assure integrity of the data.
If something happens while a user has a file open on your network (for
instance, the user turns off his PC without exiting the accounting package)
these markers remain in the file overhead.  This is of little consequence on
the network, since the other users continue on their merry way.

However, you took home a snapshot of the files, at least some of which were
still marked as being open by some user.  But now your configuration
indicates that you are not on a network, and therefore are not in a
situation where other users might indeed have files open.  This clearly
indicates that a file integrity problem might exist and the file system
refuses to go on without having run the recovery program.  The recovery
program, having gained exclusive, rather than shared, access to the file,
can safely reset the markers that indicate other users have the file open.

By installing SHARE you are fooling the file system into thinking that a
multiuser environment exists (the other user could be another process on the
same machine), so the existence of the markers does not, by itself, indicate
an integrity problem.

What to do?

Fisrt, you must be quite cautious that you are not losing transactions when
you take work home.  Apparently you are the single user of this system at
work, so this is an issue that you can control.

Second, you should run the recovery program at work on all files when no
users are using the package.  This will reset the markers that have built up
over the months.

Please be assured that while this has been an inconvenience for you, it is
better in the long run for a file system -- responsible for holding your
company's vital data -- err on the side of caution.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ss9kg$2if$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<nauH3.9069$OU1.73830@news4.mia> <01bf08ad$f7c96a80$e13563c3@default> <cdCH3.8333$E85.102162@news1.mia>`

```
    This is just a shot in the dark, but some cobol runtimes keep track of
how many times a database has been opened and closed, and if a mismatch is
found for some reason (user turns pc off without exiting program, program
abends, program ends without closing data files) it assumes that the
databases are corrupt and wants to have them rebuilt.  But when on a network
(share loaded), that rule is not enforced.
```
