<a id="t-c2659a983b"></a>
## Thread: Searching Tables — 21 this month / 28 total

### Searching Tables

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com>`

```
Just struck me I've never used indexing to search conventional text
tables, (not OO Collections/Dictionaries - which can use both
approaches, plus some others). I use :-

	perform varying n from 1 by 1 until
	  condition met or n > MaxTable size
		if condition met
		   do something, if necessary, and then
		   Exit Perform
		End-if
	End-perform

Anybody care to comment upon the difference in performance between the 
above and search using index - or are they comparable ?

Seems the sort of topic that Judson would care to comment on.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com>`

```
On Sun, 26 Sep 1999 02:48:25 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>Just struck me I've never used indexing to search conventional text
>tables, (not OO Collections/Dictionaries - which can use both
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Seems the sort of topic that Judson would care to comment on.

For sequential table searches I rarely use SEARCH, it's faster to code
my own, without the extra coding overhead of "indexed by" and using an
inded.  However, if speed is important and the table is large, I sort
it, and use SEARCH ALL  - which is worth the coding overhead to setup
because of performance gains.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7skcd7$487$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net>`

```
: On Sun, 26 Sep 1999 02:48:25 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
: wrote:

:>Just struck me I've never used indexing to search conventional text
:>tables, (not OO Collections/Dictionaries - which can use both
:>approaches, plus some others). I use :-
:>
:>	perform varying n from 1 by 1 until
:>	  condition met or n > MaxTable size
:>		if condition met
:>		   do something, if necessary, and then
:>		   Exit Perform
:>		End-if
:>	End-perform
:>


As just a tiny point (don't get upset and rant at me), it may
be better to have:

            until n > MaxTable size
               or condition met

because n may be used within the 'condition met' and this will
lead to a bound check error if it really is > MaxTable size.
Checking n first prevents this bound failure because Cobol
will short circuit the test when it sees 'TRUE or ..'.

That is, Cobol is not clever enough to know that the check for
n > MaxTable should be done before using n if it is done the
way you had written, but it is clever enough to not continue
and cause a bound failure if you give it the check first.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hmmm...interesting.

I NEVER write my own and ALWAYS use SEARCH.

I used to write my own and had some quite sexy methods which were even more
efficient than the binary chop used by SEARCH ALL on a sorted table, given
that there were certain conditions in the data (e.g. a preponderance of a
given generic key, say, more entries start with "C" than start with "F").
Most of these advanced techniques were skewed variations on binary chop. (I
don't really want to go into this as it was all a long time ago and I have
no intention of encouraging others into what I now consider were the sins of
my youth  <G>, besides, in those days, performance REALLY mattered; now it
just matters a bit...)

I offer the following observations in support of my position:

1. There is no "index overhead" and I don't know what Thane's talking about.
(Ah, sorry Thane, I just re-read what you wrote again and you are talking
about a CODING overhead. That's the time it takes to write INDEXED BY,
right?...(You should be ashamed of yourself...and you an example to new
COBOL programmers...!!<grin>).The overhead of conversion is less than it is
for a subscript, as the compiler does not have to multiply the subscript out
in order to obtain an offset into the table; the index is already held as an
offset so there is one less step in evaluating the address.  Where the index
is being manipulated, the compiler must obviously do arithmetic whether a
subscript or an index is used, but the index, being more directly under
compiler control will be in a form for optimised arithmetic; the subscript
(being defined by the programmer), may not be.

2. Defining your table with INDEXED BY and using SEARCH means that if the
table is extended, (you add new entries to it) you do NOT have to make ANY
change to the Procedure Division code. If you use PERFORM VARYING you must
go through and update the termination condition for every PERFORM which
looks it up, because you have now increased the OCCURS clause in your table
definition.

3. COBOL is a self documenting, easily maintainable language. Consider the
two following examples of table lookup:

PERFORM ...VARYING ...FROM ..BY ...
UNTIL
(Then check the termination condition AGAIN to decide if you found it or
not!)

as in the example given, which by the way, I have no issue with...

>>
>> perform varying n from 1 by 1 until
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> End-perform

OR

SEARCH  table
      AT END perform it-wasnt-found
WHEN table-entry = thing-im-looking-for
       perform i-found-it
END-SEARCH

IMHO, there is no comparison in terms of readability and ease of
maintenance, so I subscribe to the latter.

You may object in terms of "performance" but that's a smokescreen, because
the difference in performance (if any) will be so slight as to not matter.
On today's hardware platforms you are talking maybe a few milliseconds. (I
haven't done comprehensive tests on different platforms with thousands of
entries, and I have no intention of doing so...I know enough about computer
architecture to know that this is acceptable.)

Compare it with the time taken by a maintenance programmer to find that the
OR condition in your PERFORM varying termination could never be fulfilled,
because there was some dodgy input, or somebody didn't extend the
termination condition (in just one of the six places where the table is
looked up), after the table had new entries put in it (well, it compiled OK,
didn't it?) at 3:00am, having being called from a warm bed...(I was that
soldier. That was around the time I started using SEARCH...)

SUMMARY:

Use whatever you feel comfortable with, and whatever meets your personal
criteria.

Don't write off SEARCH, it is valuable for the reasons outlined above. (I've
written 1 or 2 COBOL programs in the last 30 years, and all the table
lookups I write nowadays, use SEARCH)

Pete.

>wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
peter dashwood wrote:
> 
> Hmmm...interesting.
 
> 
> as in the example given, which by the way, I have no issue with...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> End-perform

Just one thought on the above, based on Richard Plinston's comments. It
could perhaps be abbreviated to  :-

  perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxTable size
     
     if condition met
        do something, if necessary, and then
        Exit Perform ( or even Exit Method - in OO)
     End-if
 End-perform

I think I'm correct in saying that Exit Perform will neatly finalize
the perform routine, and the above means you're not testing for two
perform true/false conditions. And of course, assumption is your
compiler has Exit Perform.

Hamp shire - do we ? Still the US version did suggest to me that I had
spent my years in the WRAF not the RAF ! - wonder what WRAF means in
American English )

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sog7r$njt$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

: 2. Defining your table with INDEXED BY and using SEARCH means that if the
: table is extended, (you add new entries to it) you do NOT have to make ANY
: change to the Procedure Division code. If you use PERFORM VARYING you must
: go through and update the termination condition for every PERFORM which
: looks it up, because you have now increased the OCCURS clause in your table
: definition.

IMHO the table size should always be set as a value in a variable
and that is what should be checked.  Also a separate variable
should be used to give the current limit for entries.  In this
way the procedure division would not need to be changed if the
table size is increased.

However, this does not mean that I advocate PERFORM over SEARCH,
just that good coding practice can eliminate the disadvantage
you claim.

:>> perform varying n from 1 by 1 until
:>>   condition met or n > MaxTable size
:>> if condition met
:>>    do something, if necessary, and then
:>>    Exit Perform
:>> End-if
:>> End-perform

The repitition of 'condition met' is not required as the
EXIT PERFORM will ensure that the UNTIL will never see
this as true.

The UNTIL test only needs be UNTIL n > TableLimit where
this is the last item currently in the table, there is
no need to continue searching unused items.


: Compare it with the time taken by a maintenance programmer to find that the
: OR condition in your PERFORM varying termination could never be fulfilled,

I find it interesting that this is the exact problem with your
example.

: because there was some dodgy input, or somebody didn't extend the
: termination condition (in just one of the six places where the table is
: looked up), after the table had new entries put in it (well, it compiled OK,

Do you mean that you don't localise the searches into a performed
paragraph ?

: SUMMARY:

: Use whatever you feel comfortable with, and whatever meets your personal
: criteria.

Absolutely.


: Thane Hubbell wrote in message <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net>...
:>On Sun, 26 Sep 1999 02:48:25 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
:>wrote:
:>
:>>Just struck me I've never used indexing to search conventional text
:>>tables, (not OO Collections/Dictionaries - which can use both
:>>approaches, plus some others). I use :-
:>>
:>> perform varying n from 1 by 1 until
:>>   condition met or n > MaxTable size
:>> if condition met
:>>    do something, if necessary, and then
:>>    Exit Perform
:>> End-if
:>> End-perform
:>>
:>>Anybody care to comment upon the difference in performance between the
:>>above and search using index - or are they comparable ?
:>>
:>>Seems the sort of topic that Judson would care to comment on.
:>
:>For sequential table searches I rarely use SEARCH, it's faster to code
:>my own, without the extra coding overhead of "indexed by" and using an
:>inded.  However, if speed is important and the table is large, I sort
:>it, and use SEARCH ALL  - which is worth the coding overhead to setup
:>because of performance gains.
```

```
On 27 Sep 1999 19:23:39 GMT, Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote:

>peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

>
>IMHO the table size should always be set as a value in a variable
>and that is what should be checked. 

Why?  Doesn't this work for you:

	IF row-number > LENGTH OF TABLE / LENGTH OF table-row
		BANG THE PROGRAMMER ON THE HEAD
	END-IF

> Also a separate variable
>should be used to give the current limit for entries.  In this
>way the procedure division would not need to be changed if the
>table size is increased.
Doesn't:  MAX-ROW = LENGTH OF My-Table / LENGTH OF table-row

MAX-ROW isn't set to GIVE the number of rows, MAX-ROW is computed from the table.  One
possible error situation less
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

```
Volker Bandke wrote in message <37efc8c7.11237688@news2.ibm.net>...
>On 27 Sep 1999 19:23:39 GMT, Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>and that is what should be checked.

No, I didn't! From your snippage it looks like I wrote that and I protest
vigourously. I had a feeling SOMEBODY would suggest that the table length be
set as a variable and I had a feeling that SOMEONE  else (probably a
MicroFocus User) would suggest using the function which does this.

I don't know who actually wrote this because I can't find it on the thread
delivered by my NG server. I have suspected for some time that not ALL
messages were getting through...From your snippage it looks like it was
Richard. I take no issue with what he suggests, in fact,
I have no issue with any of these methods and have used them all myself on
various occasions in the past.

It all misses the point of my posting, which was that SEARCH is a "better"
(certainly more immediately intelligible and certainly requiring less
maintenance) option.

I personally don't care how you (not you personally, Volker; I mean
everybody here...) code your table lookups. I respect your right to do it
any way you want to. The thing that prompted me to join this thread was that
I DO care about what new COBOL programmers do and the advice they are given.
I couldn't just stand by and see people being advised to use a complex
construct (OK, it isn't complex to experienced programmers...but it can be
used for many other purposes than searching tables, so it is not immediately
apparent what it does...) when there is a simple, purpose-built Verb in the
language which does the job admirably.

In the past few weeks, since I have been reading this newsgroup, I have been
disappointed at the response to questions by junior programmers. Responses
like "RTFM" are not helpful or conducive to improving the standard of COBOL
coding in general. People should be able to come here and it should be
"safe" to post (even if the question is "stupid"). I accept that it is
tiresome when the same old questions pop up time and again, and I think Bill
is to be commended for starting a COBOL FAQ, which is the obvious answer to
this problem. But we, as professional programmers, should be prepared to
listen and help wherever possible.
Why? Apart from any moral considerations, it makes sound economic sense to
ensure that the industry is healthy. And besides, you never know when it
might be you who's called on to maintain the newbie's code...

The next thing is that we should be careful in the advice we give. Bad or
wrong advice is worse than saying nothing. I am not suggesting that using
PERFORM VARYING to look up tables is bad or wrong advice, but it should be
balanced with the alternative, which is SEARCH.

Simple, lucid, explanations without all the morass in the manual are what I
needed when I was a newbie, and I don't think that's changed.

By all means have intellectual arguments about the fine points of the
language. It's good for all of us.  But not in response to someone's
question about how to do something.

NEWBIE NOTE: Use SEARCH for table lookups and all of this is just
academic...<grin>


Pete.

table.  One
```

```
         Virtual, means never knowing where your next byte is coming from.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7soiod$cij@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7sog7r$njt$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Given part of this thread, I wanted to make certain that anyone reading it
who doesn't already know about the EXIT PERFORM enhancements in the draft of
the next Standard understands that there are TWO variations (one with and
one without the word "CYCLE"), the definitions are:

"8) The execution of an EXIT PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase
shall cause control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement
immediately following the next END-PERFORM phrase that matches the most
closely preceding, and as yet unterminated, inline PERFORM statement.

9) The execution of an EXIT PERFORM statement with the CYCLE phrase shall
cause control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement immediately
preceding the next END-PERFORM phrase that matches the most closely
preceding, and as yet unterminated, inline PERFORM statement."

The "real word" difference is that you should use the "CYCLE" key word if
you DO want the "UNTIL" to be retested - and don't use it if you want an
"unconditional" escape from the loop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7soht4$cav@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
peter dashwood <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote in message
news:37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com...
> Hmmm...interesting.
  <much very good stuff - snipped>
>
> SUMMARY:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Don't write off SEARCH, it is valuable for the reasons outlined above.
(I've
> written 1 or 2 COBOL programs in the last 30 years, and all the table
> lookups I write nowadays, use SEARCH)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

and I'll add to this summary,

- Check your compiler and see if it already provides (as an extension) the
SORT table syntax that will become a part of the next Standard - so you can
change your "sequential" SEARCH into a SEARCH ALL.   *OBVIOUSLY* this isn't
appropriate if you only search the table a "few times" in your program, but
if the overhead of the SORT is offset by frequent searches, this can be
quite a useful technique.

P.S. How an "index" is stored is compiler/operating-system specific.  It is
true that it is stored as an "offset" by many compilers - but there is
nothing in the Standard to require this - and  some (older?) compilers
definetly do NOT store it as an offset but as an "occurrence" number.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f04997.4391914@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 19:00:03 +0100, "peter dashwood"
<dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

>1. There is no "index overhead" and I don't know what Thane's talking about.
>(Ah, sorry Thane, I just re-read what you wrote again and you are talking
>about a CODING overhead. That's the time it takes to write INDEXED BY,
>right?...(You should be ashamed of yourself...and you an example to new
>COBOL programmers...!!<grin>).

<Blush>

Here's the deal.  I type fast.  But I scrolll slow.  Suddenly I find I
want to search a table.  Rather than scroll back up to the index, make
sure that the index name is not used in any of the copy books, make
sure that the indexed by is not some reused field, and then code the
search.  With the optimized compiler, there's really no difference in
speed.  But you are right - Table searches should use search.  Why
not?  Lazy? Yes I admit it, I'm lazy.  I can code the loop faster
manually than I can setup the search.  Maybe I need to learn how to
cut and paste.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F04E7B.C99F15F7@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37f04997.4391914@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 19:00:03 +0100, "peter dashwood"
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I've maintained COBOL programs that do binary search without using the
SEARCH ALL verb, because SEARCH ALL is limited to table entries that can
be satisfied with an equal condition.  But what if your table key
consists of a beginning range and ending range for each entry?  And what
if there are gaps in the ranges from entry to entry?  Your "equal
condition" is, Does my test number fall within the inclusive range?  Or
is it below the beginning range for this entry or above the ending range
for this entry?  

I wish I could do that with SEARCH ALL, but I can't.  So I have to use a
hand-coded binary search.  But I still use indexes. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990928094042.12186.00001269@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37F04E7B.C99F15F7@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <37F04E7B.C99F15F7@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> writes:

>I've maintained COBOL programs that do binary search without using the
>SEARCH ALL verb, because SEARCH ALL is limited to table entries that can
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the old days when answers had to be given in micro-seconds
,or suffer shredded checks, we found that using hand-coded binary
search logic MUCH faster than the builtin SEARCH ALL syntax.

For run of the mill batch routines where you don't have the ability to 
do program calls , I would use the SEARCH ALL syntax.  It's down
side is that you have to handle the table load procedures and ensure
that everything is ordered by your search key.  Any time the table
exceeds previous design limits you are forced to recode the table limits
(two lines , if done properly).
If speed becomes an issue, I would probably go back to the hand-coded
search routines.
If you can use external program calls, I prefer to have an external program
to read a flat control file into an INDEXED file format (automatically ordering
by the desired key) and call said external to use standard DYNAMIC read logic
to locate the desired table entry.   This method reduces memory constraints
as you no longer have to have the entire table in memory.  The only overhead is
from the program-to-program call and the DYNAMIC/INDEXED read process.

As someone else had indicated , for the student coder it is best to use the
COBOL supplied syntax SEARCH/SEARCH ALL.  As one becomes more familiar with
their development environment and end-user requirements, Optimization of
performance via hand-coded routines, and maintenance reduction via external
calls can be considered as alternatives.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f08f2f$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37f04997.4391914@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hi Thane,

I can relate to being lazy. It's no bad thing. Because people are lazy they
look for better (requiring less effort) ways to do things. I  honestly think
that if you code a few SEARCHes and get comfortable with it, you'll find it
just as easy as writing your own loops. I know I did.

Pleased you agree with the arguments for using SEARCH, in principle.

As for checking the index name is not used anywhere, why not construct your
own standard for a table index name, which ensures uniqueness? Say, the
initial letters of the table name, plus its prefix (if you use one),
followed by -x...so a table called "001-list-of-places" would be indexed
with an index called "lop001-x".  Then when you come to use it, you know
what it must be called without having to scroll back and check, and if it's
not unique (unlikely), that nice COBOL compiler will tell you....just a
thought.

Pete.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <37f04997.4391914@news1.ibm.net>...

about.
>>(Ah, sorry Thane, I just re-read what you wrote again and you are talking
>>about a CODING overhead. That's the time it takes to write INDEXED BY,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F0C2D3.31435DF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37f04997.4391914@news1.ibm.net> <37f08f2f$1@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
I like SEARCH ALL, when I can fit my needs into it.  But I have an
aversion to indices.  Subscripts can be displayed, subscripts don't have
to be set to 1 and then down by 1.  I can do arithmetic directly with
subscripts.
```

## Thread: student with run time error — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### student with run time error

- **From:** "old dog" <nospam@itesas.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7skatq$hso@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
I am a student of a beginning COBOL class working on a program that compiles
OK, but I get a run time error after it completes the calculations on the
first record.

During the process-record module on the second time through, it hangs up and
displays a message "Illegal character in numeric field (error 163)".  The
output file looks fine for the first record.

Thanks for any help. Please e-mail rclark@itexas.net .
```

#### ↳ Re: student with run time error

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7skn51$8n2@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7skatq$hso@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
You don't tell us, but I'll bet that this is Micro Focus COBOL.  The 163
run-time message means that you forgot to initialize or re-initialize a
numeric field - so it has "non-numeric" data in it.

HOWEVER, as this happens on the second record - but not the 1st, my other
"guess" is that your input file is a "text" file (or as we say in Micro
Focus COBOL "line Sequential")

If you haven't (and your input is a "text" file), then add

   Organization Line Sequential

in the proper place in your Select/Assign statement - and the 163 will
probably go away.

(There are other ways to specify line sequential - and other ways for
getting rid of 163's - but this is most likely where the problem is for a
"student in a beginning COBOL class")
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: student with run time error

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sl61t$ish$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7skatq$hso@enews4.newsguy.com> <7skn51$8n2@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Good probability that Mr. Klein is correct.  If it doesn't do the trick
a second frequent cause may be that the total size indicated by, or,
the record definition itself, is incorrect.
-trblshtr-
```

<a id="t-add7f96223"></a>
## Thread: internal v. external sort-> which do you like? — 60 this month / 84 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the internal
sort.  It gives me more control over the sort without having to learn or
write separate parm members.  I also like how internal sorts are tightly
wound into the code and usually perform better because they do less reads
and writes.  But external has advantages too.

Only 9 months in the business, so be easy on me ok?

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com...
> What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the internal
> sort.  It gives me more control over the sort without having to learn or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

Jeff,
  I think that you may be getting into another "religious war" question
here.  I think that there are definitely PROs and CONs for both sides - and
there certainly are issues related to "what platform are you on - and what
products are available to you".

For example,  on an IBM mainframe, if you are using OS/VS COBOL, then an
"external Sort" will almost always be more efficient (run faster) than an
internal sort.  On the other hand, if you have "upgraded" to VS COBOL II or
IBM COBOL  for this-and-that, (and use the FASTSRT compiler option), then an
internal sort may well be as fast (I don't think it will ever be faster)
than an external SORT.  Issues of SyncSort vs DFSort also USED TO impact
this - but I think do less so today.

On the PC (or UNIX?) there are less "portable" (universally available)
"external sort" tools (that are efficient).  Therefore, doing an "internal
sort" may well increase the portability of the code you want - and possibly
even the efficiency - Again, depending on the compiler and tools available.

As far as "maintainability" goes, this is where the "most adamant religious
wars" come in.  Depending on what you (your shop and/or those around you -
and certainly those who will be maintaining your application go) know/are
used to - different "maintainability" will occur.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>, on 27 Sep 1999 at 23:07,
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> said:

>For example,  on an IBM mainframe, if you are using OS/VS COBOL, then an
>"external Sort" will almost always be more efficient (run faster) than an
>internal sort.  ........

I've heard this argument a number of times, but am still convinced that
it's probably not true.  Internal sorts are generally more efficient (even
with OS/VS COBOL) because the sorted data does not have to be written to
disk then read again.  

In most circumstances, SORT utilities are more efficient at reading data
than COBOL because they use EXCP as opposed to QSAM.  But with an external
SORT, OS/VS COBOL still has to use QSAM to read the sorted records, so I
don't see what you gain.  

Compared to an internal SORT, an external SORT step adds the following
overhead:

1. another job step (the overhead is significant, even if it doesn't
appear on the job's CPU and I/O usage stats)

2. allocate a temp disk data set (SORTOUT)

3. write temp data set (SORTOUT)

4. delete temp data set when you're finished

Of course, if the input file is poorly blocked and SORTOUT is optimally
blocked, then an external SORT may actually be more efficient than an
internal one, but I would consider this the exception, not the rule.

Note that the SORT verb introduces some CPU overhead because of the path
length incurred at each call to the exits, but I don't think it's nowhere
near the I/O overhead associated with an external sort step.

Gilbert Saint-flour
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
One of the "overheads" that an internal sort (in an ANSI/ISO Standard
conforming COBOL program) has is that it needs to go thru "File Status"
and/or Declaratives processing for both the input and output files (assuming
that you are using USING/GIVING - rather than INPUT/OUTPUT procedures).
This is why with the "newer" (post-OS/VS COBOL) compilers, IBM documents
that you must use NOFASTSRT to be Standard conforming.  This (pretty
definitely?) adds overhead.

I think (but am not positive of this), that OS/VS COBOL - and (maybe even
the newer IBM mainframe COBOLs) are documented as almost "ignoring" blocking
and buffering when doing internal sorts.  Considering that QSAM doesn't work
(?) this way, I don't know how this could happen - but that is what I have
heard.

Finally, I do know (for certain) that internal sorts (when you use NOFASTSRT
or OS/VS COBOL) do not use any of the "faster" sort algorithms that can be
selected with DFSort or SyncSort.

Having said all of this, I am not positive of WHY I have always heard about
this "efficiency" issue.  I, most certainly, never tested in with a
multi-million record file.  But as even IBM documented it in their
Programmer Guides, I guess that I have just always believed it.  (So,
Columbus - Why do you think the world ISN'T flat??? <G>)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F15C51.FC09DBC3@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Back when we had only 32K partions on DOS/360, 160K regions on MVT or
less than 500K real memory on MVS/370 (or XA) for a working set, the
invoking program took a significant portion of the memory that could be
allotted to the sort and program combined.  Now that we are in an era
where the newer middle of the line PC's come with 64 megabytes standard
and mainframes have hundreds or thousands of megabytes of main memory,
the invoking program can the sort and the sort in turn will still have
enough memory to sort sufficient portions of the file in memory. 
Basically it is a question of how many times the internal sort will have
to write the file being sorted to a work area.  If invoking the sort
directly from the command system, JCL, etc. reduces the number of passes
because more memory is made available than would be made available to
sort from a calling program, then the external sort plus a following
program will be more efficient.  

For those who talk about maintainability, I haven't the foggiest notion
of what SORT FIELDS=(1,5,CH,A,63,4,PD,D,79,30,CH,A) is doing without
looking at the record description in a program and in the case of MVS
COBOL converting the hex offsets to decimal and adding 1.  I suppose in
a way that is good because it gives a real incentive to only add fields
to the end of a record so that existing field displacements aren't
changed.         

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> One of the "overheads" that an internal sort (in an ANSI/ISO Standard
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
    snip 
> 
> Having said all of this, I am not positive of WHY I have always heard about
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
 more snippage
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7st951$5gv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37F15C51.FC09DBC3@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
In article <37F15C51.FC09DBC3@nbnet.nb.ca>,
  Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca> wrote:

> For those who talk about maintainability, I haven't the foggiest
notion
> of what SORT FIELDS=(1,5,CH,A,63,4,PD,D,79,30,CH,A) is doing without
> looking at the record description in a program and in the case of MVS
> COBOL converting the hex offsets to decimal and adding 1.

With DFSORT R14, you could use Symbols to map the fields and constants
in records for better maintainability.  So, for example, you could code
a SORT statement like:

  SORT FIELDS=(Last_Name,A,First_Name,A)

To find out more about DFSORT Symbols, click on the following URL:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmr14.htm

or check the "DFSORT Application Programming Guide Release 14" (you can
link to it from the URL above).

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT is on the Web at URL:
http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00

```
Clark Morris wrote:

> For those who talk about maintainability, I haven't the foggiest notion
> of what SORT FIELDS=(1,5,CH,A,63,4,PD,D,79,30,CH,A) is doing without
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> changed.

Too bad we can't use a COBOL copy member here.  But my sort code looks
like this:

 SORT FIELDS=(74,07,A,       TERM/INST
              32,05,A,       SUBCODE
              19,06,A,       HOURS
              04,12,A),      MISC
        FORMAT=CH
 INCLUDE COND=(01,3,CH,EQ,C'T11')
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7stpg0$il4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <37F15C51.FC09DBC3@nbnet.nb.ca> <37F2112C.2A5822C2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <37F2112C.2A5822C2@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> Too bad we can't use a COBOL copy member here.  But my sort code looks
> like this:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  INCLUDE COND=(01,3,CH,EQ,C'T11')

As I stated in another post, you could use DFSORT Symbols.  You could
set up a mapping like this either using DD * or a permanent data set or
member:

//SYMNAMES DD *
DEPT,1,3,CH
MISC,*,12,CH
HOURS,19,6,CH
SUBCODE,32,5,CH
TERM_INST,74,7,CH
T11,C'T11'

and then use control statements like this:

  SORT FIELDS=(TERM_INST,A,SUBCODE,A,
               HOURS,A,MISC,A)
  INCLUDE COND=(DEPT,EQ,T11)

For details, see Chapter 7 "Using Symbols for Fields and Constants" of
the "DFSORT Application Programming Guide Release 14" available online
from the DFSORT home page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Symbols, Y2K, OUTFIL, ICETOOL)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JrfI3.3049$j4.83370@viper>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Most internal sorts I have seen call SORT when an internal sort is used and
generate their own control card to be passed to SORT, whatever that may be.
DFSort or SyncSort are usually linked as aliases of the executable SORT.
To verify this, check your SYSOUT for the typical output produced by DFSORT
or SyncSort. Which means the executable probably generates the control card
at execution time. Although probably insignificant this does add processing
as opposed to a separate sort step where the card simply needs allocated.
Also, as with the eexternal sort, work files can be allocated dynamically or
statically. And they both write information to the work DSNS. To verify
this, check the allocations of an internal sort for references to SYSUT1,
SYSUT2, ETC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2noI3.101$ZV1.3778@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <JrfI3.3049$j4.83370@viper>`

```
If you are using SyncSort, you can add a $ORTPARM DD to the job, specify
LIST as an option, and actually see the parameters passed to an invoked
sort. There may be an option like this for DFSort, too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7stfo8$aqc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <JrfI3.3049$j4.83370@viper> <2noI3.101$ZV1.3778@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <2noI3.101$ZV1.3778@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
  "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> If you are using SyncSort, you can add a $ORTPARM DD to the job,
specify
> LIST as an option, and actually see the parameters passed to an
invoked
> sort. There may be an option like this for DFSort, too.

With DFSORT, you can use a DFSPARM DD statement, or you can set
installation parameter PARMDDN=$ORTPARM and use a $ORTPARM DD statement.
DFSORT also supports the LIST/NOLIST parameters (LIST is the
IBM-supplied default).

Frank Yaeger
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT is on the Web at URL:
http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4-remove-this-to-reply@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Q7zN6QUiRp+nTj3XZGv3ygHWW+S@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>One of the "overheads" that an internal sort (in an ANSI/ISO Standard
>conforming COBOL program) has is that it needs to go thru "File Status"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This shouldn't be correct. When you are using the USING/GIVING options
no I/O is done by the COBOL program for those files. Declaratives and
file status should never be invoked since the file is not opened or
managed by the COBOL program while in the scope of the SORT statement.
For an input or output procedure, this would not be true as the files
are actually read and written by the COBOL program. . 

>I think (but am not positive of this), that OS/VS COBOL - and (maybe even
>the newer IBM mainframe COBOLs) are documented as almost "ignoring" blocking
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Depends. Blocking is recognized when writing the output file in that
you could specify the DCB information and it would be honored.
However, SORT did not use QSAM to write the files. On disks it would
be doing full track read/writes using its own I/O methods.. This could
be verified from the EXCP count which would not change unless you did
something really outrageous to mess up the number of records per track
like a blocking factor of 1. 

>
>Having said all of this, I am not positive of WHY I have always heard about
>this "efficiency" issue.  I, most certainly, never tested in with a
>multi-million record file.  But as even IBM documented it in their
>Programmer Guides, I guess that I have just always believed it.  (So,

Back in the early days of VS-COBOL-II, I ran a number of tests
involving multi-million record sorts to validate the FASTSRT option
with DFSORT and SYNCSORT. A COBOL II program with the FASTSRT option
with a  using/giving SORT benchmarked very close to a JCL sort.
Statistically it was close to a dead heat. 

The one combination that all the programming 'gurus'  wanted to use
was alternate indexes instead of sorts.  In any file of significant
size it was almost always possible to read a QSAM or VSAM file
sequentially feeding an internal sort and process the output faster
than you could process the same file through the AIX with no sort.

When DB2 started taking off, IBM's programming guidelines advised that
it was more efficient to work with the DBA to set up the SQL
statements to retrieve the data in sequence or using the ORDER BY to
let DB2 sort the data and present a sorted table to the program.  The
idea being that DB2 could manage the sort more efficiently.
Ironically it was still often faster to do a straight SELECT to read
the unordered table and release that into an internal sort although
the variability was much greater.  


                         
                         		Scott Peterson


Notice. Spelling mistakes left in for people
who need to correct others to make their
life fulfilled.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F36A63.8C7F6A00@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <9Q7zN6QUiRp+nTj3XZGv3ygHWW+S@4ax.com>`

```
Another place where I like external sorts is with a temporary data set
which is read in as a table by the COBOL program.  The program isn't
about sorting, and it just seems cleaner to order the table outside of
the program if it needs to do a SEARCH ALL of the table.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7svkuj$sss@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37f0c54c$2$tfs$mr2ice@news> <7squcl$63o@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <9Q7zN6QUiRp+nTj3XZGv3ygHWW+S@4ax.com>`

```
Scott Peterson <scottp4-remove-this-to-reply@mindspring.com> wrote in
message news:9Q7zN6QUiRp+nTj3XZGv3ygHWW+S@4ax.com...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> >One of the "overheads" that an internal sort (in an ANSI/ISO Standard
> >conforming COBOL program) has is that it needs to go thru "File Status"
> >and/or Declaratives processing for both the input and output files
(assuming
> >that you are using USING/GIVING - rather than INPUT/OUTPUT procedures).
> >This is why with the "newer" (post-OS/VS COBOL) compilers, IBM documents
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are correct that when FASTSRT is specified, this isn't true.  HOWEVER,
(and there was an ANSI interpretation specifically on this), the Standard
*does* require you to "honor" the file status field and declaratives.  This
is, in part (as I recall) because the Standard rules for SORT (and MERGE)
are written in terms of "as if" - followed by what you would have to do if
you coded simple input/output procedures - with specific I/O.

As I say, the IBM documentation has always (since VS COBOL II) been clear
that you need to run with NOFASTSRT to be ANSI/ISO conforming.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8t=xN4aBsNw5ivTa20=jqqC1P10M@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 23:07:11 -0500 "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>For example,  on an IBM mainframe, if you are using OS/VS COBOL, then an
:>"external Sort" will almost always be more efficient (run faster) than an
:>internal sort.  On the other hand, if you have "upgraded" to VS COBOL II or
:>IBM COBOL  for this-and-that, (and use the FASTSRT compiler option), then an
:>internal sort may well be as fast (I don't think it will ever be faster)
:>than an external SORT.  Issues of SyncSort vs DFSort also USED TO impact
:>this - but I think do less so today.

I don't think that anyone would argue for using an internal sort when both the
USING and GIVING clauses are specified. In such a case the sort should be done
out-of-line.

When either an INPUT PROCEDURE or an OUTPUT PROCEDURE is specified, the
internal sort will be faster since the input file(s) is read only once and the
output file(s) is written only once, unless one manages to have a very large
program and/or a very small region where the addition of this cobol program
causes a lot more paging while the sort is being performed OR if the data
volume is very large and the COBOL program is very(?) buggy, where if the sort
is performed out of line and the program crashes one can resume at the program
step and the file is already sorted.

As a side note, FASTSRT only applies to USING/GIVING situations.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f2dd46.173296789@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <8t=xN4aBsNw5ivTa20=jqqC1P10M@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 29 Sep 1999 12:29:25 +0200, postingid@dissensoftware.com
(Binyamin Dissen) wrote:

>When either an INPUT PROCEDURE or an OUTPUT PROCEDURE is specified, the
>internal sort will be faster since the input file(s) is read only once and the
>output file(s) is written only once, unless one manages to have a very large
>program and/or a very small region where the addition of this cobol program

I think there is a misconception there.

Input procedure reads records and writes a WHOLE SORT WORK file.
BETWEEN the input and output procedure, the file is sorted.  The
output procedure does not start till the file is sorted - thus the
whole sort work file is read again.  Internal sorts can be AS
efficient as external, but I don't think they can ever be MORE
efficient.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dJLzNztwcYhDcyN0DwZdnOgJXwZK@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7spetl$2ql@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <8t=xN4aBsNw5ivTa20=jqqC1P10M@4ax.com> <37f2dd46.173296789@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Thu, 30 Sep 1999 03:52:19 GMT redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

:>On Wed, 29 Sep 1999 12:29:25 +0200, postingid@dissensoftware.com
:>(Binyamin Dissen) wrote:

:>>When either an INPUT PROCEDURE or an OUTPUT PROCEDURE is specified, the
:>>internal sort will be faster since the input file(s) is read only once and the
:>>output file(s) is written only once, unless one manages to have a very large
:>>program and/or a very small region where the addition of this cobol program

:>I think there is a misconception there.

:>Input procedure reads records and writes a WHOLE SORT WORK file.
:>BETWEEN the input and output procedure, the file is sorted.  The
:>output procedure does not start till the file is sorted - thus the
:>whole sort work file is read again.  Internal sorts can be AS
:>efficient as external, but I don't think they can ever be MORE
:>efficient.

But you forgot - if one does an external sort - one must then READ the written
SORTOUT in order to process it.

This is the additional WRITE & READ that I referred to.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F109BE.71571D4C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> I like the internal sort.
> 
> Fewer job steps and programs to maintain.

Which is easiest to maintain - fewer, but more complicated programs, or

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1noI3.100$ZV1.3778@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <eW6I3.339$zc3.12434@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37F109BE.71571D4C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <37F109BE.71571D4C@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Michael Mattias wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>more but simpler programs?

Depends on who does the maintenance. If you are good, 'more complicated'
really isn't.

(Ok, so that's self-serving.)

Seriously though, I have one client who asked me not to use any internal
sorts, because they did not have a supply of experienced programmers to do
upgrades to them, and did not want to be always calling in an expensive
contractor/consultant for small maintenance items. (They had also never seen
the SEARCH verb used 'for real'. They thought it was really cool and a lot
better than an endless stream of IF...ELSE IF...ELSE IF....)

On IBM mainframes, if a COBOL program abends during a sort INPUT PROCEDURE
or OUTPUT PROCEDURE , the simplified dump analysis tools like ABEND-AID,
which is really easy to use in a non-sort program, can only tell you the
program failed "in the sort" even if it's a data exception error in the
COBOL program. You can only find the 'real' problem by digging through all
that hex mumbo-jumbo, and until you know what to look for, debugging is a
real dog.

My preference is for fewer programs, fewer job steps; I just find that more
job steps and more programs means there are more places to look for problems
or make upgrades, and finding a problem in / enhancing a single program is
easier. YMMV.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqk9b$82a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
  "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:
> What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the
internal sort.
Here's my personal preference with no claim that it is better than
anyone else's personal preference.
I like external, unless a program needs to find some data for the sort
key first. Gets part of the job out of the way, making the program have
less to do. I believe it also makes a smaller load module, for whatever
that's worth. This may be different now, but in the old days, an ABEND
within a program (that had an internal sort) resulted in a dump of the
SORT program, with your program buried somewhere in the middle.

It also seems that it would be easier to use a line-by-line debugging
tool if you didn't have to wait for the entire file to be sorted first.

Here's a common error in internal sorts, FYI. You end up with an
inscrutable SOC4 or SOC1 or worse. Or errors about reading files after
being closed, etc.

0000-INIT.
OPEN...
PERFORM 1000-MAIN UNTIL EOF.
CLOSE...
(forget to put GOBACK here)

1000-MAIN.
SORT SORT-FILE...
...
RETURN SORT-RECORD AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW.
...

So 1000-MAIN gets performed until EOF and then gets executed one more
time after the files are CLOSED. Boom

John










Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7sqk9b$82a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

#### ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C8XH3.2266$j4.65296@viper>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
I always use external sorts. But that is a personal belief.

I have found that it makes the processing of the programs simpler and easier
to develop, debug and maintain in the long run.

I break all of my report generators into 4 programs or JCL steps:
EXTRACT - extract information from database
FILTER - select off extraneous records
SORT - sort information using an efficient utility, DFSORT or SyncSort
REPORT - generate control break report or convert file to final output
format

Once a conversion or report generation program is broken into these 4
programs the logic of the individuals is almost always makes the logic
rediculously simple.

I am sure many others will disagree with me, but from my meager 4 years of
experience at many different MVS COBOL shops, I have found this to be the
best.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "Patrick T. M. Hathaway" <hathaway@jerseycape.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rv5k1sgp3i528@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <C8XH3.2266$j4.65296@viper>`

```
I use exactly the same approach, with, as far as possible, one further step
using a 4gl utility to do the report formating. (DEC VMS machine using
Datatrieve - an excellent utility for working with RMS files).

VMS performs very efficient external sorts also.

KISS

news:C8XH3.2266$j4.65296@viper...

easier
```

#### ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F048E4.F2D31CDF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the internal
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Jeff, 

You're obviously going to get pros and cons - but here's another avenue.
Do you really need to sort? Even after only 9 months, you'll appreciate
that it has a lot to do with your system design and objectives. Relative
files can be useful as a temporary working device and the obvious -
indexed with primekey and alternate keys. I'm not saying it is the right
way - but in 20 years I have never used a sort, (but I'm not trying to
sequence transactions for an Amex monthly credit card print <G>).

Certainly back in the '60s it was a necessity to sort - with input from
cards, paper-tape, OCR machines etc; limited memory, you had to sort
your sequential records. Now days, depending upon the size of your
application you may be adding records or transactions to already indexed
files.

Sort external, internal or indexed files - depends upon volumes and what
your application is doing. (Whether you use External/Internal sorts - I
have to bow to the knowledge of those who can cite performance
differences).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990928094043.12186.00001270@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37F048E4.F2D31CDF@home.com>`

```
In article <37F048E4.F2D31CDF@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
writes:

>
>You're obviously going to get pros and cons - but here's another avenue.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am amazed that in 20 years you have never required a sort process!
Guess you were lucky with your development environments and resources.

I've had about 20 years in development myself across a couple different
platforms,  but primarily in the financial sector.  EVERYTHING has to be sorted
at one time or another.  I have dealt with very few applications that could 
be run without sorting.  90% of what I have dealt with is batch maintenance 
programming.  Even when posting to a DMS structure or an INDEXED structure,
Processing is significantly faster when the maintenance records are sorted on 
the same KEY as the structure you are reading/updating.
Internal sorts lend themselves well to a program that must handle major update
processes and spin off secondary 'temporary' files for summary processing at
EOJ.   I have found in my current environment that the 'office paradigm' is to
use
external sorts , which doubles the processing time and number of passes of 
client files. {and they wonder why things take so long to complete}

Just my 2 cents worth here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F10423.7C8286DB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37F048E4.F2D31CDF@home.com> <19990928094043.12186.00001270@ngol03.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <37F048E4.F2D31CDF@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
> writes:
> I am amazed that in 20 years you have never required a sort process!
> Guess you were lucky with your development environments and resources.

You are justified in expressing amazement. But it is true. Now when I
was system designing, (not programming) for the big-iron, sorts -
DEFINITELY - and remember I did qualify it by saying design
concepts/volumes were the factor.

Reflect, my COBOL programming environment has been 'desktops' and even
for a multi-user, ( 6 of 'em) in a production accounting system, as
transactions left the screen they went to indexed files for both
inventory and accounting. It wasn't a deliberate attempt to avoid
sorting, but the way I saw the system should be designed. (When Union
Carbide bought the company out, they sent one of their systems honchos
to take a look-see - I was told he was very impressed with what had been
achieved on a Radio Shack clunker using RM/COBOL and Xenix).

Current system I'm working on produces various reports in several
sequences. Firstly I run the data through a program which does
calculations writing to a 'RangeFile', which is just a set of keys,
primekeys and alternate keys, producing some keys as 'negatives' to get
descending sequence. Just read the file sequentially on PrimeKey or a
particular AlternateKey for a specific report. The other option -
separate sort for each report.

As that caption says in deja.com "Share what you know......"

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> "input" was IMS or some other "odd" medium.

Interesting Bill, and by now you've seen Donald's comment. But your
contracting, were you doing it for the 'biggies' or desktops ?

As for mainframe, take a department store stock-transfer :-

	From : Store : 123 Dept : 3456
	To   :         789        1234
	Date : xxxx
	Doc #: xxxxxx

	Retail value of merchandise (Total) : $ 123.45

You gotta be nuts if you index that kind of input, with literally
thousands of records per month, for transfers 'within' the store and
transfers store-to-store. You generate two records, Credit - for sender
and Debit - for recipient.

Then you sort the whole lot once a month to produce individual store
reports and a company report. It just isn't a candidate for indexing.
And although I wasn't programming then, you can betacha it was an ICL
external sort.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990928215650.09091.00001649@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
>James J. Gavan wrote in message <37F10423.7C8286DB@home.com>...
>>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can see where real-time update type programs using indexed files 
would make sense.  I've used some DMS and Thrift structures in 
such a manner, but when dealing with report processing and mass 
file updates it would seem necessary to reference one of the primary 
keys in order for detail reports to be produced in a readable order.
your method of creating a 'Rangefile' sounds intriguing.  I think at 
one time I did something with 'tagged' key files for dealing with secondary
reporting or selected detail.  Back then there was a way of reading 
flat files randomly that allowed 'jumping' thru the file as if it were a 
Random access /indexed file.  
This did wonders for processing time when dealing with reports on only
10% of a million record flat file database.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** b123@pobox.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<23rxNxeUvT7EE+lYRKmWbX3H6Zlr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sr30e$spe$1@news.igs.net> <19990928215650.09091.00001649@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
Have you considered how much I/O you will be doing? For a two step job
to sort a file for a report and then read the file and print the
report, with an external sort you will read the file twice (once by
the external sort and then the sorted file by the report program),
write the file once (output from the external sort), and maybe have
I/O to a sort work file. With an internal sort you read the file once
and maybe have I/O to a sort work file. I/O takes a long time.

If your job also includes a selection step you can reduce the amount
of I/O more because with an internal sort you won't have to write the
records out to a work file for the external sort to read.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** b123@ix.netcom.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o8jyN3cbjoQE8vtEvdCLw+POpKA1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sr30e$spe$1@news.igs.net> <19990928215650.09091.00001649@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
Have you considered how much I/O you will be doing? For a two step job
to sort a file for a report and then read the file and print the
report, with an external sort you will read the file twice (once by
the external sort and then the sorted file by the report program),
write the file once (output from the external sort), and maybe have
I/O to a sort work file. With an internal sort you read the file once
and maybe have I/O to a sort work file. I/O takes a long time.

If your job also includes a selection step you can reduce the amount
of I/O more because with an internal sort you won't have to write the
records out to a work file for the external sort to read.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F18BC5.365A2539@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sr30e$spe$1@news.igs.net> <19990928215650.09091.00001649@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> I can see where real-time update type programs using indexed files
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 10% of a million record flat file database.

As you say there are situations where you are selectively accessing
records for reports. Whether you are using flat-files or a DB, a lot is
going to depend on the hierarchy structure - it isn't just something new
to DB. As an example what I work on :-

			ABC Oil Company

	Jumping Pound				Fish Creek
	Plant					Plant

  Vessel 1 Ves 2   Ves 3 Ves etc...	Ves 1  Ves 2  Ves 3 Ves 4
  ID ABC   ID CDE  ec...		ID XYZ ID GHJ etc...
  . 
  Items 1 H-BAND ON 4 IN. ELBOW..
	2 V-BAND ON 2 IN. PIPE (north side)
	3 etc...

	and for each Item being inspected, there can be from 1 to 24 	
Inspection points ( a Readings table 10 x 24 ) - the '10'
	being from 1 to 10 inspection dates

Get that structure concept right for flat-file or DB and you are on your
way. You would be surprised how many of the major oil companies have
their DBs screwed up because they haven't got a logical hierarchy
numbering system, even though they may have somebody with the
illustrious title DBA picking up $60-70 (CDN) p.a.

It's really no different if you are talking A/P, A/R or G/L is it ?
From my first days in systems analysis I always insisted that Primekeys
should be considered as 'holding keys'; 'properties' relating to a
record should be held as separate field 'analysis codes' - and it is
these property fields that let you do the selecting whether FF or DB :-

	01 CustomerRecord
 	   05 PrimeKey		pic x(06).
	   05 TypeofCustomer    pic 9(02).
	   05 SalesTerritory    pic 9(02).
	   etc.......

I don't know how far back you go, but the concept of
'significant-coding' was introduced with punched card systems for
tabulators; you tried to squeeze as much info as you could on to a 40 or
80-column card, which allowed you to sort and print various
permutations.

Largely, because this was supervised by accountants,
'significant-coding' carried over into EDP and design-wise we found it 
became a nightmare. Example

	Departments 1001 - 1500 are used for Dry Goods

Then the system accommodated Food :-

	1600 - Groceries
	1601 - Veggies
	1602 - Meats etc....

Ah, but the Food Division takes over Candies and Tobacco which already
exist. So lumbered with accountant's logic we now have Food Division :-

	1333 - Candies
	1344 - Tobacco & Cigs
	1600 - Groceries
	1601 - Veggies
	1602 - Meats etc....

(Oh no ! We aren't going to change the Department Numbers - everybody
knows them as 1333 and 1344 and sales staff wont remember the new number
when they key-in to the (manual) cash register).

Now of course take all those programs that produce reports and put an
exception clause in to include Candies and Tobacco in the Food Division.
As computer people we suffered this nonsense until VAT was introduced
into the UK, giving us the opportunity (akin to Y2K) to change things.
Then we added our 'analysis codes' to records :-

	Dept			Div #
	1001 - Fur Coats	01
	1010 - ...		01
	1045 - Menswear		02
	1333 - Candies		15
	1344 - Tobacco & Cigs	15
	1500 - Toys		10
	1600 - Groceries	15
	1601 - Veggies		15
	1602 - Meats etc....	15

Straight-forward enough isn't it and makes complete sense. Same concept
of 'analysis-codes' independent of PrimeKey Codes was carried through to
other modules, A/P, A/R etc... 

We now had the flexibility to produce those selective reports you write
about, from either indexed or sequential, coupled of course where
necessary, with a sort.

PS: Not sure what the situation is in N. America, but NCC Manchester,
UK, used to promote the concept of 'significant-coding' because of its
affinity to parts explosion concepts. Nothing wrong with parts-exposion
codes for manufacturers such as Ford, Boeing etc., makes eminent sense
PROVIDING it is logical and disciplined. One defect is giving you
numbers like A123-BB345-H778-x-1 for data-entry. But as you can see from
my example above, whereas it might work for autos and 'planes - it can
be a pain the ass, for undisciplined systems. (Ever tried to talk logic
to a bean-counter ?)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Hi Jeff,

You've raised a really good point and had some good responses.

Gumbo says in his IBM mainframe environment, external sorts are his
preferred answer.

Bill  Klein, as always, gives a balanced view and points out pros and cons
of both.

Jimmy Gavan says: "Why sort at all?"

I would agree with all of them (except I don't agree with Jimmy's use of the
COBOL file system [Relative or Indexed files] which is rapidly becoming
obsolete, to avoid sorting. I would use a Relational database and the
ORDERED BY clause of SQL)

So, where does it all leave you, and how do you get a simple "yes" or "no"?

My answer would be "yes", internal sorts are fine.

(But then, I WOULD say that, because I am obsessed with platform
independence...the reasons for this are too complex to go into here but
suffice to say, I get very serious about anything that can save me money and
ensure my survival in the industry...it works for me. I've been doing this
stuff for nearly 35 years and have made a very good living at it.)

There are a few  provisos:

1. They should be carefully annotated to explain what they are doing and
what the key structures are, so that if this changes, maintenance is no big
deal.

2. Use Input and Output procedures with care. Yes, you can filter records as
they are sorted, but it can have subtle impacts on the downstream processing
if it has to be changed. Gumbo is absolutely right in his separation of
processes. Having said that, if you annotate the procedures to explain why
the records are being filtered, you should have maintenance programmers
calling blessings on your name, rather than curses...

3. Jimmy is completely on the button when he says it is better to avoid
sorting altogether.

Pete.

Jeff Baynard wrote in message <7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com>...
>What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the internal
>sort.  It gives me more control over the sort without having to learn or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jeff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
A note on maintenance and development....

Using external sort can save time when the USERS change their minds on the
sort sequence, which I have run into many times. With an internal SORT the
program has to be recompiled and moved through whatever change control
process you may be using. Changing a SORT card to change the sort sequence
is typically easier due to the way most places migrate control cards through
change management.

But this is a gray area.

Using the not-so-well-known function of SyncSort and DFSort to produce
simple control break reports can also speed development up significanlty,
especially with users who can't make up their mind. Changing the EXTRACT
program to fit the necessary logic for the SORT report to work is usually
relatively simple.

Once again, probably only applies to mainframe programming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sta9k$6f5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper>`

```
"Gumbo" wrote:
>Using external sort can save time when the USERS change their minds on
>the sort sequence, which I have run into many times. With an internal
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>simple control break reports can also speed development ...

Actually, you can avoid having to recompile the COBOL program when you
change the sort sequence.  With DFSORT, you can do this by using DFSPARM
to override the SORT statement in the program, e.g.,

//DFSPARM DD *
  SORT FIELDS=(...)
/*

You can also put other DFSORT control statements in DFSPARM, such as
INCLUDE, OMIT, OUTFIL (with the break report function you mentioned),
and so on.  That way, you can use an internal sort with elements of the
external sort.  You may be able to use DFSORT's facilities for
filtering, reformatting and reporting to eliminate COBOL code,
recompiles and multiple passes over the data set, with either an
internal sort or an external sort.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT is on the Web at URL:
http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4-remove-this-to-reply@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LgzzNwc+BlkS2UMKvgsxvx95m7Ed@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper>`

```
"Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com> wrote:

>A note on maintenance and development....
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are a couple of alternative techniques that will work well in an
environment where external sorts are preferred.  All IBM compatible
SORT programs  has a number of standard exits that can invoke
subroutines written in COBOL.  

The most common (and a couple of my favorites) were the E15/E35
Input/Output exits.  

The E15 exit is presented each record as it's read. You can then
either drop it, reformat it or insert additional records as needed.

This was a great feature in reading journaling files with multiple
transactions per record. We could read in one record buy the sort,
bust it apart and output 10-20 real records to the sort in a program
with no file declarations and no file processing logic. It's all
handled by the SORT

On the output side, the E35 exit processed each record as it was
returned from the sort and could either write a report and drop the
record or perform additional processing before it was written to the
sort output file.  Again with no file declarations or file processing
logic.  

The only thing needed to invoke these was an additional control card
in the SORT. In a lot of cases this gave us the best of both worlds.
This was especially important before COBOL II FASTSRT options were
available. 




                         
                         		Scott Peterson


Notice. Spelling mistakes left in for people
who need to correct others to make their
life fulfilled.
```

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 1999 10:07:51 +0100, "peter dashwood"
<dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk> wrote:

>Jimmy Gavan says: "Why sort at all?"
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why in the world would you want to do anything so obviously slow.
Ordered by does a sort you know <G>.  Anyway, when RM COBOL first came
out, then hadn't implemented SORT - so we did our own with their
indexed file system.  When Realia came out, their sort was (is) hard
to beat on the PC.  I use Realia sorts with output procedures to
display reports on the screen. 

Anyway - this is also another matter of "Perspective".  I agree, there
are systems (I am working on them now at work!) where we are moving
away from the indexed and flat files and moving EVERYTHING to the
database.  Our experience has been fair thus far.  If the database
server is up to the task, things work fine - otherwise....

At any rate, I also develop applications for individuals and small
businesses.  Asking them to purchase and manage a relational database
(even MS Jett) is not feasable, and the performance is poor.  Indexed
files (COBOL is one of the only languages that offers them as part of
it's standard) are here to stay.  Everything has it's place.

Other very valid comments and caveats snipped....
```
