```
type application might use 10-15 megs of data.  Say 500 customers, maybe 100
vendors, and 7 or 8 thousand yearly transactions.  That would be typical for
a doctor or lawyer's office.

The typical PC these days has 64 megs of memory, not to mention disk.  Here
we are, debating sorts that were designed to order cards, using tapes as
devices.  Why not just load the whole damned file into memory, and do a
table lookup?
```

```
probably just load all the data into memory.  The only real problem is when
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f2e5f1@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37f185a1.85310119@news1.ibm.net> <37F1A813.240F9DBD@home.com> <7suc6l$j12$3@news.igs.net>`

```

donald tees wrote in message <7suc6l$j12$3@news.igs.net>...
>It seems to me that perhaps we are behind the time here. A typical PC VAR
>type application might use 10-15 megs of data.  Say 500 customers, maybe
100
>vendors, and 7 or 8 thousand yearly transactions.  That would be typical
for
>a doctor or lawyer's office.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good one, Donald!

You are absolutely right, of course.

However, there are, and will probably continue to be for the foreseeable
future (that's about 24 hours for most people...<g>) applications that do
need permanent storage and it makes sense to hold the data on this permanent
storage in a form where it can be readily accessed and cross referenced.
(difficult with a single table in memory).

When disks were first invented many of them had "head-per-track" and it was
really like a giant dustbin lid with read/write heads strung across it.
There was no way to remove the disk so any data on it had to be sequentially
loaded (usually from tape, but sometimes from cards), processed, then saved
sequentially back to tape again (or punched into a new output deck).

You may wonder why we bothered, but these devices allowed random access
which meant we didn't have to sort. ("SORT" was a four letter word in those
days...hey, it STILL is (I just counted...<G>)). So we designed our systems
using report chains in the records, loaded them to disk, processed, ran off
the reports which were chained in the correct sequences, then wrote
everything back before loading the next application.

(You wouldn't believe what a nightmare it was keeping chains in
sequence...if you lost one link the entire sequence had to be followed
through to the drop point and then EVERY record had to be checked to see
which was next in that particular sequence...Some systems had up to 20
reports chained through the records in this way....It WAS a good grounding
in the use of COBOL Relative access methods as EVERYTHING had a disk
address, and we spent many hours devising faster hashing algorithms to
convert alpha numeric data to a disk address. Ah, happy days!)  But I
digress...

I'm sure you can see that your suggested solution is very similar in
principle to what I've outlined above... That is not knocking it. It COULD
work. Especially if you had very fast methods of getting stuff in and out of
memory.

There is a technology called "Content Addressable File Store" which is very
similar to what you describe but its day is yet to come. In this technology
EVERYTHING is stored in "Memory".

It is a future technology (yes, I know ICL built one but it is/was still
disk based...) because it will not come into its own until memory is so
vast, so cheap, and so fast, that we can afford to do it.

In CAFS, records and data do not have addresses (well that's not strictly
true, but it is totally different to the addresses on your PC or mainframe
disks) Remember, I am talking about a device which only exists on paper at
the moment; when it arrives, details may have changed).  Instead, the data
you require is requested by its CONTENT ..."Get me all the Smiths,
please..." and a "giant memory lookup" ensues.

I could speculate on the Access methods which will be used when CAFS
arrives, but I'm in enough hot water already in this thread, so I won't tell
you that some people I know are already writing a RDB based on the Codd/Date
Relational Model, to be accessed in standard SQL, for implementation on
CAFS....So far I know of nobody writing ISAM for it...<g>

Pete.
```

```
Oh Goody, at last  get to see what you guys have been saying...

I would've responded to Thane's message but I never received it so I'll do
so now. Comments interspersed to both Thane's and Jimmy's message.

Pete.

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37F1A813.240F9DBD@home.com>...
>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >I would agree with all of them (except I don't agree with Jimmy's use of
the
>> >COBOL file system [Relative or Indexed files] which is rapidly becoming
>> >obsolete, to avoid sorting. I would use a Relational database and the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Why in the world would you want to do anything so obviously slow.

Because it ISN'T slow when I do it. Not on a PC, a mid-range, or a
Mainframe.
First of all, it depends on how large the tables are (mine are invariably
small), how many fields you sort on (seldom more than 3 for me), how well
your database is designed (redundant data etc.), and the volume of records
to be "ORDERED" (i.e the number of records in the selection set, NOT the
number of rows in the base tables)

Secondly, "SLOW" is relative. The snail thinks the tortoise is fast.

>> Ordered by does a sort you know <G>.

Oh Really? What if the field in the ORDERED BY is indexed? What if there is
only ONE record in the selection set? The point is you can't know HOW the
records get ordered in any particular case unless you issue an SQL EXPLAIN
and look at what it does. I agree that in most cases the result set will be
sorted, but it is just that, a sort on the result set, and it is NOT slow.
(At least, it needn't be if you organise things properly...)

And for your information Thane Hubbell, I was writing sort software using
Fibonnacci series, balanced merges, tertiary Gaussian series, and primary
key transitions, while you were still a sparkle in your father's eye, so
there! Yah boo!<G>

 Anyway, when RM COBOL first came
>> out, then hadn't implemented SORT - so we did our own with their
>> indexed file system.  When Realia came out, their sort was (is) hard
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> are systems (I am working on them now at work!)

Aha! Moving into the real world at last are we? So why the change in Company
Policy? Have they been reading some of the stuff I've been writing in
various places?...hope so <grin>

>>where we are moving
>> away from the indexed and flat files and moving EVERYTHING to the
>> database.  Our experience has been fair thus far.  If the database
>> server is up to the task, things work fine - otherwise....

That's like saying: " well, I tried running it on a washing machine but the
spin cycle was a bit slow" Of course the server has to be up to it. There is
no "otherwise". Would you consider running a COBOL file system on a system
that wasn't up to it? Say, the toaster, f'rinstance?


>>
>> At any rate, I also develop applications for individuals and small
>> businesses.  Asking them to purchase and manage a relational database
>> (even MS Jett) is not feasable, and the performance is poor.

No Thane, that's really bollocks. "Poor" in relation to WHAT? Ever
investigate to try and find out WHY the performance was "poor"? Maybe learn
from it and fix it? No, because you are blinkered by loyalty to a file
system that is what you've always used and is a standard part of your
favourite language. Besides, it does everything you want, right?

OK, I shared those same feelings for many years. Why am I advocating a move
to RDB?  The reasons are huge and I have just finished a 15 page treatise on
the subject which has been distributed to a number of people who frequent
this forum and expressed their interest by requesting tools to help them
access RDBs from COBOL. The 2 main reasons (and there are many more...) are:

1. I want to be able to have other standard marketplace software access my
database. The COBOL file system is a pariah; it can only be accessed by
COBOL programs. I want to be able to run Crystal Reports against my
database, export it (or parts of it) to EXCEL, in fact I want ANY OLE
enabled software to be able to use my database. Try exporting your ISAM
files to anything. You have to write specific .CSV software and extract.
(How "slow" is that, BTW, when, if it was OLE enabled, standard software
could talk to it directly...?) Crystal Reports will analyse and give you a
point and click interface to just about any existing RDB; point it at an
ISAM file...result nothing...

2. I want to be able to transport my database (NOT convert it) to any
platform, including ones where COBOL may not be supported (like new parallel
processors or whatever flavour they come out with next year...) In other
words, I want a future-proof file system. RDB is the accepted standard. It
is mathematically pure and unlikely to be superseded. I'll go with that.

As for small customers unable to afford JET, then let 'em use ACCESS. It's a
few hundred dollars if they haven't got it already (ACCESS includes a
version of JET, anyway. We talk to the JET engine via ODBC). If they can't
pay a few hundred bucks for a standard database system, how can they afford
to pay the exorbitant fees you charge...<G> (OK, then, the exorbitant fees,
I charge...<G>) How can you say it's not "feasable" (sic). I put it to you
you don't KNOW what's feasible, can't even spell the word <G> (Sorry Thane,
that was a cheap shot...I know we all make errors when we're tired, but that
one was SO perfect...)

Seriously, I also have quite a number (although I'd like to have more...) of
small customers. It is perfectly feasible to get them started on MS-ACCESS.
In fact, it is an ideal "growth path".

>> Indexed
>> files (COBOL is one of the only languages that offers them as part of
>> it's standard) are here to stay.

Wanna bet?!

>> Everything has it's place.

Yep! And there's was back in the dark ages before the Relational Model was
postulated.
>>
>> Other very valid comments and caveats snipped....
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Covered this in private mail, Jimmy. Hopefully what is here will stir up a
hornets nest (I just hope I can get to read it, having major trouble with my
news server). I'm not writing this for the sake of stirring trouble (my time
is too valuable for that). I really hope that this will stimulate some
people to think about the "unthinkable".

If anyone wants a complete, detailed, transcript of my thoughts on the
subject and sample code on how to do it (same as you have received, and the
others who requested DECLGEN), please mail me privately:

pete_dashwood@compuserve.com

NOT the address this comes from.

Regards,

Pete.
```

```
Lets talk about the "real world", Peter<S>.

I am a VAR.  My code is in Cobol.  I quote an entire system ... hardware,
software, LAN, hubs, training, everything.  A typical system might run in
the $25 thousand range, and include three PC's.  One of those is a server.

Adding a 400 to that, even if it replaces a $900 server takes several
thousand dollars out of *my* pocket, not the customers.  Tell me, would you
be so cavalier about the money involved if it were yours instead of the
client's?

The statement above, if taken seriously, would cut my personal earnings per
year from about $250,000 to about $25,000.  What would you do if you were in
*my* position, beef up the hardware, or take more time on the software?
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

See www.mysql.com for a cheap database and ODBC driver.  I am running
on AIX server with ODBC front end, total cost $0.00.  I could install
a linux server on a 486 (about $100.00 complete and tested here) and
have reliable multi user application for peanuts.

Or maybe go to oracle,  personal oracle is a few hundred not really
expensive when you consider what you are buying.

The expense of computers is negligible, the run time overhead is not
noticeable, the ability of multi-user update is built in, transaction
processing (multiple updates with a commit) does not have to be coded.

The largest expense is the programmer time.  Our out source rates here
start at $100 per hour (Joe average) and up.

Ken
```

- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37f185a1.85310119@news1.ibm.net> <37F1A813.240F9DBD@home.com> <37F1E26A.CD8ACFD3@zip.com.au> <37F23853.CD0C7419@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message

<snippage>

>However - here is something more problematical if we were all to start
>switching to DBs. Exactly what do we put in Thane's "COBOL in 24 Hours"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>whole concept of COBOL with files missing ????


With modern tools, and large enough computers, I have introduced the
standard that ALL screen I/O and ALL file I/O is done through subroutine
calls.  That is, one subroutine per file, and multiple entry points for the
various operations.  That method replaces my old standard of using a
copybook for those functions.
(For precisely the above reason ... one customer may use Cobol Isam, while a
second uses a database manager).
```

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 1999 23:48:03 -0600, James J. Gavan wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>> server is up to the task, things work fine - otherwise....

No.  It's slow.  SQL is slow.  If "Order by" is used, especially with
a
Cursor, all the records are retrieved and then sorted.  It's slow.

>
>My other concern, you've already covered - telling a small user they have
>to go and spend $xxx on a DB before they can run your COBOL programs.
>

The expense doesn't bother me (you can get DB2 for the Palm Pilot now)
-
it's the administration and design.  Designing a good database, is as
hard
or harder than designing a good indexed file structure.

>Indications from what I've read in the NG so far are, it appears DBs are
>a winner with mainframes. I suppose the right thing is to address this to
>desktop developers - do you consider COBOL flat-files dead and if so,
>what are you substituting ?
>

Mainframes have the horsepower to make the databases feasable.  We
have a
LARGE process here that is made up of Vsam files (one for each state),
that services information requests from varying platforms via a socket
server.  No database there.  Flat files are used extensively (on disk
and
tape) in constructing the data for load into the VSAM files.  I would
not
say flat files are dead on any platform.
```

#### ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the internal
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

I probably use internal sort too often.  After decades of doing
maintenance, I will say that lots of little programs with sort/merge
logic is much easier to debug, much easier to change, and much less
likely to have bugs in it than bigger programs with internal sorts.

That said, you have to look at each application separately.
```

## Thread: COMP-3 — 15 msgs

### COMP-3

- **From:** "David Schell" <dschell@wic.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oQKH3.343$hU2.1668171@bunson.tor.sfl.net>`

```
I have an old legacy RMS file along with a file cobol file layout. I am
trying to interpert the file, but do not understand how information is
stored in comp-3 ... can anyone point me to a source on the Internet that
explains comp-3 is and how the bytes are stored/interpited.

thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E8OH3.326$Cv4.14564@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<oQKH3.343$hU2.1668171@bunson.tor.sfl.net>`

```
For a tutorial on all the COBOL data types, you can download (free) file
COBDATA.ZIP from http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C9SUpDALK773EwPA@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<oQKH3.343$hU2.1668171@bunson.tor.sfl.net>`

```
COMP-3 means packed numeric data. Ordinary numerics such as 12345 are in
zoned decimal which means that each character is stored in one byte with
bits 1 to 4 set to 1111 (in EBCDIC) and bits 5 to 8 representing the
number. Thus 123 is in hex 'F1F2F3'. Packed decimal removes bits 1 to 4
from each number, adds a trailing sign hex'F' and shuffles the others
together, thus F1F2F3 becomes hex '123F' where F represents the sign (F
and C are positive, D and E are negative). 

123  becomes hex '123F' or hex '123C'
-123 becomes hex '123D' or hex '123E' 

In article <oQKH3.343$hU2.1668171@bunson.tor.sfl.net>, David Schell
<dschell@wic.ca> writes
>I have an old legacy RMS file along with a file cobol file layout. I am
>trying to interpert the file, but do not understand how information is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
is positive, then
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>

```

Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> wrote in message
news:37EFDFEE.65DB@NOSPAMguysoftware.com...
> Volker Bandke wrote:
> >
> > On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 19:08:11 +0100, Alistair Maclean
<LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
> > wrote:

> > Are you sure about htis?  I know the so-called CAFE rule:  If the
sign nibble is from the
> > word CAFE, it is positive (or unsigned in the case of F), else it
is negative.   X'123E'
> > is positive, then

I vote for F being positive. I heard it as the FACE rule.

> How would you be able to handle ten possible final digits with only
four possible codes
> for positive numbers?

There are no digits in the last nibble. The last four bits represent
the sign - F,A,C,or E
means the number is positive, B or D means the number is negative.
```

```
Strictly speaking, F is 'unsigned'.  That functions as 'positive'.
```

```
Sorry, I do NOT understand.  To clarify  X'123C' is the same as X'123A' is the same as
X'123E' and (nearly the) same as X'123F', they all represent the nonnegative number 123

I really would only need two codes, X'C for plus and X'D' for minus
```

```
try that in a key-field and you miss...
```

```
In article <37EFDFEE.65DB@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>,
  Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> wrote:
> Volker Bandke wrote:
> >
> > On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 19:08:11 +0100, Alistair Maclean
<LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
> > wrote:
> >
> > >COMP-3 means packed numeric data. Ordinary numerics such as 12345
are in
> > >zoned decimal which means that each character is stored in one
byte with
> > >bits 1 to 4 set to 1111 (in EBCDIC) and bits 5 to 8 representing
the
> > >number. Thus 123 is in hex 'F1F2F3'. Packed decimal removes bits 1
to 4
> > >from each number, adds a trailing sign hex'F' and shuffles the
others
> > >together, thus F1F2F3 becomes hex '123F' where F represents the
sign
> >
> > (F>and C are positive, D and E are negative).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Are you sure about htis?  I know the so-called CAFE rule:  If the
sign nibble is from the
> > word CAFE, it is positive (or unsigned in the case of F), else it
is negative.   X'123E'
> > is positive, then
>
> How would you be able to handle ten possible final digits with only
four possible codes
> for positive numbers?
>
> To convert something like this, you might consider a generic parser
like ParseRat which
> will look after these details for you.
(http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm).

Does parserat take into account the fact that COMP-3 storage is
implementation specific?
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RHtwXkAqQR83EwXq@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<oQKH3.343$hU2.1668171@bunson.tor.sfl.net>`

```
I was wrong. See quoted text from another thread:

From IBM's "Principles of Operations":

A.1.2 Decimal Integers


Decimal integers consist of one or more decimal digits and a sign. Each
digit and the sign are represented by a 4-bit code. The decimal digits
are in binary-coded decimal (BCD) form, with the values 0-9 encoded as
0000-1001. The sign is usually represented as 1100 (C hex) for plus and
1101 (D hex) for minus. These are the preferred sign codes, which are
generated by the machine for the results of decimal-arithmetic
operations. There are also several alternate sign codes (1010, 1110, and
1111 for plus; 1011 for minus). The alternate sign codes are accepted by
the machine as valid in source operands but are not generated for
results.From IBM's "Principles of Operations":


In article <oQKH3.343$hU2.1668171@bunson.tor.sfl.net>, David Schell
<dschell@wic.ca> writes
>I have an old legacy RMS file along with a file cobol file layout. I am
>trying to interpert the file, but do not understand how information is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-5d3ce900ec"></a>
## Thread: Platform Independence (was: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.) — 9 msgs

### Re: Platform Independence (was: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF53C9.AE8B9DAB@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37ed9a4e@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37EEB74C.46301887@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> peter dashwood wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

I will stand with a foot in both camps.

Where possible use the simplest portable constructs to perform the
function at hand.  For efficiency do use specific constructs where
they are necessary.  What is efficiency - run time and programmer
time.

The logic is simple keeping the capacity to move in the code costs
little in most cases.  The trick is to know what works and what does
not you really need a couple of compilers (PC and MVS for example) to
guarantee it, if you are testing with a PC this is not a great problem
though.

There is a cost to doing this cross platform stuff (I am doing a PC,
Unix, MVS solution now and it is 'fun').  This cost should not be
added to the project just because the programmer wants to do it. 
There must be a justification for it.

There you go just toss a coin and you have my opinion,
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Platform Independence (was: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EFE945.D7009CD2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37ed9a4e@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37EEB74C.46301887@att.net> <37EF53C9.AE8B9DAB@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> William Lynch wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

Well, Ken has put his one cent in, (stingy bastards these Aussies). Pete
- are you just as thrifty on the two little islands SE of him ? 
(Still, I bet you haven't yet bought Stephen Gennard that pint you
promised him back on Compuserve - remember ? Anybody checking in from
Newbury - confirm with Stephen).

Was going to cover this from what you had written to me privately, but
as you have introduced the topic here, I'll also query here.

I agree with your comments about Platform Independence, and the other
comment you got, though probably not meant that way, did sound like
"Big-iron versus small-iron". You and I go back to Noah's time, me doing
the political bit talking to the users, and at least initially, you
'talking' to the machine. So it makes sense that you have built up a
considerable repertoire of coding routines over the years which can be
transferred to different compilers.

I know that both you and Thane have done the switcheroo from M/F to
Fujitsu - and though Bill Klein believes, probably also applies to a lot
of the current Merant clan, (if I read him right), that the cheap price
for Visoc was its downfall - admit it, both you and Thane were largely
influenced by the price for Fujitsu, and at the time Visoc was issued it
lacked Dialog System that you had both been using in the DOS version.

Correct me as necessary, but the only common elements that transfer from
COBOL to COBOL are syntax, COBOL file-handling and SCREEN SECTION. When
it comes to your statements about not using compiler specific stuff:-

- what alternatives do you offer me for the CBL_FILE routines if I
transfer from M/F - I use them extensively

- Logically, I shouldn't use N/E OO and GUI classes because they aren't
transferable. (Sorry, I haven't got the bucks to go out and buy a Big
Blue Mainframe to find out what OO is about).

- Thane is using SP2 but you are using Fujitsu routines - I doubt what
you are using is transferable.

- I note your enthusiasm for going data base as opposed to conventional
file handling. I really can't get a handle on this one. I tried to raise
the topic back in Compuserve days - and the only taker was Pat
Magee(Merant) who strongly supported SQL as a language and file system.
Similarly here, raised it as a topic again. Naturally our good friend
Warren Simmonds jumped in, (why not, he was on the original DB committee
back when....). The only other taker here was Thane Hubbell - he was
doing things in COBOL but could think of an instance where it might have
been better using DB. Also, interesting, Thane said it was SLOW - his
emphasis, not mine. Nobody jumped in to refute Thane's comment on speed.
So to scratch a few ticks, and get it started again, what gives with
this DB approach as being better than COBOL data files ? (I can think of
an obvious one, the DB approach makes it easier to work with other
languages). Re other languages, I recall you were 'underwhelmed' with
JAVA.

(Just a rider about DB - I recall Warren mentioning it was very
effective in a parts explosion type of application - but in effect I
achieved the same using RM/COBOL data-files on a Radio Shack Model 16 +
Xenix. Agricultural chemicals/liquids - they had two basic 'soups' to
which you added other ingredients - from the input recipe and
quantities/volumes, and the finished packaging,  I gave them the
manufacturing cost per unit)

Not disputing what you say, just trying to get a handle on it, and
hopefully it will cause others to jump in on merits/demerits of doing it
in various ways - and exactly what is transferable for platform
independence.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Platform Independence (was: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.)

- **From:** paulr@delphi.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f153bf$1$cnhye$mr2ice@news.stonemedia.com>`
- **References:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37ed9a4e@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37EEB74C.46301887@att.net> <37EF53C9.AE8B9DAB@zip.com.au> <37EFE945.D7009CD2@home.com>`

```
In <37EFE945.D7009CD2@home.com>, on 09/27/99 
   at 09:53 PM, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> said:

Note that with a DB you can do things in COBOL that are 
deucedly difficult otherwise. For example,  combining the inputs from
say, 6 or 7 tables (files), selecting just the fields you are 
interested in is not difficult in DB2. In fact, it is one call.  Doing
the same with 6 or 7 VSAM files (or ISAM or whatever  native file system
you are use) is much more difficult. 

Along those same lines, a well optimized database, 
along with a well optimized hunk of SQL can usually outperform COBOL
file performance for searching and sorting and
certainly on joining. The big secret is to use static SQL,  and that
applies to just about ever DBMS that I am familiar  with. It certainly
applies to DB2. 

You get some other benefits too, though you don't often see those on PC
platforms. Scalability is the #1 benefit that comes to my mind. Client
server follows quickly, and limited transactional  capabilty WITHOUT a
third party transaction manager. DB2
and Oracle often provide all the transaction management needed within
some organizaitons. Of course, they work exceedingly well with CICS too,
albeit with some limitations. 

The COBOL coding is about the same level of complexity as
coding a base call to a VSAM file, requiring about the same amount of
setup. Compile time is a little longer though. 

Yours,
-Paul

[ Lots of good stuff clipped... ]

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

>effective in a parts explosion type of application - but in effect I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>manufacturing cost per unit)


>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Platform Independence (was: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F0C5CA.70707E6@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37ed9a4e@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37EEB74C.46301887@att.net> <37EF53C9.AE8B9DAB@zip.com.au> <37EFE945.D7009CD2@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> - Logically, I shouldn't use N/E OO and GUI classes because they
> aren't transferable. (Sorry, I haven't got the bucks to go out and buy
> a Big Blue Mainframe to find out what OO is about).

Sorry Jimmy but I have not got the GUI screen to work on a 3270
terminal yet so we will hold off on this one for a while.  :-}

> Similarly here, raised it as a topic again. Naturally our good friend
> Warren Simmonds jumped in, (why not, he was on the original DB 

You picked one of the LEAST portable of all.

When is a standard not a a standard, when it is SQL.  Each vendor
gives close but different results to SQL. How do you really optimise
Oracle SQL and how do you really optimise DB2 SQL there are subtle
differences.

How do you hook the database for Server X.  Some use an interface
routine, some use a pre-processor.   To my knowledge none actually
work using SQL implementations for another vendor.

If you write for server X (DB2 or oracle) you can get platform
independence.   Servers are great, you do not have to concern yourself
with file locking, you can process data 'sets' consolidating totals
and simplify your programming logic.

How much does this cost, traditional estimates place these costs at
about 10% overhead.

I really like databases they make my life easier,
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Platform Independence (was: LE Runtime or COBOL Intrinsic? That is the Question.)

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f175c9@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<7sdh88$ade$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37ed9a4e@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <37EEB74C.46301887@att.net> <37EF53C9.AE8B9DAB@zip.com.au> <37EFE945.D7009CD2@home.com> <37F0C5CA.70707E6@zip.com.au>`

```
What the Hell is this all about?!

When did a discussion on LE Runtime or COBOL intrinsic functions become a
garbled conversation on platform independence? Are you guys running another
server somewhere?

My system shows 4 messages in this thread since (and including) the message
by Bill Thompson on the 23rd, responded to by Bill Klein on the 23rd, and me
on the 26th. Now this message from Ken pops up dated the 28th, referring to
a message by Jimmy, which I cannot find anywhere in the Newsgroup, certainly
not in this thread.

What's going on? Are there different versions of comp.lang.cobol? Maybe my
ISP is not relaying it correctly...Any ideas?

Anyway, my comments to Ken are interspersed below. I'd like to have read the
full context, particularly Jimmy's posting...

Ken Foskey wrote in message <37F0C5CA.70707E6@zip.com.au>...
>"James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>differences.

The SQL standard is becoming ever more stable. It is NOT true as a sweeping
statement that "Each Vendor gives close but different results to SQL". It IS
true that SOME constructs and clauses vary by implementation.

How do you optimise ORACLE or DB2? You don't. (Not unless you are having
serious performance problems).

The problem is that in order to "optimise" you MUST use vendor-specific
clauses (we are not talking untrained user ignorance here, searching across
multiple keys that are not indexed, and all the other "silly" things users
do because the system says they can, etc.). It's no accident. The vendors
planned it that way. To lock you into their platform, just as vendors have
been doing with programming languages and DB access methods since commercial
computers became available. If you fall for it and start coding platform
specific SQL you can hear them shouting "Sucker!" all the way to the Bank.

The best compromise I've found so far is to use ODBC, write standard SQL,
and tell the vendor if it doesn't perform you'll move the lot to another
platform...you'd be amazed at how this gets their attention.

We write everything in standard embedded SQL in COBOL, for ODBC to
(initially) MS-ACCESS . We test and debug using ACCESS. We even implement
for smaller companies using ACCESS. THEN we port to ORACLE, INGRES, DB2,
UDB, or DATACOM. So far we never had a problem Excellent ODBC drivers are
available for all of the above and they allow cross platform, enterprise
wide data access.

In fact, it is so platform independent that I use an online, mainframe DB2
reference, as a quick lookup to obtain SQL statement formats, when I'm not
sure, or senility gets the better of me.... It seems to work for all of the
mentioned platforms, as long as I DON'T do anything DB2 specific. Yes, I
have MS-ACCESS SQL reference and I also have DELPHI's Borland Database SQL
reference, but I like the simplicity of the DB2 one from LEGENT.

We need to look ahead and future-proof our systems as much as possible.
Standard SQL and platform independence is the best way to do this.

>
>How do you hook the database for Server X.  Some use an interface
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and simplify your programming logic.

Amen to that!


>
>How much does this cost, traditional estimates place these costs at
>about 10% overhead.

>
>I really like databases they make my life easier,

Me too Ken. And it's just as well. I believe within 10 years they will be
the ONLY data repositories available to us. (Of course, by then, standard
SQL will actually be a standard...<G>)

Pete.
```

```
peter dashwood wrote:

I ROARED WITH LAUGHTER at your message - what makes you so damn
different? Let me spell a similar situation out to you :-

	1. Ray Leach writes to me about N/E
	2. Thane Hubbell replies to Ray
	3. Then I jump in and say 'What the Hell's going on'.

of the originator's message gets to me direct. I suppose the 'Net is
packaging it into 'delivery modules' ???

Want a scapegoat - call him Bill - no honest Bill, I'm kidding. Chances
are you will NEVER get my initial message, so I'll copy and send it to
you direct, (promise) - then you can reply through here on anything you
aren't currently covering.

Of course we've got separate NewsGroups, one for N.America, one for UK,
and that probably excludes people living in the Norfolk Broads. (AND -
BEFORE Michael or one of the others adds a quip 'Broads' is a
geographical location not somebody you say to "Voulez vous couchez avec
moi ce soir s'il vous plait ?")

Behind all that hurrumphh - you gotta be smiling mon Pierre <G> ?

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-aa1ee2f05e"></a>
## Thread: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **From:** ppoggione@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I need to convert COBOL data imported to MS Access that contains
numeric data in the format S9(5)V99. Importing the data is no problem
but I need to run a script that will convert the trailing digit to make
the number useful. ie: 000230{

I'm not a COBOL programmer but I do understand that in this format the
decimal is implied and the last character determines both the last
digit as well as the sign of the number. Can anyone give me the mapping
for the last character? I see a lot of A's, B's, C's, {'s and }'s etc..
If someone would be kind enough to enlighten me I would greatly
appreciate it.

The sad thing is that I've done this once before about 18 months ago
but have lost the code that I used!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7KPH3.674$Xv.88526@news21b.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

<ppoggione@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I need to convert COBOL data imported to MS Access that contains
> numeric data in the format S9(5)V99. Importing the data is no problem
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> appreciate it.

{ = +0     A-I = +1 thru +9
} = -0      J-R = -1 thru -9

The sign and the last digit are combined in the last character.  For
PIC S9(5)V99   and a value of "000230{" represents a value of +23.00

HTH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sq5ee$ten$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
the

etc..

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

-trblshtr-
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EFE104.1218@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
ppoggione@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I need to convert COBOL data imported to MS Access that contains
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> appreciate it.

As I noted in another post, there are utilities that will do this for you. ParseRat 
(http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm) can handle this with its "binary" input 
option (which can handle "zoned" decimals like this as well as "packed") and its ability 
to do a /100 to get to two places of decimals.  It will export as fixed format, 
delimited, mailmerge or, more useful for your need, dBase.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p2XH3.2263$j4.65186@viper>`
- **References:** `<7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
As indicated by your posting the SIGN IS TRAILING. Assuming this follows
standards, The hig order nibble will be hexadecimal C for positive and
hexadecimal D for negative. The low order value will contain the absolute
value of the numeric value. All other digits will have a hexadecimal F in
the high order nibble and the absolute numeric value in the low order
nibble. ZERO always being stored as a positive number. The } indicates +0.

Using your example:
Display    Hexadecimal
0                    F0
0                    F0
0                    F0
2                    F2
3                    F3
}                    C0

for +0002300Display    Hexadecimal

0                    F0
0                    F0
0                    F0
2                    F2
3                    F3
???               D0

for -002300, not sure of the DISPLAY representation for hex "D0"
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F1343B.B3E863BE@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
ppoggione@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I need to convert COBOL data imported to MS Access that contains
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> appreciate it.

Then you can use Data2Fkat converter from Siber Systems.
It can read your Cobol data files and convert them
to CVS (comma-separated values) files that can be easily
imported into MS Access.

More info on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL numerics to ASCII

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F06736.1DB26FFA@att.net>`
- **References:** `<7sofjm$ol0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
From IBM's "Principles of Operations":

A.1.2 Decimal Integers


Decimal integers consist of one or more decimal digits and a sign. Each
digit and the sign are represented by a 4-bit code. The decimal digits
are in binary-coded decimal (BCD) form, with the values 0-9 encoded as
0000-1001. The sign is usually represented as 1100 (C hex) for plus and
1101 (D hex) for minus. These are the preferred sign codes, which are
generated by the machine for the results of decimal-arithmetic
operations. There are also several alternate sign codes (1010, 1110, and
1111 for plus; 1011 for minus). The alternate sign codes are accepted by
the machine as valid in source operands but are not generated for
results.
```

## Thread: Compiler Examples — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler Examples

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EFC744.8509EDD@home.com>`

```
Addressed to Bill Klein but others can chip in.

So here you are William, no longer a junior, with a whole daffy of
knowledge from your days with M/F. You pick up Fujitsu and start playing
with it. Surprise, surprise, just like I've had to struggle with
NetExpress you are now asking 'How do I do this in Fuji ?".

It really is bloody dumb of these manufacturers. Just how many hours are
being wasted around the COBOL universe with people re-inventing the
wheel. If you compiler folk are looking in, Acu, Unisys, Liant, Fujitsu,
Merant et al - start giving it some serious thought - provide a more
comprehensive set of examples as to how to use your products, (in the
REAL WORLD), particularly where the module(s) is/are specific to your
compiler.

Want an analogy. You go buy a new vehicle from the salesman at the auto
showroom. He helps you push the car out on to the main road, then with a
smile says, "You're on you own......".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-33692e3abc"></a>
## Thread: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7so97a$b3r$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<nauH3.9069$OU1.73830@news4.mia>`

```

J. Keith Jackson wrote in message ...
>The first indication of a problem with the notebook is an error message,
>"IO-ERROR 98,1 on SYSTEM-DESC-FILE D:\CMSFIL\SD.".  It also gives the
module
>names of the calling .COB files.

Cobol-85 is not a Cobol vendor, it is a standard, however. It might be that
you are dealing with Acucobol, which uses the term Cobol-85, in that case
the 98,01 error is due to the index being broken. See if you can find a
utility: vutil.exe (possibly vutil32.exe). and use to it to rebuild the
index of the particular file:

    vutil -rebuild <filename>

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with COBOL85 Error Codes

- **From:** frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37efcafc.9270973@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<nauH3.9069$OU1.73830@news4.mia> <7so97a$b3r$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
On Mon, 27 Sep 1999 10:24:19 -0700, "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>
>J. Keith Jackson wrote in message ...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In this particular case it seems this program is 
a RM/COBOL one.
The solution found by him was the command "share"
missing from the computer in question.

The way to try to find the vendor is by looking
on the startup command and see if it is a ".bat"
or other. If the first then look inside it to
determine the command used.
If the second then try and execute it without
parameters in case the normal execution is with
them.
and finally use a hexa editor to look inside the
executable if nothing else works.

FF
```

```
I will fire IBM or rather ATT.
I'll send them a letter stating that the ONLY reason
I'm still using IBM.net is the pain associated with changing
my well-known e-mail address. ATT says that after one year
the IBM.NET address is no longer supported.
My letter will state that unless they let me keep
the address as long as I pay them my money
to keep the account, I'll switch, and switch
NOW rather than wait a year. I hope many
others will do so, so that we customers
can force ATT to be customer friendly.
If not, it's time to vote with your feet.
Ah, I'm getting all fired up (no pun intended) now...
```

```
A million years ago, long before peecees we had monochrome terminals
Then came the colour ones, they wern't that much more expensive
I was sent to 'evaluate' them.  I liked them.  Set up a demo.
But the boss wasn't impressed, said that the difference wasn't worth a dime
```

<a id="t-b054abd77f"></a>
## Thread: help with subscripts — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help with subscripts

- **From:** wescraven_1@excite.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7smq5h$k2t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
i am trying to use a subscript to go through a table which has a salary
that can occur 3 times but not always and i want it to go through
depending on if there is a salary and if not to stop right there. need
help                              Thank you to anyone who helps

   PERFORM
       VARYING EMP-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL EMP-SALARY (EMP-SUB) > 3
           AND EMP-SALARY (EMP-SUB) NOT = 0


   END-PERFORM.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: help with subscripts

- **From:** bbello5778@aol.com (BBello5778)
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990927071457.28360.00003106@ng-da1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7smq5h$k2t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>PERFORM
>       VARYING EMP-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
>           UNTIL EMP-SALARY (EMP-SUB) > 3
>           AND EMP-SALARY (EMP-SUB) NOT = 0

try UNTIL EMP-SALARY (EMP-SUB) > 3      OR EMP-SALARY (EMP-SUB) = 0

Good luck
```

#### ↳ Re: help with subscripts

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7snsua$ajs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7smq5h$k2t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I won't do your homework assignment for you, but here's some things to
look at that should lead you to the solution.

Since your control variable is EMP-SUB, and EMP-SUB has a max value 3,
you should be checking for EMP-SUB > 3.  As it is, you're checking for
a particular occurance of EMP-SALARY > 3.  Also, consider the impact of
joining two condition with AND vs. joining two conditions with OR.

Others will disagree for valid reasons, but my personal style is to
have only one condition in the UNTIL clause, and that condition is
checking for the control subscript > its max value. Any other
conditions that could cause an 'early bailout' are checked as part of
the processing of each value of the subscript.

In article <7smq5h$k2t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  wescraven_1@excite.com wrote:
> i am trying to use a subscript to go through a table which has a
salary
> that can occur 3 times but not always and i want it to go through
> depending on if there is a salary and if not to stop right there. need
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: help with subscripts

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7snv4j$c99$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7smq5h$k2t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

>
>    PERFORM
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There's an obvious mistake in the UNTIL.. line.

Here's a solution (pseudocode).
Set a SALARY-DONE-SW switch to 'N', with an '88' SALARY-DONE VALUE 'Y'.
PERFORM VARYING ... UNTIL EMP-SUB > 3 OR SALARY-DONE.
If you hit EMP-SALARY(x) = 0, MOVE 'Y' TO SALARY-DONE-SW.

John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: In search of a PL/I compiler — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I compiler

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ehIH3.27130$ei1.49276@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com> writes: > I stand corrected
> 
> But an anybody tell me why the bank I'm consulting to insisted on updating
> to 2.5 on the basis that prior releases were not Y2K confomant- that is,
> where did they get their information?

OS PL/I ver 2 Rel 3 IS Y2k compliant, along with ver 1 rel. 5.1.
Just for the record, IBM DOS ver 1 release 6.0 is Y@K compliant,
as are PL/I for VSE/ESA and MVS & VM.

Note that PLI for VSE/ESA, MVS and VM and ver 1 release 5.1
all require patches for COMPILEDATE, and ver 1 rel 5.1 and MVS & VM
require patches for DATE.

> V2.5 has been horribly unreliable and
> they're now talking about upgrading to 2.7+ ASAP. Everybody was yelling
> about 2.5 before it went live on the production systems.
```

<a id="t-bef7ab7fdb"></a>
## Thread: Searching Tables — 2 msgs

### Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF5B25.51993BB9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ee4b57.122921762@news2.ibm.net> <37EE7DFC.3F71B6A1@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Volker Bandke wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I prefer the search statement.  Adding the index is really a small
price to pay especially since you need to define a subscript anyway
and there is a performance benefit for the index in most cases.

The other thing is the implicit bounds checking performed by search
with OCCURS DEPENDING ON tables.  I have experienced too many errors
with turning debugging (such as SSRANGE in MVS).

This is one of the selling points of Cobol.  The main trap is having
to set the index to 1 before you start with 'standard' search but not
with search all.  This is useful for locating multiple occurrences of
the same key in the same table but causes many hours of debugging. 
(Must write this into the inspection guide for Cobol).

Junior programmer challenge:  How do you find the first key entry from
a table where the ordered key is duplicated multiple times using a
search all.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EFA8A3.8261AB94@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ee4b57.122921762@news2.ibm.net> <37EE7DFC.3F71B6A1@home.com> <37EF5B25.51993BB9@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

Thanks Thane, Volker and Ken,

I was mulling over this one this morning as I was having my first cuppa.

In response to Volker's first query "Why haven't you considered....".
Good question, with a simple answer. Never had the luxury of going to
school to learn COBOL; taught myself out of necessity to earn a living.
May not be the most efficient coder, but with a systems background -
I'll not be modest - I can put a good design together. In some respects,
that's perhaps an advantage over 'tweeking' at code to save
milli/microseconds.

For Volker's information - dare I say it - my end-user is a German
perfectionist <G>, highly respected in his field, and was invited by
Chevron to lecture to their plant managers on Non-Destructive Testing.
So we are constantly playing a game, me trying to anticipate the next
short-cut he might look for in my design, and he, always coming back,
with another neat twist to automate his data entry even further -
sometimes frustrating for me, but also fun -  keeps this old guy
mentally alert.

Nevertheless, as I would say to anybody who cares to ask, unashamedly
'steal' other peoples code if you find their technique clearer/more
efficient than what you have been doing.

Volker has indicated he teaches COBOL - so any comment on the following. 
Seems to me the academic world should be a good resource for us
bread-and-butter guys. A big ZIP in Calgary. Probably the major centre
for computing in Canada because of the oil/gas, and I have no idea, but
there could be in excess of 1,000 COBOL programmers chugging away on
those mainframes.

We have a University and Tech Institute, both with glitzy web-sites.
Seeking general background on collections/dictionaries, (I know they
have OO courses), posted an e-mail to a likely candidate at the two
institutions. Got zippo back - not even a polite acknowledgement.

Some 5 years ago phoned the COBOL department of another Tech College
with separate queries some two years apart. Both times got the same
Chinese instructor, (no, I'm not being racist, I recognised his Hong
Kong accent). On both occasions he was extremely defensive and both
times started the conversation with "Why are you phoning me, you should
be phoning your instructor". Absolutely aghast that somebody from the
real world was asking for his advice. Both occasions he got off the
phone as quickly as possible.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-a5ace3b805"></a>
## Thread: Help — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help

- **From:** "Colin Lyons" <colin.r.lyons@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7smak0$aos$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
I'm new to cobol and i am trying to figure out this problem:

The program logic below is supposed to read through a file and display all
the
odd numbered records (first record, third record, fifth, etc). I need help
to fill in the blanks..
any takers

OPEN INPUT TEST-FILE.
MOVE SPACES TO FILE-SWITCH.
PERFORM UNTIL FILE-SWITCH = 'EOF'
    READ TEST-FILE
       AT END
          MOVE EOF TO FILE-SWITCH
    ___________________
          DISPLAY TEST-RECORD
          READ TEST-FILE
            AT END
               MOVE 'EOF' TO FILE SWITCH
          _____________________
       ___________________________
END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF5F2B.3318E4ED@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7smak0$aos$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Colin Lyons wrote:
> 
> I'm new to cobol and i am trying to figure out this problem:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Here is a minor tidy up to get you on your way, not a complete
solution assumes you have a passably recent compiler:

 OPEN INPUT TEST-FILE.
 MOVE SPACES TO FILE-SWITCH.
 PERFORM UNTIL FILE-SWITCH = 'EOF'
     READ TEST-FILE
        AT END
           MOVE EOF TO FILE-SWITCH
        NOT AT END
           DISPLAY TEST-RECORD
           READ TEST-FILE
             AT END
                MOVE 'EOF' TO FILE SWITCH
           END-READ
     END-READ
 END-PERFORM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EF9DA8.4B60459E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7smak0$aos$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <37EF5F2B.3318E4ED@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Colin Lyons wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END-PERFORM.

Ken's got you started; consider why he put in those end-reads. OK, you
are new at the game and you've probably got mental blockage about your
'odds' and 'evens' - Next Clue - go read your Programming text - look at
the syntax for the DIVIDE verb.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-d73abdcfe0"></a>
## Thread: FUNCTION Reserved Word — 5 msgs

### FUNCTION Reserved Word

- **From:** "Steve Wright" <swright@bfsec.bt.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7snpcs$9lg$1@pheidippides.axion.bt.co.uk>`

```
Folks,

Does anyone know a web address or can give me a brief description of the
FUNCTION keyword.  How do you define a function and is it only arithmetic
operations that can be performed with it ?

Any help much appreciated.

Cheers
Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: FUNCTION Reserved Word

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7snqdq$787@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7snpcs$9lg$1@pheidippides.axion.bt.co.uk>`

```
Tell us which compiler, which release, and on which platform you are
working - and we can point you to the correct documentation.  The original
ANSI'85 Standard did not include support for Intrinsic Functions - they were
added by an amendment in '89.  Therefore, your compiler may NOT support them
(yet?).  If your compiler does support them, it may include some additional
ones as extensions.

Current COBOL does not support "user-defined" functions.  However, in MOST
cases, those who are looking for "user-defined functions" in COBOL can get
the exact same functionality (or at least what they are really looking for)
by writing COBOL subprograms.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FUNCTION Reserved Word

- **From:** "Steve Wright" <swright@bfsec.bt.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7snrbb$aeu$1@pheidippides.axion.bt.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7snpcs$9lg$1@pheidippides.axion.bt.co.uk> <7snqdq$787@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The current compiler does not support functions, but we are going to be
upgrading to a new compiler soon.

The platform is an IBM MVS Mainframe and the new Compiler is COBOL for MVS
and VM and is part of LE/370.  That's all the information I have on the new
compiler.  I'm just trying to find out about the features of the new
compiler, but have drawn a blank with our own people about this one.

Thanks
Steve
```

```
OK,
  With that information, I can help.  First, check out
     http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/coblib.htm#COBOLOS390VM

which has links to all the latest IBM COBOL's latest manuals.

Three that you want to look at are:

1) Migration Guide
     (which will tell you everything - well lots - about what will be
new/changing)

2) The LRM
    (which gives you the syntax for functions)

3) The Programming Guide
   (which tells you how to use all the various types of functions).

"Normally" I would give you the web-links for all the manuals, but the IBM
bookserver seems to be "Monday morning busy" - and I can't get a link to it
at the moment.
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf0947$48c41040$dd17063e@default>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF6985128D6@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```

> I'm not sure, but this look like a generic popup routine, triggered by
> button 3.
> And, indeed, it is at window level (not field level)
> Now what I really want, is a way to include entry-field-specific
information
> (e.g. chain to a specific help item, or whatever), without doing anything
in
> dialog at field level.

You are mistaken, the example that I attached is in fact at field level,
which is what you asked?.

Take the code again and understand it.

           move p2ce-button3down to EventValue
           move "mouseclickedC " to methodName
           invoke EntryCallback "new" using methodname
               returning aCallback
           invoke oTextEditField "setEvent" using EventValue aCallback

There is a callback which is set to oTextEditField (an instance of an
particular edit field)

So when p2ce-button3down in oTextEditField, method methodName in this case
"mouseclickedC " is invoked.

However there is an easier way from doing this(depending on your structure)
code as follows:

           map event p2ce-button3down upon oTextEditField to  
               method z"mouseclickedC" upon self 
  
map event is syntax defined from the standard MF guibase.if file.



I knew i should have been a professor ;)

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

## Thread: Please ( change the name of Wrun32.exe ) — 1 msgs

### Re: Please ( change the name of Wrun32.exe )

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7so8n4$ajr$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7skq84$f0t$1@talia.mad.ttd.net>`

```
Oscar Jofre wrote in message <7skq84$f0t$1@talia.mad.ttd.net>...
>I'm working with AcuCobol.
>
>Can I change the name of the Wrun32.exe ( in the Process Administration )
in
>Windows Nt with the name of the program ??
>
>So when I run \:> wrun32 prog1, I'd like to see running prog1.exe not
>wrun32.exe. I add funcitions with c++ in AcuCobol.

Yes you can rename wrun32.exe, just remember to rename the license file too
(not the extension!)

However, if the purpose is to specify a window title, you may do that inside
your cobol app:

    DISPLAY WINDOW TITLE "My title"

Or you may put it in your configuration file:

    window-title    My title

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-5f87010d14"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL - Loading a List — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL - Loading a List

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37EEE506.5632CC3C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37EE9E55.97F17493@home.com> <19990926213143.08460.00000806@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <37EE9E55.97F17493@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> to write all the list entries back out to the external source.

If by your last paragraph you mean write records back to the data-file -
no way Jose. That's the cop-out MicroFocus use in their demo telephoone 
application. Read in a sequential file, change entries in the ListBox
and then write a new output file when the program is exited. That just
isn't the real world; and suppose a user had spent three hours changing
records in the Listbox and the machine goes belly-up - he/she's gonna
really love ya.

My data is fairly compact related to one oil plant, but I would have to
speculate that if I were in a 'large' environment, huge customer file
and multi-users - then I would probably consider a timer to close, then
re-open the data file based on user activity or how many records had
been updated - at least that way a whole day's entries aren't lost.

As I said my data is compact per gas plant - whereas the oil companies,
God bless 'em - they think real big, (call it mainframe mentality if you
wish), shoving all data into one huge DB - and boy do they have fun when
the DB crashes. 

To be fair, and as background to what I'm doing - I'm creating OO
collections of variable length, I can set ordered or sorted
when designing the Listbox in M/F Dialog Editor (which is a spin-off of
M/S Dialog Editor, plus 4 additional controls). The Dialog Editor is 
set to sorted by default (property, if you like), but I can override it. 

In terms of updating the Listbox it is as follows, bearing in mind I
have a pointer(index) to where a current record appears in the list and
I have the PrimeKey for the datafile  :-

	*> Extracts from my Class : ClientsDBI 

	*> NOTE : I've already updated the Client Datafile before 	
	*> triggering these updates to the collection - if the file
	*> update gives me a file-status error, not much point in
	*> attempting to update the collection - is there <G>

	OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
	01 os-Table.
           05 os-Collection occurs 2		object reference.

	   *> Collection 1 = RecType 1 - Clients
	   *> Collection 2 = RecType 2 - Facilities (for this Client)
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "add-to-collection".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-string                        object reference.
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-record.
          copy "clients2.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
       01 lnk-Collection                object reference.

       Procedure Division using     lnk-record
                          returning lnk-Collection.

          move lnk-record to CLIENT-record-contents
          move CLIENT-recordtype to ws-CurrentRectype
          invoke self "field-positions"
          invoke self "make-list-record" returning ls-string
          invoke os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype) "add"
                 using ls-string returning ls-string
          set lnk-Collection to os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype)
       End Method "add-to-collection".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "change-collection".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-Index1                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-Index2                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-string1                        object reference.
       01 ls-string2                        object reference.
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-index                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-record.
          copy "clients2.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
       01 lnk-Collection                    object reference.
       Procedure Division using     lnk-index
                                    lnk-record
                          returning lnk-Collection.

         move lnk-record to CLIENT-record-contents
         move CLIENT-recordtype to ws-CurrentRectype
         invoke self "field-positions"
         move lnk-index to ls-Index1 ls-Index2
         invoke os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype) "at"
                using ls-index1 returning ls-string1
         invoke self "make-list-record" returning ls-string2
         invoke os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype) "replaceAll"
                using ls-string1 ls-string2 ls-index1 ls-index2
         set lnk-Collection to os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype)
       End Method "change-collection".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "delete-from-collection".
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-string                         object reference.
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-index                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 lnk-rectype                       pic 9(01).
       01 lnk-Collection                    object reference.

       Procedure Division using     lnk-index
                                    lnk-rectype
                          returning lnk-Collection.

         move lnk-rectype to ws-CurrentRectype
         invoke os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype)
                "removeAtIndex" using lnk-index returning ls-string
         set lnk-Collection to os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype)
       End Method "delete-from-collection".
       *>------------------------------------------------------------

Having updated the os-Collection in the DBI(Data Base Interface), via
linkage, it is passed back to the Edit program which tells the Dialog to
re-display the ListBox using the current object reference for the
collection.

Now there is a kicker to all this, even with OO - what is an acceptable
size for the collection, based on the machine you are using. That is why
I started a new thread 'Scrolling records', but it got little response;
Michael Mattias suggested using a relative file as a workfile and
SkippyB mentioned that in a mainframe environment he set a table-size to
22 records which was what could be displayed on a terminal at one time.
I know I'm pretty safe with the volumes I'm handling, but this requires
serious consideration in large data systems.

To an extent it is comparing apples with oranges, but what is being done
in an OO design can be emulated in a text environment by setting a
maximum table size and using OCCURS DEPENDING; say table-size was a max
of 100 then you would need to store First and Last Keys in the table to
be able to READ NEXT or READ PREVIOUS as the user attempts to move
forwards/backwards through the list. NetExpress provides a Table Sort
(not sure if other compilers do) so a 'changed' text list for a non-OO
application could be re-sorted before re-displaying in the Listbox. As
an alternative, record pointers within Fujitsu probably provide the same
ability to re-sequence. (?)

Hope this clarifies the points I was making - with a qualifier - depends
upon the concepts you use for designing the system, rather than any
constraints from the compiler.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Vï¿½ a http://babelfish.altavista.com/cgi-bin/translate? e vocï¿½ pode
escrever em Portugese e traduzir ao inglï¿½s. Eu emito-lhe agora zipfiles.
Diga-me se vocï¿½ receber zipfiles. 

Jimmy
--------------
Thurs Sep 30

Jimmy 
se compreendes portugues, dondes falas ? 
America ? 

reparei hoje que envias-te mails para o grupo de cobol, acontece que
havia uma pessoa 
que tinha o meu user. 
Hoje o jcapucho@teleweb.pt, jï¿½ ï¿½ meu. 

Vou esperar o teu correio, OK 
Josecapucho
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do you speak Spanish ?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A3A311DE574FC1D.A65B43401DCCF5F8.B688BCBEA5387974@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<37F051A9.578CE220@home.com> <37F0A64A.5C3B9D3B@njeb.att.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 1999 07:28:11 -0400, Alex Flinsch
<aflinsch@njeb.att.com> enlightened us:

>
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

James,

I have a similar problem communicating with my brother-in-law who only
speaks Italian.  What I do is type my email in as plain and simple
English as I can.  I then copy the message into the clipboard (Ctrl-C)
then go to:  http://babelfish.altavista.com/cgi-bin/translate?

Paste the English into their dialogue box and have it translated.  I
then copy and paste the translation into the email and send it off.  I
do the same with my brother-in-laws responses.  It isn't 100%
accurate, but it is close enough.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

What If the Hokey Pokey Is Really What It's All About?

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

```
http://www.thekrib.com/Lights/ballast.html.  My girlfriend says she
had heard the expression with the word "catfish".  According to

for me to believe.

Interesting, but not germane.  Aquarium fish are rarely skinned.  The
expression would have to come from an area where large catfish are
plentiful.
```

## Thread: boolean search string - Help! — 17 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### boolean search string - Help!

- **From:** rambo@goodnet.com (Douglas Flowers)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>`

```
I would like for a user to be able to enter a boolean searh string,
much lile an internet search engine and translate it into a logical
statement that will execute in a cobol porgram.  

Example:   the user enters    BILL  AND BOB AND JOE
                   
                                 or        NOT BILL  OR  JOE

The program searches each line of a text file for line that conforms
to the loginal expression.  How would I code the translation of the
the user's "search string" into an executable cobol expression ?



Doug Flowers
Phoenix, AZ

rambo@goodnet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: boolean search string - Help!

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LVfI3.3055$j4.83980@viper>`
- **References:** `<37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>`

```
If you are using ISPF, you could train the USERS ton how to use the
SEARCHFOR option of the utilities menu. The control cards would look
something like:

SRCHFOR 'BILL'
SRCHFORC 'BOB'
SRCHFORC 'JOE'

Would be the equivalent of BILL AND BOB AND JOE

SRCHFORC 'BOB'
DPLINE 'JOE'
DPLINE 'BILL'

Would be the equivalent of BOB NOT ( BILL OR JOE)

To properly parse your input first you might want to remove multiple spaces
from the input, then use the UNSTRING command and DELIMITTED BY SPACES to
pull off each key word for processing. However the rst of the explaination
would be far too lengthy for a posting, so I will leave it at that.
```

#### ↳ Re: boolean search string - Help!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gW6I3.341$zc3.12434@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>`

```
Douglas Flowers wrote in message <37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>...
>I would like for a user to be able to enter a boolean searh string,
>much lile an internet search engine and translate it into a logical
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I've always resisted using NOT and OR in the same sentence.

Make sure you understand the second one the same way the user will:

is it  (NOT BILL) OR JOE  (True when JOE)
or    NOT (BILL OR JOE)  (False when JOE)

MCM
```

```
It can easily be converted to do Booleans too.
```

```
parenthesis.  Unstring and parse using the standard stack methods.
```

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7srdbf$rq1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
  rambo@goodnet.com (Douglas Flowers) wrote:
> I would like for a user to be able to enter a boolean searh string,
> much lile an internet search engine and translate it into a logical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                                  or        NOT BILL  OR  JOE

UNSTRING will work for the example given but not if you add brackets.
If the expression is NOT (BILL  OR  JOE) you would need to use the open
and close brackets as delimiters and still keep them as extracted data.
```

#### ↳ Re: boolean search string - Help!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqdp7$kff@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>`

```
Douglas Flowers <rambo@goodnet.com> wrote in message
news:37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com...
> I would like for a user to be able to enter a boolean searh string,
> much lile an internet search engine and translate it into a logical
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

At first I thought that this sounded like a "homework" request - then I
thought that it sounded like a REALLY HARD homework request - then I thought
that it is a good prompt for the "right tool for the right job" statement.
(I would fully expect a REXX or PERL - or even SAS or SPITBOL fan to jump in
at any time to tell us how easy it would be in one of those languages), but
regardless of the background, let me outline how I would go about solving
this NON-trivial task.

First, as I understand it, you have two "inputs" - one includes the "boolean
command" and one the "string to be searched".  (Assuming this is correct), I
think you need to

1) Tokenize your boolean command.  Then syntax scan it (for "valid" commands
and "strings" and combinations of the two).

2) Once you have the tokens to be looked for (from the boolean command), you
need to set up a "truth table" based on the boolean operators in this input.
(Again, a non-trivial task - for example, what do you do about parenthesis -
does NOT apply only to the following operator or all subsequent operators -
can you parenthesis what is after the not, etc, ETC)

3) Finally, (and this is almost the easiest part - in COBOL), you need to
scan the other input for each of the strings requested by the boolean
commands - and check their presence/absence with your truth table.

   ***

As I say, in COBOL, (certainly depending on what constraints there are on
your 2 inputs), this would be a fairly complex "assignment".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: boolean search string - Help!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```

>

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                                  or        NOT BILL  OR  JOE
<Snip>

Maybe what Bill is suggesting, put in other words.

Sketchy outline -  but if as a combination you take Howard's 'unstring'
and Michael's tests and perhaps create a Decision table with flags set
true or false on the input string, you can test very efficiently using
the EVALUATE for a variety of combinations ????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: boolean search string - Help!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F1156B.A071689D@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com> <7sqdp7$kff@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <37F11026.C74B88C8@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> Maybe what Bill is suggesting, put in other words.
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I kind of like turning the unstrung words into Reverse Polish as someone
else suggested (I forgot who).  But obviously the first thing to do is
to make sure everybody agrees on how to parse compound and complex
statements, whether you have parenthesis and other punctuation touching
the words, etc.

Just because there's a "correct" way to parse compound OR and AND
clauses doesn't mean the user will be happy with that method.
```

```
that can handle arbitrarily complicated arithmetic and Boolean expressions
with parentheses nested to any depth, did not find favor with the
NG. What is wrong here? Was it too long? Too general?
It is a NON-trivial problem that has to be approached with
respect and care.
```

- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>`

```
In article <37f06c38.273361@News.goodnet.com>,
  rambo@goodnet.com (Douglas Flowers) wrote:
> I would like for a user to be able to enter a boolean searh string,
> much lile an internet search engine and translate it into a logical
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the user's "search string" into an executable cobol expression ?

Its not that difficult if you use recursion.  The following MF COBOL
code shows how it can be done.

And a rigidly raised digit to all those who believe that recursion has
no utility or that you can do the same thing just as easily with tables
and subscripts <G>.

BTW, MF has recursion because its entire package including the compiler
is written in COBOL.  It almost certainly uses the same type of
methodology to resolve COBOL logical expressions.
```
