### mf cobol question - student

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WDfI3.546$Yn5.3038@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

I'm using Micro Focus 2.0, and can't seem to get my DISPLAY statements in
color.  I've tried   -with foreground-color is XX-, which will work on one
line only.  If I try to make the next line (display) a different color, it
overwrites the first line.  I'm not sure if I can describe it properly -
here's the code....

200-DISPLAY-SALESPER-RECORD.
           DISPLAY " "
               WITH BLANK SCREEN
           DISPLAY "                 NAME: " SR-NAME
               WITH FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 5
           DISPLAY " "
           DISPLAY " "
           DISPLAY " "
           DISPLAY " "
           DISPLAY "   SALESPERSON REGION: " SR-REGION
               WITH FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 1

           DISPLAY " "

Now, I have a bunch of  -display " " - in there, trying to get some blank
lines in.  But still, the "salesperson region" overwrites the "name".  Also,
I tried to do two "with" clauses (blank screen and color) many different
ways and couldn't get a clean compile.

Hope this makes some sense, I'm not so good at explaining, I think.  Our
book doesn't really explain it and I'm just flying by the seat of my pants.
Our teacher isn't knowledgeable on MF, either.

Thanks,
Pat
```

```
Oooh. It's a while since I did anything for DOS-type display. Your code
looks like straight ANSI display, but it can't be, because you are using
Foreground-color etc - so it has to be SCREEN SECTION. Try using LIN and
COL values to move down the screen - unless I'm completely out of wack.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mf cobol question - student

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ssmra$rp6$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<WDfI3.546$Yn5.3038@newsfeed.slurp.net> <37F1A370.BABF54DC@home.com>`

```
Pat,
    The comments made by James below are absolutely correct (except for
SCREEN SECTION, you are not using that). To give you a little more
information, ANSI standard ACCEPTs and DISPLAYs are sent directly to the
screen at the current position, the position being updated after each.
Extended ACCEPTs and DISPLAYs ie those with colour information and/or
positioning are handled separately and the module used keeps its own current
position, so mixing ADIS and ANSI ACCEPTs is prone to exactly the sort of
peculiarities you have encountered. Therefore I would recommend that if you
want to use colour, remove the DISPLAY " " lines and add

AT LINE 4 COLUMN 1

or

AT 0401

(both do the same).

I hope this helps.



James J. Gavan wrote in message <37F1A370.BABF54DC@home.com>...
>Pat wrote:
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Dr. Klone" <snedunuri@hotmail.com>

```
Semantic Designs announces the availability of COBOL CloneDR (TM)-
enterprise-class software for the automatic detection and removal of COBOL
clones. Designed to handle large-scale applications (up to1 million lines of
code), the CloneDR helps organizations reduce maintenance costs, increase
programmer productivity, and raise reliability and availability.


Feature Set
--------------
Automatic Clone Detection AND REMOVAL

UNICODE support

Large-scale application support

SMP support for true parallel processing

Full-program processing support:
 Preprocessor Directives
 Data Declarations
 Procedural Code
 Comment Lines

Windows NT Runtime Environment

Evaluation Copy Availability
----------------------------
An Early Adopter Program (EAP) version of the CloneDR that detects clones
and provides an informative report is available at
 www.semdesigns.com/Products
(Requires a Pentium based machine running Windowst NT 4.0 and at least 128M
of memory)

Further Information can be obtained from the author or www.semdesigns.com
```

```
I talk about cloning pieces of code (I never wrote an old style
SELECT/ASSIGN statements from scratch), to pulling out a whole program
as a starting point for my new program.  The last decade or so though, I
have found that it's quicker just to write new code than to clone and
prune.

I still grab a linkage division and modify it though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Semantic Designs Announces COBOL Clone Doctor

- **From:** "Martin Ellison" <martin@ics.mq.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software-eng
- **Message-ID:** `<7ssrp7$20r$1@sunb.ocs.mq.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<rv2b8kaa3i520@corp.supernews.com> <37f1942b.89033103@news1.ibm.net> <37F1E7FD.A42583C@zip.com.au>`

```
> > Seriously, WHAT IS a COBOL Clone

> I suspect that it is repeated code patterns (even when slightly
different commands are used

There is a common coding practice of taking a copy of something
that works and making any necessary changes to create a new
program. This is sometimes known as 'cloning' (as in Dolly the
sheep).

The problem is that a maintenance change may well have to be made
to all the clones as well as the original.

This it would be desirable if (at least) all the clones could be
located and (preferably) could be merged into one.

The problems are looking through a large code base to find the
clones and in carefully comparing the clones to determine which
changes have been made. Both of these are potentially
automatable.

COBOL is not really relevant here except that the toolmaker is
addressing COBOL. Also, COBOL n (n < 2000) lacks some facilities
such as templates that could be used instead of cloning. But then
I suspect that programmers use cloning in C++ as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Semantic Designs Announces COBOL Clone Doctor

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **References:** `<rv2b8kaa3i520@corp.supernews.com> <37f1942b.89033103@news1.ibm.net> <37F1E7FD.A42583C@zip.com.au> <7ssrp7$20r$1@sunb.ocs.mq.edu.au>`

```
Martin Ellison wrote:
> 
> > > Seriously, WHAT IS a COBOL Clone
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> I suspect that programmers use cloning in C++ as well.

And back at the ranch..... on the topic of "Using copy files" ? From
what Martin says, this is copying taken to an extreme, and then
forgetting what you did !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Semantic Designs Announces COBOL Clone Doctor

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Dr. Klone" <snedunuri@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.software-eng
- **Message-ID:** `<rv4q2bec3i558@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<rv2b8kaa3i520@corp.supernews.com> <37f1942b.89033103@news1.ibm.net> <37F1E7FD.A42583C@zip.com.au> <7ssrp7$20r$1@sunb.ocs.mq.edu.au>`

```
hello, and thanks to everyone that responded to the post. Sorry for not
responding sooner, but I read news at most once a day and I didn't expect
this many responses in such a short time. Martin's reply below is correct.
Clones are essentially repeated idioms that occur redundantly in the code
base. As Martin says, its quite common for developers to cut and paste some
existing code in several places, usually with some modifications. The
CloneDR finds these occurrences of the original idiom despite the
modifications, and automatically constructs a replacement abstraction
(currently a COPY lib, but we're currently working on replacing with called
sections and external procedures). The savings can be huge. We've found up
to 50% redundancy in some real Cobol code bases. And yes it happens in C++
as well though to a slightly lesser extent (more like 15%). Finding the
clones can also reveal maintenance bugs where one clone was patched but the
other occurrences were not. A more detailed explanation is available at our
web site www.semdesigns.com.

And once again, thank you all for your interest (and the DEVO references.
"Whip it good" is indeed our motto when it comes to clones)

-- sn

Martin Ellison <martin@ics.mq.edu.au> wrote in message
news:7ssrp7$20r$1@sunb.ocs.mq.edu.au...
> > > Seriously, WHAT IS a COBOL Clone
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: what should we do? Need a GUI for MF Cobol UNIX based app — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### what should we do? Need a GUI for MF Cobol UNIX based app

- **From:** jason.at.comlinkusa.com
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f12498.18710670@news.remarq.com>`

```
Hi Group,

We have a UNIX based COBOL app that consists of millions of lines of
code.  We are looking into developing a GUI that simply calls the UNIX
objects.   We will probably develop the frontend/screens in
powerbuilder.   Do you have experience and or knowledge on this type
of situation?  If so I have some questions.   I know these are not
really COBOL questions but I thought someone that reads this group
might have some answers.

1.  What should we use to handle the communication between the
frontend and the UNIX based cobol objects?

2.  Is Powerbuilder a good choice or would something like MF's
NetExpress make things much easier?

If you have developed a similar solution I would REALLY appreciate
hearing your input, opinions, and methods used.

Thanks in advance for your time!

Jason Martin
email  jasonatcomlinkusa.com  (replace "at" with @)
```

#### ↳ Re: what should we do? Need a GUI for MF Cobol UNIX based app

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f19477.89108733@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37f12498.18710670@news.remarq.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 1999 20:46:21 GMT, jason.at.comlinkusa.com wrote:

>Hi Group,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

ACK!!!!  GIANT COST SAVINGS ALERT!

Wrong answers.  

You can STAY with your current COBOL and use COBOL sp2 from Flexus
with their Windows Thin client - and get the GUI on the desktop and
leave the programs on Unix.  EASILY!

see:  http://www.flexus.com or e-mail info@flexus.com

I do not work for them.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: what should we do? Need a GUI for MF Cobol UNIX based app

- **From:** JohndeV <johndevNOjoSPAM@yahoo.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1bf56ec8.b61468cc@usw-ex0102-014.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<37f12498.18710670@news.remarq.com> <37f19477.89108733@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Have to love it.

I love Thane's answer, where product X can do MIRACLES and
easily on top of that.

Experience will show you that there are no easy answers
to this type of situations just possibilities.

If you want to look at MF for possibilities you might want
to have a look at NetExpress.  For the GUI side of your
application Dialog System is one possibility as well
as the Web enablement tools that they provide.  If used
in conjunction with their AAI middleware you can
create an interesting, platform independant, client/server
application.

BUT you also have to look at other possibility to construct
your front-end.  Might it be Visual Basic, PowerBuilder or
whatever else you might think of, you have the pick the
set of tools that you will feel comfortable with on the
development side as well as looking ahead for the
maintenance cycles that this application will have to go
through in the future.

I am not saying that Thane's suggestion of using Flexus is
wrong, far from it.  Flexus would not be able to survive as
a company if their products/solutions were inefficient.

I am just saying that the statement is gratuitous at best
and lacks any value.

I suggest that you contact the different vendors such as
Micro Focus(Merant) or Flexus and ask to be put in contact
with customers that have gone through such a project.

Good luck on your project Jason.




* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what should we do? Need a GUI for MF Cobol UNIX based app

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f2dfbf.173930378@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37f12498.18710670@news.remarq.com> <37f19477.89108733@news1.ibm.net> <1bf56ec8.b61468cc@usw-ex0102-014.remarq.com>`

```
On Wed, 29 Sep 1999 05:28:16 -0700, JohndeV
<johndevNOjoSPAM@yahoo.com.invalid> wrote:

>I am not saying that Thane's suggestion of using Flexus is
>wrong, far from it.  Flexus would not be able to survive as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You don't understand Thin Client.  The program runs on the server -
the WHOLE THING - the client is simply a "Gui" processor that takes
the Sp2 calls (screen intefface) and shows them to you on your PC.
I'm telling you that with a DIAL UP internet connection you could be
running a "Windows" application - you could not tell the difference -
and the "host" program is running on the Unix box. (written in MF
COBOL).  

It really is as magic as I said it was.  The ONLY issue is the same as
the issue would be for converting text mode applications to GUI.

If a user was already using the Motif SP2 version, they could go to
thin client in SECONDS.   

I know I sound rabid.  But it really IS that easy.  

Thin client does not mean a slow clunky HTML interface, it does not
mean ASP or JAVA programs.  It's native windows API calls on the
client - with the program running on the server (unix).
```

### Re: Fixed -Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DlXH3.2270$j4.65656@viper>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net> <Yk8C3.125$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37da9191.355700@news.indigo.ie> <YxqC3.15437$ei1.31103@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4KMC3.16029$ei1.31353@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <bRjD3.9247$mo.362609@viper> <ZhmD3.17051$ei1.34393@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <iAFD3.9928$mo.389559@viper> <7rq246$1em$10@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7rra24$poo@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7rsss5$kj0$3@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7ru798$4cn@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The control character used in RECORD SEQUENTIAL files (to quote William
Kline) varies depending on whether you use FBA or FBM format on the output
file. Regardless the control character is the first character of the record.
Both formats have a fixed set of characters to indicate SINGLE, DOUBLE AND
TRIPLE spacing as well as a PAGE BREAK.

The only one I know of the top of my head is FBA or FA:
" " single spaced
"1" double spaced

not sure of the others. And of course this may be incorrect, but there
definitely is a fixed set of characters to determine line spacing and page
breaks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fixed -Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F0CD5F.AE5B755@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<nm6C3.14899$ei1.29469@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7rav46$lgn$4@news.igs.net> <Yk8C3.125$D84.6202@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <37da9191.355700@news.indigo.ie> <YxqC3.15437$ei1.31103@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4KMC3.16029$ei1.31353@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <bRjD3.9247$mo.362609@viper> <ZhmD3.17051$ei1.34393@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <iAFD3.9928$mo.389559@viper> <7rq246$1em$10@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7rra24$poo@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7rsss5$kj0$3@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7ru798$4cn@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <DlXH3.2270$j4.65656@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
> 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


" "  skip one line (standard line feed)
1  Skip to channel 1 (99.9% of the time) always top of page
0  Skip two lines
-  Skip three lines (that's a hyphen)
+  Overstrike (print this line over the last).

You can define 2-9 as additional channels but no-one actually does
this.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fixed -Really wierd problem!!!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F1E395.7264D3CC@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37da9191.355700@news.indigo.ie> <YxqC3.15437$ei1.31103@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4KMC3.16029$ei1.31353@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <bRjD3.9247$mo.362609@viper> <ZhmD3.17051$ei1.34393@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <iAFD3.9928$mo.389559@viper> <7rq246$1em$10@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7rra24$poo@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7rsss5$kj0$3@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <7ru798$4cn@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <DlXH3.2270$j4.65656@viper> <37F0CD5F.AE5B755@zip.com.au> <37f0ed51.48829823@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 29 Sep 1999 Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

As indicated it really is a printer setup issue.  Anyone who does not
set it up to top of form is looking for trouble.

I used to do Xerox DJDE output (a sort of extended FBA format) and the
print groups are positioned all over the page and each is addressed by
a skip to channel 1.  Image what would happen if you had a blank
address and you suppressed the skip code, the invoice detail suddenly
appears in the address box (yep had that happen).

If you want to be bored out of your mind I can go on, and on, and
on...
Ken
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ Availability (the saga continues) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ Availability (the saga continues)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sr8es$1fe@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I just received the "attached" note from my "friends" at MindSpring. So far,
I have left my bandwidth as "expanded" - because my upgrade from Word97 to
Word2000 means that the HTML (as Huib predicted) as DOUBLED in size. (So
far, I haven't put this out on the web yet - I'm still considering
alternatives on this.)

Bottom-line is that the FAQ is available and hopefully will REMAIN
available.  Depending on when/if I post the Word2000 version, this MAY
impact my "bandwidth usage" in October.

   ***
MindSpring note follows:

Dear Customer,

As you are probably aware, the web bandwidth statistics for the month of
September were incorrectly measured for your web site.  The unfortunate
result was your site being made unavailable because the monthly bandwidth
limit was "exceeded."  The problem manifested itself by erroneously
accounting for the amount of bandwidth used for the 12th through the 21st
of September.

We discovered the root of the problem when one of your fellow customers
posted a very detailed explanation of what he was observing in the
<netcom.general> newsgroup.  Please understand that MindSpring recognizes
that this is the worst possible way for us to identify a service problem!

We have now regenerated all web statistics for Netcom web hosting accounts
for the month of September.  As we turn your web page back on for this
month, we will credit your account for one month's service.  If you
increased your account's bandwidth allocation because of this problem and
you don't feel your page will need the extra bandwidth, please call our
Customer Service department (1-800-719-4660) and they will reverse the
change and any affiliated charges.  Before deciding to do this, please
check your web stats at
<https://secure.mindspring.net/acct-mgmt/webstats.html> to verify how much
bandwidth you actually have used this month.

We apologize for any and all of the trouble this has caused.  We here at
MindSpring want your experiences with us to be positive.  If there is
anything that you would like to see us do better, let us know.

Sincerely,

Jon Rietmulder
VP Network Services
MindSpring Enterprises, Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL FAQ Availability (the saga continues)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F1E746.2835DD67@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7sr8es$1fe@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I have left my bandwidth as "expanded" - because my upgrade from
> Word97 to Word2000 means that the HTML (as Huib predicted) as DOUBLED
> in size. (So far, I haven't put this out on the web yet - I'm still
> considering alternatives on this.)


It should be easy to strip the trash from this HTML by a program.

Cut 1:

Seek &NBSP  and replace by a single space.  In most cases you do not
want the extra space anyway.  Replace all space runs with a single
space.

Cut 2:

Remove all the extra commands other than the basic set is (assume the
/ version as well):

<HTML>,  <BODY>,  <P>,  <TABLE>  and the table stuff, <TITLE> <HEAD>,
<HR>, <EM>, <A>, <LI> <UL> <OL>, <DL> <DT> <DD>, etc.

We should write a public domain HTML stripper.
Ken
```

<a id="t-4dc39def28"></a>
## Thread: Semantic Designs Announces COBOL CloneDR — 1 msgs

### Semantic Designs Announces COBOL CloneDR

- **From:** "Dr. Klone" <snedunuri@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rv2bbmk3i588@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Semantic Designs announces the availability of COBOL CloneDR -
enterprise-class software for the automatic detection and removal of COBOL
clones. Designed to handle large-scale applications (up to1 million lines of
code), the CloneDR helps organizations reduce maintenance costs, increase
programmer productivity, and raise reliability and availability.


Feature Set
--------------
Automatic Clone Detection AND REMOVAL

UNICODE support

Large-scale application support

SMP support for true parallel processing

Full-program processing support:
 Preprocessor Directives
 Data Declarations
 Procedural Code
 Comment Lines

Windows NT Runtime Environment

Evaluation Copy Availability
----------------------------
An Early Adopter Program (EAP) version of the CloneDR that detects clones
and provides an informative report is available at
 www.semdesigns.com/Products

(Note: CloneDR  Requires a Pentium class machine with at least 128M memory
running Windows NT 4.0)

Further Information can be obtained from the author or www.semdesigns.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ "Static" Reserved Words (was Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sq596$62k@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698512B57@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <7spp27$1tc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7spp27$1tc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl> wrote:
> :>
   <snip>
>
> : Check your harddisk for a table with COBOL reserved words that came
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Well, with IBM's mainframe compiler they give you a utility to translate the
reserved words into other languages (as synonyms) and "recompile" the table
to a new "static load module".  (Check out the WORD compiler option.)  There
are 2 interesting problems with this:

A) They don't have the utility for their Workstation products (so if you use
it on the mainframe, you must develop/maintain your code there too)
B) (Historically) they have been AWFUL about providing "migration" tools for
taking the "old" source (much less object) code and upgrading it - when new
reserved words ARE added.

    ***
Micro Focus (OTOH) does allow you to create synonyms (ADDRSRV directive) "on
the fly" (not in a file - unless you COPY it in) or to use "vocabularies" -
which also mean that YOU have to maintain them, instead of them.
```

```
vocabs are nice, but can really slow down the compile process if they
contain long copybooks such as "windows.cpy"...i used them all over, then
decided to get rid of them because of the extremely long compile times...

If there was a way to include the copybook only once, rather than in every
verb, then it would be OK...

kenmullins
```

<a id="t-7f46d4b4d5"></a>
## Thread: WINAPI32 Call under Microfocus — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### WINAPI32 Call under Microfocus

- **From:** "Robert Sellers" <rsellers@esiusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sr2rd$cte$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
While creating a mini-server,  I'm attempting to call a windows api call for
CreateMailslot.   The error code returned in the handle is
Error_Invalid_Data.   I've tried about everything I can think of.   I have a
feeling that it's probably tied to the ws-ms-secattributes, but am not sure.
Compiles clean, runs, but returns a -1 in the ws-ms-handle and the extended
error code is a 13, error_invalid_data.

Any assistance here would be appreciated.

Code follows.

Working Storage

    01  ws-ms-name  pic x(72).
    01  ws-ms-maxmsgsize  pic S9(9) comp-5 value 0.
    01  ws-ms-readtimeout pic S9(9) comp-5 value  0.
    01  ws-ms-secattributes.
         05  ws-sec-att-Length pic S9(9) comp-5.
         05  ws-sec-descriptor pic S9(9) comp-5.
         05  ws-sec-bhandle    pic S9(9) comp-5.
    01  ws-ms-handle  pic S9(9) comp-5.

Procedure Division

   string "\\.\mailslot\monitor"
      delimited by size into ws-ms-name.

    call winapi32 "CreateMailslotA" using
        by reference ws-ms-name
        by value ws-ms-maxmsgsize
        by value ws-ms-readtimeout
        by reference ws-ms-secattributes
        giving ws-ms-handle
    end-call

    if ws-ms-handle = -1
       call winapi32 "GetLastError"
            giving ws-lasterror
       end-call
    end-if
    .
Robert Sellers
```

#### ↳ Re: WINAPI32 Call under Microfocus

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7srhbr$bl8$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7sr2rd$cte$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
by reference ws-ms-name needs to point to a null terminated string...

might be better off just to use by reference z'\\.\mailslot\monitor' in the
call it'self instead of the ws field...or insure the ws field is null
terminated...

kenmullins
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: LOGIC ERROR

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f10c9c.3709664@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990928011906.21641.00002478@ng-bd1.aol.com> <37F05332.1ED6C00B@home.com>`

```
WlknB wrote:
>> 
>> HI, I AM A NEW COBOL STUDENT AND I AM HAVING A PROBLEM WITH MY DATA FILE.  WHEN
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>  Bew
Well, you do not provide very much input data, but I have had time where I had to be
content with even less.

The one clue that you give is that 

> ONLY HALF (LIKE EVERY OTHER ONE) OF THE DATA IS SENT TO THE OUTPUT

My guess (and I could be way off here

Your program reads Data from SYSIN using ACCEPT

Your program WRITES the ACCEPTed data to some output

If the declaration of the input record is larger than the implied record size for the
accept statement, COBOL will read a second record from input to fill the rest of the
record buffer
Obviously, you will never "see" this record, and thus loose half of your data

     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         From an actual insurance claim: I have romantic fever.
```

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00

```
In article <7sqe15$kif@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:

>
>Does the Unisys product run "in WINDOWS 98" which is the environment
>mentioned by the original post?  My guess is that we still don't know what
>compiler the original "requester" was asking about.
>

Well, that all depends on how you want to look at it.
The low end Unisys A-Series machine , as I remember it, WAS 
a 386PC with a special card using parallel processors.
It booted in DOS then kicked over to the Unisys MCP running off
 the parallel processors.
This is sort of like the IBM S/36 that cranked up a 386PC and then 
started whater OS IBM AS400 pre-decessors used.  Although the 
S/36 I worked on had an UGLY alliance of external expansion cabinets
to house the extra storage and communication controllers.
```

<a id="t-e97df562b1"></a>
## Thread: re-entrant MF Cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### re-entrant MF Cobol

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqn6f$ad8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Subject: re-entrant MF Cobol

Back to the well one more time �

I have a pgm linked as a DLL, being called by Powerbuilder, VisualBasic
6; and other cobol pgms compiled with Net Express.  My DLL is compiled
and linked under MF Cobol 4.0.07.  Initial tests went well, but now the
VB programmers are coming claiming the DLL is not re-entrant.  If they
push it hard enough by wrapping it into a VB COM object, then calling
that object multiple times (not unreasonable) their demo app returns
�error 7 � out of memory.�  A cobol �error 7� is �disk full� and I�m
not writing  (or reading) any files, so we don�t believe it is coming
from the cobol RTS.  However, they (and thus, our boss) is inclined to
believe it has to be the cobol pgm at fault.  So I�m tasked with making
the pgm re-entrant.

Here is a skeleton of the pgm:

$ set ans85
$ set noosvs
$ set mf
$ set defaultbyte"00"
* set litlink
$ set case
$ set deffile
$ set deffiletype"win"
 identification division.
 program-id. mycbldll.
 environment division.
 configuration section.
 special-names.
     call-convention 74 is apientry.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 linkage section.
 01  lk-parm-1                     pic x(64).
 01  lk-parm-2                       pic x(64).
 01  lk-parm-3                         pic x(64).
 01  lk-parm-4                      pic x(64).
 procedure division.
     exit program.

*MyEntrySection section.
 entry "MYENTRY" using by reference
                          lk-parm-1
                          lk-parm-2
                          lk-parm-3
                          lk-parm-4.
* do a bunch of stuff, modifying the linkage section fields
     exit program

*======== pgm end ============

A seach of this NG on �re-entrant� got a couple of hits.  Suggestion
there is to put all data in Linkage, one big block to account for all
of the current WS (in addition to the parms being passed by the caller)
but I�ve gotten a bit rusty and don�t remember how to set up the
acquiring of memory and addressing. I know it�s not the same as when I
used to do something similar in CICS pgms.  Other suggestion seemed to
be to declare data �local.�  I�ve seen reference to that in the manual,
but it is all new territory for me.

All suggestions appreciated, including how to put the ball back in the
VB court!
```

#### ↳ Re: re-entrant MF Cobol

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sqt87$jm9$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7sqn6f$ad8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com> wrote:
>My DLL is compiled
>and linked under MF Cobol 4.0.07.  Initial tests went well, but now the
>VB programmers are coming claiming the DLL is not re-entrant.

[Snip]

Try adding a local-storage section - this indicates to the compiler that 
the program is expected to be re-entrant, and so provision is made. 

From memory, the section can be empty but you should put any 
working-storage items in there that need to be unique each time the 
program is (re-)entered.

Steve.
```

---

<a id="t-5ce3fb8d49"></a>
## Thread: Acuserver on Linux — 3 msgs

### Acuserver on Linux

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f0f270$0$6415@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
I recently installed Acuserver 4.2.0 for Linux ELF RedHat 5-Intel
on our RedHat 5.2 Server.
We used to have Acuserver 4.2.0 successfuly running on SCO 5.05.
There were no problems on the SCO box.

I used the same a_srvcfg file on the Linux box as I used on the SCO box.
This is because I anticipate opening more than 32 files at once and SCO's
Acuserver configuration successfully catered for that.

I can see Acuserver running on the Linux box and it does allow a connection
however as soon as a second user attempts to connect, Acuserver sends
an error to the server console announcing ' /etc/Acuserver  Too many files
opened'
On the client side, a 94.10 (74 status code) appears.
This indicates  Too many files open by the current process. (open)
Each user only opens 9 files.

My MAX-FILES in a_srvcfg is set to 127, way greater than the default 32.
Yet this problem still occurs.

I suspect that my Linux kernel has a restriction on the number of files
that each process can open, perhaps a security feature....

Does anyone have Linux/Acuserver experience/suggestions.

_________________________
Robert Annandale

I have never let my schooling interfere with my education - Mark Twain.
```

#### ↳ Re: Acuserver on Linux

- **From:** "Pete Withey" <peterw@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sr2mb$a7m$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37f0f270$0$6415@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Robert:

  One thing rings a bell here. The default for MAX_FILES is 32 unless
changed.  Try running acuserver with a trace and make sure that the a_srvcfg
file is being picked up.  You do this by starting acuserver:

acuserve -start -t3 -l -e errfl

And then reproducing the behavior and looking at "errfl" (any name will do)
If it is getting the correct config file then check the
max_files_per_process kernal tuning parameter (if Linux has one).  I don't
know the specific name for it nor do I know for sure if Linux has it but
most UNIX's seem to.  Hope this is helpful.  If not give us a call at
1-800-399-7220.

Peter Withey
Sr. Technical Support Analyst
Acucorp Technical Support

Robert Annandale <rob_a@unipharm.com> wrote in message
news:37f0f270$0$6415@fountain.mindlink.net...
> I recently installed Acuserver 4.2.0 for Linux ELF RedHat 5-Intel
> on our RedHat 5.2 Server.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I can see Acuserver running on the Linux box and it does allow a
connection
> however as soon as a second user attempts to connect, Acuserver sends
> an error to the server console announcing ' /etc/Acuserver  Too many files
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-2b174b5585"></a>
## Thread: Platform "independency" and efficiency — 1 msgs

### Platform "independency" and efficiency

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sq8jn$1pg@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Obviously, this is a follow up on several recent and ongoing threads - but
I thought it was a topic that deserves its own thread.)

One thing that I would suggest that PAYING customers do, is to press their
vendors to improve the run-time efficiency of code created with a vendor's
compilers when "Standard conforming directives/options" are selected.

I know this best from both Micro Focus and IBM (mainframe) compilers - but I
really think that this is true of many, if not most, compilers.

The developers just don't think that there is "user demand" to spend their
time optimizing performance for programs when "Standard conformance" is
selected.  Instead, they assume that all their customers who are interested
in "efficiency" will always pick the full "optimized for this
platform/compiler" set of options - and those interested in cross-platform
independence, don't worry about performance on any specific platform.

Now, I think it is (probably) true that no vendor is ever going to be able
to get MORE efficient (or possibly even as efficient) code when "Standard
conformance" is selected (for example, just think about what the COBOL
"truncation" rules require for USAGE binary items) but I really think that
at LOT of improvements could be made in this area for all the compilers that
I know of.

Therefore, if you are interested in "cross-platform independence" but you
still want the compiler to produce as efficient code as possible, I think
that this is something that you need to communicate (probably fairly
VOCALLY) to your vendors - as I don't think they are hearing it today.

As usual, this is one of those areas that lots of people ASSUME someone else
is telling the vendors (or that they already know).  However, I really do
think that COBOL compiler vendors do listen to those customers who TELL them
what they want (especially what they are willing to pay for).
```

---

<a id="t-371b268960"></a>
## Thread: Another mf cobol color display question...sigh (but interesting!) — 6 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Another mf cobol color display question...sigh (but interesting!)

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asBI3.732$QP5.4775@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

Thanks to the help of some folks on this newsgroup (Jimmy and Gael), I've
gotten my DISPLAY commands to show up in color.  Now I have a strange thing
happening - on a small test program I wrote just to play with this feature,
I have these commands:
................
PERFORM UNTIL SW-END IS EQUAL TO "N" OR "n"
       DISPLAY "THIS IS A TEST PROGRAM"
           WITH BLANK SCREEN
                FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 1
                AT 0117
       DISPLAY "DO YOU WISH TO CONTINUE?"
           WITH FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 3
           AT 1010
      .........

Now, when I **do** exit this program, from now on my DOS screen and commands
(actually, the response y or n, too) are the first color I specified, red in
this case.  As a variation, right after the perform I had a blank
line(display " ") then the "with blank screen" after that, **then** the
other two display lines.  When I exited, the DOS was normal old white on
black.

I'd appreciate any insight, if someone has the time.  I know this isn't very
important, but these little things drive me (somewhat pleasantly, I must
admit) nuts.  I'm in my 2nd year of a 2 year programming degree, and just a
couple weeks into cobol.

Thanks again!
Pat
```

#### ↳ Re: Another mf cobol color display question...sigh (but interesting!)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZKJI3.9659$K33.73041@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<asBI3.732$QP5.4775@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
The reason for the behavior is that the DOS (actually BIOS) screen I/O
routines (without ANSI.SYS) do not support color.  Each character on a
text display is represented in the video adapter RAM by two bytes.  The
first byte is the character, the second byte is the attribute byte.
When the screen is cleared, all the attribute bytes are set to white on
black.  When the screen is scrolled up, the attribute bytes on the last
row are not cleared, leaving the colors for the next text displayed
there.  Once you write some other color in line 25, from that point
on until you do a clear screen, every line will scroll up with the
same color attributes.  The reason you got subsequent lines in the
first color is because that happened to be the last color written to
last line on the screen.  What I usually do when I exit a program is
to blank the screen with the default white on black.
```

#### ↳ Re: Another mf cobol color display question...sigh (but interesting!)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t06h8$3gt$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<asBI3.732$QP5.4775@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
The problem is that DOS uses the attributes that are currently on the screen
when it runs in TTY mode.  If you blank the screen with the appropriate
colour before you exit, DOS will retain it.  So to get back to white on
black, do something like

       DISPLAY space
           WITH BLANK SCREEN
                FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 7
                BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 0
                AT 0000.

(I might have those reversed).

Pat wrote in message ...

>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

commands
>(actually, the response y or n, too) are the first color I specified, red
in

>line(display " ") then the "with blank screen" after that, **then** the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'd appreciate any insight, if someone has the time.  I know this isn't
very
>important, but these little things drive me (somewhat pleasantly, I must
>admit) nuts.  I'm in my 2nd year of a 2 year programming degree, and just a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

#### ↳ Re: Another mf cobol color display question...sigh (but interesting!)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F2FB2D.A299C9EC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<asBI3.732$QP5.4775@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Pat wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pat

You're a persistent bugger aren't you - but you ask so nicely. Boy, did
I have to scratch the grey-matter to even figure this. Not saying this
is picture perfect, but it works :-


       Program-id. scrntst.

       Working-storage section.
       01 FlowFlag                 pic 9.
          88 ContinueProgram       value 0.
          88 CancelProgram         value 1.
       01 answer                   pic x.
          88 noLower               value "n".
          88 noUpper               value "N".

       Procedure Division.
         set ContinueProgram to true
         perform until CancelProgram
            display "This is a test Program"
                     at 0117 with
                     Blank Screen foreground-color 1
            display "Do you wish to continue ? Y/N"
                     at 1010 with foreground-color 3
            accept answer at 1050 with prompt auto

            if   Nolower or NoUpper
                 set CancelProgram to true
            End-if
       End-perform

       STOP RUN.
```

```
I don't think other editors will interact with the debugger. But, if
all you want is a 'decent' editor, be sure to get one that:

1. Requires uniformly ascending line number in columns 1-6,
2. Won't allow code in 72-80, and
3. Discards everything past column 80.

It would also be nice if the editor would punch out cards.

Making paper tape would be a plus.
```

<a id="t-6b887d5a28"></a>
## Thread: Connecting to a web server — 5 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Connecting to a web server

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf0af6$9ccd5c40$5e14063e@default>`

```
Has anyone ever coded a solution which would download
a file from a web page?.

For example, enduser clicks on, Help on the
drop down menu then chooses, Update Database
and the Cobol program connects to the webserver
using a pre-defined address, and downloads it to
a pre-defined location.

any ideas most welcome.

Simon R Hart
Eaton.
```

#### ↳ Re: Connecting to a web server

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<muuI3.2740$E7.71608@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<01bf0af6$9ccd5c40$5e14063e@default>`

```

Simon R Hart <hart1@technologist.com> wrote in message
news:01bf0af6$9ccd5c40$5e14063e@default...
> Has anyone ever coded a solution which would download
> a file from a web page?.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Eaton.

Here's some pseudocode that might help.

    parse the address (http://hostname/path) into it's component parts
    resolve the hostname to an IP address using gethostbyname()

    set up a request buffer
        string "GET " delimited size path delimited space x"0d0a" delimited
size into buffer
    send a TCPIP request (port=80)
    get the response

Some API functions that are required for TCP/IP communications:
        socket()    % create a socket
        connect()
        send()
        do recv() until all the document is received
         closesocket()    % destroy socket

I'm sure your favorite software vendor has a product that will do this. I
know my company ships the code to do this in their sample directory. Sorry,
I can't post it without violating copyright restrictions.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7sum44$v33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<01bf0af6$9ccd5c40$5e14063e@default>`

```
Simon R Hart <hart1@technologist.com> wrote:
: Has anyone ever coded a solution which would download
: a file from a web page?.

That's what ftp is for.  If the site is running an ftp
server (as well as the http server) it should be able
to process ftp requests.

Win95 has an ftp client that is command line driven
so issueing an ftp command from Cobol should work.
```

```
Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> writes:
> Actually, the B*5500* supported virtual storage long before the 6700.  I
> won't argue with your chronology, but my recollection is that Burroughs
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Definitely ahead of their time. 

I think that the B5000 was the first in that series, Don't know whether
it had virtual storage.  I think that this system cam out around
1961.

> Gilbert Saint-flour wrote:
> > >The Burroughs big mainframe of the banking world - back in 1972
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Gilbert Saint-flour
```

#### ↳ Re: Virtual Storage

- **From:** "Shmuel (Seymour J.) Metz" <smetz@nsf.gov>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F27F6B.5DCAEA94@nsf.gov>`
- **References:** `<3FmI3.28888$ei1.51924@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
robin wrote:

> I think that the B5000 was the first in that series, Don't know whether
> it had virtual storage.  I think that this system cam out around
> 1961.

Yes and yes; the B5500 was a minor upgrade of the B5000, and both
supported segmented memory; they key word to understanding the B5000 and
B6500 families is "descriptor".
```

```
is digital, i.e. either On or Off.
```

## Thread: Is COBOL more powerful... — 4 this month / 5 total

### Is COBOL more powerful...

- **From:** "James Richardson" <james@richyme.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi, I'm a C++ programmer from England. I was wondering what the advantages
COBOL offered over other languages and what it's main implementation was in
the software world. Could you tell me?
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL more powerful...

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aAsI3.127$SS2.4579@dfw-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
In article <7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>,
James Richardson <james@richyme.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
>Hi, I'm a C++ programmer from England. I was wondering what the advantages
>COBOL offered over other languages and what it's main implementation was in
>the software world. Could you tell me?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL more powerful...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F25E7C.50ED4DE5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
James Richardson wrote:
> 
> Hi, I'm a C++ programmer from England. I was wondering what the advantages
> COBOL offered over other languages and what it's main implementation was in
> the software world. Could you tell me?

Does this help you at all :-

"Currently there are over 3 million COBOL programmers in the world.
According to the Gartner Group, COBOL programmers represent 80% of all
programmers in the world. COBOL is taught in many business information
systems departments, thus continuing to produce COBOL programmers ( +++
as we know from this NewsGroup - lots of institutions are giving up on
COBOL - they want to teach the new fads - continuation of quote +++).
A recent study reports that COBOL programming continues to be a highly
demanded job skill - higher than any other language except 'C'. A
different survey indicated that over 70% of all entry-level programming
positions value knowledge of COBOL.

COBOL is the dominant programming language of the corporate world, with
over 80% of ALL code written in COBOL. For the existing base of
software, estimates range from 70 billion lines of COBOL code to over
200 billion. Although the estimates vary, it is fair to say that there
are billions of lines of COBOL code."

 E.Reed Doke & Bill C. Hardgrave "An Introduction to Object COBOL" 1998	

from one James to another - Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL more powerful...

- **From:** "Einar Clementz" <eclementz@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g9GI3.4250$cPf.193455104@news.telia.no>`
- **References:** `<7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

Hi.

Yes, there are many good things in COBOL. As You see in another posting,
there are a tremedous amout of programs out there giving You services every
day, bank, insurance, utilities, hospitals etc.

On the mainframe area and in transcations systems there are more than 60
billion transactions running every day and most transacations runs in COBOL,
a large figure. How many hits are there on the internet every day?

COBOL is very strong in file handling and record processing.
COBOL is easy to learn and use.
It is easy to produce readable programs, hence the programs are
maintainable, which is a very important asset to a language.
There are a rich set of statements to use, all part of the COBOL standard,
hence COBOL is very portable.


Regards, Einar Clementz, Oslo, Norway

-------------------

news:7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk...
```

```
Richard;

When I first saw MF on a unix box and had a similar question about some
function/implementation/extension, it took me quite a while to 1) find some
sort of documentation; 2) understand that meager, poorly written and
organized information; and 3) figure out how to get past the compile errors
to test and see what happens.

I did find out that some testing of some functions of MF on an HPUnix box
can cause the kernel to crash - or at least that's what the SysAdmin guys
were trying to get me to stop doing.

I was not COBOL ignorant but could be called that for a while, I came from a
VSE shop where I was the entire Assembler and half the COBOL programming
staff.  At this new job I was beside myself to discover that there was a
thing called inline performs.  It was almost 6 months before I discovered
reference modification.

In short, I'd say that if the originator and his company were as bad off as
you portray, it's pretty much their problem, but the situation as it is
might not be the one you see.

Remember, God has a sense of humor....grin....

Bill
```

<a id="t-2ea87b6c0c"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus DOS zero CPU overhead under WiN — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus DOS zero CPU overhead under WiN

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I need a way of releasing execution of my DOS applications under the Windows
based op.sys's
(including CITRIX). I'm using Micro Focus Workbench 2,4 & don't wish to
upgrade as newer versions have much larger execution overheads. Several of
my routines operate in the following way;

PERFORM Get-System-Time

PERFORM UNTIL 88-exit-true

    PERFORM Check-For-Key-Press

    IF  NOT keyboard-empty
       DO SOMETHING
    ELSE
       PERFORM Get-System-Time
    END-IF

END-PERFORM

Ideally I would like to CALL a .bin file which would wait for 0.5 sec's with
ZERO CPU overhead whilst returning the system time.

Any Idea's ? alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus DOS zero CPU overhead under WiN

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t4e31$rii$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
    If you use NT 4.0, you probably need do nothing.  It seems to be good at
squeezing dos programs down - I have written small test programs, and my
400MHz NT does fewer loops than my Win98 120MHz Pc in a tight loop checking
the time (without checking the keyboard).  Win98 is fairly good at choking a
program that sits in a tight loop on the keyboard if you set propertys
right.

    It is not so good at squeezing programs that loop on an accept T from
time.
I suppose that ignoreing the clock until a keystroke arrives would work.

    For win 3.1/95/98, either use Leif's suggestion (great job Leif!), of
try TAME out.  TAME seems to be a relic from DESQview, and has not been
changed in a while, but is highly tuneable and might work.  Of course, since
you have the source code ...

    Do NOT call the BBS if you have the number.  Some poor soul now has that
number.  I wonder how many 3AM calls he gets now.  It (TAME) can be found
at www.mindspring.com/~dgthomas/tame.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus DOS zero CPU overhead under WiN

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f3596f_2@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

Alister Munro <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk...
> I need a way of releasing execution of my DOS applications under the
Windows
> based op.sys's
> (including CITRIX). I'm using Micro Focus Workbench 2,4 & don't wish to
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ideally I would like to CALL a .bin file which would wait for 0.5 sec's
with
> ZERO CPU overhead whilst returning the system time.
>

The following does what you want. It is written for Realia that has a
different parameter convention from MF, but can easily be converted
to MF (ten minutes work). Then you can assemble the result and
you get an .obj file which you can link with your programs.

If this does it for you, I could make the conversion and post the obj
file. There might also be other people out there in the NG, can
would do this.

here goes:

TITLE   WAITMSEC
PAGE    55,132
.MODEL  SMALL
.486 ; .386 OK too

;-----------------------------------------------------.
;           Author:     Leif Svalgaard                :
;                                                     :
;           written:    95/11/22                      :
;           revised:    96/12/19                      :
;-----------------------------------------------------:
;           Cobol:      REALIA COBOL 2.x/3.x/4.x      :
;-----------------------------------------------------'

; structure of arguments

OPTION      PROC: PRIVATE, NOSCOPED
OPTION      OLDSTRUCTS
PARAM1      STRUCT
TASK_ID     DB  4 DUP(?)     ; task id  : used for debug purpose
OP          DB        ?      ; operation: (W)ait millisecs
KEY         DB        ?      ; key value: NUL if no key struck
MSECLO      DW        ?      ; wait time: wait this many milliseconds
MSECHI      DW        ?      ;   32 bit
PARAM1      ENDS             ;

WAITMSEC$ASM SEGMENT
            ASSUME      CS:WAITMSEC$ASM, DS:WAITMSEC$ASM
            PUBLIC      WAITMSEC
WAITMSEC    PROC        FAR
            JMP         BEGIN_PROG

STOP_MSECLO DW        0      ;
STOP_MSECHI DW        0      ;

; ES:[DI] points to param1

BEGIN_PROG:                              ; begin
            MOV         AL,ES:[DI].OP    ;   AL = operation
            CMP         AL,"W"           ;   if operation = W goto Wait
            JE          WAIT_OP          ;

NUL_RETURN:                              ; NUL Return:
            MOV         AL,00h           ;   key value = NUL
            JMP         RETURN           ;   return

; ----------------------------------------

WAIT_OP:                                 ; Wait:
            CALL        GET_MSEC         ;   get milliseconds now
            ADD         AX,ES:[DI].MSECLO;
            ADC         DX,ES:[DI].MSECHI;   add msecs_to_wait
            MOV         STOP_MSECLO,AX   ;   giving when to stop
            MOV         STOP_MSECHI,DX   ;
IDLE_LOOP:
            MOV         AH,0Bh           ;   DOS: interrupt check KBD status
            INT         21h              ;        also enables CTL+BREAK
            CMP         AL,0FFh          ;        if key struck
            JE          KEY_STRUCK       ;            goto Key Struck

            MOV         AH,86h           ;
            MOV         CX,0             ;   For OS/2:
            MOV         DX,5000          ;        BIOS: wait 5 msec
            INT         15h              ;

            MOV         AX,1680h         ;   For Windows:
            INT         2Fh              ;        Windows idle call

            CALL        GET_MSEC         ;   get millisecs
            SUB         AX,STOP_MSECLO   ;   compare to stop time
            SBB         DX,STOP_MSECHI   ;   if isn't there yet
            JS          IDLE_LOOP        ;       keep looping

            JMP         NUL_RETURN       ;   return NUL

KEY_STRUCK:                              ; Key Struck:
            MOV         AH,08h           ;   DOS: interrupt(Get key no echo)
            INT         21h              ;
            CMP         AL,00h           ;        if key = 00
            JNE         RETURN           ;
EXTENDED_KEY:                            ;            Extended key:
            MOV         AH,08h           ;            get key again
            INT         21h              ;            add 128 to key value
            ADD         AL,128           ;

RETURN:     MOV         ES:[DI].KEY,AL   ;    set key
            RET                          ;    return

GET_MSEC    PROC NEAR                    ; Get Millisecs:
            MOV         AH,2Ch           ;   DOS: interrupt(Get time)
            INT         21h              ;
            PUSH        DX               ;   save secs, hundredths
            MOV         BL,60            ;
            MOV         AL,CH            ;
            MUL         BL               ;   (CH) hours * 60
            ADD         AL,CL            ;              + (CL) mins
            ADC         AH,0             ;   AX = mins (hours,mins)
            MOV         BX,60000         ;
            MUL         BX               ;   DX,AX = millisecs (hours,mins)
            MOV         CX,AX            ;
            MOV         SI,DX            ;   SI,CX = millisecs (hours,mins)

            POP         DX               ;   get secs, hundredths
            MOV         BL,100           ;
            MOV         AL,DH            ;
            MUL         BL               ;   (DH) secs * 100
            ADD         AL,DL            ;             + (DL) hundredths
            ADC         AH,0             ;   AX = hundredths (secs,hund's)
            MOV         BX,10            ;
            MUL         BX               ;   DX,AX = millisecs (secs,hund's)
            ADD         AX,CX            ;
            ADC         DX,SI            ;   DX,AX = millisecs (total)
            RET                          ;
GET_MSEC    ENDP

WAITMSEC    ENDP
WAITMSEC$ASM ENDS
            END;
```

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>

```
Ken,

Alister's app is a 16-bit DOS program written using V2.4. Therefore it
cannot access 32-bit Windows APIs unfortunately. On the 32-bit Micro Focus
products the calls I referred to in my previous post call Sleep, so it
should not be neccesary to call the API directly unless you want an
application to sleep for a specific amout of time.

Ken Mullins wrote in message <7svqtn$69t$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>...
>Use the windows API 'Sleep'...
>
>kenmullins
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus DOS zero CPU overhead under WiN

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t0f3c$gvt$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk> <7svqtn$69t$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken Mullins <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote:
: Use the windows API 'Sleep'...

That's OK for Windows API programs, but not for DOS applications.

: kenmullins

: Alister Munro <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
: news:7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk...
:> I need a way of releasing execution of my DOS applications under the
: Windows
:> based op.sys's
:> (including CITRIX). I'm using Micro Focus Workbench 2,4 & don't wish to
:> upgrade as newer versions have much larger execution overheads. Several of

You need to use the DOS/Windows MultiPlex interrupt 0x2F with
AX set to 0x1680.  This does not do a sleep but a despatch which
will release the current time-slice.

Use CALL X"84" with various flags and settings to do this.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7svan4$6nr$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7suoq2$q11$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

Have you tried calling CBL_YIELD_RUN_UNIT, or if that is not implemented in

_COYIELD ?
```

<a id="t-6970ff4962"></a>
## Thread: DB-Frontends in COBOL instead of VBA? — 4 this month / 6 total

### DB-Frontends in COBOL instead of VBA?

- **From:** André Wender <02018290-2365@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F35D71.BED65C36@t-online.de>`

```
Howdy folks,

we have different Access 2.0 (on Win 3.11 Systems) and Access 97 (on NT
4.0 WS) databases (with up to 70 tables).
For those DBs we've developed frontends using Access-Basic / VBA. The
databases mentioned before
are no standalone-DBs, all are used in different networks. We're actual
transforming all the ACC 2.0 stuff
to ACC 97 on Win-NT-machines.
This only for information.
I've learned COBOL 14 years ago on Siemens Mainframes under BS 2000, but
since this time I've never touched
a COBOL-compiler again. So I'll be able to say "yep, this is a
COBOL-Program", but I would not be able to
write a program in COBOL, will say that I would have to learn it again
from the beginning.
Yesterday I searched the net and was very surprised that there are
different COBOL-Compilers like
the one from Fujitsu which are designed for WINDOWS-Platforms (NT, 98).
A COBOL-Compiler running under Win-NT with 
a GUI like VB / VC++... unbelieveable, I thought that COBOL today is
only used on mainframes.
Well, what I'm interested in now is your opinion 'bout my idea to start
again with learning COBOL and then
leave this VBA / VB stuff and develop our frontends using COBOL. By the
way: those frontends are used for
employee-administration etc., lots of reports...., we have no
bound-forms/controls etc., we've done all this with VBA.
IMHO COBOL was / is the language for business-apps, but will it make
sense to use this language on PCs under NT instead
stuff like VB, VBA, Delphi?

Andrï¿½
```

#### ↳ Re: DB-Frontends in COBOL instead of VBA?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f371f9@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`
- **References:** `<37F35D71.BED65C36@t-online.de>`

```
Andre,

COBOL is an excellent language for the purposes you describe.

I am mailing to you separately some software which will enable you to
extract COBOL definitions off your ACCESS tables, so you can include them in
COBOL programs which use embedded SQL to access the databases. This is an
approach which we have used in my company with great success.

There's nothing wrong with VB, but COBOL is often more easily maintained,
easier to describe business logic with, and second to none when it comes to
file/DB manipulation.

Along with the software is a full description (with sample code) of Why,
Where, and How to use COBOL for accessing RDBs

Good Luck, and if you need any help write me privately:
pete_dashwood@compuserve.com

Pete.
```

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>

- **Message-ID:** `<7svt86$pgc$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Andr� Wender <02018290-2365@t-online.de> wrote in message
<37F35D71.BED65C36@t-online.de>...
>Howdy folks,
>the one from Fujitsu which are designed for WINDOWS-Platforms (NT, 98).
>A COBOL-Compiler running under Win-NT with
>a GUI like VB / VC++... unbelieveable, I thought that COBOL today is

Acucobol has a complete IDE too. Check www.acucorp.com

>Well, what I'm interested in now is your opinion 'bout my idea to start
>again with learning COBOL and then
>sense to use this language on PCs under NT instead
>stuff like VB, VBA, Delphi?

With experience from both Cobol, Delphi and Visual C++, I would say: Yes.

All languages has their pros and cons, but, as stated out in many fine
contributions to this NG, when it comes to business logic, nothing beats
Cobol.

One issue is the built in support for numeric presentation, another one is
the built in database and finally, in my opinion, one of the best things
about Cobol: The readability.

Of course, there are things that are easier done using Delphi and VC++,
however, how likely is it that you will need them? And if you need them,
todays Cobol is open and willing to connect to other languages. My Cobol
knowledge is to Acucobol, but, from what I have understood in this NG, both
MF Cobol an Fujitsu Cobol are open to speak with other languages as well.

An other interesting phenomena here, is that those things that are hard to
accomplish using Cobol, most often are just a minor part of your development
project. While the tasks that make the most of your project are
significantly easier using Cobol than either of Delphi, VC++, VB. Well,
screen design and layout are easily accomplished using Delphi and presumably
VB, but, database access is slightly more complicated on the other side.

Everybody talk about performance. There is no performance issue. From my
point of view, they're all alike. The profit one may gain from one language
to another in one field, is most often punched back in another field.

The interesting stuff for this comparition is, when we start talking about
portability. How about going on the web without a number of add ons? How
about have your stuff run on Unix? How about, how about, how about...

Given otherwise equality, I would vote for portability. Not that one really
need it today, but who knows?

Anyway, I do think that you should offer some time experiencing modern
Cobol. Definitely.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-035bce150a"></a>
## Thread: The Last Post? — 2 msgs

### The Last Post?

- **From:** "peter dashwood" <dashwood@freewebaccess.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f371fa@eeyore.callnetuk.com>`

```
Dear All,

as I have been having major problems with this NG and seem unable to rectify
them (I currently receive 80 messages out of over 300 on the NG) I intend to
unsubscribe from it.

Although I have enjoyed the lively debate and good points raised by many of
you, it is a bit pointless if I cannot see the full context of what is being
said.

I was reluctantly dragged into this forum by Jimmy Gavan after some private
mail which he felt would be of interest to members of the NG. I really have
enjoyed my time here (despite the outrageous demands it has made on a very
busy schedule) and I hope that at least some of the 97 messages I have
contributed have been of some use to somebody...if so, I consider the time
well spent. (If not, I still had fun <G>)

It is pleasing to see that guys like Thane, Jimmy, the two Bills and many
others too numerous to mention, are providing high standard help and
assistance to the COBOL community and ensuring that the language is alive
and well.

My respect, regards, and thanks to all of you.

If anyone wants to reach me (and I am always available to people who need
help, advice, or another perspective) please do so through my web site
(enter my name into Yahoo), or by my personal e-mail address:
pete_dashwood@compuserve.com

Best Wishes and 'bye,

Pete.
```

## Thread: "low-values" — 2 this month / 5 total

### "low-values"

- **From:** sysdev <sysdev@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
We have been experiencing problems with data containing binary zeros. We
want to check the data for low values, to exclude the records with this
kind of data. However,
the low-values parameter does not seem to work in our system. We are
using Micro-focus cobol, Aix 4.3 and Oracle 8. We have inserted binary
values into the data
but it seems to ignore them. Any suggestions?
```

#### ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-09-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<P4QI3.897$L4.110210@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

sysdev <sysdev@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca...
> We have been experiencing problems with data containing binary zeros. We
> want to check the data for low values, to exclude the records with this
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> but it seems to ignore them. Any suggestions?

How is the file you are reading defined?  If it is line sequential it might
interpret the low-value as the end of a string of data, a la C.  Reading it
as a binary file might get around this but you would have to insure each
logical record is exactly the correct length.  YMMV
```
